  • Normal cardiovascular findings in pregnancy are:
  • Apex beat is shifted to the 4th intercostal space; 2.5 cm outside the mid-clavicular line. PR increases Mildly elevated JVP Ejection Systolic murmur at apical/pulmonary area (grade 1/2) Rarely (20%): A soft diastolic murmur may be heard Mammary murmur – left 2nd and 3rd intercostal space – due to increased flow in the internal mammary vessels. S3 – due to rapid diastolic filling S4 – rare ECG – normal pattern; sometimes left axis deviation.
  • Ref: Page no 61, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 61, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following hormones is primarily secreted by fetal adrenal cortex?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. DHEAS

Educational Objective:

  • The fetus and the placenta interact in the formation of steroid hormones . The placenta synthesizes pregnenolone and progesterone from cholesterol. Some of the progesterone enters the fetal circulation and provides the substrate for the formation of cortisol and corticosterone in the fetal adrenal glands. Some of the pregnenolone enters the fetus and, along with pregnenolone synthesized in the fetal liver , is the substrate for the formation of dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate (DHEAS) and 16-hydroxydehydroepiandrosterone sulfate (16-OHDHEAS) in the fetal adrenal. Some 16-hydroxylation also occurs in the fetal liver . DHEAS and 16-OHDHEAS are transported back to the placenta, where DHEAS forms estradiol and 16-OHDHEAS forms estriol. The principal estrogen formed is estriol, and since fetal 16-OHDHEAS is the principal substrate for the estrogens, the urinary estriol excretion of the mother can be monitored as an index of the state of the fetus.
  • Ref: Page 281, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page 281, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 29-year-old G2P1 woman at 28 weeks’ gestation, who had normal haemoglobin level 4 weeks ago at her first prenatal visit, complains of 1 week of fatigue and now has a haemoglobin level of 7.0 g/ dL. She noted dark-coloured urine after taking an antibiotic for a urinary tract infection. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. G6PD deficiency anemia

Educational Objective:

  • G6PD deficiency results in increased sensitivity to drug induced haemolysis on intake of oxidant drugs . This can be seen in pregnancy also.
  • Ref: Page 2739, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page 2739, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following best describes the function of pregnancy associated plasma protein A (PAPP-A)?
options: Carrier protein for estrogen and progesterone
Maintains colloid osmotic pressure in maternal blood

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Immunosuppression against fetal antigens

Educational Objective:

  • Pregnancy-associated plasma protein-A (PAPP-A) largest of the pregnancy associated proteins is produced by both the embryo and the placenta (syncytio-cytotrophoblasts) during pregnancy. This protein is thought to have several different functions, including preventing recognition of the fetus by the maternal immune system, matrix mineralization and angiogenesis. Levels of PAPP-A rise from first detection in the first trimester until term. Maternal serum concentrations are related to subsequent fetal growth and this relationship has suggested that it can be used as a diagnostic test for adverse pregnancy outcomes ( intrauterine growth restriction, premature birth, preeclampsia, and stillbirth ).
  • Ref: Page no 863, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 863, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: W hich of the following hormones is primarily responsible for the physiological increase in maternal insulin resistance during pregnancy?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Progesterone is the hormone primarily responsible for the physiological increase in maternal insulin resistance during pregnancy . This resistance helps divert glucose to the developing fetus , ensuring an adequate energy supply . The combination of elevated progesterone and other hormones like human placental lactogen contributes to this insulin resistance .

Educational Objective:

  • Educational Objective: Pregnancy is a diabetogenic state
  • There is hyperinsulinemia due to hypertrophy/ hyperplasia of beta cells of pancreas. However, despite this, there is postprandial hyperglycemia due to peripheral insulin resistance mediated primarily by progesterone and also by estrogen, cortisol and hPL. There is also mild fasting hypoglycemia. There is no change in plasma glucagon levels.
  • Ref: Page No 56, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page No 56, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 28-year-old pregnant woman at 32 weeks of gestation presents with lower extremity edema. She is concerned about the swelling and asks the physician about the reason for this condition. Which of the following physiological changes in pregnancy primarily contributes to peripheral edema?

explanation.detail:
Educational Objective:

  • The primary physiological change in pregnancy contributing to peripheral edema is increased capillary permeability. Hormones like progesterone and estrogen cause the dilation of small blood vessels and increased capillary permeability . This allows for the escape of fluid and proteins into the extracellular space , leading to edema , particularly in the lower extremities.
  • Ref: Page No 55, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page No 55, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 24-year-old woman, who is 12 weeks pregnant, complains of frequent urination and a sensation of urinary urgency. Which of the following physiological changes in pregnancy is primarily responsible for her symptoms?
options: Decreased blood flow to the kidneys

explanation.short: A. Uterine compression of the bladder
explanation.detail:
Educational Objective:

  • The frequent urination and urinary urgency experienced by the pregnant woman are primarily due to uterine compression of the bladder. As the uterus enlarges and presses against the bladder, it reduces the bladder's capacity , leading to increased urinary frequency.
  • Ref: Page No 68 , William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page No 68 , William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

question: During pregnancy, the maternal cardiovascular system undergoes significant changes to support the growing fetus. Which of the following adaptations is a physiological change unique to pregnancy that contributes to maintaining systemic blood pressure and uteroplacental perfusion?

explanation.short: D. Activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system
explanation.detail:
Educational Objective:

  • Blood, red blood cell, and plasma volume
  • Systolic, diastolic, and mean arterial pressure
  • Pulmonary capillary wedge pressure
  • Left ventricular ejection fraction
  • Left ventricular diastolic function

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 23-year woman presents to the OPD. She is recently married and has missed her period and is overdue by 2 weeks. She wants to know if she is pregnant or not. If she is pregnant which will NOT be seen on clinical examination?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Robert’s sign

Explanation:

  • Let’s Discuss all Options:
  • Option D. Robert sign is the presence of a gas shadow in the aorta and other large vessels following intra-uterine fetal demise. Option A, B, C. Jaquemier, Hegar and Osiander signs are all seen in pregnancy.

Educational Objective:

  • Finding it Difficult to remember? These may help!
  • “Jack & Chad had a blue van. Ossy’s pulse increased.” “A soft cervix is good”

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 23-year primigravida presents at 6 weeks with spotting. She is very anxious. On ultrasound, there is a single live intrauterine fetus corresponding to 6 weeks and cardiac activity is seen. She is reassured that this spotting could be normal and due to fusion of the decidua. What is this sign known as?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Placental sign

Explanation:

  • Placental sign is mild bleeding/ spotting which may be seen before complete fusion of the decidua vera with the decidua capsularis and is also called implantation bleed ; It may be cyclical and may last till 12 weeks.
  • Palmer sign, Piscacek’s sign and Hegar sign are all clinical signs of pregnancy in the 1st trimester

Educational Objective:

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 25-year woman has missed her period. She does a urine pregnancy test at home to see if she is pregnant. Which is correct regarding the urine pregnancy test?
options: It can detect βhCG in the urine and appears before βhCG can be detected in the blood.
Most pregnancy test detect the α subunit of beta hCG
Maximum level of hCG is reached at about 50 days of a missed period
Tests based on ELISA are positive on the 1st day of a missed period.

explanation.short: D. Tests based on ELISA are positive on the 1st day of a missed period.
explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Tests based on ELISA are positive on the 1stday of a missed period.

Explanation:

  • Let’s Discuss all options:
  • Option A. It can detect βhCG in the urine and appears before βhCG can be detected in the blood. This is incorrect. First beta hCG appears in the blood and then in the urine Option B. Most pregnancy tests detect the α subunit of beta hCG: This is also incorrect. Most tests detect the beta subunit of hCG
  • Option C. Maximum level of hCG is reached at about 50 days of a missed period. This is also incorrect. Maximum levels of beta hCG are at 10 weeks (70-80 days)
  • Option D. Tests based on ELISA are positive on the 1st day of a missed period. This is a correct statement.

Educational Objective:

  • Pregnancy tests depend on detection of the antigen (hCG); usually the beta unit, present in the maternal urine or serum with antibody either polyclonal or monoclonal available commercially Maximum levels of beta hCG are at 10 weeks (70-80 days) Serum detection of hCG is earlier than detection of hCG in the urine ELISA based tests are positive as early as on the 1st day of a missed period (urine) and 4-5 days before a missed period (serum)
  • Ref: Page 62, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics; 9th edition
explanation.ref: Page 62, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics; 9 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 28-year G2 presents at 5 weeks pregnancy with mild abdominal pain. On examination, there is no pallor, and her vital signs are stable. An ultrasound reveals the following. Which is correct regarding this?
options: A follow up ultrasound after 2 weeks is advised
Beta hCG to be done with serial monitoring

explanation.short: A. A follow up ultrasound after 2 weeks is advised
explanation.detail:
Ans. A. A follow up ultrasound after 2 weeks is advised

Explanation:

  • This image shows an intra-uterine gestational sac which is seen at 5 weeks of pregnancy As a gestational sac is seen, the next best step would be to follow up with a repeat ultrasound after 10 – 14 days to look for the fetal pole and cardiac activity.
  • Option B. Beta hCG to be done with serial monitoring: This is indicated when the ultrasound does not show anything, i.e. a pregnancy of unknown location. Option C. Laparoscopy and salpingotomy: This is indicated in an unruptured ectopic pregnancy. In this case the gestational sac is seen in the uterus. Option D. Inj Methotrexate 50mg/m 2 : This is indicated in a patient with unruptured ectopic pregnancy but in this case, the gestational sac is seen in the uterus

Educational Objective:

  • Features of an intra-uterine gestational sac: Eccentric Regular and round Double decidual sac sign seen Intra-decidual sac sign seen Yolk sac seen (at 5.5 weeks) Associated peripheral vascularity seen Order of appearance of structures on a Trans-vaginal Scan in an intra-uterine pregnancy (These appear 1 week later on a Trans-abdominal scan) 5 weeks: Gestational sac 5.5 weeks: Yolk sac (1st anatomic structure to be seen in the gestational sac) 6 weeks: Fetal pole with cardiac activity
  • Ref: Page 158, William’s Obstetrics; 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page 158, William’s Obstetrics; 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A woman presents to the antenatal OPD. She is not sure of her dates. On abdominal examination, external ballottement is elicited. When is the earliest, this sign can be seen?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. 18-20 weeks

Explanation:

  • External Ballotment is as shown below. It appears at 18-20 weeks

Educational Objective:

  • This is External Ballotment. External Ballottement can be elicited after 18-20 weeks by a push to the fetal parts with one hand abdominally and the other hand receiving the impulse
  • This is internal Ballottement. It can be elicited at 16 weeks by a push to the fetal parts with the two fingers through the anterior fornix.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 27-year primigravida visits the antenatal OPD at 24 weeks. An intern examines the patient. Which is NOT an expected finding in this patient?
options: The uterine fundus is palpated at the upper border of the umbilicus

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Hegar’s sign

Educational Objective:

  • In the 2nd trimester, the following are seen:
  • On Inspection: Striae gravidarum and linea nigra are seen On Palpation: Uterus palpable at 12 – 14 weeks, lower border of umbilicus at 20 weeks and upper border at 24 weeks External ballotment at 18-20 weeks Internal ballotment after 16 weeks Fetal Heart rate can be auscultated as early as 20 weeks

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: An intra-decidual sign is seen in:

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. An intrauterine pregnancy

Explanation:

  • 2 important signs on USG in early intrauterine pregnancy:
  • Intra-decidual sign – An anechoic center surrounded by a single echogenic rim Double decidual sign – 2 concentric echogenic rings surrounding the gestational sac
  • Option A. A blighted ovum : In a blighted ovum, on ultrasound, no fetal pole is seen even at a mean sac diameter of 25mm Option B. A missed abortion: This is defined as either the absence of cardiac activity even when the fetal pole is 7mm or the disappearance of an earlier present cardiac activity Option C. An ectopic pregnancy: An unruptured ectopic will have several ultrasound signs including A pseudo sac A gestational sac in the adnexal region Bagel sign Blob sign Ring of fire sign

Educational Objective:

  • 2 important signs on USG in early intrauterine pregnancy:
  • Intra-decidual sign – An anechoic center surrounded by a single echogenic rim Double decidual sign – 2 concentric echogenic rings surrounding the gestational sac
  • Double decidual sac sign:
  • is a useful feature on early pregnancy ultrasound to confirm an early intrauterine pregnancy (IUP) Especially useful when the yolk sac or embryo is still not visualized It consists of the decidua parietalis (decidua lining the uterine cavity) and decidua capsularis (decidua lining the gestational sac) It is seen as two concentric rings surrounding an anechoic gestational sac.
  • Where the two adhere is the decidua basalis, and is the site of future placental formation
  • Earliest sign of an intrauterine pregnancy on ultrasound Gestational sac seen eccentric to central endometrial cavity line Rapidly progresses to double decidual sac sign if the pregnancy is normal

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 23-year primigravida presents at 8 weeks. She complains of nausea and occasional vomiting. Which is true regarding nausea and vomiting in pregnancy?
options: Majority of women complain of increased vomiting in the morning
Excessive vomiting is seen more in women with single than multiple fetuses
It more commonly occurs in women with a h/o migraine

explanation.short: D. It more commonly occurs in women with a h/o migraine
explanation.detail:
Ans. D. It more commonly occurs in women with a h/o migraine

Educational Objective:

  • Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy is a common symptom; It is seen in about 70 % of all pregnant women. Erroneously called morning sickness as it can occur at any time and is not specific to the morning Starts in the 1st trimester 50% are relieved by 14 weeks and 90% by 22 weeks. More common in Multiple pregnancy Molar pregnancy (Increased βhCG) h/o nausea and vomiting in an earlier pregnancy h/o nausea and vomiting with OCPs Mother or sister with morning sickness during pregnancy h/o migraine
  • Ref: Page 158, William’s Obstetrics; 25th edition; Page no 117, Gabbe Obstetrics 8th edition
explanation.ref: Page 158, William’s Obstetrics; 25 th edition; Page no 117, Gabbe Obstetrics 8 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 21-year primigravida presents at 21 weeks. She is anxious as she has still not perceived “Quickening”. All are possible reasons except?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Multiple pregnancy

Explanation:

  • Multiple pregnancy will result in earlier quickening and not in delayed quickening Delayed quickening is seen in Anterior placenta Maternal obesity Polyhydramnios or oligohydramnios Fetal demise

Educational Objective:

  • “Quickening” is “feeling of life” It is usually felt at about the 18th week in primis and 2 weeks earlier in multis. Its appearance is a useful guide to see how accurate the dating is especially in women who are unsure of their LMP and do not have an early trimester ultrasound. Delayed quickening is seen in Anterior placenta Maternal obesity Polyhydramnios or oligohydramnios Fetal demise
  • Ref: Page no 63, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics, 9th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 63, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics, 9 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 28-year primigravida presents with amenorrhoea. Her UPT is positive. She is not sure of her LMP. An ultrasound is done which shows the following. Which is NOT a possible diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Missed abortion

Explanation:

  • The ultrasound shows uterus with a normal endometrial lining. There is no evidence of an intra-uterine gestational sac seen. The following possibilities thus arise : (Option A, B and D)
  • A very early pregnancy. An intrauterine gestational sac is visible on ultrasound only when the serum beta hCG crosses a certain value. This is called the discriminatory zone and is 6000IU/ml for transabdominal scans and 1500 IU/ml for transvaginal scans. If the beta hCG is below 1500, a gestational sac will not be seen An ectopic pregnancy: The image seen is only of the uterus. An ectopic pregnancy which is very early also may not be apparent on ultrasound A pregnancy of unknown location: This is when there is no evidence of pregnancy on the ultrasound despite the urine pregnancy test being positive. Serial beta hCG tests are done in such scenarios
  • Option C. In a missed abortion, on ultrasound, there will be an intrauterine gestational sac seen on ultrasound. A missed abortion is diagnosed when an earlier cardiac activity is not seen or when the CRL is 7 mm but no cardiac activity is seen.

Educational Objective:

  • In a missed abortion, there will be an intrauterine gestational sac seen on ultrasound. A missed abortion is diagnosed when an earlier cardiac activity is not seen or when the CRL is 7 mm but no cardiac activity is seen.
  • Ref: Page 158, William’s Obstetrics; 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page 158, William’s Obstetrics; 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 23-year woman presents with 6 weeks amenorrhoea. She is not unsure if she is pregnant or not. Which is the next best step?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Clinical examination

Explanation:

  • In the question, the next best step is asked A clinical examination always precedes any investigation
  • Option B. Serum HCG: This is indicated only in situations with a positive urine pregnancy test, but nothing seen on the ultrasound (pregnancy of unknown location)
  • Option C. Urine HCG: This would be the most practical step especially and is usually done in a patient with suspected pregnancy. However, in the ideal setting, and for answering in an exam, a clinical exam always precedes tests.
  • Option D. Once pregnancy is suspected on clinical exam or the urine pregnancy test is positive, a TVS is done.

Educational Objective:

  • In a patient with suspected pregnancy, examination is always the next immediate step
  • Ref: Page 60; DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics; 9th edition
explanation.ref: Page 60; DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics; 9 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 28-year G2 presents to the antenatal OPD. She is 14 weeks gestation and this is her 1 st visit. She has 1 child who is 3 years old delivered vaginally. Which will be seen on examination in this patient?
options: Uterus will be palpable just above the pubic symphysis

explanation.short: D. Uterus will be palpable just above the pubic symphysis
explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Uterus will be palpable just above the pubic symphysis

Educational Objective:

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: The most accurate determination of gestational age in a pregnancy is by:

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. CRL on ultrasound

  • Crown-Rump Length (CRL) is the most accurate measurement of gestational age with a variation of ± 5 days.
  • Other options : LMP, date of coitus or date of quickening are not as accurate as CRL for determination of gestational age.

question: A 35-year-old woman with irregular menstrual cycles presents with nausea and breast tenderness. She suspects pregnancy and wants to know the earliest test that can provide accurate results. Which diagnostic test is the most sensitive for early pregnancy detection?

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 30-year-old woman in her first trimester presents for a prenatal visit. Her last menstrual period (LMP) was 8 weeks ago. On physical examination, the uterus is not palpable abdominally. What is the next best step in confirming the gestational age of the pregnancy?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Transvaginal ultrasound

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 26-year-old woman with a history of ectopic pregnancy presents with abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding. She suspects she might be pregnant. Which diagnostic test is essential in this situation to rule out an ectopic pregnancy?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Transvaginal ultrasound

Explanation:

  • In a woman with a history of ectopic pregnancy and symptoms of abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding, it is crucial to rule out another ectopic pregnancy. Transvaginal ultrasound is the preferred diagnostic test as it can visualize the uterus and adnexa and determine the location of the pregnancy, which is essential in cases of suspected ectopic pregnancy. If the TVS is inconclusive, then the next best step would be to do a serum bhCG doubling (0h and 48h)
  • Option A: Serum hCG will not be the initial test do diagnose an ectopic. Only if the ultrasound is inconclusive, do we check the serum bhCG at 0h and 48h Option B: Urine bhCG just confirm pregnancy. It does not tell us the site of pregnancy Option D: Serum progesterone levels increase in pregnancy

Educational Objective:

  • A patient with amenorrhoea (early pregnancy) and bleeding or pain, the first test done is always a Transvaginal scan (TVS)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A middle-aged woman came to OPD with a twin pregnancy. She already had 2 first trimester abortions and she has a 3-year-old female child who was born at the end of the 9 th month of gestation. Which of the following is her accurate representation?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Determining the Parity Index/ Obstetric Formula
  • Gravida (G): The number of times a woman has conceived including the present pregnancy irrespective of the gestational age or the outcome. Para (P): The number of times a pregnancy has crossed the period of viability (taken as 28 weeks for all practical purposes) EXCLUDING the present pregnancy. Living (L): The total number of living children the woman has. Abortion (A): The total number of times the pregnancy has resulted in an abortion/ pregnancy has not crossed the period of viability.
  • In this patient, this is her 4th pregnancy, so she is G4; she has 1 pregnancy who was born at term, so she is P1; she has 2 abortions so she is A2 and she has 1 living child so she is L1.
  • *** IMPORTANT: A Twin pregnancy which has crossed the period of viability is considered as Para 1 NOT Para 2. But if it results in 2 living children it will be Live 2. ***
  • 2. P A-B-C-D (A: term; B: preterm; C: abortions; D: Living)
  • This is another way to write the formula.

Educational Objective:

  • Gravida (G): The number of times a woman has conceived including the present pregnancy irrespective of the gestational age or the outcome. Para (P): The number of times a pregnancy has crossed the period of viability (taken as 28 weeks for all practical purposes) EXCLUDING the present pregnancy. Living (L): The total number of living children the woman has. Abortion (A): The total number of times the pregnancy has resulted in an abortion/ pregnancy has not crossed the period of viability.
  • Ref: Page 160, William’s Obstetrics; 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page 160, William’s Obstetrics; 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All are true regarding the definition of para except:
options: It includes all pregnancies that have crossed the period of viability.
It is taken as 2 in case of a previous twin pregnancy.
It does not include spontaneous abortions.

explanation.short: C. It is taken as 2 in case of a previous twin pregnancy.
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. It is taken as 2 in case of a previous twin pregnancy

Explanation:

  • Let’s Discuss all Options
  • Option A: It excludes the present pregnancy: Para (P) is the number of times a pregnancy has crossed the period of viability (taken as 28 weeks for all practical purposes) EXCLUDING the present pregnancy Option B: It includes all pregnancies that have crossed the period of viability: Para (P) is the number of times a pregnancy has crossed the period of viability (taken as 28 weeks for all practical purposes) EXCLUDING the present pregnancy Option C: It is taken as 2 in case of a previous twin pregnancy. This is wrong. In a previous twin pregnancy, P is 1 as it is the number of pregnancies that are counted and not the number of fetuses. Option D: It does not include spontaneous abortions: Spontaneous or induced abortions are denoted by A.

Educational Objective:

  • The obstetric formula is written as:
  • Gravida (G): The number of times a woman has conceived including the present pregnancy irrespective of the gestational age or the outcome. Para (P): The number of times a pregnancy has crossed the period of viability (taken as 28 weeks for all practical purposes) EXCLUDING the present pregnancy. Living (L): The total number of living children the woman has. Abortion (A): The total number of times the pregnancy has resulted in an abortion/ pregnancy has not crossed the period of viability.
  • *** IMPORTANT: A Twin pregnancy which has crossed the period of viability is considered as Para 1 NOT Para 2. But if it results in 2 living children it will be Live 2. ***
  • 2. P A-B-C-D (A: term; B: preterm; C: abortions; D: Living)
  • This is another way to write the formula.
  • Ref: Page 160, William’s Obstetrics; 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page 160, William’s Obstetrics; 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Identify the examination being done in the image below:

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Lateral grip

Explanation:

  • The maneuver shown in the image is the Lateral Grip or Leopold’s 2nd maneuver

Educational Objective:

  • Leopold in 1894 described 4 abdominal maneuvers to diagnose fetal presentation. These maneuvers may be difficult in
  • Obesity Oligohydramnios and polyhydramnios Anterior placenta Fundal grip (A): to assess which fetal pole (cephalic or podalic) occupies the fundus. The breech gives the sensation of a large nodular mass; the head feel hard, round and mobile The Lateral grip (B): determines the side of the back (hard, resistant) and the limbs (numerous, small irregular mobile parts) The 1st pelvic grip (3rd maneuver aka Pawlik grip - C): determining fetal presentation. The 2nd pelvic grip (4th maneuver - D): determines the degree of descent. This maneuver is done facing the feet of the patient and trying to approximate the hands of the examiner. IF the hands approximate; the head has not descended into the pelvis. If the hands diverge, the head has descended into the pelvis.
  • Ref: Page 424, William’s Obstetrics; 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page 424, William’s Obstetrics; 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All the following methods can be used to calculate the EDD except:
options: Adding 9 months and 7 days to the LMP
Subtracting 3 months and adding 7 days to the LMP

explanation.short: B. Adding 38 weeks to the LMP
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Normal duration of pregnancy: 280 days (40 weeks); hence Option B is incorrect. To arrive at the EDD, we add 280 days or 40 weeks to the LMP.
  • Calculating the EDD: Naegele’s formula . To the LMP (1st day of the last menstrual day Add 9 months + 7 days Subtract 3 months and add 7 days For IVF Pregnancies: add 38 weeks to the date of the embryo transfer

Educational Objective:

  • Calculating the EDD: Naegele’s formula . To the LMP (1st day of the last menstrual day) Add 9 months + 7 days Subtract 3 months and add 7 days For IVF Pregnancies: add 38 weeks to the date of the embryo transfer Remember the EDD falls at 40 weeks (280 days) from the LMP Dating the pregnancy: If discrepancy in dates/ unsure of LMP; the best way to determine the dating is by ULTRASOUND 1st trimester CRL: most accurate (till 14 weeks) BPD in the 2nd trimester
  • Ref: Page no 168, William’s Obstetrics; 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 168, William’s Obstetrics; 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A primigravida was given dietary advice in the 2nd trimester. What should be the extra calorie intake?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Pregnancy requires a total additional of 80,000 Kcal; mostly in the latter half An average caloric increase of 300 Kcal/ day recommended If we divide this trimester wise, 0Kcal/ day in 1st trimester 340Kcal/ day in 2nd trimester 452Kcal/ day in 3rd trimesters

Educational Objective:

  • Pregnancy requires a total additional of 80,000 Kcal; mostly in the latter half An average caloric increase of 300 Kcal/ day recommended If we divide this trimester wise, 0Kcal/ day in 1st trimester 340Kcal/ day in 2nd trimester 452Kcal/ day in 3rd trimesters If a question is asked regarding extra calorie in the 1st trimester, the answer will be NO Additional Requirement. Women who breastfeed require approximately 500 additional kcal/day; beyond what is usually recommended for non-pregnant women RDA of proteins in pregnancy: 60 – 65g/ day (increase by 15g/ day)

question: A 25-year Primi gravida is at 24 weeks pregnancy. Her pre-pregnancy BMI was 22. She is concerned she hasn’t been gaining enough weight during pregnancy. What should her ideal weight gain (kg)/ week be?

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All are components of pre-conceptional counselling except?
options: Identifying potential high-risk factors like Hypertension, anemia, diabetes etc
Starting women on folic acid preconceptionally
Assessing for genetic diseases and offering counselling

explanation.short: D. Offering rubella immunization post conception
explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Offering rubella immunization post conception

Explanation:

  • Let’s Discuss all Options:
  • Option A: Identify high risk factors: Diabetes (HBA1C), HTN, Epilepsy, Thyroid disorders (TSH), Anemia (Hb) Option B: Pre-conceptional Folic Acid (0.4 mg/ day (400mcg per day) to low risk; 4 mg/ day to high risk for neural tube defects - NTD). Women at high risk for NTD are H/o previous baby with NTD Pre-gestational Diabetes Mellitus Folic acid deficiency Obesity Epilepsy/ on Antiepileptic Drugs (AED) Option C: Assess for genetic diseases (h/o sickle cell ds, cystic fibrosis, thalassemia etc.) Option D: Immunization status: Rubella; if unimmunized or status unknown, Rubella vaccination is to be given with advice to avoid conception till 1 month following vaccination as it is a live vaccine with potential teratogenic side effects to the fetus.

Educational Objective:

  • Components of pre-conceptional counseling:
  • Identify high risk factors: Diabetes (HBA1C), HTN, Epilepsy, Thyroid disorders (TSH), Anemia (Hb) Immunization status: Rubella; if unimmunized or status unknown, Rubella vaccination is to be given with advice to avoid conception till 1 month following vaccination as it is a live vaccine with potential teratogenic side effects to the fetus. Check the BMI and advise optimization prior to planning pregnancy Stop/change any teratogenic drug (antiepileptic, warfarin, ACE inhibitor, ARB) Pre-conceptional Folic Acid (0.4 mg/ day (400mcg per day) to low risk; 4 mg/ day to high risk for neural tube defects - NTD). Women at high risk for NTD are H/o previous baby with NTD Pre-gestational Diabetes Mellitus Folic acid deficiency Obesity Epilepsy/ on Antiepileptic Drugs (AED) Assess for genetic diseases (h/o sickle cell ds, cystic fibrosis, thalassemia etc.) Smoking, alcohol, drug abuse – Advise to STOP
  • Ref: Page no,172, 147 Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no,172, 147 Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: What is the WHO recommendation for Antenatal Visits?
options: At least 4 visits, including the registration visit
At least 4 visits excluding the registration visit
At least 8 visits with 1 in the 1st trimester, 2 in the 2nd trimester and 5 in the 3rd trimester
At least 8 visits, 2 in the 1st trimester, 2 in the 2nd trimester and 4 in the 3rd trimester

explanation.short: C. At least 8 visits with 1 in the 1st trimester, 2 in the 2nd trimester and 5 in the 3rd trimester
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. At least 8 visits with 1 in the 1sttrimester, 2 in the 2ndtrimester and 5 in the 3rdtrimester

Explanation:

  • As per the WHO, 8 contact visits are recommended in the antenatal period.

Educational Objective:

  • Protocol for Antenatal Visits
  • MOHFW (Indian guidelines) At least4 visits including the first (Registration visit) – Minimum requirement Suggested schedule 1st Visit: Within 12 weeks 2nd Visit: 14 – 26 weeks 3rd Visit: 28 – 34 weeks 4th visit: 36 weeks – term WHO (2016): 8 visits. 1st Trimester: Contact 1: upto 12 weeks 2nd Trimester Contact 2 (20 weeks) & Contact 3 (26 weeks) 3rd Trimester Contact 4 (30 week), Contact 5 (34 weeks), contact 6 (36 weeks), contact 7 (38 weeks) & Contact 8 (40 weeks)
  • Ideally (& as per ACOG): once every month till 28 weeks, every 2 weeks till 36 weeks and then weekly till 40 weeks: So total of 12 – 14 visits.
  • Ref: WHO Recommendations on Antenatal care for a positive pregnancy experience , MOHFW guidelines and Williams Obstetrics Pg 164 , 25th edition
explanation.ref: WHO Recommendations on Antenatal care for a positive pregnancy experience , MOHFW guidelines and Williams Obstetrics Pg 164 ,  25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Use of folic acid to prevent neural tube defects should be best initiated:
options: At least 3 months prior to conception
At least 6 months prior to conception

explanation.short: C. At least 3 months prior to conception
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. At least 3 months prior to conception

Explanation:

Educational Objective:

  • Lowers the risk of developing NTDs Daily intake of 400 mcg of folic acid in the periconceptional period can prevent NTDs The neural tube has already closed by 6 weeks hence it should be started much before this time

question: Iron and Folic acid supplementation dose for pregnant women in Anemia Mukt Bharat?

500 mcg folic acid and 60 mg iron daily
400 mcg folic acid and 100 mg iron daily
400 mcg folic acid and 60 mg daily

Ans. B. 500 mcg folic acid and 60 mg iron daily

  • Pregnant and Lactating women (of 0-6 months child): Should receive:

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A pregnant woman had a previous baby with history of neural tube defect. Which routine antenatal screening test should be offered?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) screening

Explanation:

  • Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) screening is a routine antenatal test typically offered around 15-20 weeks of gestation to assess the risk of neural tube defects and other fetal anomalies. Abnormal AFP levels may indicate the need for further diagnostic tests, such as amniocentesis.
  • Option A. NST : This is a test of antepartum fetal assessment and is usually done after 32 weeks Option B. Amniocentesis : Diagnostic amniocentesis is usually done for detecting fetal chromosomal anomalies Option D. Fetal kick count : This is a test of antepartum fetal assessment and is advised after 28 weeks

Educational Objective:

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following vaccines is NOT contraindicated in Pregnancy?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Rabies

Explanation:

  • Rabies vaccine is not contra-indicated in pregnancy and should be given when indicated
  • Most live vaccines (Rabies, rubella, varicella) are contra-indicated in pregnancy. However, yellow fever which is a live vaccine is an exception and can be given to pregnant women when travelling to an endemic area.

Educational Objective:

  • Rabies vaccine is safe in pregnancy and can be given when indicated

question: A 24-year woman in her 1 st antenatal checkup reveals to her Doctor that she had taken the 1 st dose of Hepatitis B immunization prior to conception. She now asks if she should continue with the immunization schedule or not. What is the correct response?
options: Continue with the scheduled immunization schedule.
Delay the completion of immunization till after delivery
Terminate the pregnancy and continue the immunization
Delay the immunization till after 20 weeks as organogenesis will be complete by then.

explanation.short: A. Continue with the scheduled immunization schedule.
explanation.detail:
Ans. A) Continue with the scheduled immunization schedule

Explanation:

  • Hepatitis B vaccination is safe in pregnancy and the immunization schedule can be continued if the patient becomes pregnant.

Educational Objective:

  • Those women completing their immunization schedule which was started pre conception High risk groups – Household contacts, partners are positive, multiple sexual partners, chronic liver disease, HIV +ve Dose: 0.5 ml IM at 0, 1, 6 months

question: What is the preferred schedule of immunization in pregnancy regarding tetanus as per the ministry of health and family welfare?
options: Inj Td: 1st dose: early in pregnancy, 2nd dose: After 4 weeks; booster: if received 2 Td doses in a pregnancy within the last 3 years
Inj TT 1st dose early in pregnancy, 2nd dose after 4 weeks and booster if received 2 TT doses in a pregnancy within the last 3 years
Inj Tdap 1st dose in the 2nd trimester and 2nd dose in the 3rd trimester
Inj TT or Td 1st dose in early pregnancy and 2nd dose after 4 weeks.

explanation.short: A. Inj Td: 1st dose: early in pregnancy, 2nd dose: After 4 weeks; booster: if received 2 Td doses in a pregnancy within the last 3 years
explanation.detail:
Ans. A) Inj Td: 1stdose: early in pregnancy, 2nddose: After 4 weeks; booster:  if received 2 Td doses in a pregnancy within the last 3 years

Explanation:

  • Vaccines given in pregnancy as per Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
  • TT, Td or Tdap Td has replaced TT (as per MOHFW) 1st dose: early in pregnancy 2nd dose: After 4 weeks Booster: if received 2 Td doses in a pregnancy within the last 3 years
  • **As per ACOG Committee Guidelines 2017, Tdap: One dose of Td replaced with Tdap preferably given between 27-36 weeks of gestation

  • TT, Td or Tdap Td has replaced TT (as per MOHFW) 1st dose: early in pregnancy 2nd dose: After 4 weeks Booster: if received 2 Td doses in a pregnancy within the last 3 years

question: A 25-year woman has received the 1 st dose of Gardasil vaccine. Before she receives the 2 nd dose, she conceives. What is the next best step?

Restart the entire vaccine schedule post-delivery

explanation.short: B. Continue the vaccine schedule post-delivery
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Continue the vaccine schedule post-delivery

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 32-year woman who is planning to conceive visits her doctor for a pre conceptional visit. The Doctor asks her if she has been immunized against Rubella. She is not sure so she is advised to get a IgG rubella done which is positive. What is the next best step?
options: Advise her not to conceive for the next 3 months.
Advise her not to conceive for the next 1 month.
Tell her she can safely conceive.

explanation.short: D. Tell her she can safely conceive.
explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Tell her she can safely conceive

Explanation:

  • A positive IgG rubella means that she is immunized against rubella She does not require any vaccination and can safely plan conception If she was IgG rubella negative, then she is susceptible to rubella in pregnancy and should be advised rubella vaccination As it is a live vaccine, she should not conceive for at least 1 month following vaccination

Educational Objective:

  • A positive IgG rubella indicates the patient is immune and does not require pre-conceptional rubella vaccination
  • Ref: Page no 499, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 28-year-old pregnant woman is scheduled for her prenatal visit. She inquires about vaccines recommended during pregnancy. Which vaccine is generally considered safe and recommended for pregnant women to protect both the mother and the newborn?
options: Tetanus, diphtheria, and acellular pertussis (Tdap) vaccine

explanation.short: B. Tetanus, diphtheria, and acellular pertussis (Tdap) vaccine
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Tetanus, diphtheria, and acellular pertussis (Tdap) vaccine

Explanation:

  • The Tdap vaccine is generally considered safe and recommended for pregnant women, typically between 27-36 weeks of gestation. This vaccine helps protect the mother from tetanus, diphtheria, and pertussis (whooping cough), and it also provides passive immunity to the newborn, as pertussis can be severe in infants.
  • Option C: The HPV vaccine is not typically given during pregnancy. It is usually recommended for females before becoming pregnant to prevent HPV infection, which can lead to cervical cancer. If a woman becomes pregnant during the HPV vaccination series, it is generally recommended to complete the series after pregnancy, as there is limited data on its use during pregnancy. Option A: The MMR vaccine is not typically recommended during pregnancy because it is a live attenuated vaccine, meaning it contains weakened forms of the viruses. Live vaccines are generally contraindicated during pregnancy due to potential risks to the developing fetus.

Educational Objective:

  • Tdap is safe in pregnancy; given between 27-36 weeks; can replace the 2nd dose of Td; provides immunity to mother and newborn against tetanus, diphtheria, and pertussis.
  • Ref: Page no 172, William’s Obstetrics, 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 172, William’s Obstetrics, 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 25-year-old pregnant woman, who is a healthcare worker, asks about receiving the hepatitis B vaccine during pregnancy. What is the most appropriate recommendation regarding hepatitis B vaccination for pregnant healthcare workers?
options: Administer the hepatitis B vaccine during pregnancy.
Delay hepatitis B vaccination until after delivery.
Hepatitis B vaccination is not necessary for pregnant healthcare workers.
Recommend hepatitis B vaccination after assessing the patient's risk factors.

explanation.short: D. Recommend hepatitis B vaccination after assessing the patient's risk factors.
explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Recommend hepatitis B vaccination after assessing the patient's risk factors.

Explanation:

  • Hepatitis B vaccination is recommended for pregnant healthcare workers, but the timing should be individualized. Pregnant healthcare workers should be assessed for their risk of exposure to hepatitis B and, if indicated, should receive the vaccine after careful consideration of the potential benefits and risks.

Educational Objective:

  • Hepatitis B vaccine is safe in pregnancy and is recommended to high risk groups
  • Ref: Page no 172, William’s Obstetrics, 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 172, William’s Obstetrics, 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 30-year-old pregnant woman is planning to travel to a region with a high risk of malaria. She inquires about receiving the yellow fever vaccine during pregnancy. What is the most appropriate recommendation regarding yellow fever vaccination for pregnant travelers?
options: Administer the yellow fever vaccine in the puerperium
Yellow fever vaccination is not necessary for pregnant travelers.
Advise against travel to yellow fever-endemic areas during pregnancy.

explanation.short: D. Advise against travel to yellow fever-endemic areas during pregnancy.
explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Advise against travel to yellow fever-endemic areas during pregnancy.

Explanation:

  • All live vaccines are contra-indicated in pregnancy However, an exception to this rule is the yellow fever vaccine as the benefits outweigh the risks. Pregnant women planning travel to yellow fever endemic areas should be advised to postpone travel until after pregnancy or avoid travel to those regions if possible; but if the travel cannot be avoided, then the yellow fever vaccine can be administered.

Educational Objective:

  • Yellow fever vaccine, even though a live vaccine can be administered to women traveling to an endemic area
  • Ref: Page no 172, William’s Obstetrics, 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 172, William’s Obstetrics, 25 th edition

options: Administer the influenza vaccine during pregnancy, regardless of the trimester.
Delay influenza vaccination until after delivery.
Administer the influenza vaccine only if there is an ongoing flu outbreak in the community. Influenza vaccination is not necessary for pregnant women.
Influenza vaccine cannot be administered during pregnancy or during lactation

explanation.short: A. Administer the influenza vaccine during pregnancy, regardless of the trimester.
explanation.detail:
Ans. A) Administer the influenza vaccine during pregnancy, regardless of the trimester.

Explanation:

Educational Objective:

  • The ACOG recommends influenza vaccine (inactivated) to all pregnant women irrespective of the trimester during the influenza season
  • Ref: Page no 172, William’s Obstetrics, 25th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following is not included in quadruple test for antenatal detection of Down syndrome?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Inhibin -B

Explanation:

  • The triple and Quadruple test are done between 15 – 21 weeks.
  • Components of Triple test are: Free β hCG, unconjugated Estriol (uE3) and Maternal Serum α-fetoprotein. Components of the Quadruple test are: Free β hCG, unconjugated Estriol (uE3), Maternal Serum α-fetoprotein. And Inhibin A. Inhibin A is a component of quadruple. Not Inhibin B
  • *Easy to remember: B for B ig; so in Down’s syndrome; B eta hCG and Inhi B in A: B ig/ Increased
  • Option A (AFP), Option B (beta hCG) and Option C (Estriol) are all components of the Quadruple marker

Educational Objective:

  • The Triple and Quadruple test are 2nd trimester biochemical screening tests for Down Syndrome and are done between 15 – 21 weeks.
  • Components of Triple test are: Free β hCG, unconjugated Estriol (uE3) and Maternal Serum α-fetoprotein. Components of the Quadruple test are: Free β hCG, unconjugated Estriol (uE3), Maternal Serum α-fetoprotein. And Inhibin A.
  • Ref: Page no 284, William’s Obstetrics, 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 284, William’s Obstetrics, 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 23-year primigravida is diagnosed to have Down syndrome of her fetus at 18 weeks. If a triple test was done, what would have been the report?
options: High HCG, low ue3, low AFP
Low HCG, high ue3, high AFP
High HCG, high ue3, low AFP
Low HCG, low uE3, high AFP

explanation.short: A. High HCG, low ue3, low AFP
explanation.detail:
Ans. A) High HCG, low ue3, low AFP

  • *Easy to remember: B for B ig; so in Down’s syndrome; B eta hCG and Inhi B in A: B ig/ Increased

Educational Objective:

  • The Triple and Quadruple test are 2nd trimester biochemical screening tests for Down Syndrome and are done between 15 – 21 weeks.
  • Components of Triple test are: Free β hCG, unconjugated Estriol (uE3) and Maternal Serum α-fetoprotein. Components of the Quadruple test are: Free β hCG, unconjugated Estriol (uE3), Maternal Serum α-fetoprotein. And Inhibin A.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Non-Invasive Prenatal Testing (NIPT) is?
options: Amniocentesis for evaluation of fetal chromosomal anomalies
Pre-conceptional gene testing of an ova
Maternal serum for evaluation of Down syndrome

explanation.short: C. Maternal serum for evaluation of Down syndrome
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Maternal serum for evaluation of Down syndrome

Explanation:

  • NIPT or cell free DNA screening: Salient features:
  • Introduced in 2011 It is a screening test (NOT DIAGNOSTIC) In 2020 (Oct), ACOG guidelines state that along with serum analytes and NT, cell free DNA can be offered to all women as the initial screening modality for Down syndrome. Prior to this the indications were Age ≥ 35 y A positive 1st /2nd trimester analyte-based screening test USG with minor (soft) aneuploidy marker Prior pregnancy with autosomal trisomy Known carriage of a balanced translocation Identification of DNA fragments that are derived primarily from apoptotic trophoblasts. Done after 9 – 10 weeks Results in 7 – 10 days 99% Detection rate for Down syndrome Limitations: Cost The DNA component may not actually reflect fetal DNA and instead may reflect: Placental mosaicism Early demise of an aneuploid twin Maternal mosaicism Occult maternal malignancy Limited role in multiple pregnancy In 4 – 8%: No result due to low fetal fraction of DNA
  • Option A. Amniocentesis for evaluation of fetal chromosomal anomalies: Amniocentesis is a diagnostic method for fetal chromosomal anomalies. It is preferably done after 15 weeks of gestation. Option B. Preconceptional gene testing for an ova: There is no such thing; however preimplantation genetic testing can be done of an embryo in IVF, prior to embryo transfer. Option D. Testing of maternal tissue: This is not a test for screening or diagnosis of Down syndrome

Educational Objective:

  • NIPT or cell free DNA screening: Salient features:
  • Introduced in 2011 It is a screening test (NOT DIAGNOSTIC) In 2020 (Oct), ACOG guidelines state that along with serum analytes and NT, cell free DNA can be offered to all women as the initial screening modality for Down syndrome. Prior to this the indications were Age ≥ 35 y A positive 1st /2nd trimester analyte-based screening test USG with minor (soft) aneuploidy marker Prior pregnancy with autosomal trisomy Known carriage of a balanced translocation Identification of DNA fragments that are derived primarily from apoptotic trophoblasts. Done after 9 – 10 weeks Results in 7 – 10 days 99% Detection rate for Down syndrome Limitations: Cost The DNA component may not actually reflect fetal DNA and instead may reflect: Placental mosaicism Early demise of an aneuploid twin Maternal mosaicism Occult maternal malignancy Limited role in multiple pregnancy In 4 – 8%: No result due to low fetal fraction of DNA
  • Ref: Page No 284, 285; William’s Obstetrics, 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page No 284, 285; William’s Obstetrics, 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following aneuploidies is most likely to be missed in prenatal screening?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Klinefelter Syndrome

Explanation:

  • Let’s Discuss all the Options
  • Option A and B. Prenatal aneuploidy screening is primarily used to detect Down syndrome (Trisomy 21). It can also detect Trisomy 18 (Edward) and Trisomy 13 (Patau). Option C. Turner Syndrome: Nuchal Translucency (NT) is used in prenatal aneuploidy screening and is increased in several conditions other than Down syndrome, and this includes Turner’s syndrome. Turner syndrome also has several other ultrasound features like Cystic hygroma Non-immune fetal hydrops Cardiac anomalies (mostly compromising the left outflow tract - CoA, HLHS, bicuspid aortic valve) Renal anomalies Pleural effusions Brachycephaly IUGR Option D. Klinefelter’s syndrome does not have any significant USG finding especially in the 1st and 2nd trimesters and hence potentially can be easily missed on prenatal screening.
  • Ref: Page No 282, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page No 282, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All are true regarding amniocentesis except?
options: It is the most commonly performed prenatal diagnostic procedure
Chromosomal microarray can be performed on uncultured amniocytes
Blood-tinged amniotic fluid is seen in transplacental passage of the needle
Procedure related loss rate is 1 -2% when performed by an experienced provider

explanation.short: D. Procedure related loss rate is 1 -2% when performed by an experienced provider
explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Procedure related loss rate is 1-2 % when performed by an experienced provider

Explanation:

  • Let’s Discuss all Options:
  • Option A. Amniocentesis is the most common prenatal diagnostic procedure. Option B. Chromosomal microarray can be performed on uncultured amniocytes: Following amniocentesis, analysis can be done using
  • Chromosomal microarray (CMA) – to assess copy number gains or losses: can be performed directly on uncultured amniocytes Karyotype analysis for aneuploidy: requires cultures amniocytes FISH to identify gain or loss of specific chromosomes.
  • Option C. Anterior placentation seen in 60% and blood-tinged fluid is more frequent if there is transplacental passage of the needle. Option D. Procedure related loss: 0.1 – 0.3 % when performed by an experienced provider. Early amniocentesis (11-14 weeks): significant higher rate of procedure related complications; like clubfoot, amnionic fluid leakage and fetal loss. Not done anymore.

Educational Objective:

  • Amniocentesis is the most common prenatal diagnostic procedure. Indications for amniocentesis include: Diagnosis of fetal genetic disorders Diagnosis of Congenital infection Evaluation of anemia in Rh alloimmunization Determination of Fetal lung maturity Following amniocentesis, analysis can be done using
  • Chromosomal microarray (CMA) – to assess copy number gains or losses: can be performed directly on uncultured amniocytes Karyotype analysis for aneuploidy: requires cultures amniocytes FISH to identify gain or loss of specific chromosomes.
  • Procedure: Ultrasound guided Done between 15- 20weeks Anterior placentation seen in 60% and blood-tinged fluid is more frequent if there is transplacental passage of the needle. For fetal karyotype, about 20 -30 ml withdrawn. Procedure related loss: 0.1 – 0.3 % when performed by an experienced provider. Early amniocentesis (11-14 weeks): significant higher rate of procedure related complications; like clubfoot, amnionic fluid leakage and fetal loss. Not done anymore.
  • Ref: Page No 292, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page No 292, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All are true regarding prenatal Down syndrome screening except?
options: The risk of a woman carrying a Down syndrome baby is about 1 in 70 at 40 years
Ultrasound features of Down syndrome at 11 – 14 weeks include increased NT and an absent nasal bone
Combined test includes an NT measurement and the dual marker
Ultrasound at 18 – 20 weeks will miss about 50% of Down syndrome fetuses

explanation.short: D. Ultrasound at 18 – 20 weeks will miss about 50% of Down syndrome fetuses
explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Ultrasound at 18 – 20 weeks will miss about 50% of Down syndrome fetuses

Explanation:

  • Let’s Discuss all the Options
  • Option A. The risk of Down syndrome increases with maternal age. At the age of 40y, the risk os about 1 in 70 Option B. At 11-14 weeks, ultrasound features of Down syndrome include An increased nuchal translucency An absent nasal bone Tricuspid regurgitation An abnormal flow in the ductus venosus Option C. Combined test refers to a combination of the NT and Dual marker Option D. An ultrasound at 18-20 weeks will detect upto 70% of Down syndrome babies. Hence this option is correct

Educational Objective:

  • Screening of Down Syndrome: Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome); is the most common non-lethal trisomy Background risk is based on age (At 25 y: 1 in 5000, 30 y: 1 in 1000, 33 y: 1 in 500, 35 y: 1 in 250, 40 y: 1 in 70, 45 y: 1 in 20) Ultrasound at 11 – 14 weeks: Features of Down Syndrome include: Increased NT Absent nasal bone Abnormal flow in the Ductus venosus Tricuspid Regurgitation 1st trimester screening for Down Syndrome includes the Dual marker and the NT. Detection rate for Down Syndrome by a 2nd trimester ultrasound is 70%
  • Ref: Page no 282, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 282, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All are serum analytes used in prenatal screening for Down Syndrome in the 2 nd trimester except?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D) PAPP-A

Explanation:

  • Serum analytes used in prenatal screening of Down syndrome are: 1st trimester (11 – 14 weeks): Dual marker: β hCG ( ↑ ) and PAPP-A ( ↓ ) 2nd trimester (15 – 21 weeks): Triple marker: β hCG ( ↑ ), unconjugated Estriol uE3 ( ↓ ) and Maternal serum Alpha fetoprotein – MSAFP ( ↓ ) Quadruple marker: β hCG ( ↑ ), Inhibin – A ( ↑ ), unconjugated Estriol uE3 ( ↓ ) and Maternal serum Alpha fetoprotein – MSAFP ( ↓ )
  • Thus, Option D) PAPP-A is not used in the second trimester.

Educational Objective:

  • Serum analytes used in prenatal screening of Down syndrome are: 1st trimester (11 – 14 weeks): Dual marker: β hCG ( ↑ ) and PAPP-A ( ↓ ) 2nd trimester (15 – 21 weeks): Triple marker: β hCG ( ↑ ), unconjugated Estriol uE3 ( ↓ ) and Maternal serum Alpha fetoprotein – MSAFP ( ↓ ) Quadruple marker: β hCG ( ↑ ), Inhibin – A ( ↑ ), unconjugated Estriol uE3 ( ↓ ) and Maternal serum Alpha fetoprotein – MSAFP ( ↓ )

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: The image below shows the Nuchal translucency (NT) on Ultrasound. A high NT is associated with fetal aneuploidies and poor perinatal outcome. At what gestational period is the NT measured?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D) 11 weeks – 13 weeks 6 days

Explanation:

  • Component of first trimester aneuploidy screening Represents maximum thickness of the subcutaneous translucent area between the skin and soft tissue overlying the fetal spine at the back of the neck. If NT increased, risk for Fetal aneuploidy Various structural anomalies: CVS, hydrops, cystic hygroma Measured between 11 weeks – 13 weeks 6 days (or 11- 14 weeks)

Educational Objective:

  • Component of first trimester aneuploidy screening Represents maximum thickness of the subcutaneous translucent area between the skin and soft tissue overlying the fetal spine at the back of the neck. If NT increased, risk for Fetal aneuploidy Various structural anomalies: CVS, hydrops, cystic hygroma Measured between 11 weeks – 13 weeks 6 days (or 11- 14 weeks) Criteria to measure the NT CRL between 45 – 84 mm Fetus in mid sagittal plane Fetus in neutral position Image must be magnified Widest space should be measured The amnion must be seen separately The ‘+’ calipers must be placed on the inner borders of the nuchal space
  • NT alone of used: 65% detection rate for Down Syndrome Combined test: NT + Dual marker (beta hCG + PAPP-A): 80% Detection rate NT > 3 mm/ > 99th percentile: Significant
  • Ref: Page no 186, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: The below ultrasound done at 12 weeks shows what measurement being taken?
options: Deepest vertical pocket of amniotic fluid

explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Nuchal Translucency

Explanation:

  • Let’s Discuss all the Options:
  • Option A: Deepest pocket of amniotic fluid: This measurement is used to determine the amniotic fluid volume. A normal DVP is 2-8 cm Option B: Measurement of cystic hygroma: A cystic hygroma is a large neck swelling seen in the fetus and is seen in Turner syndrome fetuses Option C: Nuchal fold thickness: This is a measurement taken at the level 2 scan and is seen in various anomalies and chromosomal aneuploidies Option D: The measurement shown here is the nuchal translucency. An increased NT is a marker of fetal aneuploidies and various fetal structural anomalies

Educational Objective:

  • Component of first trimester aneuploidy screening Represents maximum thickness of the subcutaneous translucent area between the skin and soft tissue overlying the fetal spine at the back of the neck. If NT increased, risk for Fetal aneuploidy Various structural anomalies: CVS, hydrops, cystic hygroma
  • Measured between 11 weeks – 13 weeks 6 days (or 11- 14 weeks) Criteria to measure the NT CRL between 45 – 84 mm Fetus in mid sagittal plane Fetus in neutral position Image must be magnified Widest space should be measured The amnion must be seen separately The ‘+’ calipers must be placed on the inner borders of the nuchal space
  • NT alone of used: 65% detection rate for Down Syndrome Combined test: NT + Dual marker (beta hCG + PAPP-A): 80% Detection rate NT > 3 mm/ > 99th percentile: Significant

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: What is this procedure?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A) Amniocentesis

Explanation:

  • The image shows an amniocentesis. Amniocentesis is aspiration of the amniotic fluid for therapeutic or diagnostic procedures
  • Option B: Chorionic villous sampling is as shown below. It is a procedure done between 10-13 weeks for diagnosis of chromosomal and genetic abnormalities
  • Option C: Percutaneous blood sampling or cordocentesis: This is as shown in the image below and is aspiration of blood from the umbilical vein for diagnostic or therapeutic purposes. It can be done > 18 weeks.
  • Option D: Measuring cell free DNA: This is a screening test for Down syndrome wherein a maternal blood sample is taken and fetal DNA is extracted.

Educational Objective:

  • Most Commonly performed prenatal diagnostic procedure Done at 15 – 20 weeks for prenatal diagnosis but can be done later also Indications: Diagnosis of fetal genetic disorders (prenatal diagnosis) Diagnosis of congenital infections Rh alloimmunization (measurement of amniotic fluid bilirubin to determine severity of fetal anemia) Assessment of fetal lung maturity (Lecithin/ Sphingomyelin ratio and measurement of amniotic fluid phosphatidyl glycerol in the amniotic fluid)
  • Technique: 20 – 22 -gauge spinal needle Ultrasound guidance Needle inserted into amniotic fluid pocket Avoid umbilical cord, fetal parts Aspirate 10 – 20 ml if sending for fetal karyotype/FISH or chromosomal microarray Discard initial 1-2 ml (may be contamination with maternal blood) If Rh D negative: and unsensitized: Give Anti-D Complications: Procedure related loss: 0.1-0.3% Amniotic fluid leakage Vaginal spotting Early amniocentesis 11- 14 weeks Higher procedure related loss, increased incidence of talipes equinovarus (clubfoot) Not done now
  • Ref: Page No 291, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page No 291, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Identify this procedure:

explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Chorionic Villous Sampling

Explanation:

  • This is an image showing Chorionic Villous Sampling (CVS). This is a procedure done between 10 – 13 weeks wherein chorionic villi obtained via transabdominal or transcervical route for diagnosis of fetal chromosomal or genetic abnormalities
  • Option A. Artificial Rupture of membranes (ARM): is a procedure as shown below. In this, the membranes are ruptured for induction of labor or augmentation of labor
  • Option C. Cordocentesis: This is as shown in the image below and is aspiration of blood from the umbilical vein for diagnostic or therapeutic purposes. It can be done > 18 weeks.
  • Indications: Diagnosis of fetal genetic disorders (prenatal diagnosis) Diagnosis of congenital infections Rh alloimmunization (measurement of amniotic fluid bilirubin to determine severity of fetal anemia) Assessment of fetal lung maturity (Lecithin/ Sphingomyelin ratio and measurement of amniotic fluid phosphatidyl glycerol in the amniotic fluid)

Educational Objective:

  • Chorionic Villous sampling is done between 10-13 weeks wherein chorionic villi obtained via transabdominal or transcervical route for diagnosis of fetal chromosomal or genetic abnormalities
  • Advantage: do not have to wait till 15 weeks for amniocentesis, results quicker Complications Overall loss rate: higher than amniocentesis because of background losses Procedure related loss: same as amniocentesis (0.1 -0.3%) If done < 7 weeks: limb reduction defects & oro mandibular defects Vaginal spotting
  • Ref: Page no 293, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 293, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: False statement regarding MSAFP is:
options: It is a component of the dual marker
It’s concentration in fetal plasma is 50000 times that in maternal plasma
It is increased in defects in fetal integument
Using an upper limit of MSAFP > 2.5 MoM, the detection rate of anencephaly is 90%

explanation.short: A. It is a component of the dual marker
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Dual marker consists of βhCG and PAPP-A
  • Option B. The concentration of MSAFP in fetal plasma is 50000 times greater than in maternal plasma Option C. It is increased in defect s in fetal integument like open neural tube defects (NTD’s) and defects of ventral wall (Gastroschisis and omphalocele) Option D. Using an upper limit of MSAFP > 2.5 MoM, the detection rate of anencephaly is 90%

Educational Objective:

  • MSAFP: Usually measured between 15 – 20 weeks It is increased in defects of fetal integument like open neural tube defects (NTD’s) and defects of ventral wall (Gastroschisis and omphalocele) It is the major protein in the fetal plasma Concentration is 50000 times that in maternal serum Using an MSAFP of > 2.5 MoM as the upper limit of normal, the NTD detection rate is 90% for anencephaly and 80% for spina bifida.

question: The following biochemical predictors are decreased in a woman at high risk of developing preeclampsia except:

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • PAPP-A, PlGF and VEGF: All are decreased in women at risk of developing preeclampsia

Educational Objective:

  • Screening of preeclampsia is based on history (Risk factors), Mean arterial pressure, Uterine artery Doppler at 11-14 weeks and biochemical factors.
  • Other predictors of preeclampsia:
  • Biophysical markers: Raised PI in the uterine Artery (11 – 14 weeks); notching of uterine Artery waveform (mid trimester). Biochemical markers:
  • Women at high risk of developing pre-eclampsia based on these parameters should be started on Low dose aspirin 75-150 mg before 16 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A raised uterine pulsatility index at 11-14 weeks on uterine artery doppler is a predictor for?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Preeclampsia

Explanation:

  • Uterine artery blood flow is estimated to rise from 50ml/min early in gestation to 500-700 ml/ min by term Doppler waveform of the uterine artery shows high diastolic flow velocity and markedly turbulent flow. Greater resistance to flow (High PI) and diastolic notching on a uterine artery Doppler at 11-14 weeks are predictors of Preeclampsia Gestational HTN Fetal growth restriction
  • Thus, option D) Pre-eclampsia is correct.
  • A raised uterine PI is not related to gestational diabetes, Down syndrome or placenta previa

Educational Objective:

  • Greater resistance to flow (High PI) and diastolic notching on a uterine artery Doppler at 11-14 weeks are predictors of Preeclampsia Gestational HTN Fetal growth restriction

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 26-year-old primigravida presents to the antenatal OPD for her first visit at 16 weeks. She opts for the triple screen. Her report shows an elevated maternal serum AFP level. An elevated maternal serum AFP level puts her at an increased risk for which of the following?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Gastroschisis

Educational Objective:

  • Fetal Conditions associated with an increased MSAFP
  • Neural tube defect Gastroschisis, Omphalocele Cystic hygroma Esophageal or intestinal obstruction Liver necrosis Renal anomalies Cloacal dystrophy Osteogenesis imperfecta Sacrococcygeal teratoma
  • Maternal conditions associated with an increased MSAFP:
  • Underestimated maternal age Placental abruption Oligohydramnios Preeclampsia Fetal growth restriction
  • Conditions associated with a decreased MSAFP
  • Obesity Diabetes Trisomy 21 GTD Fetal death Overestimated gestational age
  • Ref: Page no 284, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 30-year-old woman in her first trimester presents for prenatal care. She inquires about prenatal screening for chromosomal abnormalities. What is the most appropriate initial screening test for assessing chromosomal abnormalities in the first trimester?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Nuchal translucency ultrasound

Explanation:

  • Option A: AFP is usually done at16-18 weeks for detecting neural tube defects in the fetus as it is high in such patients. Option B: Noninvasive prenatal testing (NIPT) or cell free DNA is a screening test with a very high detection rate for fetal genetic abnormalities. It can be done after 9 weeks of pregnancy. However, it is usually not prescribed as an initial test due to its expense. In women who are elderly (> 35y) or other risk factors, it can be offered as a preliminary test Option C: Chorionic villous sampling: This is a diagnostic test for fetal genetic abnormalities done between 11-13 weeks.

Educational Objective:

  • The initial tests prescribed in a low risk pregnant woman for screening for chromosomal abnormalities include Nuchal translucency (11-14 weeks) Dual marker (βhCG and PAPP-A) Combined marker (NT + Dual marker) NIPT after 9 weeks (Can be offered as an initial test but is expensive so usually offered in elderly mothers or any other risk factor)
  • Ref: Page no 281, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 281, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 35-year-old woman presents for her first prenatal visit. She is concerned about the risk of chromosomal abnormalities due to her advanced maternal age. Which of the following prenatal screening tests is specifically recommended for women aged 35 and older?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Noninvasive prenatal testing (NIPT)

Explanation:

  • Option A: AFP is for detecting neural tube defects where it is found to be raised. Done between 16-18 weeks Option B & Option C: Amniocentesis and CVS are diagnostic tests and are advised if a screening test is positive.

Educational Objective:

  • The initial tests prescribed in a low risk pregnant woman for screening for chromosomal abnormalities include
  • Nuchal translucency (11-14 weeks) Dual marker (βhCG and PAPP-A) Combined marker (NT + Dual marker) NIPT after 9 weeks (Can be offered as an initial test but is expensive so usually offered in elderly mothers or any other risk factor)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 28-year-old pregnant woman is at her second trimester prenatal visit. She inquires about screening for neural tube defects. Which prenatal screening test is used to assess the risk of neural tube defects, such as spina bifida, in the second trimester?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A) Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) screening

Explanation:

  • Option B: Nuchal Translucency (NT): Done at 11-14 weeks as screening for Down syndrome. It is increased in fetuses with Down syndrome and also increased in other chromosomal abnormalities and congenital abnormalities like cardiac diseases. Option C & Option D: CVS and Amniocentesis are diagnostic techniques for detecting chromosomal abnormalities.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 32-year-old pregnant woman presents for her first prenatal visit. She is concerned about her risk of carrying a baby with a genetic disorder due to a family history of cystic fibrosis. What prenatal screening test should be offered to assess the risk of cystic fibrosis in this pregnancy?

explanation.short: C. Carrier screening for cystic fibrosis
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Carrier screening for cystic fibrosis

Explanation:

  • Cystic fibrosis is an autosomal recessive disease. Cystic Fibrosis is caused due to a mutation in the CFTR gene located on chromosome 7 and affects chloride channels. To assess the risk of carrying a baby with cystic fibrosis, carrier screening for cystic fibrosis is appropriate. This test analyzes the parents' carrier status for the cystic fibrosis gene mutations and provides information about the risk of having an affected child. If both parents are carriers, invasive tests such as CVS and amniocentesis should be offered.

Educational Objective:

  • Cystic fibrosis has an autosomal recessive pattern of inheritance and hence only if both partners are carriers, is diagnostic testing (CVS or amniocentesis) warranted
  • Ref: Page no 289, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 289, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 35-year-old pregnant woman with a family history of a genetic disorder is considering invasive prenatal testing. What are the two common invasive prenatal diagnostic tests that can provide definitive information about chromosomal or genetic abnormalities in the fetus?
options: Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) screening and nuchal translucency ultrasound
Chorionic villus sampling (CVS) and noninvasive prenatal testing (NIPT)

explanation.short: D. Chorionic villus sampling (CVS) and amniocentesis
explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Chorionic villus sampling (CVS) and amniocentesis

Explanation:

  • Amniocentesis Chorionic Villous Sampling Fetal blood sampling/ Cordocentesis/ Percutaneous Umbilical Vein Sampling
  • Most Commonly performed prenatal diagnostic procedure Done at 15 – 20 weeks for prenatal diagnosis but can be done later also Indications: Diagnosis of fetal genetic disorders (prenatal diagnosis) Diagnosis of congenital infections Rh alloimmunization (measurement of amniotic fluid bilirubin to determine severity of fetal anemia Assessment of fetal lung maturity (Lecithin/ Sphingomyelin ratio and measurement of amniotic fluid phosphatidyl glycerol in the amniotic fluid) Complications : Procedure related loss: 0.1-0.3% Amniotic fluid leakage Vaginal spotting
  • 2. Chorionic Villous Sampling
  • Done at 10 – 13 weeks Chorionic villi obtained via transabdominal or transcervical route Advantage: do not have to wait till 15 weeks for amniocentesis, results quicker Complications Overall loss rate: higher than amniocentesis because of background losses Procedure related loss: same as amniocentesis (0.1 -0.3%) If done < 7 weeks: limb reduction defects & oro mandibular defects Vaginal spotting
  • AFP is a noninvasive test (blood sample) for detection of neural tube defects Nuchal translucency is a screening test for Down syndrome done by ultrasound NIPT is cell free DNA. It is a screening test done after 9 weeks for fetal chromosomal abnormalities

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page no 293, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 293, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Identify the maternal condition that can lead to the findings in the baby as shown below.

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Pre – gestational diabetes

Explanation:

  • The image shown is of Sirenomelia/ mermaid syndrome Sirenomelia is the most specific anomaly seen in pregestational (overt) diabetes The most common anomaly seen in pregestational (overt) diabetes is cardiovascular anomalies (VSD).
  • Option B. Gestational diabetes is NOT associated with an increased incidence of fetal anomalies
  • Option C. Sodium Valproate is an antiepileptic drug (AED) and is associated with orofacial clefting, neural tube defects (NTDs) and cardiac anomalies. Amongst all AEDs, Valproate has the highest incidence of congenital anomalies (4-8-fold higher)
  • Option D. ACE inhibitors are associated with fetal renal ischemia and oligohydramnios-pulmonary hypoplasia sequence.

Educational Objective:

Gestational diabetes is NOT associated with an increased incidence of fetal anomalies

question: What could be a possible teratogenic agent that has caused this anomaly?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Thalidomide

Explanation:

  • The characteristic anomaly caused by Thalidomide is phocomelia (Absence of 1 or more long bone): Described in the Thalidomide tragedy in the USA form 1956-1960
  • Methotrexate: Craniosynostosis with a clover leaf skull, wide nasal bridge, low set ears and micrognathia Phenytoin: Fetal hydantoin syndrome (upturned nose, midfacial hypoplasia, distal digital hypoplasia) Doxycycline: tetracyclines: yellowish-brownish discoloration of deciduous teeth when used after 25 weeks of gestation

Educational Objective:

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following medication should never be used in pregnancy?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Methotrexate

Explanation:

  • Methotrexate is a folic acid antagonist and is potent teratogen
  • Methotrexate causes craniosynostosis with a clover leaf skull, wide nasal bridge, low set ears and micrognathia.
  • Chloroquine use should not be withheld where indicated on account of pregnancy. Cyclosporine and pyrazinamide are category C in pregnancy (should be used only when benefits outweigh the risks)
