subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Maternal use of which of the following hormone may cause hypospadias in a male child?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Diethylstilbesterol

Explanation:

  • Diethylstilbestrol (DES) is a synthetic nonsteroidal estrogen that was used to prevent miscarriage and other pregnancy complications between 1938 and 1971 In the United States. In 1971, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration issued a warning about the use of DES during pregnancy after a relationship between DES and the development of clear cell adenocarcinoma of the vagina and cervix was found in women whose mothers had taken diethylstilbestrol while they were pregnant. Effects of DES exposure in utero on girls Cervix: hypoplastic cervix, Cockscomb cervix, cervical collar, pseudo polyp Vagina: Clear cell adenocarcinoma, adenosis Uterus: T-shaped uterus Effects of DES exposure in utero on boys: Epididymal cysts Microphallus Undescended testes and small testes. Hypospadias
  • Option A. Tolbutamide: Insufficient studies; no major teratogenic effect noted till now Option B. Clomiphene: Some association with defects like NTDs and hypospadias has been found but larger studies are required. Option D. Clonazepam: Associated with orofacial clefts
  • *Hence the answer to this question should be DES and not Clomiphene as the evidence for DES causing hypospadias is higher

Educational Objective:

  • Diethylstilbestrol (DES) is a synthetic nonsteroidal estrogen that was used to prevent miscarriage and other pregnancy complications between 1938 and 1971
  • In the United States. In 1971, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration issued a warning about the use of DES during pregnancy after a relationship between DES and the development of clear cell adenocarcinoma of the vagina and cervix was found in women whose mothers had taken diethylstilbestrol while they were pregnant. Effects of DES exposure in utero on girls Cervix: hypoplastic cervix, Cockscomb cervix, cervical collar, pseudo polyp Vagina: Clear cell adenocarcinoma, adenosis Uterus: T-shaped uterus Effects of DES exposure in utero on boys: Epididymal cysts Microphallus Undescended testes and small testes. Hypospadias
  • Ref: Page no 244, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition; AAFP 2004: DES exposure
explanation.ref: Page no 244, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition; AAFP 2004: DES exposure

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Mobius syndrome in fetus may be caused by maternal intake of

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Misoprostol

Explanation:

  • A rare congenital neurological disorder Characterized by facial paralysis and the inability to move the eyes from side to side Most people with Möbius syndrome are born with complete facial paralysis and cannot close their eyes or form facial expressions. Limb and chest wall abnormalities sometimes occur with the syndrome. Associated with prenatal exposure to misoprostol and thalidomide
  • Methotrexate is a folic acid antagonist and is a potent teratogen; Methotrexate can cause craniosynostosis with a clover leaf skull, wide nasal bridge, low set ears and micrognathia. Mifepristone is not teratogenic. However, in early pregnancy it can lead to abortions and is also used for induction of labor at term Dinoprostone is Prostaglandin E2; it is not teratogenic and is used for induction of labor at term

Educational Objective:

  • A rare congenital neurological disorder Characterized by facial paralysis and the inability to move the eyes from side to side Most people with Möbius syndrome are born with complete facial paralysis and cannot close their eyes or form facial expressions. Limb and chest wall abnormalities sometimes occur with the syndrome. Associated with prenatal exposure to misoprostol and thalidomide

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A woman comes for pre-conceptional counselling. She is a known hypertensive and is taking losartan. She is explained that she needs to change the antihypertensive to a safer non teratogenic drug. She asks you what the possible effects of losartan on the fetus are.

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Renal anomalies

Explanation:

  • ACE inhibitors and ARBs cause:
  • Fetal renal hypoperfusion leading to subsequent ischemia and anuria. This leads to oligohydramnios and pulmonary hypoplasia. This leads to: limb contractures, calvarium maldevelopment and Fetal growth restriction.

Educational Objective:

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 26-year-old Primigravida presents at 33 weeks in preterm labor. All the following can be given as tocolysis except?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Indomethacin

Explanation:

  • Indomethacin is a tocolytic but may cause constriction of the fetal ductus arteriosus and subsequent pulmonary hypoplasia.
  • Risk of closure is higher is taken after 32 weeks for > 72 hours Hence, beyond 32 weeks, indomethacin is not given for tocolysis.
  • Other options A) Atosiban, C) Nifedipine and D) Magnesium sulphate can be safely given in this case.

Educational Objective:

  • Indomethacin is a tocolytic but may cause constriction of the fetal ductus arteriosus and subsequent pulmonary hypoplasia.
  • Risk of closure is higher is taken after 32 weeks for > 72 hours Hence, beyond 32 weeks, indomethacin is not given for tocolysis.

question: Lithium in pregnancy is associated with an increased incidence of?

  • This is an otherwise rare anomaly but has a 3% risk in women on Lithium. Fetal ECHO is indicated in women on Lithium

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A pregnant woman with a known cardiac disease presents to you in the first trimester. She gives a history of warfarin intake. What should be advised now?
options: Replace warfarin with heparin in the 1st trimester

explanation.short: D. Replace warfarin with heparin in the 1st trimester
explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Replace Warfarin with heparin in the 1sttrimester

Explanation:

  • A patient who is on warfarin is at risk of warfarin embryopathy so she should be shifted to heparin in the 1st trimester. In the 2nd trimester, warfarin can be re-started. In the 3rd trimester, we again must shift to heparin.
  • Option A. Continue warfarin throughout pregnancy: Warfarin is unsafe in the 1st trimester (risk of warfarin embryopathy) and in the 3rd trimester (Risk of intra-cranial hemorrhage in the fetus) Option B. Give coumarin: Warfarin is a coumarin Option C. Stop warfarin: This will increase her risk of thrombo-embolism so it should not be stopped

Educational Objective:

  • Warfarin is known to crosses the placenta and is teratogenic and causes warfarin embryopathy Warfarin embryopathy - stippled epiphysis and nasal hypoplasia It can also cause CNS defects and developmental delay Results from fetal exposure between the 6th and 9th weeks In the 3rd trimester there is a risk of hemorrhage into fetal structures Hence, switch over to heparin preconceptionally or in the first trimester and then again in the third trimester.
  • Ref: Page no 247, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 247, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Tobacco in pregnancy is associated with all the following birth defects except

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Renal anomalies

Explanation:

  • Renal anomalies are unlikely to be seen in tobacco and cigarette smoking
  • Tobacco and cigarette smoking have been associated with the following adverse effects:
  • Poland sequence (2-fold higher risk): unilateral aplasia of chest wall muscles and webbed fingers Cardiac anomalies Hydrocephaly, microcephaly Omphalocele, gastroschisis Cleft lip and palate Hand abnormalities Fetal growth restriction Preterm birth Placenta previa Abruptio placentae Spontaneous abortion Sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS) Increased risk of childhood asthma and obesity

Educational Objective:

  • Poland sequence (2-fold higher risk): unilateral aplasia of chest wall muscles and webbed fingers Cardiac anomalies Hydrocephaly, microcephaly Omphalocele, gastroschisis Cleft lip and palate Hand abnormalities Fetal growth restriction Preterm birth Placenta previa Abruptio placentae Spontaneous abortion Sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS) Increased risk of childhood asthma and obesity

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 23-year woman has been taking isotretinoin for acne. She now is recently married and is planning conception. After how long of stopping isotretinoin should she conceive?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. 3 weeks

Educational Objective:

  • Isotretinoin is contraindicated during pregnancy as one of the stronger teratogen Retinoic acid embryopathy— involve the CNS, face, heart, and thymus. Specific anomalies may include ventriculomegaly, maldevelopment of the facial bones or cranium, microtia or anotia, micrognathia, cleft palate, conotruncal heart defects, and thymic aplasia or hypoplasia Two reliable forms of birth control must be used at the same time (unless abstinence is the chosen method) for 1 month before, during, and for at least 1 month after concluding treatment with isotretinoin. The most recent, and most stringent, system aimed to avoid exposure to isotretinoin is an Internet-based, performance-linked system called iPLEDGE Isotretinoin and its active metabolites cross the human placenta. Isotretinoin is one of the strongest human teratogens known today. The prevalence of birth defects after exposure during embryogenesis is as high as 30%.
  • Ref: Page no 245, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 245, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

question: A 34-year primi is started on sertraline for depression at 24 weeks gestation. She is worried about the risks the fetus can have because of the drug. What can the fetus develop?

Ans. C. Primary pulmonary HTN

  • Primary pulmonary hypertension if started late in pregnancy. Neonatal behavioral syndrome Paroxetine is associated with fetal cardiac anomalies like VSD

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 28-year-old pregnant woman is concerned about potential teratogenic effects on her developing fetus. Which of the following medications, if taken during pregnancy, is associated with an increased risk of CNS defects?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Warfarin

Explanation:

  • Option A: Folic acid supplementation is used for the prevention of neural tube defects. Option C and D : Indomethacin and aspirin are NSAIDs. Aspirin is not a major risk factor for birth defects. Indomethacin when taken late in pregnancy may cause constriction of the fetal ductus arteriosus and fetal pulmonary hypertension.

Educational Objective:

  • Warfarin is an anticoagulant medication that, if taken during pregnancy causes stippled epiphysis and nasal hypoplasia, it is also associated with an increased risk of CNS defects. It should be avoided during pregnancy, and alternative anticoagulation methods should be considered.
  • Ref: Page no 247, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 32-year-old pregnant woman is prescribed a medication for her chronic medical condition. She asks if this medication is a teratogen. The physician explains that this medication can potentially cause limb and ear malformations if taken during pregnancy. Which medication is the physician most likely referring to?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Valproic acid

Explanation:

  • Option A, B and D: (penicillin, prednisone, omeprazole) are generally considered safer choices during pregnancy.

Educational Objective:

  • Valproic acid, an antiepileptic medication, is associated with an increased risk of limb and ear malformations when taken during pregnancy. It is considered a teratogen, and alternative medications should be considered for pregnant women with epilepsy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 30-year-old pregnant woman seeks advice about alcohol consumption during pregnancy. What is the most appropriate recommendation regarding alcohol use during pregnancy?
options: Occasional moderate alcohol consumption is safe during pregnancy.
Heavy alcohol use is safe in the first trimester.
Alcohol consumption should be completely avoided during pregnancy.
Alcohol is safe to consume after the first trimester.

explanation.short: C. Alcohol consumption should be completely avoided during pregnancy.
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Alcohol consumption should be completely avoided during pregnancy.

Explanation:

  • The most appropriate recommendation regarding alcohol use during pregnancy is that it should be completely avoided. Prenatal alcohol exposure can lead to a range of developmental and behavioral issues, collectively known as fetal alcohol spectrum disorders (FASD). These include defects of the cardiac, skeletal, renal system as well as eye and ear defects.

Educational Objective:

  • Prenatal alcohol exposure can lead to a range of developmental and behavioral issues, collectively known as fetal alcohol spectrum disorders (FASD). These include defects of the cardiac, skeletal, renal system as well as eye and ear defects.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 35-year-old pregnant woman with a history of uncontrolled diabetes is concerned about the potential teratogenic effects of her condition on her developing fetus. What is the common teratogenic risk associated with maternal diabetes during pregnancy?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Cardiac malformations

Explanation:

  • Maternal diabetes during pregnancy is associated with an increased risk of cardiac malformations in the developing fetus. The most common anomaly seen in pregestational (overt) diabetes is cardiovascular anomalies (VSD). Poorly controlled maternal blood glucose levels can lead to structural heart defects, making it important to manage diabetes rigorously during pregnancy to reduce this risk.

Educational Objective:

  • The most common anomaly seen in pregestational (overt) diabetes is cardiovascular anomalies (VSD).
  • Ref: Page no 1100, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 22-year-old pregnant woman of 13 weeks gestation age informs about accidental exposure to radiation during chest X-ray at 7 weeks of gestation. What should be the next step advised to her?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Continue pregnancy

Explanation:

  • Most diagnostic radiographic procedures are associated with minimal fetal risk and hence pregnancy can be continued in the case given
  • Option B: Termination of pregnancy is not required as the exposure of radiation in a chest X Ray is negligible and will not cause fetal harm Option C: Pre-invasive diagnostic test: This is not indicated unless a screening test is positive Option D: Chromosome screening: Is indicated as usual in all pregnant women and not specifically for women with a Chest X Ray exposure. Also, this is done routinely between 11-14 weeks (combined screening)

Educational Objective:

  • Ionizing radiation: Max energy is in X rays and gamma rays Can cause structural change in DNA – risk of Abortion Malformations Fetal growth restriction Increased incidence of childhood cancers NOAEL (No observed adverse effect level): 0.05 Gy; Below this level: No Effects on Pregnancy (To reach this level, about 500 Chest X Rays are required. Chest Xray: < 0.0001 Gy / view IVP: increased exposure: 7 - 14 mGy Increased exposure with CT scans USG – safe MRI – safe (does not use ionizing radiation) Gadolinium (contrast) in MRI is contraindicated.
  • Ref: ACOG Committee Opinion on Guidelines for Diagnostic Imaging During Pregnancy and Lactation 2017
explanation.ref: ACOG Committee Opinion on Guidelines for Diagnostic Imaging During Pregnancy and Lactation 2017

question: All are features of a true sac except:
options: Yolk sac is seen at 5.5 weeks

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Intra-decidual sign is negative

Explanation:

  • Intra-decidual sign is seen in a true sac
  • Option B: A true sac is eccentric in position Option C: Peripheral vascularity is seen in a true gestational sac Option D: Yolk sac is seen at 5.5 weeks in a TVS

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Double Decidual Sac sign is seen in:

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Early Intra – uterine pregnancy

Explanation:

  • Double decidual sac sign is seen in an intra-uterine pregnancy.
  • Option B: An intra-uterine pseudo sac is seen usually in an ectopic pregnancy, It is fluid collection in the uterus Option C: Tubal ectopic pregnancy: in this a double decidual sac sign wont be seen. USG signs of a tubal pregnancy include bagel sign, blob sign and ring of fire sign Option D: In Molar pregnancy , the characteristic sign seen is the snowstorm appearance

Educational Objective:

  • Double Decidual sac sign:
  • Is a useful feature on early pregnancy ultrasound to confirm an early intrauterine pregnancy (IUP) Especially useful when the yolk sac or embryo is still not visualized. It consists of the decidua parietalis (lining the uterine cavity) and decidua capsularis (lining the gestational sac) It is seen as two concentric rings surrounding an anechoic gestational sac. Where the two adhere is the decidua basalis, and is the site of future placental formation.
  • Another sign of an intra-uterine pregnancy is the intra-decidual sign.
  • Ref: Page no 159, William’s Obstetrics

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following abnormalities can be diagnosed in the 1 st trimester of pregnancy?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Anencephaly

Educational Objective:

  • Most anomalies are detected in the 2nd trimester. The anomalies that can be detected in the 1st trimester (11-14 weeks scan) are: Acrania/ Anencephaly Encephalocele Alobar holoprosencephaly Iniencephaly Exomphalos Gastroschisis Body-Stalk anomaly Megacystis Missing Limbs

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: The first structure to appear in an intrauterine gestational sac on ultrasound is?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Yolk sac

Explanation:

  • The yolk sac is the first anatomical structure to appear in an intra-uterine gestational sac. It appears on a TVS at 5.5 weeks.
  • Option A: Cardiac activity in the fetal pole is seen at 6 weeks in TVS or at 7 weeks on a transabdominal scan (TAS)
  • Option B: This is a sign seen in early pregnancy on ultrasound at about 6 weeks
  • Option C: Embryo: Or fetal pole: This is seen on the TVS at 6 weeks (7 weeks on TAS)

Educational Objective:

  • 1st anatomical structure identified within the g sac (5 weeks) Undetectable after 10 weeks Large yolk sac: (≥ 6mm) is associated with poor obstetrical outcome
  • On a Transvaginal Sonogram (TVS): The gestational sac appears at 5 weeks Yolk sac at 5.5 weeks Embryo with cardiac activity at 6 weeks On Transabdominal sonography these appear 1 week later

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Identify the condition as shown in the image below:

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Posterior Placenta Previa – Partial

Explanation:

  • This is an Obstetric ultrasound showing the lower uterine segment with the bladder anteriorly. The cervix is seen and the placenta is seen posteriorly (as it is opposite to the bladder). It is seen to partially cover the os. So, this is Post placenta previa (partial).

Educational Objective:

  • Placenta previa is classified as follows:
  • Type 1 ---Mild degree ----- Type 2 Type 3--- Major degree ----- Type 4
  • Type 1: Low lying: implantation in lower uterine segment but not touching the os.
  • Type 2: Marginal: placenta is at the edge of the os but not covering it
  • Type 3: Partial Placenta Previa: Placenta partially covering the internal os.
  • Type 4: Complete Placenta Previa: Placenta completely covering the internal os.
  • Placenta previa can also be classified on USG as (Newer system) True placenta previa : the placenta covers the internal os either partially or completely Low lying placenta: placenta is implanted in the lower uterine segment such that the edge does not reach the os but remains outside a 2 cm wide perimeter around the os.
  • Ref: Page 774, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page 774, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Identify this anomaly:

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Anencephaly

Explanation:

  • The anomaly seen is an anencephaly About Anencephaly: Anencephaly is the most severe form of NTD Characterized by an absence of cortical tissue & absence of the cranial vault Detected by USG as early as 11 weeks. Frog face appearance on ultrasound Associated with polyhydramnios If undiagnosed, patient may present with face or breech presentation at term
  • Option B: Cleft lip: It is an opening in the upper lip due to failure of fusion of tissues forming it. It can occur alone or as a part of a genetic syndrome and may also have an accompanying cleft palate. Ultrasound can be best diagnosed on a 3D scan. Option C: Encephalocele: It is a sac like protrusion of the brain and the membranes covering it through a opening in the skull. Option D: Facial deformities are best seen on a 3D scan

Educational Objective:

  • The anomaly seen is an anencephaly About Anencephaly: Anencephaly is the most severe form of NTD Characterized by an absence of cortical tissue & absence of the cranial vault Detected by USG as early as 11 weeks. Frog face appearance on ultrasound Associated with polyhydramnios If undiagnosed, patient may present with face or breech presentation at term

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: The best measurement for estimating gestational age on ultrasound is:

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Crown-Rump Length

Explanation:

  • CRL: This is the Crown-Rump Length and is the most accurate method to establish and confirm the gestational age
  • Option A: Biparietal diameter (BPD): The BPD is a measurement of the fetus done after 14 weeks. It is the most accurate estimation of gestational age after 14 weeks. Option C: Head circumference: The HC is a measurement done after 14 weeks Option D : Femur length is also a fetal biometric measurement done after 14 weeks.

Educational Objective:

  • About Crown Rump Length (CRL)
  • Most accurate method to establish/ confirm gestational age It is measured in the mid – sagittal plane Embryo in neutral position Till 13 weeks: accuracy of 5 – 7 days In the 2nd trimester the best parameter to estimate gestational age in: BPD If dolichocephaly/ brachycephaly; HC better Abdominal Circumference (AC): greatest variation
  • Ref: Page no 159, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition

Left sided: 75% cases Can be detected as early as 14-15 weeks but some may be undetected till the 3rd trimester Always look for associated cardiovascular anomalies Amenable to fetal therapy: FETO (Fetal endoscopic tracheal occlusion)

question: This is an image of an ultrasound in twin pregnancy. What is the chorionicity?

  • Option B: Dichorionic Monoamniotic: There is no such type of twin Option C: Monochorionic Diamniotic: This is a type of monzygotic twinning wherein on ultrasound, an inverted "T" sign will be seen Option D: Monochorionic Monoamniotic: This is a type of monozygotic twinning in whch there is a single chorion and amnion and there will not be any inter-twin membrane visible on the ultrasound

Educational Objective:

  • Determined best by ultrasound in the 1st trimester (8- 10 weeks) Twin peak sign (Lambda sign) – Dichorionic Diamniotic Twins Inverted T sign – Monochorionic Diamniotic No intervening membrane in Monochorionic Monoamniotic Twins

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All are indications for a fetal ECHO except:

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Gestational Diabetes mellitus

Explanation:

  • In gestational diabetes mellitus, there is no additional risk of congenital anomalies, so an ECHO is not required. Only in overt diabetes, there is an increased risk of congenital anomalies, especially cardiovascular anomalies.

Educational Objective:

  • The following are indications for doing a fetal ECHO
  • Suspected fetal cardiac anomaly Extracardiac anomaly in the fetus Fetal chromosomal anomaly Fetal arrythmia Fetal Hydrops Thick NT Previous baby with Congenital heart disease (CHD) Either parent with CHD Conception through IVF Maternal anti Ro/ anti La (Congenital heart blocks ) Exposure to Lithium (Ebsteins anomaly) / Anti-epileptic drugs/other teratogenic drugs Pregestational DM/ Overt diabetes Phenylketonuria (associated with co-arctation of aorta, hypoplastic left heart)
  • Ref: Page no 201, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition

question: Magnetic Resonance Imaging is used in obstetrics in all these situations except?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Contracted pelvis

Explanation:

  • MRI is not useful in diagnosing contracted pelvis. Contracted pelvis is best diagnosed by clinical pelvimetry. Actually, the best test of a pelvis is the fetal head itself.

Educational Objective:

  • MRI is safe in pregnancy Due to cost and as it is not portable and time consuming, its role is limited Benefit in Adjunct to USG in fetal anatomic survey Placental invasion Maternal pelvic masses

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 28-year-old pregnant woman with a history of type 1 diabetes is concerned about fetal well-being. Which fetal imaging modality is commonly used to assess placental and fetal blood flow, as well as fetal oxygenation during pregnancy?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Doppler ultrasound

Explanation:

  • Doppler ultrasound is commonly used to assess placental and fetal blood flow, as well as fetal oxygenation during pregnancy. This imaging modality can help monitor fetal well-being, especially in high-risk pregnancies.
  • Option A: 2D ultrasound is routinely used for all ANC patients. This tells us No of fetuses Placental position Growth parameters: BPD, HC, AC and FL Amniotic fluid volume Fetal anomalies Adnexal lesions Option B: MRI is useful for specific indications such as Adjunct to USG in fetal anatomic survey, placental invasion or maternal pelvic masses. Option D: 3D USG is useful in detecting surface anomalies in the fetus like cleft lip. It is alwso very helpful in diagnosing Mullerian anomalies

Educational Objective:

  • Seeing the flow in uterine arteries (Screening for preeclampsia) Flow in the umbilical artery in IUGR, utero-placental insufficiency and antepartum fetal monitoring in high risk pregnancies Flow in the middle cerebral artery (IUGR, Rh isoimmunized fetuses) Screening of Down syndrome at 11-14 weeks (Tricuspid regurgitation, Abnormal ductus venosus)

References:

  • ↳ Reference: William’s textbook of Obstetrics 25th edition page 213-214
explanation.ref: William’s textbook of Obstetrics 25 th edition page 213-214

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 35-year-old pregnant woman presents for a fetal ultrasound at 7 weeks of gestation. What is the most appropriate type of ultrasound for dating the pregnancy accurately?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Transvaginal ultrasound

Explanation:

  • Transvaginal ultrasound is the most appropriate type of ultrasound for dating the pregnancy accurately in the first trimester. It provides better resolution and access to the developing embryo. The CRL is most accurate for estimation of age in the first trimester.
  • Option A: Transabdominal ultrasound: Is usually preferred after 12 weeks in pregnancy as the uterus is an abdominal organ after 12 weeks. Option B: 3D USG is useful in detecting surface anomalies in the fetus like cleft lip. It is alwso very helpful in diagnosing Mullerian anomalies Option D: Doppler ultrasound is commonly used to assess placental and fetal blood flow, as well as fetal oxygenation during pregnancy.

Educational Objective:

  • A transvaginal scan (TVS) is preferred over a transabdominal scan (TAS) in the 1st trimester A CRL done between 8 to 12 weeks is the best determinant of gestational age In a 1st trimester scan, the following structures are seen.

References:

  • ↳ Reference: Williams textbook of Obstetrics 25th edition page 183.
explanation.ref: Williams textbook of Obstetrics 25 th edition page  183.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 30-year-old pregnant woman in her second trimester presents for a routine prenatal ultrasound. What is the primary purpose of this mid-pregnancy ultrasound?
options: To assess for neural tube defects
To evaluate fetal growth and anatomy

explanation.short: B. To evaluate fetal growth and anatomy
explanation.detail:
Ans. B. To evaluate fetal growth and anatomy

Explanation:

  • The primary purpose of a mid-pregnancy ultrasound (typically performed between 18-22 weeks) is to evaluate fetal growth and anatomy, including assessing the development of various organs and structures in the fetus. This scan may also be referred to as anomaly scan/ malformation scan/ Targeted imaging for fetal anomaly (TIFFA scan)

Educational Objective:

  • Educational Objective: Routine ultrasounds done in pregnancy include
  • 1st trimester (6-10 weeks): For confirmation of pregnancy, excluding ectopic pregnancy, number of fetuses, viability, adnexal lesions 11-14 weeks: Level 1 scan: For Down syndrome screening and uterine artery doppler for preeclampsia screening 18-22 weeks: TIFFA scan (Targeted imaging for fetal anomaly) > 28 weeks: Fetal growth and well being, placental localization, amniotic fluid volume, confirming presentation.

References:

  • ↳ Reference: Williams textbook of Obstetrics 25th edition page 187
explanation.ref: Williams textbook of Obstetrics 25 th edition page 187

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: The onset of oogenesis starts during:

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Fetal period

Explanation:

  • In the female gonad , the germ cells undergo a number of rapid mitotic divisions and differentiate into oogonia The number of oogonia reaches its maximum at the 20th week (about 7 million) Majority of oogonia continue to divide Some oogonia enter into the 1st meiotic division and are called primary oocytes At birth , no more mitotic division and all oogonia are replaced by primary oocytes. (about 2 million) 1st meiotic division is completed at puberty

Educational Objective:

  • In the female gonad , the germ cells undergo a number of rapid mitotic divisions and differentiate into oogonia The number of oogonia reaches its maximum at the 20th week (about 7 million) Majority of oogonia continue to divide Some oogonia enter into the 1st meiotic division and are called primary oocytes At birth, no more mitotic division and all oogonia are replaced by primary oocytes. (about 2 million) 1st meiotic division is completed at puberty

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Identify the correct statement about oogenesis:
options: Follicles undergo meiosis in the fetal ovary
Oogenesis involves unequal division of cells
The number of oocytes are maximum at birth

explanation.short: B. Oogenesis involves unequal division of cells
explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Oogenesis involves unequal division of cells

Explanation:

  • Option A: Follicles undergo meiosis in the fetal ovary: Follicles do not undergo meiosis. The primary oocyte begins to undergo meiosis 1 in the fetal ovary but is arrested at prophase 1
  • Option C: Oogonia get arrested in prophase 1: The primary oocyte gets arrested in prophase 1
  • Option D: The number of oocytes are maximum at birth: The maximum number of oocytes are at 20 weeks of fetal life (6-7 million)

Educational Objective:

  • Ref : Page no 15, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics, 9th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 15, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics, 9 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All are true regarding oogenesis except?
options: The maximum number of oocytes are at birth
Primary oocytes are arrested in prophase 1 of meiosis
The 1st polar body is formed at ovulation

explanation.short: A. The maximum number of oocytes are at birth
explanation.detail:
Ans. A. The maximum number of oocytes are at birth

Explanation:

  • Max oogonia are at the 20th week of fetal life (7 million) These reduce (by atresia and apoptosis) to 1-2 million at birth 300-400,000 at puberty 100 at menopause Only 400-500 primary oocytes are likely to ovulate during the reproductive life of a woman The oogonia enter meiosis to form the primary oocyte which rest in the diplotene phase of prophase 1 until puberty Ovulation occurs just after meiosis 1 is complete resulting in a secondary oocyte and the 1st polar body Meiosis 2 is completed at fertilization resulting in the female pro-nucleus and the 2nd polar body Oogonia and primary oocytes are diploid Secondary oocytes, ovum and polar bodies are haploid

Educational Objective:

  • Max oogonia are at the 20th week of fetal life (7 million) These reduce (by atresia and apoptosis) to 1-2 million at birth 300-400,000 at puberty 100 at menopause Only 400-500 primary oocytes are likely to ovulate during the reproductive life of a woman The oogonia enter meiosis to form the primary oocyte which rest in the diplotene phase of prophase 1 until puberty Ovulation occurs just after meiosis 1 is complete resulting in a secondary oocyte and the 1st polar body Meiosis 2 is completed at fertilization resulting in the female pro-nucleus and the 2nd polar body Oogonia and primary oocytes are diploid S econdary oocytes, ovum and polar bodies are haploid

question: The primary oocyte divides into:
options: The secondary oocyte and the 2nd polar body
The secondary oocyte and the 1st polar body

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. The secondary oocyte and the 1st polar body

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 21 y primigravida presents at 6 weeks. An ultrasound reveals the following image showing an intra-uterine gestational sac. The double decidual sac sign is demonstrated. This sign is formed by which 2 layers of the decidua?

explanation.short: B. Decidua capsularis and Decidua parietalis
explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Decidua capsularis and decidua parietalis

Explanation:

  • After implantation , the decidua (thickened endometrium of pregnancy) is differentiated into 3 parts
  • Decidua basalis Portion of the decidua which lies between the embryo and the muscular wall of the uterus Forms the site of attachment of the placenta Decidua capsularis Portion of decidua which intervenes between the embryo and the uterine cavity Decidua parietalis (aka Decidua vera) Portion of decidua not related to the site of implantation , but lines the rest of the uterine cavity
  • The double decidual sac sign seen on the ultrasound is formed by the Decidua parietalis and the Decidua capsularis

Educational Objective:

  • The double decidual sac sign seen on the ultrasound is formed by the Decidua parietalis and the Decidua capsularis
  • Ref : Page no 159, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition, Page no 22, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetric 9th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 159, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition, Page no 22, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetric 9 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Spermiogenesis includes all the following components except:

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Sperm capacitation

Explanation:

  • Spermiogenesis is the process of conversion of spherical spermatids into longitudinal spermatozoa without any change in chromosome number (haploid). It consists of the following: Formation of acrosome Formation of neck, middle piece and tail Nuclear condensation Shedding of most of the cytoplasm Sperm capacitation is the physiological change in which the sperm becomes hyper motile and is able to bind and fertilize a secondary oocyte . This occurs in the fallopian tube.

Educational Objective:

  • Spermiogenesis is the process of conversion of spherical spermatids into longitudinal spermatozoa without any change in chromosome number (haploid). It consists of the following: Formation of acrosome Formation of neck, middle piece and tail Nuclear condensation Shedding of most of the cytoplasm

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: At what stage of the cell cycle are primary oocytes arrested?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Prophase 1

Explanation:

  • In the fetal female gonads, the germ cells multiply by mitosis to differentiate into oogonia Max oogonia are at the 20th week of fetal life (7 million) These reduce (by atresia and apoptosis) to 1-2 million at birth 300-400,000 at puberty 100 at menopause Only 400-500 primary oocytes are likely to ovulate during the reproductive life of a woman The oogonia enter meiosis to form the primary oocyte which rest in the diplotene phase of prophase 1 until puberty

Educational Objective:

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All are steps in implantation of the zygote except?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Cleavage

Explanation:

  • Implantation is the process by which the blastocyst penetrates the uterine mucosa to get implanted in the endometrium It starts on the 6th day of ovulation (20th day of LMP) It involves 4 steps Apposition Adhesion Penetration Invasion Cleavage refers to the sub-division of the fertilized ovum into smaller cells

Educational Objective:

  • Implantation is the process by which the blastocyst penetrates the uterine mucosa to get implanted in the endometrium It starts on the 6th day of ovulation (20th day of LMP) It involves 4 steps Apposition Adhesion Penetration Invasion

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Cleavage of the zygote to the 2-cell stage is reached how many hours after fertilization?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. 30

Explanation:

  • The 2-cell stage is reached approximately 30 hours after fertilization Each contains equal cytoplasmic volume and chromosome numbers Cleavage continues into the 4,8,16 cell stage until a cluster of cells called a ‘ morula’ is formed On the 4th day , the morula enters the uterine cavity (16-64 cell stage) Blastocyst formation occurs on the 5th day Implantation occurs on the 6th day (corresponds to the 20th day of a regular menstrual cycle )

Educational Objective:

question: A 32 year old woman is undergoing IVF-ET. After in-vitro fertilization, the following embryo has been decided to be transferred. On what day following fertilization, is this embryo seen?

explanation.detail:

Explanation:

  • This is a microscopic image of an 8-cell embryo which is seen on Day 3 Usually in IVF, a Day 3 embryo is transferred in the uterine cavity or a Day 5 (blastocyst) embryo is transferred in the uterine cavity.
  • Microscopic images of different cleavage stages

  • Microscopic images of different cleavage stages
  • Ref: Page no 19, Textbook of Obstetrics by JB Sharma, 1st edition
explanation.ref: Page no 19, Textbook of Obstetrics by JB Sharma, 1 st edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: The incorrect statement regarding fertilization is:
options: Completion of the 2nd meiotic division of the oocyte happens at ovulation
The fertilizable life span of an oocyte is 12-24 hours and that of a sperm is 48 – 72 hours
Penetration of the zona pellucida is facilitated by release of hyaluronidase from the acrosomal cap of the sperm
Zona reaction prevents the entry of other sperms

explanation.short: A. Completion of the 2nd meiotic division of the oocyte happens at ovulation
explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Completion of the 2ndmeiotic division of the oocyte happens at ovulation.

Explanation:

  • Option A: Completion of the 2nd meiotic division of the oocyte happens at ovulation : This is an incorrect statement. Completion of the 2nd meiotic division occurs at the time of fertilization resulting in a female pronucleus and the 2nd polar body .
  • Option B: The fertilizable life span of an oocyte is 12-24 hours and that of a sperm is 48 – 72 hours . This is correct .
  • Option C: Penetration of the zona pellucida is facilitated by release of hyaluronidase from the acrosomal cap of the sperm . This is a correct statement. Penetration of the zona pellucida is facilitated by release of hyaluronidase from the acrosomal cap of the sperm. More than 1 sperm may penetrate the zona pellucida but soon after sperm fusion, penetration of other sperms is prevented by zona reaction (hardening) or oolemma block due to release of cortical granules by exocytosis from the oocyte.
  • Option D: Zona reaction prevents the entry of other sperms. This is also a correct statement

Educational Objective:

  • The fertilizable life span of an oocyte is 12-24 hours and that of a sperm is 48 – 72 hours Penetration of the zona pellucida is facilitated by release of hyaluronidase from the acrosomal cap of the sperm . More than 1 sperm may penetrate the zona pellucida but soon after sperm fusion , penetration of other sperms is prevented by zona reaction (hardening) or oolemma block due to release of cortical granules by exocytosis from the oocyte . Completion of the 2nd meiotic division occurs at the time of fertilization resulting in a female pronucleus and the 2nd polar body .
  • Ref : Page no 18, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics, 9th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 18, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics, 9 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: In females, what is the outcome of meiosis I in oogenesis?
options: Formation of one secondary oocyte and one polar body

explanation.short: C. Formation of one secondary oocyte and one polar body
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Formation of one secondary oocyte and one polar body

Explanation:

  • In females, meiosis I in oogenesis results in the formation of one secondary oocyte and one polar body . The secondary oocyte proceeds to meiosis II , while the polar body often disintegrates.

Educational Objective:

  • Primary oocyte gets arrested in prophase 1 of meiosis 1 and resumes only at puberty. Meiosis 1 occurs at ovulation with the formation of a secondary oocyte and a polar body. Meiosis 2 occurs at fertilization with the formation of zygote (fertilized oocyte) and the 2nd polar body .
  • Ref : Page no 16, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics, 9th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 16, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics, 9 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: During oogenesis, when is meiosis II completed in a female's reproductive system?

explanation.short: B. After fertilization by a sperm
explanation.detail:
Ans. B. After fertilization by a sperm

Explanation:

  • Meiosis II in oogenesis is completed after fertilization by a sperm . Prior to fertilization, the secondary oocyte is arrested in metaphase II . Fertilization triggers the completion of meiosis II , resulting in a mature fertilized ovum (zygote) and a second polar body.

Educational Objective:

  • Primary oocyte gets arrested in prophase 1 of meiosis 1 and resumes only at puberty Meiosis 1 occurs at ovulation with the formation of a secondary oocyte and a polar body Meiosis 2 occurs at fertilization with the formation of zygote (fertilized oocyte) and the 2nd polar body.
  • Ref : Page no 16, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics, 9th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 16, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics, 9 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: During which stage of embryonic development does implantation occur in the uterine wall?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Blastocyst

Explanation:

  • Implantation occurs during the blastocyst stage of embryonic development . The blastocyst, a hollow structure, attaches to the uterine wall , beginning the process of pregnancy. Implantation begins on the 6th day (corresponds to the 20th day of a regular menstrual cycle) and is completed by the 10th - 11th day.

Educational Objective:

  • Implantation occurs at blastocyst stage. Begins on the 6th day ; complete by 10th day.
  • Ref : Page no 19, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics, 9th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 19, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics, 9 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 34y G3P2 presents to the OPD at 34 weeks. She has had 2 previous lower segment cesarean sections and an done at 28 weeks shows an anterior low-lying placenta. She does not complain of bleeding. On examination, the uterus is 32 weeks, cephalic presentation and the FHR is 145 bpm. What is the next best step?
options: Order an ultrasound with a Doppler

explanation.short: A. Order an ultrasound with a Doppler
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • She is at an increased risk of placental accrete syndromes as she has 2 previous cearean sections and an anterior placenta previa The risk of placenta accreta increases with the number of previous cesareans Any patient with a previous cesarean, with placenta previa in the current pregnancy is at an increased risk for placenta acrrete syndromes The investigation of choice in placenta accreta syndromes is an ultrasound doppler and this should be done in this patient
  • Opton B: As the patient does not have to be delivered right now, steroids are not indicated Option C: An Elective cesarean section may have to be planned if placenta previa (At 37 weeks) or if placenta acrrete syndromes (between 34 - 37 weeks) but the question asked is the next best step and this is to rule out placenta acrrete syndromes by doing a Doppler Option D: A repeat ultrasound may have to be done but that again is not the next best step.

Educational Objective:

  • The risk of placenta acrrete syndromes (PAS) increases with the number of prior cesarean sections. A patient with placenta previa with a h/o previous cesarean section should be investigated for placenta accrete syndromes (PAS)
  • Ref: Page 1967, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page 1967, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which is incorrect regarding the pathophysiology of placenta accrete syndromes?
options: The abnormal adherence is due to partial or total absence of the decidua basalis and imperfect development of the fibrinoid or Nitabuch layer
Microscopically, the placental villi are found attached to smooth muscle fibers
There is a close relationship between cesarean scar pregnancy (CSP) and later development of Placenta accrete syndromes in the same pregnancy
About 15% of all accrete syndromes are placenta percreta

explanation.short: D. About 15% of all accrete syndromes are placenta percreta
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • The ratio of occurrence of placenta accreta: increta: percreta is 80:15:5; i.e. 5% of all adherent placentas are placenta percreta and hence this statement is incorrect The term placenta accreta spectrum (PAS) describes aberrant placentation characterized by abnormally implanted, invasive, or adhered placenta. It is also referred to as morbidly adherent placenta, The major problem if placenta accreta syndrome occurs is failure of placenta tto separate normally from the myometrium after fetal delivery. Attempts to seperate an adherent placenta can lead to torrential hemorrhage This abnormal adherence stems in part from partial or total absence of the decidua basalis and imperfect development of the fibrinoid or Nitabuch layer If the decidual spongy layer is lacking either partially or totally, the physiological line of cleavage is absent, and some or all cotyledons are densely anchored. Microscopically, placental villi attach to smooth muscle fibers rather than to decidual cells. Factors implicated in the pathogenesis An antecedent “constitutional endometrial defect” in most cases is found like a history of a previous cesarean section Some tissue specimens show immunologically mediated “hyperinvasiveness” Some genes that code for remodeling and for adherence may be highly expressed There is a close relationship between cesarean scar pregnancy (CSP) and later development of PAS in the same pregnancy.

Educational Objective:

  • In Placenta Accrete Syndromes, the abnormal adherence stems in part from partial or total absence of the decidua basalis and imperfect development of the fibrinoid or Nitabuch layer
  • Ref: Page no 1974, Williams Obstetric 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1974, Williams Obstetric 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 34y G2P1 with a previous LSCS presented at 30 weeks with painless vaginal bleeding. The following image was seen on ultrasound doppler. Which of the following is not seen in placenta accreta?
options: Disruption of the bladder-uterine serosal interface
Bridging vessels from the placenta to the bladder-serosal interface

explanation.short: A. Thickening of the retroplacental myometrium
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Thinning of the retro-placental myometrium is seen on ultrasound in Placenta Accrete Syndromes (PAS)
  • Five characteristic sonographic findings suggest Placenta Acceta Syndromes are:
  • Placental lacunae Thinning of the retroplacental myometrium Disruption of the bladder-uterine serosal interface Bridging vessels from the placenta to the bladder-serosal interface Placental bulge that pushes outward and distorts the contour of the uterus

Educational Objective:

  • Remember: The investigation of choice to diagnose placenta acrrete syndromes is Ultrasound + Color Doppler
  • Ref: Page 1979, Williams Obstetrics, 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page 1979, Williams Obstetrics, 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 24y G2P1 with a previous LSCS presented at 28 weeks with painless mild vaginal bleeding. She had a previous similar episode 2 weeks earlier. A scan done was suspicious for placenta accreta and this was confirmed on a Doppler. What is the next best step in management?
options: Plan an elective LSCS at 37 weeks
Plan an elective classical section at 37 weeks

explanation.short: B. Plan an elective classical section at 37 weeks
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Management of placenta accreta involves Planning delivery between 34-37 weeks Delivery is usually by a classical cesarean (upper segment) to avoid the placenta Following delivery of the baby, a hysterectomy is carried out with the placenta in situ Conservative approaches like leaving the placenta in situ and resection and repair of uterus are less commonly done.
  • Option A & C: Lower segment cesarean section (LSCS) is not done for placenta accreta syndromes as the placenta is usually adherent in the lower segment Option D: As the patient is currently stable and bleeding is mild, immediate cesarean is not indicated

Educational Objective:

  • Management of placenta accreta involves Planning delivery between 34-37 weeks Delivery is usually by a classical cesarean (upper segment) to avoid the placenta Following delivery of the baby, a hysterectomy is carried out with the placenta in situ
  • Page no 1985, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: The investigation of choice for placenta accreta is:

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • USG with color Doppler has a high sensitivity and specificity for diagnosing placenta accreta syndromes Five characteristic sonographic findings suggest Placenta Acceta Syndromes are: Placental lacunae Thinning of the retroplacental myometrium Disruption of the bladder-uterine serosal interface Bridging vessels from the placenta to the bladder-serosal interface Placental bulge that pushes outward and distorts the contour of the uterus
  • Option A: Plain Ultrasound is not as useful as Doppler for diagnosing Placenta accreta Option C: MRI can be added on in doubtful cases especially if the placenta is posteriorly situated Option D: CECT: CT scans are generally contra-indicated in pregnancy and are not useful in diagnosing placenta acrrete syndromes

Educational Objective:

  • USG with color Doppler has a high sensitivity and specificity for diagnosing placenta accreta syndromes and is the investigation of choice
  • Ref: Page no 1980, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1980, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Ligation of which of the following vessels in the retroperitoneum will reduce the pulse pressure in a patient who is bleeding during surgery for placenta accreta syndrome?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Internal iliac artery ligation is performed in obstetric hemorrhage like PPH and placenta accreta syndromes It is part of devascularization surgeries to reduce blood loss in massive obstetric hemorrhage along with uterine artery and utero-ovarian artery ligation The most important mechanism of action with internal iliac artery ligation is an 85-percent reduction in pulse pressure in those arteries distal to the ligation and a 50% reducition in blood flow to the uterus This converts an arterial pressure system into one with pressures approaching those in the venous circulation. This creates vessels more amenable to hemostasis via pressure and clot formation.
  • Option A: Uterine artery ligation is the 1st vessel ligated in devascularization surgery but it does not reduce the pulse pressure as effectively as does internal iliac artery ligation
  • Option C: External iliac artery: Is not ligated as part of devascularization as it does not supply the uterus
  • Option D: utero-ovarian anastomoses ligation is done as part of devasculariazation surgery but it does not reduce the pulse pressure as effectively as does internal iliac artery ligation

Educational Objective:

  • Internal iliac artery ligation is performed in obstetric hemorrhage like PPH and placenta accreta syndromes It is part of devascularization surgeries to reduce blood loss in massive obstetric hemorrhage along with uterine artery and utero-ovarian artery ligation The most important mechanism of action with internal iliac artery ligation is an 85-percent reduction in pulse pressure in those arteries distal to the ligation and a 50% reducition in blood flow to the uterus
  • Ref: Page no 2027, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 2027, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which layer of the placenta directly interfaces with the maternal uterine tissue and is responsible for nutrient and gas exchange?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Decidua basalis

Explanation:

  • Chorionic villi: The chorionic villus is the functional unit of the placenta that contains a capillary network derived from fetal circulation ad mesenchyme Syncytiotrophoblasts: As the outermost layer of all villi, the syncytio-trophoblast covers all placental villi as one single layer covering all villi of a single placenta Amnion: This is a membranous structure that covers and protects the embryo

Educational Objective:

  • Educational Objective: Structure of chorionic villi
  • Ref: Page no 90, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition.
explanation.ref: Page no 90, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Ultrasound examination of a patient with previous caesarean section shows placental implantation on the posterior wall in the lower uterine segment completely covering the internal cervical os, what is the possible diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Placenta previa

Explanation:

  • Placenta accreta, increta and percreta and forms of placenta accrete syndromes (PAS) In PAS, there is deficient Nitabuch layer and the placenta adheres, invades or perforates the myometrium It is diagnosed on USG Doppler (ix of choice) or an MRI

Educational Objective:

  • Placenta previa is a placenta implanted in the lower uterine segment It is diagnosed on ultrasound Risk factors include Previous cesarean/ other surgeries on the uterus IVF conception Multiple pregnancy Smoking High parity Increasing maternal age Uterine abnormalities
  • Ref: Page no 774, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition.
explanation.ref: Page no 774, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 30-year-old primi presented with placental abruption, what could be a common associated condition in this patient?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Preeclampsia

Explanation:

  • Preeclampsia, a condition characterized by hypertension and organ damage, is commonly associated with an increased risk of placental abruption. It can result in compromised placental blood flow. 50% of all abruptions will be associated with a hypertensive disorder of pregnancy

Educational Objective:

  • Abruptio Placentae is premature separation of a normally implanted placenta. Risk factors include: HTN in pregnancy Multiple pregnancy APLA Previous abruption Multiparity Higher age Folate deficiency Smoking Polyhydramnios Abdominal trauma
  • Ref: Page no 767, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition.
explanation.ref: Page no 767, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which immunoglobulin is involved in maternal-fetal immune reactions, and may lead to hemolytic disease of the newborn if the mother is Rh-negative and the baby is Rh-positive?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. IgG

Explanation:

Educational Objective:

  • The prevalence of red cell alloimmunization in pregnancy approximates 1% Most cases of severe fetal anemia requiring antenatal transfusion are attributable to anti-D, anti-Kell, anti-c, or anti-E alloimmunization Maternal blood type and antibody screen are routinely assessed at the first prenatal visit. Unbound antibodies in maternal serum are detected with an indirect Coombs test If the result is positive, the specific antibodies are identified; their immunoglobulin subtype is determined as either immunoglobulin G (IgG) or M (IgM); and the titer is quantified. Only IgG antibodies are concerning because IgM antibodies do not cross the placenta
  • Ref: Page no 302, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition.
explanation.ref: Page no 302, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: The vessel carrying oxygenated blood from the placenta to the fetus is:

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Option A: Umbilical vein: This carries oxygenated blood from the placenta to the fetus Option B: Umbilical artery: This carries deoxygenated blood from the fetus to the placenta Option C: Ductus venosus: This shunts blood from the umbilical vein to the IVC Option D: Ductus arteriosus: This shunts blood from the pulmonary artery to the aorta

Educational Objective:

  • Oxygenated blood enters the fetus through: Umbilical Vein Functional closure: Occurs after closure of umbilical artery Forms the ligamentum teres Deoxygenated Blood exits the fetus through Umbilical arteries Functional closure of umbilical arteries: immediately after birth Anatomical closure: 2 – 3months Proximal part form the superior vesical arteries Distal part form the lateral umbilical ligaments Ductus venosus: Shunts blood from umbilical vein to IVC Functional closure 10 – 96h after birth Anatomic closure: 3 -7 days Forms the ligamentum venosum Ductus arteriosus: Shunts blood from Pulmonary artery to Aorta Functional closure: 12 – 24 h Anatomic closure: 2 – 3 weeks Foramen ovale Functional closure soon after birth Anatomical closure at 1 year
  • Ref: Page no 129, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 129, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: How does Immunoglobulin G (IgG) cross the placenta?
options: It does not cross the placenta
Facilitated diffusion involving calcium binding protein

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Most substances with a molecular mass < 500Da pass readily through placental tissue by simple diffusion These include CO2, water, most electrolytes and anesthetic gases Insulin, steroid hormones and thyroid hormones cross the placenta but very slowly Hormones synthesized in the syncytiotrophoblasts (hCG and hPL) enter both maternal and fetal circulation but concentration is higher in maternal circulation High molecular weight substances do not traverse the placenta EXCEPT IgG (molecular weight is 160,000) – This is transferred by a specific trophoblastic receptor mediated mechanism IgG is the only immunoglobulin to cross the placenta

Educational Objective:

  • Most substances with a molecular mass < 500Da pass readily through placental tissue by simple diffusion These include CO2, water, most electrolytes and anesthetic gases Insulin, steroid hormones and thyroid hormones cross the placenta but very slowly Hormones synthesized in the syncytiotrophoblasts (hCG and hPL) enter both maternal and fetal circulation but concentration is higher in maternal circulation High molecular weight substances do not traverse the placenta EXCEPT IgG (molecular weight is 160,000) – This is transferred by a specific trophoblastic receptor mediated mechanism IgG is the only immunoglobulin to cross the placenta
  • Ref: Page no 140, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 140, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: What is the uteroplacental blood flow at term?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Maternal blood within the intervillous space is the primary source of maternal–fetal transfer. Blood from the maternal spiral arteries directly bathes the trophoblast layer that surrounds the villi. Substances transferred from mother to fetus first enter the intervillous space and are then transported to the syncytiotrophoblasts Intervillous and uteroplacental blood flow increases throughout pregnancy At term, uteroplacental blood flow near term ranges from 700 to 900 mL/min, and most of this blood apparently goes to the intervillous space

Educational Objective:

  • Maternal blood within the intervillous space is the primary source of maternal–fetal transfer. Blood from the maternal spiral arteries directly bathes the trophoblast layer that surrounds the villi. Substances transferred from mother to fetus first enter the intervillous space and are then transported to the syncytiotrophoblasts Intervillous and uteroplacental blood flow increases throughout pregnancy At term, uteroplacental blood flow near term ranges from 700 to 900 mL/min, and most of this blood apparently goes to the intervillous space

explanation.ref: Page no 370, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following statements about leptin is true?
options: It is produced exclusively by the placenta
Concentrations peak in amniotic fluid at term
95% of placental production enters the fetal circulation
Abnormal levels have been associated with FGR

explanation.short: D. Abnormal levels have been associated with FGR
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Leptin is a polypeptide hormone which was originally identified as a product of adipocytes and a regulator of energy homeostasis by curbing appetite. It also contributes to angiogenesis, hemopoiesis, osteogenesis, pulmonary maturation, and neuroendocrine, immune, and reproductive functions Leptin is produced by the mother, fetus, and placenta. It is expressed in syncytiotrophoblasts and fetal vascular endothelial cells. Of placental production, 5% enters the fetal circulation & 95 percent is transferred to the mother. Leptin concentrations peak in amnionic fluid at mid-pregnancy Fetal serum leptin levels begin increasing at approximately 34 weeks and are correlated with fetal weight. This hormone is involved in the development and maturation of the heart, brain, kidneys, and pancreas, and its levels are decreased with fetal-growth restriction. Abnormal levels are also associated with fetal-growth disorders, gestational diabetes, and preeclampsia, but not maternal obesity

Educational Objective:

  • Leptin is a polypeptide hormone which was originally identified as a product of adipocytes and a regulator of energy homeostasis by curbing appetite. It also contributes to angiogenesis, hemopoiesis, osteogenesis, pulmonary maturation, and neuroendocrine, immune, and reproductive functions Leptin is produced by the mother, fetus, and placenta. It is expressed in syncytiotrophoblasts and fetal vascular endothelial cells. Of placental production, 5% enters the fetal circulation & 95 percent is transferred to the mother. Leptin concentrations peak in amnionic fluid at mid-pregnancy Fetal serum leptin levels begin increasing at approximately 34 weeks and are correlated with fetal weight. This hormone is involved in the development and maturation of the heart, brain, kidneys, and pancreas, and its levels are decreased with fetal-growth restriction. Abnormal levels are also associated with fetal-growth disorders, gestational diabetes, and preeclampsia, but not maternal obesity
  • Ref: Page 364, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page 364, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following statements of the fetal thyroid gland is true?
options: The fetal thyroid starts to make hormones starting at 36 weeks
By 12 weeks, the fetal thyroid is concentrating iodide more avidly than the maternal thyroid
After birth, cooling to room temperature causes a sudden and marked decrease in secretion of TSH which results in low serum T4 with a nadir at 24-36h of life
Maternal and fetal levels of TBG decrease throughout pregnancy

explanation.short: B. By 12 weeks, the fetal thyroid is concentrating iodide more avidly than the maternal thyroid
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Let us discuss all the options
  • Option A: The fetal thyroid starts to make hormones starting at 36 weeks: This is incorrect; the feta thyroid starts secreting hormones at 1- - 12 weeks Option B: By 12 weeks, the fetal thyroid is concentrating iodide more avidly than the maternal thyroid: Ths is correct. The placenta actively concentrates iodine on the fetal side, and by 12 weeks and throughout pregnancy, the fetal thyroid concentrates iodine more avidly than does the maternal thyroid. Option C: After birth, cooling to room temperature causes a sudden and marked decrease in secretion of TSH which results in low serum T4 with a nadir at 24-36h of life: This is incorrect. Immediately after birth, thyroid function and metabolism undergo major change. Cooling to room temperature evokes a sudden and marked increase in TSH secretion. This in turn causes a progressive increase in serum T4 levels that are maximal 24 to 36 hours after birth. Option D: Maternal and fetal levels of TBG decrease throughout pregnancy. This is incorrect. Normal fetal levels of free thyroxine (T4), free triiodothyronine (T3), and TBG rise steadily throughout gestation

Educational Objective:

  • By 10 to 12 weeks, the thyroid gland can synthesize hormones The placenta actively concentrates iodine on the fetal side, and by 12 weeks and throughout pregnancy, the fetal thyroid concentrates iodine more avidly than does the maternal thyroid. Normal fetal levels of free thyroxine (T4), free triiodothyronine (T3), and TBG rise steadily throughout gestation Fetal thyroid hormone plays a role in the normal development of virtually all fetal tissues, especially the brain Immediately after birth, thyroid function and metabolism undergo major change. Cooling to room temperature evokes a sudden and marked increase in TSH secretion. This in turn causes a progressive increase in serum T4 levels that are maximal 24 to 36 hours after birth.
  • Ref: Page 359, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page 359, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following will increase fetal urine production?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • The fetal kidneys start producing urine at 12 weeks. By 18 weeks, they are producing 7 to 14 mL/d, and at term, this increases to 650 mL/d Urine production is increased with Diabetes mellitus (fetal hyperglycemia) Increase blood flow to the fetus (like in recipient twin in TTTS) Maternally administered furosemide Urine production is decreased in Utero placental insufficiency (Option C) Fetal-growth restriction (Option B) Obstruction of the urethra, bladder, ureters, or renal pelvis (Option A)

Educational Objective:

  • The fetal kidneys start producing urine at 12 weeks. By 18 weeks, they are producing 7 to 14 mL/d, and at term, this increases to 650 mL/d Urine production is increased with Diabetes mellitus (fetal hyperglycemia) Increase blood flow to the fetus (like in recipient twin in TTTS) Maternally administered furosemide Urine production is decreased in Utero placental insufficiency Fetal-growth restriction Obstruction of the urethra, bladder, ureters, or renal pelvis
  • Ref: Page no 358, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 358, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following gives meconium its greenish-black color?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Fetal bowel contents consist of various products of secretion, such as glycerophospholipids from the lung, desquamated fetal cells, lanugo, scalp hair, and vernix. It also contains undigested debris from swallowed amnionic fluid. The dark greenish-black color forms from bile pigments, especially biliverdin. Meconium can pass from normal bowel peristalsis in the mature fetus or from vagal stimulation. It can also pass when hypoxia stimulates arginine vasopressin (AVP) release from the fetal pituitary gland. AVP stimulates colonic smooth muscle to contract, resulting in intra-amnionic defecation Meconium is toxic to the respiratory system, and its inhalation can result in meconium aspiration syndrome.

Educational Objective:

  • Fetal bowel contents consist of various products of secretion, such as glycerophospholipids from the lung, desquamated fetal cells, lanugo, scalp hair, and vernix. It also contains undigested debris from swallowed amnionic fluid. The dark greenish-black color forms from bile pigments, especially biliverdin. Meconium can pass from normal bowel peristalsis in the mature fetus or from vagal stimulation. It can also pass when hypoxia stimulates arginine vasopressin (AVP) release from the fetal pituitary gland. AVP stimulates colonic smooth muscle to contract, resulting in intra-amnionic defecation Meconium is toxic to the respiratory system, and its inhalation can result in meconium aspiration syndrome.
  • Ref: Page no 355, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 355, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: At what gestational age does swallowing begin?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Swallowing begins at 10 to 12 weeks, coincident with the ability of the small intestine to undergo peristalsis and to actively transport glucose

Educational Objective:

  • Swallowing begins at 10 to 12 weeks, coincident with the ability of the small intestine to undergo peristalsis and to actively transport glucose

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Where does the biosynthesis of surfactant take place?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Biosynthesis of surfactant takes place in the type II pneumocytes. Phospholipid is the primary surface tension–lowering component of surfactant, whereas the apoproteins aid the forming and reforming of a surface film. The major apoprotein is surfactant A (SP-A), which is a glycoprotein with a molecular weight of 28,000 to 35,000 Da. It is synthesized in the type II cells, and its content in amnionic fluid increases with gestational age and fetal lung maturity Antenatal betamethasone and dexamethasone increase surfactant production when administered antenatally (given in women expected to deliver preterm) and are given for antenatal lung maturation.

Educational Objective:

  • Biosynthesis of surfactant takes place in the type II pneumocytes. Phospholipid is the primary surface tension–lowering component of surfactant, whereas the apoproteins aid the forming and reforming of a surface film. The major apoprotein is surfactant A (SP-A), which is a glycoprotein with a molecular weight of 28,000 to 35,000 Da. It is synthesized in the type II cells, and its content in amnionic fluid increases with gestational age and fetal lung maturity Antenatal betamethasone and dexamethasone increase surfactant production when administered antenatally (given in women expected to deliver preterm) and are given for antenatal lung maturation.
  • Ref: Page 353, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page 353, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Approximately what percentage of the total hemoglobin is hemoglobin F in a term fetus?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Hb is composed of two copies of two different peptide chains, which determine the type of hemoglobin produced Hemoglobin types transition throughout pregnancy. Alpha (α) and zeta (ζ) globin chains are interchangeable. The beta (β) globin chain can be replaced by epsilon (ε), gamma (γ), or delta ( δ) globin chains. In embryonic life (stage I), ζ and ε globin chains exist In fetal life (stage 2): Hemoglobin F is the main type (α and γ globin chains) After birth (stage III), HbA1 predominates (α and β chains)
  • The amount of hemoglobin F in fetal erythrocytes begins to decrease in the last weeks of pregnancy. At term, approximately three fourths (75%) of total hemoglobin levels are hemoglobin F. During the first 6 to 12 months of life, the hemoglobin F proportion continues to decline and eventually reaches the low levels found in adult erythrocytes

Educational Objective:

  • Hemoglobin types transition throughout pregnancy. Alpha (α) and zeta (ζ) globin chains are interchangeable. The beta (β) globin chain can be replaced by epsilon (ε), gamma (γ), or delta ( δ) globin chains. In embryonic life (stage I), ζ and ε globin chains exist In fetal life (stage 2): Hemoglobin F is the main type (α and γ globin chains) After birth (stage III), HbA1 predominates (α and β chains)
  • Ref: Page 353, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page 353, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: After birth, the intra-abdominal remnants of the umbilical vein become which of the following?

explanation.detail:
Educational Objective:

  • Oxygenated blood enters the fetus through: Umbilical Vein Functional closure: Occurs after closure of umbilical artery Forms the ligamentum teres Deoxygenated Blood exits the fetus through Umbilical arteries Functional closure of umbilical arteries: immediately after birth Anatomical closure: 2 – 3 months Proximal part form the superior vesical arteries Distal part form the lateral umbilical ligaments Ductus venosus: Shunts blood from umbilical vein to IVC Functional closure 10 – 96h after birth Anatomic closure: 3 -7 days Forms the ligamentum venosum Ductus arteriosus: Shunts blood from Pulmonary artery to Aorta Functional closure: 12 – 24 h Anatomic closure: 2 – 3 weeks Foramen ovale Functional closure soon after birth Anatomical closure at 1 year
