subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Sonographic estimation of Amniotic fluid by Amniotic Fluid Index (AFI) is:

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Semi-quantitative

Explanation:

  • Correct quantitative measurement is done only in research settings using dye-dilution method, In clinical settings - We do semiquantitative measurement by ultrasound. This is done by 2 methods: Amniotic Fluid Index (AFI) Single deepest vertical pocket measurement
  • Let us look at each option:
  • Option A. Quantitative : A quantitative measurement would imply a precise, numerical estimation of the amniotic fluid volume. While AFI does provide numerical values, it's not a direct measurement of the exact volume but rather an index based on ultrasound findings.
  • Option B. Qualitative: Qualitative measurements are descriptive and non-numerical.
  • Option C. Semi-quantitative (Correct Answer): AFI is considered semi-quantitative because it provides a numerical estimate based on the depth of fluid pockets in four quadrants of the uterus. It gives a numerical value but is not a direct or precise measurement of total fluid volume, hence 'semi-quantitative'.
  • Option D. Semi-qualitative: This term would imply a combination of numerical and descriptive assessment. Since AFI is primarily focused on providing a numerical index rather than a descriptive analysis, this term is less accurate in describing it.

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page no 226, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 226, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 26-year primi undergoes an ultrasound at 37 weeks for fetal wellbeing. The report says the amniotic fluid index is normal. What is a normal AFI?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B) 5 – 25

Explanation:

  • Amniotic Fluid Index (AFI)
  • The ultrasound transducer is held perpendicular to the floor and parallel to the long axis of the woman The uterus is divided into 4 equal quadrants – rt and left upper and lower quadrants The AFI is the sum of the single deepest pocket from each quadrant. So the unit of measurement is mm/ cm Normal value: 5 – 25 (ACOG)

Educational Objective:

  • A normal Amniotic Fluid Index (AFI) ranges from 5 to 25 cm. This range is important for assessing fetal wellbeing, as it indicates a sufficient volume of amniotic fluid for the fetus to move freely and develop properly .
  • Ref: Page no 227, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 227, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Single deepest pocket of amniotic fluid can be used to estimate the amniotic fluid volume. The normal value is:

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. 2 – 8 cm

Explanation:

  • Or Maximal vertical pocket The ultrasound probe is held perpendicular to the floor and parallel to the long axis of the woman. The largest vertical pocket of fluid is identified and measured. Unit of measurement: cm/ mm The single deepest pocket of liquor is considered normal if between 2 - 8 cm More useful in 2nd trimester assessment of amniotic fluid and in multiple gestation.

Educational Objective:

  • The single deepest pocket (SDP) method is a valuable tool for estimating amniotic fluid volume in pregnancy, with the normal range being 2 to 8 cm. This measurement helps in assessing the adequacy of amniotic fluid, which is crucial for the normal development and wellbeing of the fetus.
  • Ref: Page no 227, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 227, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: The image below shows measurement of?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A) Amniotic fluid index

Explanation:

  • Amniotic Fluid Index (AFI)
  • The ultrasound transducer is held perpendicular to the floor and parallel to the long axis of the woman. The uterus is divided into 4 equal quadrants – right and left upper and lower quadrants
  • The AFI is the sum of the single deepest pocket from each quadrant.
  • So the unit of measurement is mm/ cm
  • Normal value: 5 – 25 (ACOG)
  • Let us look at the other options:
  • Option B. Deepest Vertical Pocket (DVP) of Liquor : The DVP method measures the single deepest vertical pocket of amniotic fluid without fetal parts or umbilical cord. This measurement is taken as a single deepest unobstructed vertical length. In the image, if only one pocket is being measured for its vertical length, it could represent the DVP.
  • Option C. BPD and HC (Biparietal Diameter and Head Circumference) : These are measurements of fetal head size. BPD is the diameter between the two sides of the head, and HC is the circumference around the head. These measurements are not related to amniotic fluid and are typically not assessed in the same ultrasound view that is used for AFI or DVP.
  • Option D. Cervical Length : This measures the length of the cervix, usually to assess the risk of preterm labor. This is not what is shown in the image, as cervical length measurements are taken by transvaginal ultrasound and focus solely on the cervix, not the amniotic fluid.

Educational Objective:

  • In ultrasound assessment of a pregnant woman, the Amniotic Fluid Index (AFI) is calculated by summing the depths of the largest pockets of amniotic fluid in four quadrants of the uterus. It is a crucial parameter for assessing the well-being of the fetus, as both low and high volumes of amniotic fluid can have clinical implications.
  • Ref: Page no 227, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 227, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All are causes of polyhydramnios except:

explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Posterior urethral valve

Explanation:

  • Idiopathic: Most Common (66%) Fetal Anomalies Causing impaired swallowing CNS – Anencephaly, Hydranencephaly, Holoprosencephaly Craniofacial – Cleft palate, micrognathia Causing Obstruction to Swallowing: Cystic hygroma Congenital high airway obstruction sequence (CHAOS) GI Obstruction Esophageal atresia Tracheo-esophageal fistula Duodenal atresia Neurological Myotonic dystrophy High output cardiac failure Ebstein’s anomaly (Seen in Lithium intake) Tetralogy of Fallot Cardiomyopathy Metabolic: Baarter Syndrome Placental Chorioangioma Circumvallate placenta Along with fetal hydrops Immune hydrops (Rh isoimmunization) Non immune hydrops e.g. associated with CMV, toxoplasmosis, syphilis and parvovirus) Diabetes mellitus : Fetal hyperglycemia causes fetal osmotic diuresis Multifetal gestation : Twin to twin transfusion syndrome
  • Let us look at each option:
  • Option A. Bartter Syndrome : This is a group of rare inherited disorders that affect the kidneys and can result in an imbalance of electrolytes, among other issues. While it's not a direct and common cause of polyhydramnios, any severe imbalance in the fetal environment that affects urine production or fluid balance could potentially contribute to polyhydramnios.
  • Option B. Ebstein Anomaly : This is a congenital heart defect where the tricuspid valve is malformed and positioned lower than normal in the right ventricle. It can lead to heart failure and hydrops in the fetus, which can cause polyhydramnios due to an increase in fetal plasma volume and urine production.
  • Option C. Fetal Myotonic Dystrophy : This is a form of muscular dystrophy that can present in the fetus or at birth. Affected infants may have a poor swallowing reflex and decreased fetal movements, leading to polyhydramnios because the fetus is unable to swallow and absorb the amniotic fluid adequately.
  • Option D. Posterior Urethral Valve (PUV) : This is an obstructive congenital anomaly found in males, where there is a blockage in the urethra caused by excess tissue growth. This condition typically causes oligohydramnios (reduced amniotic fluid) rather than polyhydramnios, due to the obstruction of urinary flow out of the bladder, leading to less fluid being contributed to the amniotic sac.

Educational Objective:

  • Idiopathic: Most Common (66%) Fetal Anomalies Causing impaired swallowing CNS – Anencephaly, Hydranencephaly, Holoprosencephaly Craniofacial – Cleft palate, micrognathia Causing Obstruction to Swallowing: Cystic hygroma Congenital high airway obstruction sequence (CHAOS) GI Obstruction Esophageal atresia Tracheo-esophageal fistula Duodenal atresia Neurological Myotonic dystrophy High output cardiac failure Ebstein’s anomaly (Seen in Lithium intake) Tetralogy of Fallot Cardiomyopathy Metabolic: Baarter Syndrome Placental Chorioangioma Circumvallate placenta Along with fetal hydrops Immune hydrops (Rh isoimmunization) Non immune hydrops e.g. associated with CMV, toxoplasmosis, syphilis and parvovirus) Diabetes mellitus : Fetal hyperglycemia causes fetal osmotic diuresis Multifetal gestation : Twin to twin transfusion syndrome
  • Ref: page no 229, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: page no 229, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 20-year primigravida is diagnosed to have duodenal atresia on her routine level 2 scan done at 20 weeks. Which of the following would NOT have been seen on the ultrasound?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Macrosomia

Explanation:

  • Duodenal atresia is associated with other anomalies (Cardiac, renal & vertebral in 10%) On ultrasound – Double bubble sign Polyhydramnios
  • Duodenal atresia is a congenital defect in which there is a closure or absence of a portion of the lumen of the duodenum. It can be diagnosed prenatally during a routine level 2 scan at around 20 weeks of gestation. Let's look at each option:
  • Option A. Associated Cardiac Anomalies : Duodenal atresia is commonly associated with other anomalies, including cardiac anomalies. About 30% of infants with duodenal atresia also have congenital heart disease.
  • Option B. Double Bubble Sign : This sign refers to two distinct fluid-filled areas seen on ultrasound, representing the stomach and the proximal duodenum. It is a classic sign of duodenal atresia as the duodenum is distended due to the obstruction.
  • Option C. Macrosomia : This term refers to a newborn who is significantly larger than average, often defined as a birth weight of more than 4,000 to 4,500 grams. Macrosomia would not be a finding in the case of duodenal atresia, especially at 20 weeks, since it generally refers to the condition of a newborn at birth. Duodenal atresia may cause growth issues due to nutrient malabsorption, but it is not associated with an increased fetal size in utero.
  • Option D. Polyhydramnios : Since the fetus is unable to properly process and absorb amniotic fluid due to the atresia, this can lead to an accumulation of fluid in the amniotic sac.

Educational Objective:

  • On a prenatal ultrasound, the "double bubble" sign is indicative of duodenal atresia, and associated cardiac anomalies may also be present. Polyhydramnios can occur due to the fetus's inability to swallow and absorb amniotic fluid properly. Macrosomia, which describes a newborn with a significantly larger than average birth weight, is not related to duodenal atresia and would not be seen on the ultrasound at 20 weeks of gestation
  • Page no 229, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 30-yearG2P1 is found to have oligohydramnios at 28 weeks. All are causes of oligohydramnios except:

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Prematurity

Explanation:

  • Decreased urine production Uteroplacental insufficiency – preeclampsia, FGR Placental maturity (post term) Medication: ACE-I ARB NSAIDs Fetal renal anomalies: B/l renal agenesis B/l multicystic dysplastic kidneys Infantile form of Autosomal recessive polycystic kidney disease Obstruction to the Renal outflow tract Posterior urethral valve (keyhole sign) Urethral atresia/ stenosis Megacystis microcolon intestinal hypoperistalsis syndrome Sirenomelia Loss of Amniotic fluid PPROM Others Twin to twin transfusion syndrome Idiopathic
  • Let us look at each option:
  • Option A. Prelabor Rupture of Membranes (PROM) : PROM refers to the breaking of the amniotic sac and leakage of amniotic fluid before the onset of labor. This can lead to a significant decrease in the amount of amniotic fluid, causing oligohydramnios. This condition is more common in the later stages of pregnancy but can occur at any time after the mid-second trimester.
  • Option B. Bilateral Multicystic Dysplastic Kidneys : This is a condition where the kidneys develop cysts and are nonfunctional from birth. Since the kidneys are responsible for producing urine, which contributes significantly to amniotic fluid, their dysfunction can lead to reduced fluid production and oligohydramnios.
  • Option C. Prematurity (Correct Answer): Prematurity, per se, is not a direct cause of oligohydramnios. Oligohydramnios is more typically associated with specific fetal or maternal conditions rather than the gestational age of the fetus. Prematurity may be a consequence of other complications in pregnancy but is not a cause of oligohydramnios.
  • Option D. Posterior Urethral Valve : This condition is an obstruction of the male urethra. It can lead to a decrease in the amount of urine reaching the amniotic sac, which in turn can result in oligohydramnios.

Educational Objective:

  • Oligohydramnios, a condition marked by insufficient amniotic fluid, can be caused by factors such as the prelabor rupture of membranes, renal abnormalities like bilateral multicystic dysplastic kidneys, and obstructive uropathies like posterior urethral valve. Prematurity itself is not a cause of oligohydramnios; rather, it may be a result of complications including oligohydramnios during pregnancy. Early detection and management of oligohydramnios are crucial for optimizing fetal outcomes .

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 36-yearprimigravida presented for an anomaly scan at 20 weeks. The following image was noted on the ultrasound along with polyhydramnios. What is the diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Duodenal atresia

Explanation:

  • Duodenal atresia is associated with other anomalies (Cardiac, renal & vertebral in 10%) On ultrasound – Double bubble sign (as seen in the image in the question) Polyhydramnios Posterior urethral valve and rena l agenesis will have Oligohydrmanios Cystic hygroma will appear as a septated swelling:
  • Let us look at each option :
  • Option A. Cystic Hygroma : This is a fluid-filled sac that results from a blockage in the lymphatic system. It typically appears as a large, cystic mass in the neck region of the fetus. While cystic hygroma can be associated with other abnormalities, it does not directly cause polyhydramnios .
  • Option B. Duodenal Atresia (Correct Answer): This is characterized by a blockage in the duodenum, which is part of the small intestine. On an ultrasound, duodenal atresia is suggested by the "double bubble" sign – two distinct fluid-filled areas seen in the fetal abdomen, representing the stomach and the proximal duodenum. Polyhydramnios is often present because the fetus cannot swallow and absorb amniotic fluid normally due to the intestinal obstruction.
  • Option C. Posterior Urethral Valve : This condition, which occurs only in males, is characterized by a blockage in the urethra due to abnormal tissue growth. The key ultrasound finding is typically a dilated bladder and, in some cases, hydronephrosis (swelling of the kidneys). This condition is more likely to lead to oligohydramnios (reduced amniotic fluid) rather than polyhydramnios.
  • Option D. Renal Agenesis : This is the absence of one or both kidneys. When both kidneys are absent (bilateral renal agenesis), there is usually oligohydramnios because the fetus cannot produce urine to contribute to the amniotic fluid.

Educational Objective:

  • The diagnosis of duodenal atresia is suggested by the presence of a "double bubble" sign on ultrasound. This finding, combined with polyhydramnios, is indicative of an obstruction in the fetal gastrointestinal tract, specifically at the duodenum, leading to an accumulation of amniotic fluid.
  • Ref: Page no 229, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 229, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Keyhole sign on ultrasound is seen in:

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Posterior urethral valve

Explanation:

  • Posterior urethral valve causes obstruction to the outflow of urine in male fetus On ultrasound Oligohydramnios Distended bladder seen giving the appearance of keyhole
  • Let us look at the other options:
  • Option A. Renal Agenesis : This is the absence of one or both kidneys at birth.There would be an absence of the kidney(s) and possibly associated findings related to the lack of urine production.
  • Option B. Ureteropelvic Junction Obstruction : This condition involves a blockage in the area where the ureter joins the kidney, impeding the flow of urine into the bladder. Ultrasound findings might include hydronephrosis.
  • Option D. Dysplastic Kidneys : Renal dysplasia involves the malformation of the kidneys during fetal development. The ultrasound might show enlarged kidneys with cysts and other abnormalities.

Educational Objective:

  • The keyhole sign on prenatal ultrasound is indicative of posterior urethral valve obstruction. This sign reflects the dilation of the posterior urethra and the bladder caused by the obstruction and is an important diagnostic clue for this condition. Detecting PUV is critical because it can lead to significant morbidity if not addressed promptly after birth
  • Ref : Page 230, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page 230, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Potter's sequence is seen in all the following except?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Tracheoesophageal fistula

Explanation:

  • Potter's sequence is the effect chronic oligohydramnios has on the fetus. It includes
  • Growth restriction Pulmonary hypoplasia Potter facies (low set ears, flattened nose and micrognathia) Limb contractures and club foot.
  • Causes of early onset oligohydramnios like bilateral fetal renal anomalies or very preterm rupture of membranes can cause Potters sequence. Tracheo-esophageal fistula causes polyhydramnios
  • Let's look at each option :
  • Option A. Bilateral Polycystic Kidney Disease : This condition can cause the kidneys to be nonfunctional due to the replacement of normal kidney tissue with multiple cysts. It can result in oligohydramnios and lead to the development of Potter's sequence .
  • Option B. Renal Agenesis : This refers to the congenital absence of one or both kidneys. Bilateral renal agenesis is directly associated with Potter's sequence because without kidneys, the fetus cannot produce urine, leading to a significant reduction in amniotic fluid.
  • Option C. Early Preterm Premature Rupture of Membranes (PPROM) : PPROM can lead to the leakage of amniotic fluid long before the pregnancy has reached term, which can result in prolonged oligohydramnios. This can cause the physical deformities characteristic of Potter's sequence due to the lack of cushioning fluid around the fetus.
  • Option D. Tracheoesophageal Fistula : This is a congenital anomaly where there is an abnormal connection (fistula) between the trachea and the esophagus. While this condition can result in polyhydramnios due to the inability of the fetus to swallow and absorb amniotic fluid, it does not cause oligohydramnios.

Educational Objective:

  • Potter's sequence is associated with conditions that lead to oligohydramnios, such as bilateral renal agenesis, bilateral polycystic kidney disease, and situations causing chronic leakage of amniotic fluid like early PPROM. It is characterized by a set of physical deformities that arise due to the lack of amniotic fluid and the resulting compression of the developing fetus.
  • Ref: page no 231, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: page no 231, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: The pH of the amniotic fluid is?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A) 7.2

Explanation:

  • The pH of the amniotic fluid is slightly alkaline (7.2)
  • Other features of amniotic fluid:
  • Color: straw colored / colorless 1st half of pregnancy: composition is identical to a transudate of plasma; altered later once the main component becomes fetal urine. Faintly alkaline Low specific gravity (1.010) Hypotonic at term; osmolarity; 250mOsm /I at term (decreases with increasing age) Color Contains lanugo, exfoliated squamous epithelial, vernix caseosa etc.

Educational Objective:

  • The normal pH of amniotic fluid is slightly alkaline, usually around 7.2. Amniotic fluid pH is a vital sign of the fetal environment, and deviations from the normal range can indicate potential clinical concerns that may necessitate further investigation and intervention.
  • Ref: page no 226, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: page no 226, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 25 -year primi is diagnosed to have polyhydramnios. She complains of severe discomfort due to the over distended abdomen. You decide to start indomethacin to reduce the liquor. Which of the following is a contra indication for using this drug?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A) Gestational age > 32 weeks

Explanation:

  • Indomethacin and sulindac are used in the treatment of severe polyhydramnios However, if given after 32 weeks: there is risk of premature closure of the ductus arteriosus and hence are contra-indicated after 32 weeks
  • Premature closure can lead to pulmonary hypertension and heart failure in the fetus.
  • Let's look at the other options:
  • Option B. Polyhydramnios due to anencephaly : Polyhydramnios can occur in this setting because the fetus cannot swallow amniotic fluid normally, leading to its accumulation. While indomethacin may be used to reduce fluid volume, it doesn't address the underlying condition, which is incompatible with life outside the womb.
  • Option C. Muscular dystrophy in the fetus : Fetal muscular dystrophy may be associated with polyhydramnios due to decreased fetal swallowing and movement. Indomethacin would not be a contraindication unless the gestational age is beyond 32 weeks or other specific risks are identified.
  • Option D . Non-immune hydrops : This is a condition characterized by abnormal accumulation of fluid in two or more fetal compartments. While indomethacin may reduce the amniotic fluid component, it's not the treatment for the underlying causes of non-immune hydrops. Indomethacin use is not strictly contraindicated by the presence of hydrops itself .

Educational Objective:

  • Indomethacin is contraindicated for use in reducing amniotic fluid volume in the context of polyhydramnios when the gestational age is greater than 32 weeks due to the increased risk of adverse fetal effects, such as premature closure of the ductus arteriosus .
  • Ref: Page no 229, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 229, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 30-year G3 is in labor at 37 weeks. At 5cm cervical dilatation, an amniotomy is done. Golden color of amniotic fluid is seen. What could be a possible cause?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Rh isoimmunization

Explanation:

  • In early pregnancy: It is colorless Straw colored: Near term Deep yellow/ golden color: Fetal anemia Green (meconium stained): fetal distress or post-maturity Red: Abruption Dark colored/ tobacco like: Fetal demise
  • Now, let us look at each option:
  • Option A. Fetal Demise : Amniotic fluid may become discolored due to the breakdown of fetal red blood cells and the release of bile pigment. The fluid is more likely to appear dark or brownish, often referred to as "port-wine" colored due to hemolysis.
  • Option B. Fetal Distress : Fetal distress can lead to the passage of meconium into the amniotic fluid, which typically gives the fluid a greenish tint.
  • Option C. Rh Isoimmunization : This occurs when an Rh-negative mother becomes sensitized to Rh-positive fetal red blood cells. The immune response leads to hemolysis of the fetal red blood cells, and the release of bilirubin, which can give the amniotic fluid a yellow or golden color. This condition is also known as hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn (HDFN).
  • Option D. Post Maturity : Post maturity refers to a pregnancy that extends beyond 42 weeks. Post maturity is more commonly associated with oligohydramnios (low amniotic fluid volume) and the presence of meconium, which would make the fluid greenish if the fetus has passed stool.

Educational Objective:

  • In early pregnancy: It is colorless Straw colored: Near term Deep yellow/ golden color: Fetal anemia Green (meconium stained): fetal distress or post-maturity Red: Abruption Dark colored/ tobacco like: Fetal demise
  • Ref: Page no 226, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 226, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 24-yearlady presents to the OPD at 32 weeks gestation. On examination the uterus is 36 weeks in height and the fetal parts are not easily palpable. The FHR is heard distant and faint. The most probable diagnosis is?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Polyhydramnios

Explanation:

  • Important Clinical Features in Polyhydramnios
  • Symptoms: Discomfort mainly Signs: Pedal edema P/A Inspection: Overdistension, umbilicus everted, stretched shiny skin Palpation: Fetal parts not felt easily Ballotability easy to elicit Fluid thrill Auscultation: FHR faint
  • Let us look at the other options:
  • Option A. Oligohydramnios : This condition is characterized by a lower-than-normal amount of amniotic fluid. It would typically present with a smaller uterine size relative to gestational age, not larger. Fetal parts may be more easily palpable due to the decreased cushioning from the amniotic fluid, and the FHR would not necessarily be distant or faint.
  • Option B. Multiple Pregnancy : A twin or multiple pregnancy can cause an enlarged uterus. However, in such cases, fetal parts are usually more readily palpable, and the FHRs would be more distinct and possibly multiple.
  • Option C. Fibroid Uterus : Fibroids are benign tumors of the uterus that can distort the uterine shape and size. While a fibroid uterus can cause an enlarged uterus, it typically wouldn't make the FHR distant or faint. Additionally, fibroids do not directly affect the amniotic fluid volume.

Educational Objective:

  • Important Clinical Features in Polyhydramnios
  • Symptoms: Discomfort mainly Signs: Pedal edema P/A Inspection: Overdistension, umbilicus everted, stretched shiny skin Palpation: Fetal parts not felt easily Ballotability easy to elicit Fluid thrill Auscultation: FHR faint
  • Ref: page no 228, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: page no 228, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 34y G3P2 presents to the OPD at 34 weeks. She has had 2 previous lower segment cesarean sections and an done at 28 weeks shows an anterior low-lying placenta. She does not complain of bleeding. On examination, the uterus is 32 weeks, cephalic presentation and the FHR is 145 bpm. What is the next best step?
options: Order an ultrasound with a Doppler

explanation.short: A. Order an ultrasound with a Doppler
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • She is at an increased risk of placental accrete syndromes as she has 2 previous cearean sections and an anterior placenta previa The risk of placenta accreta increases with the number of previous cesareans Any patient with a previous cesarean, with placenta previa in the current pregnancy is at an increased risk for placenta acrrete syndromes The investigation of choice in placenta accreta syndromes is an ultrasound doppler and this should be done in this patient
  • Opton B: As the patient does not have to be delivered right now, steroids are not indicated Option C: An Elective cesarean section may have to be planned if placenta previa (At 37 weeks) or if placenta acrrete syndromes (between 34 - 37 weeks) but the question asked is the next best step and this is to rule out placenta acrrete syndromes by doing a Doppler Option D: A repeat ultrasound may have to be done but that again is not the next best step.

Educational Objective:

  • The risk of placenta acrrete syndromes (PAS) increases with the number of prior cesarean sections. A patient with placenta previa with a h/o previous cesarean section should be investigated for placenta accrete syndromes (PAS)
  • Ref: Page 1967, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page 1967, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which is incorrect regarding the pathophysiology of placenta accrete syndromes?
options: The abnormal adherence is due to partial or total absence of the decidua basalis and imperfect development of the fibrinoid or Nitabuch layer
Microscopically, the placental villi are found attached to smooth muscle fibers
There is a close relationship between cesarean scar pregnancy (CSP) and later development of Placenta accrete syndromes in the same pregnancy
About 15% of all accrete syndromes are placenta percreta

explanation.short: D. About 15% of all accrete syndromes are placenta percreta
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • The ratio of occurrence of placenta accreta: increta: percreta is 80:15:5; i.e. 5% of all adherent placentas are placenta percreta and hence this statement is incorrect The term placenta accreta spectrum (PAS) describes aberrant placentation characterized by abnormally implanted, invasive, or adhered placenta. It is also referred to as morbidly adherent placenta, The major problem if placenta accreta syndrome occurs is failure of placenta tto separate normally from the myometrium after fetal delivery. Attempts to seperate an adherent placenta can lead to torrential hemorrhage This abnormal adherence stems in part from partial or total absence of the decidua basalis and imperfect development of the fibrinoid or Nitabuch layer If the decidual spongy layer is lacking either partially or totally, the physiological line of cleavage is absent, and some or all cotyledons are densely anchored. Microscopically, placental villi attach to smooth muscle fibers rather than to decidual cells. Factors implicated in the pathogenesis An antecedent “constitutional endometrial defect” in most cases is found like a history of a previous cesarean section Some tissue specimens show immunologically mediated “hyperinvasiveness” Some genes that code for remodeling and for adherence may be highly expressed There is a close relationship between cesarean scar pregnancy (CSP) and later development of PAS in the same pregnancy.

Educational Objective:

  • In Placenta Accrete Syndromes, the abnormal adherence stems in part from partial or total absence of the decidua basalis and imperfect development of the fibrinoid or Nitabuch layer
  • Ref: Page no 1974, Williams Obstetric 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1974, Williams Obstetric 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 34y G2P1 with a previous LSCS presented at 30 weeks with painless vaginal bleeding. The following image was seen on ultrasound doppler. Which of the following is not seen in placenta accreta?
options: Disruption of the bladder-uterine serosal interface
Bridging vessels from the placenta to the bladder-serosal interface

explanation.short: A. Thickening of the retroplacental myometrium
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Thinning of the retro-placental myometrium is seen on ultrasound in Placenta Accrete Syndromes (PAS)
  • Five characteristic sonographic findings suggest Placenta Acceta Syndromes are:
  • Placental lacunae Thinning of the retroplacental myometrium Disruption of the bladder-uterine serosal interface Bridging vessels from the placenta to the bladder-serosal interface Placental bulge that pushes outward and distorts the contour of the uterus

Educational Objective:

  • Remember: The investigation of choice to diagnose placenta acrrete syndromes is Ultrasound + Color Doppler
  • Ref: Page 1979, Williams Obstetrics, 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page 1979, Williams Obstetrics, 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 24y G2P1 with a previous LSCS presented at 28 weeks with painless mild vaginal bleeding. She had a previous similar episode 2 weeks earlier. A scan done was suspicious for placenta accreta and this was confirmed on a Doppler. What is the next best step in management?
options: Plan an elective LSCS at 37 weeks
Plan an elective classical section at 37 weeks

explanation.short: B. Plan an elective classical section at 37 weeks
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Management of placenta accreta involves Planning delivery between 34-37 weeks Delivery is usually by a classical cesarean (upper segment) to avoid the placenta Following delivery of the baby, a hysterectomy is carried out with the placenta in situ Conservative approaches like leaving the placenta in situ and resection and repair of uterus are less commonly done.
  • Option A & C: Lower segment cesarean section (LSCS) is not done for placenta accreta syndromes as the placenta is usually adherent in the lower segment Option D: As the patient is currently stable and bleeding is mild, immediate cesarean is not indicated

Educational Objective:

  • Management of placenta accreta involves Planning delivery between 34-37 weeks Delivery is usually by a classical cesarean (upper segment) to avoid the placenta Following delivery of the baby, a hysterectomy is carried out with the placenta in situ
  • Page no 1985, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: The investigation of choice for placenta accreta is:

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • USG with color Doppler has a high sensitivity and specificity for diagnosing placenta accreta syndromes Five characteristic sonographic findings suggest Placenta Acceta Syndromes are: Placental lacunae Thinning of the retroplacental myometrium Disruption of the bladder-uterine serosal interface Bridging vessels from the placenta to the bladder-serosal interface Placental bulge that pushes outward and distorts the contour of the uterus
  • Option A: Plain Ultrasound is not as useful as Doppler for diagnosing Placenta accreta Option C: MRI can be added on in doubtful cases especially if the placenta is posteriorly situated Option D: CECT: CT scans are generally contra-indicated in pregnancy and are not useful in diagnosing placenta acrrete syndromes

Educational Objective:

  • USG with color Doppler has a high sensitivity and specificity for diagnosing placenta accreta syndromes and is the investigation of choice
  • Ref: Page no 1980, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1980, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Ligation of which of the following vessels in the retroperitoneum will reduce the pulse pressure in a patient who is bleeding during surgery for placenta accreta syndrome?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Internal iliac artery ligation is performed in obstetric hemorrhage like PPH and placenta accreta syndromes It is part of devascularization surgeries to reduce blood loss in massive obstetric hemorrhage along with uterine artery and utero-ovarian artery ligation The most important mechanism of action with internal iliac artery ligation is an 85-percent reduction in pulse pressure in those arteries distal to the ligation and a 50% reducition in blood flow to the uterus This converts an arterial pressure system into one with pressures approaching those in the venous circulation. This creates vessels more amenable to hemostasis via pressure and clot formation.
  • Option A: Uterine artery ligation is the 1st vessel ligated in devascularization surgery but it does not reduce the pulse pressure as effectively as does internal iliac artery ligation
  • Option C: External iliac artery: Is not ligated as part of devascularization as it does not supply the uterus
  • Option D: utero-ovarian anastomoses ligation is done as part of devasculariazation surgery but it does not reduce the pulse pressure as effectively as does internal iliac artery ligation

Educational Objective:

  • Internal iliac artery ligation is performed in obstetric hemorrhage like PPH and placenta accreta syndromes It is part of devascularization surgeries to reduce blood loss in massive obstetric hemorrhage along with uterine artery and utero-ovarian artery ligation The most important mechanism of action with internal iliac artery ligation is an 85-percent reduction in pulse pressure in those arteries distal to the ligation and a 50% reducition in blood flow to the uterus
  • Ref: Page no 2027, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 2027, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 28-year-old pregnant woman, G4P3 at 28 weeks of gestation, presents with recurrent episodes of painless vaginal bleeding over the past 1 week. Her medical history is significant for 1 previous cesarean delivery and 1 dilatation and curettage of a previous incomplete abortion. On examination, her vital signs are stable, and the fetal heart rate is reassuring. Abdominal ultrasound reveals a placenta located near the cervix with multiple hypoechoic areas and irregular vascular spaces within the placenta. Laboratory results show an Hb of 8g%. Which of the following is the most appropriate management option?
options: Immediate cesarean followed by bilateral internal iliac artery ligation
Administration of corticosteroids, transfuse blood, followed by a planned cesarean at 37 weeks.
Serial ultrasound monitoring with plans of delivery if evidence of increasing vascularity in the placenta
Admit, transfuse blood, give steroids and plan an elective cesarean delivery with hysterectomy at 36 weeks of pregnancy

explanation.short: D. Admit, transfuse blood, give steroids and plan an elective cesarean delivery with hysterectomy at 36 weeks of pregnancy
explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Admit, transfuse blood, give steroids and plan an elective cesarean delivery with hysterectomy at 36 weeks of pregnancy.

Explanation:

  • This is a clinical scenario of placenta accreta syndromes as the history of previous cesarean and D & C with the current ultrasound findings support this diagnosis Treatment of placenta accreta syndromes includes Stabilization of the patient Planning an elective cesarean delivery (preferably a classical cesarean) at 34-37 weeks, leaving the placenta in situ followed by a hysterectomy as any attempts to remove an adherent placenta result in severe hemorrhage
  • Option A: As the patient is stable currently, immediate cesarean is not warranted as she is only 29 weeks Option B: This would have been correct but option D is a better option in the scenario of accreta. If only placenta previa, this would have been the preferred option Option C: There is no role of looking at increasing vascularity on ultrasound and it will not change the management.

Educational Objective:

  • Treatment of placenta accreta syndromes includes Stabilization of the patient Planning an elective cesarean delivery (preferably a classical cesarean) at 34-37 weeks, leaving the placenta in situ followed by a hysterectomy as any attempts to remove an adherent placenta result in severe hemorrhage
  • Ref: Page 1973, Williams Obstetrics, 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page 1973, Williams Obstetrics, 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: What is the best management option for a patient diagnosed with placenta increta at 36 weeks pregnancy?
options: Planned elective classical cesarean at 37 weeks; placenta to be left in situ
Planned elective classical cesarean at 37 weeks followed by hysterectomy
Emergency classical cesarean section now followed by hysterectomy
Emergency classical cesarean section now; placenta to be left in situ

explanation.short: B. Planned elective classical cesarean at 37 weeks followed by hysterectomy
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Placenta accrete syndromes includes placenta accreta, placenta increta and placenta percreta As the placenta is adherent to the myometrium, any attempt at separation of the placenta at delivery leads to torrential hemorrhage The best management option thus is to plan an elective cesarean between 34-37 weeks (before the patient goes into labor). Avoid an emergency cesarean unless really required. The cesarean is usually a classical cesarean (upper segment) as the most common site of adherence is in the lower segment (usually at the site of a previous cesarean section) After delivery of the baby, the cord is clamped; no attempt at separation of placenta is done and a hysterectomy is done. Leaving the placenta in situ is not preferred to be done but has been occasionally reported by a few. It may lead to secondary haemorrhage and/ or infection thereby necessitating hysterectomy.
  • Option A: Planned elective classical cesarean at 37 weeks; placenta to be left in situ: Although the 1st part is correct, leaving the placenta in situ isn’t advocated as most studies say that the incidence of secondary hemorrhage/ infection is very high Option C: Emergency classical cesarean section now followed by hysterectomy There is no indication for doing an emergency cesarean section. An elective planned cesarean is always preferred in placenta accrete syndromes Option D: Emergency classical cesarean section now; placenta to be left in situ There is no indication for an emergency and as mentioned above the placenta should not be left in situ

Educational Objective:

  • The best management option for Placenta Accrete Syndromes (PAS) is to plan an elective cesarean between 34-37 weeks (before the patient goes into labor). Avoid an emergency cesarean unless really required. The cesarean is usually a classical cesarean (upper segment) as the most common site of adherence is in the lower segment (usually at the site of a previous cesarean section) After delivery of the baby, the cord is clamped; no attempt at separation of placenta is done and a hysterectomy is done.
  • Ref: Page 1984, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition

question: A 23y primi presents in labor. She is an unbooked patient and by her LMP, she is 42 weeks and 4 days. On examination she is 6 cm dilated, well effaced and the vertex is at 0 station. A CTG taken is as shown below. What could be the reason for the findings in the CTG shown below?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • The patient is post term, in labor and the CTG is showing variable decelerations (As there is no relation between the decelerations and the uterine contractions) Variable decelerations occur because of cord compression which is seen in oligohydramnios which is seen in post term pregnancy
  • Option A: Cord prolapse will usually present with prolonged deceleration/ fetal bradycardia/ fetal death. Option B: Meconium, if associated with fetal hypoxia will present with later decelerations. Option D: Uteroplacental insufficiency will present as late decelerations.
