subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 34y man undergoes a no scalpel vasectomy. What is the post operative advise?
options: Does not need any additional contraceptive
Needs additional contraception till a semen analysis shows azoospermia

explanation.short: C. Needs additional contraception till a semen analysis shows azoospermia
explanation.detail:
Ans. C.

Explanation:

  • After vasectomy, clients can resume sexual activity as soon as it is comfortable for them. This usually takes at least two to three days after the procedure. Counsel clients that vasectomy does not work immediately, and they can still get their partners pregnant. Post vasectomy clients have to use condoms or their partners should use another family planning method consistently and correctly for at least three months. After three months, client should return to the clinic for a semen analysis to make sure that the vasectomy was successful. All vasectomy clients should be provided with instructions and practise on the correct use of condoms for use immediately after the vasectomy.

Educational Objective:

  • After vasectomy, clients can resume sexual activity as soon as it is comfortable for them. This usually takes at least two to three days after the procedure. Patients should be counseled that vasectomy does not work immediately, and they can still get their partners pregnant. Post vasectomy clients have to use condoms or their partners should use another family planning method consistently and correctly for at least three months. After three months, client should return to the clinic for a semen analysis to make sure that the vasectomy was successful.

References:
explanation.ref: ↳ https://nhm.gov.in/images/pdf/programmes/family-planing/guidelines/Reference_Manual_for_Male_Sterilization-NSV-Oct_2013.pdf

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 35y man undergoes vasectomy. After 3 months, the semen analysis still shows the presence of sperm. What is the next best step?

explanation.short: A. Repeat semen analysis after 1 month
explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Repeat semen analysis after 1 month

Explanation:

  • A certificate of sterilization should be issued only after the semen analysis shows no sperm.
  • Tell client that he should report to the clinic for semen analysis after three months. If sperms are still present in semen after three months, then client is tested every month till six months. Do not declare failure of vasectomy till three months. If sperms are still present after six months consider re-vasectomy

Educational Objective:

  • Tell the patient that he should report to the clinic for semen analysis after three months. If sperms are still present in semen after three months, then client is tested every month till six months. Do not declare failure of vasectomy till three months. If sperms are still present after six months consider re-vasectomy

References:
explanation.ref: ↳ https://nhm.gov.in/images/pdf/programmes/family-planing/guidelines/Reference_Manual_for_Male_Sterilization-NSV-Oct_2013.pdf

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which method of tubal ligation is associated with maximum failure rate?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Discussing all the options:
  • The following are the techniques used in tubal ligation in a laparotomy/ mini-laparotomy/ concomitant with cesarean section
  • 1. Pomeroy (Option A): A loop is made at the isthmus and 1cm cut out. This is recommended by GOI. It has a low failure rate
  • 2. Parkland (Option B): This is like a partial salpingectomy. More efficacious than Pomeroy as the ends are not brough together
  • The tube is grasped with Babcock clamps.
  • 3. Madlener (Option D): In this the tube is crushed. Not cut. Hence associated with a very high failure rate.
  • 4. Uchida (Option C): As shown in image, fallopian tube is stripped off its serosa and cut, the proximal end is buried into the mesosalpinx and the distal end is ligated and turned away from the proximal end. Very low failure rate
  • 5. Irving: Similar to Uchida: Very low failure rate;
  • 6. Kroener: Fimbriectomy; very low failure rate
  • 7. Aldridge: This is burying the fimbria in the meso-salpinx. It has a high failure rate and isn’t used.

Educational Objective:

  • Madlener method of tubal ligation is associated with a very high failure rate
explanation.ref: https://www.glowm.com/section-view/heading/Surgical%20Procedures%20for%20Tubal%20Sterilization/item/399#

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A resident is doing a tubal ligation for the 1 st time. She is unsure which part of the tube she needs to ligate. Which part of the tube is ligated?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • The operating surgeon should identify each fallopian tube clearly, following it right up to the fimbria. The site of the occlusion of the fallopian tube must always be within 2–3 cm from the uterine cornua in the isthmal portion (this will improve the possibility of reversal if required in the future) Care must be taken to avoid damage to the blood vessels, ovaries, and surrounding tissues Excision of 1 cm of the tube should be done. Use of cautery and crushing of the tube should be avoided. A loop is made using a Babcock’s forceps and ligated with catgut. A 1 cm loop is then cut The same is repeated on the other side

Educational Objective:

  • Technique of Pomeroy's tubal ligation
  • The operating surgeon should identify each fallopian tube clearly, following it right up to the fimbria. The site of the occlusion of the fallopian tube must always be within 2–3 cm from the uterine cornua in the isthmal portion (this will improve the possibility of reversal if required in the future) Care must be taken to avoid damage to the blood vessels, ovaries, and surrounding tissues Excision of 1 cm of the tube should be done. Use of cautery and crushing of the tube should be avoided. A loop is made using a Babcock’s forceps and ligated with catgut. A 1 cm loop is then cut The same is repeated on the other side
explanation.ref: https://nhm.gov.in/images/pdf/guidelines/nrhm-guidelines/family-planning/std-for-sterilization-services.pdf

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: The Anti-progesterone compound RU-486 is effective for inducing abortion if the duration of pregnancy is?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. 63 days

Explanation:

  • RU-486 is Mifepristone and along with Misoprostol, it is used for medical methods of abortion.
  • A combi pack of mifepristone and misoprostol (one tablet of mifepristone 200mg and four tablets of misoprostol 200mcg each) has been approved by the Central Drugs Standard Control Organization, Directorate General of Health Services, for the medical termination of pregnancy up to n ee9ks (63 days) LMP in December 2008 However; CAC Training & Service Delivery Guidelines, 2016 by MoHFW, GoI recommends its use up to 49 days (7 weeks) in public sector sites, in line with the MTP Rules 2003. A proposed amendment to the MTP Act, 1971, to increase the gestation limit for MMA to 9 weeks in public sector sites is under process. Till the proposal is approved , MMA in public health facility would be allowed only up to 7 weeks. So if the question asks approved for use in public health facility, the answer will be 49 days, but it can be given till 9 weeks of pregnancy
  • Option B, C and D all have duration > 63 days. Betwen 9 - 12 weeks, suction evacuation is preferred. After 12 weeks, a medical abortion is again preferred (i.e. different regimes of mifepristone f/b misoprostol have been described).

Educational Objective:

  • A combi pack of mifepristone and misoprostol (one tablet of mifepristone 200mg and four tablets of misoprostol 200mcg each) has been approved by the Central Drugs Standard Control Organization, Directorate General of Health Services, for the medical termination of pregnancy up to n ee9ks (63 days) LMP in December 2008 However; CAC Training & Service Delivery Guidelines, 2016 by MoHFW, GoI recommends its use up to 49 days (7 weeks) in public sector sites, in line with the MTP Rules 2003.
explanation.ref: https://nhm.gov.in/images/pdf/programmes/maternal-health/guidelines/MMA_Handbook.pdf

question: A 34y G3P2L2 at 9 weeks opts for a surgical evacuation of the uterus following an unwanted pregnancy. Which is NOT a long term complication of dilatation and evacuation except?

  • Long term sequelae of suction evacuation/ dilatation and evacuation are:
  • Pelvic inflammation Subfertility Cervical insufficiency Preterm labor Ectopic pregnancy Intra uterine synechiae
  • Endometriosis is NOT a complication of Dilatation and Evacuation

question: As per the 2021 amendment of the MTP act, the upper limit of gestational age for terminating a fetus with substantial anomalies is:

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • As per the 2021 MTP amendment: act, a few important changes which you have to know are that the upper limit of gestational age has been increased as per the following:
  • Till 20 weeks for original indications (maternal indication, fetal indication, failure of contraception and rape); opinion of 1 RMP required Till 24 weeks for select indications like fetal maformation, rape, humanitarian grounds and maternal diability; opinion of 2 RMPS required After 24 weeks: Only for substantial fetal malformations (No upper limit but this has to be allowed by a medical board)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: An intern is assisting the OBGYN resident for a dilatation and evacuation for a surgical abortion. The resident asks him what this instrument is. Identify the instrument:

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • The above instrument is a Hegar’s dilator. It is a metal dilator It is used for mechanical cervical dilatation prior to procedures like dilatation and curettage/ dilatation and evacuation
  • Uterine sound: This is to measure the size and direction of the uterus prior to procedures like an intra-uterine device insertion or a dilatation and curettage
  • Uterine curette: This instrument is used to curette out (or clear) out the contents of the uterus following a vacuum aspiration
  • Laminaria Tent: These are hygroscopic cervical dilators which can be used for any procedure requiring cervical dilatation and are even used in indiction of labor. They are not commonly used in India

Educational Objective:

  • This is a Hegars metal dilator and it is used for mechanical cervical dilatation prior to procedures like dilatation and curettage/ dilatation and evacuation

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 20 year unmarred girl presents to the OPD for a medical termination of pregnancy. Which of the following are contraindications to medical methods of abortion?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Contra-indications to Medical methods of abortion
  • Anemia (Hb < 8g%) Confirmed or suspected ectopic pregnancy (** Important) Uncontrolled HTN Known case of: Heart problems such as angina, valvular disease, arrhythmia which can lead to sudden cardiovascular collapse Renal, liver or respiratory disease (Bronchial asthma is not a contraindication since Misoprostol is a bronchodilator) Current long-term systemic corticosteroid therapy Uncontrolled seizure disorder Chronic adrenal failure Hypersensitivity to Mifepristone/Misoprostol or other prostaglandins Inherited porphyria
  • Option B: Medical methods can be safely done if she is HIV +ve Option C : Mifepristone or misoprostol are not c/i in breastfeeding (However, methotrexate is c/i in breastfeeding) Option D : The standard protocol for medical abortion will not change for multiple pregnancy

Educational Objective:

  • Contra-indications to Medical methods of abortion
  • Anemia (Hb < 8g%) Confirmed or suspected ectopic pregnancy (** Important) Uncontrolled HTN Known case of: Heart problems such as angina, valvular disease, arrhythmia which can lead to sudden cardiovascular collapse Renal, liver or respiratory disease (Bronchial asthma is not a contraindication since Misoprostol is a bronchodilator) Current long-term systemic corticosteroid therapy Uncontrolled seizure disorder Chronic adrenal failure Hypersensitivity to Mifepristone/Misoprostol or other prostaglandins Inherited porphyria

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 20y woman chooses the medical method of abortion for termination of pregnancy at 7 weeks pregnancy. After giving misoprostol, the woman should be called for follow-up on which day:

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Procedure for medical MTP as per MOHFW (2016)
  • On the 15th day (Follow up day), the following is done:
  • 1. Note relevant history/check medical method of aborton (MMA) follow-up card
  • 2. Carry out pelvic examination to ensure completion of abortion process/continuation of pregnancy
  • 3. Reiterate contraceptive counselling and services
  • complete expulsion of POC not confirmed
  • continuation of pregnancy suspected
  • 5. Ask the woman to report back if there are no periods within six weeks

Educational Objective:

  • Procedure for medical MTP as per MOHFW (2016)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Identify this instrument:

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A woman comes to the OPD with 6 weeks amenorrhea. She has previous 2 cesarean deliveries. She does not want to continue the pregnancy and wants an abortion. Which of the following methods of abortion is most appropriate?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • The standard regime of medical methods of abortion is to be followed (i.e. mifepristone + misoprostol) Previous cesarean is not a contra-indication to 1st trimester medical abortion
  • Option A: Methotrexate + Misorpostol: This is not the preferred regime though is used rarely
  • Option C: Only mifepristone: Mifepristone alone is not effective for abortion
  • Option D: Only misoprostol: The success of using misoprostol alone is much less than mifepristone + misoprostol

Educational Objective:

  • The standard regime of medical methods of abortion is to be followed (i.e. mifepristone + misoprostol) Previous cesarean is not a contra-indication to 1st trimester medical abortion

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Identify the instrument:

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • This is a manual vacuum aspirator (MVA) It is used in 1st trimester MTP till 12 weeks of gestation It can be easily used in low resource settings for abortions till 12 weeks It consists of A 60 ml cannula A plunger A double valve apparatus The loaded syringe (this means a syringe wherein vacuum has been created) is attached to the Karman cannula and once instroduced into the dilated cervix, just beyond the internal os, the valves are released and the products of conception get aspirated into the syringe.
  • 1. Menstrual regulation syringe:
  • Menstrual Regulation Syringe refers to the induction of uterine bleeding that has been delayed either to 14 days beyond expected onset or 42 days from the start of the last menstrual period and invovles vacuum aspiration of the uterus with a hand syringe (as shown) and a Karman cannula.
  • 2. Karman's cannula: These are color coded cannula of varying sizes and are attached to the MVA syringe to aspirate contents of the uterus
  • 3. Uterine curette: This is an instrument as shown below, used to curette the endometrium after the products have been evacuated by aspiration. An empty cavity will have a grating sensation when the curette is used.

Educational Objective:

  • This is a manual vacuum aspirator (MVA) It is used in 1st trimester MTP till 12 weeks of gestation It can be easily used in low resource settings for abortions till 12 weeks It consists of A 60 ml cannula (PYQ) A plunger A double valve apparatus The loaded syringe (this means a syringe wherein vacuum has been created) is attached to the Karman cannula and once instroduced into the dilated cervix, just beyond the internal os, the valves are released and the products of conception get aspirated into the syringe.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 25-year-old G2P1L1 at 8 weeks pregnancy wants a medical termination of pregnancy for failure of contraception. She wants to know the advantages of medical abortion over surgical methods. All are true except?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Advantages of medical abortion:
  • Lesser incidence of haemorrhage, PID, perforation, long term sequelae like subfertility, cervical insufficiency and Asherman syndrome Non invasive No anesthesia required
  • 1. Success rate less than surgical abortion (95-99% with medical abortion; almost 100% with surgical evacuation)
  • 2. Takes about 2 weeks for completion (Surgical procedure is quicker and gets done in a 15-30 minutes.
  • Option A: Less incidence of hemorrhage is an advantage of medical MTP Option C: Non invasive: is an advantage of medical MTP Option D: Less incidence of PID: Is an advantage of medical MTP

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: While performing a manual vacuum aspiration for a 6 weeks pregnancy, the 1 st year gyn resident feels that her instrument is passing through without any resistance. The patient is otherwise hemodynamically stable. She immediately stops the procedure and informs her senior. What should be the next step in the management?
options: Complete the procedure under guidance of the senior obstetrician
Complete the procedure in the OT under laparoscopic guidance
Shift the patient to OT and immediately do a laparotomy and exploration
Observe the patient and complete the procedure after 2 weeks once the perforation has healed

explanation.short: B. Complete the procedure in the OT under laparoscopic guidance
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Management protocol of uterine perforation:
  • Whenever there is a suspected perforation while doing an D&C or a D&E, the procedure is stopped Observation Start Antibiotics If procedure is incomplete, d iagnostic laparoscopy to assess the perforation and complete the procedure under laparoscopy guidamce Sometimes, in the event of a lateral cervical tear which can enter the uterine arteries or causea a broad ligament hematoma: the patient rapidly goes into shock and a laparotomy + repair/ hysterectomy has to be done I n the question given, the best option is to complete the procedure under laparoscopy guidance
  • Option 1: A senior obstetrician should be involved but better than his/ her guidance, the procedure should be completed under actual vision Option 3: As the patients vitals are stable, laparotomy is not required Option 4: Waiting for 2 weeks and then completing the procedure is NOT done as this would unecessarily lead to delay with additional complications like bleeding/ infection

Educational Objective:

  • Whenever there is a suspected perforation while doing an D&C or a D&E, the procedure is stopped Observation Start Antibiotics If procedure is incomplete, d iagnostic laparoscopy to assess the perforation and complete the procedure under laparoscopy guidamce Sometimes, in the event of a lateral cervical tear which can enter the uterine arteries or causea a broad ligament hematoma: the patient rapidly goes into shock and a laparotomy + repair/ hysterectomy has to be done
  • Ref: Page no 526, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics 9th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 34y woman has opted for medical method of MTP at 6 weeks after she had an unplanned pregnancy after failure of contraception. She wants to resume contraception after the medical abortion. Which contraceptive methods can be started on the day of taking Misoprostol?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Combined (estrogen and progestogen) or progestin-only, can be started on the day of the Misoprostol administration (day 3) or day 15 of the medical method of abortion regimen.
  • Other contraceptive options in relation to medical MTP:
  • Injectable hormonal methods like Depot Medroxy Progesterone Acetate (DMPA) can also be started on day 3 or 15 IUCD can be inserted after confirmed complete abortion (and after ruling out infection) on day 15. Condoms can be used as soon as she resumes sexual activity after abortion Tubal ligation can be done after the first menstrual cycle. However, if desirous of concurrent tubal ligation, dilatation and evacuation is preferred Vasectomy, if chosen, can be done independent of the procedure
  • Option A: Tubal ligation can be done after the first menstrual cycle. However, if desirous of concurrent tubal ligation, dilatation and evacuation is preferred Option B: IUCD can be inserted after confirmed complete abortion (and after ruling out infection) on day 15 Option D: Vasectomy, if chosen, can be done independent of the procedure

question: Which of the following drugs is commonly used for hormone replacement therapy in post menopausal women to alleviate menopausal symptoms?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Estrogen/ progestin combination therapy

Explanation:

  • Estrogen/ progestin therapy is commonly used as HRT to alleviate post-menopausal symptoms Estrogen primarily alleviates symptoms and progesterone is added to protect the lining of the uterus and reduces the risk of endometrial hyperplasia and cancer
  • Option A: SSRIs are not standard therapy for post-menopausal symptoms but may be prescribed in specific cases to address mood relate menopausal symptoms Option B: Bisphosphonates are medications used to prevent and treat osteoporosis Option C: GnRH agonists are not used for HRT

Educational Objective:

  • Estrogen/ progestin therapy is commonly used as HRT to alleviate post-menopausal symptoms Estrogen primarily alleviates symptoms and progesterone is added to protect the lining of the uterus and reduces the risk of endometrial hyperplasia and cancer So in women who have a uterus, in HRT: Estrogen + Progesterone both are given In women without a uterus (post hysterectomy): in HRT, only estrogen is suffiecient

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 42-year woman presents with heavy menstrual bleeding and dysmenorrhoea. Pelvic ultrasound reveals an intramural fibroid measuring 3 cm. The woman desires medical management rather than surgical intervention. Which of the following would be the most appropriate 1 st line drug in this woman?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Antifibrinolytic with NSAID

Educational Objective:

  • Fibroids can be managed medically or surgically or by newer techniques
  • Drugs for Medical Management
  • NSAIDs Anti-fibrinolytics (Tranexamic acid)
  • A combination of Antibrinolytic and NSAIDs are usually tried as a 1st line management in women with HMB and dysmenorrhoea
  • 3. Combined Estrogen and Progesterones: these are effective in reducing bleeding and pain during periods in women with fibroids.
  • 4. SPRMs: Mifepristone (RU 486) and Ulipristal acetate
  • Is very effective to reduce fibroid size and also menorrhagia. It reduces the size of the fibroid significantly. Long-term therapy with mifepristone is avoided as it causes endometrial hyperplasia. Ulipristal acetate is also used for 3 months. IT is associated with liver toxicity
  • Can reduce the volume of a fibroid slightly. Because of androgenic side effects, danazol is used only for a period of 3–6 months. Not commonly used now
  • Drugs commonly used are goserelin, leprolin, buserelin or nafarelin Mechanism of action is sustained pituitary down regulation and suppression of ovarian function. Optimal duration of therapy is 3 months. Addback therapy may be needed to combat hypoestrogenic symptoms Reduces size of fibroid by upto 50% at 3 months
  • Cetrorelix or ganirelix causes immediate suppression of pituitary and the ovaries. They do not have the initial stimulatory effect. Benefits are same as that of agonists Onset of amenorrhea is rapid.
  • 8. Levonorgestrel-releasing Intrauterine System (LNG-IUS):
  • Reduces blood loss and uterine size. However, this is not recommended when the uterine size is >12 weeks or if the cavity is distorted.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 28-year-old woman presents with infertility and is diagnosed with anovulatory cycles. Which of the following medications can be used for ovulation induction in this patient?

explanation.short: C. Both clomiphene citrate and letrozole
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Both clomiphene citrate and letrozole

Explanation:

  • A. Clomiphene citrate is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) that blocks the negative feedback of estrogen on the hypothalamus, leading to increased production and release of follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and luteinizing hormone (LH). This promotes follicular growth and ovulation.
  • B. Letrozole is an aromatase inhibitor that reduces the conversion of androgens to estrogen, leading to decreased estrogen levels and increased FSH production. It has been found to be equally effective or even superior to clomiphene citrate in some cases, particularly in women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS).
  • Hence Option C (Option A + B) is the answer
  • Option D. Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonists are not typically used for primary ovulation induction. They are more commonly used to suppress ovulation temporarily in certain fertility treatment protocols or for medical management of specific conditions, such as endometriosis.

Educational Objective:

  • Both letrozole and clomiphene citrate are commonly used ovulation induction agents. Gonadotropins (FSH and HMG) are 2nd line drugs. The choice between clomiphene citrate and letrozole may depend on various factors, including the patient's individual characteristics, response to prior treatments, and underlying conditions such as PCOS. In some cases, a healthcare provider may choose to use one medication over the other based on individual patient factors. Rememebr: If PCOS is mentioned, Letrozole would be the preferred agent for ovulation induction, even though clomiohene citrate can also be used

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following is a correct indication for the use of gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonists in gynecology?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Treatment of endometriosis

Explanation:

  • Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonists are commonly used in gynecology for the treatment of endometriosis. GnRH agonists work by initially stimulating the release of gonadotropins, which leads to an initial increase in estrogen production. However, with continued use, they suppress the production of gonadotropins, leading to a decrease in estrogen levels and subsequent reduction in the growth and symptoms associated with endometriosis. Other uses of GnRh agonists include Central precocious puberty IVF cycles (long protocol and also as a trigger in women at high risk of developing OHSS) Endometriosis Fibroid uterus AUB Hirsutism Pre-menstrual syndrome
  • Option B: Ovulation induction in infertility: Drug of choice is clomiphene citrate and letrozole
  • Option C: Hormone replacement therapy in menopause: Drug of choice is estrogen + progesterone
  • Option D: Management of polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS): Treatment is Lifestyle measures + Depending on the patients concerns:
  • Infertility: Letrozole Delayed cycles: Combined OCPs (with preferably a 4th generation progesterone like drosperinone) Hirsutism: Antiandrogrens or an OCP with the progesterone component being an anti-androgen (like cyproterone acetate)

Educational Objective:

  • Uses of GnRh agonists include Central precocious puberty IVF cycles (long protocol and also as a trigger in women at high risk of developing OHSS) Endometriosis Fibroid uterus AUB Hirsutism Pre-menstrual syndrome
  • Ref: Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

  • Option B: Etonorgesterel: This is the progesterone component of the vaginal ring and also of implanon

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following statements regarding ormeloxifene is correct?
options: Ormeloxifene is a selective progesterone receptor modulator (SPRM)
Ormeloxifene is primarily used as an emergency contraceptive
Ormeloxifene is approved for long-term contraception and the treatment of heavy menstrual bleeding
Ormeloxifene is contraindicated in women with a history of breast cancer.

explanation.short: C. Ormeloxifene is approved for long-term contraception and the treatment of heavy menstrual bleeding
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Ormeloxifene is approved for long-term contraception and the treatment of heavy menstrual bleeding.

Explanation:

  • Ormeloxifene is commonly known as CENTCHROMAN/ CHHAYA
  • It is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) Developed by the Central Drug Research Institute, Lucknow It is a non – hormonal, synthetic contraceptive Low cost Initially marketed as Saheli and now as Chhaya by the Govt of India Contains 30 mg Recommended dose for contraception: 1 tablet twice weekly from the 1st day of menstruation for 3 months followed by once a week It has recently been used as a drug for heavy menstrual bleeding 99% effective Contra-indications to Ormeloxifene PCOS (Absolutely c/i) Liver disease (Absolutely c/i) DVT (Relatively c/i) SLE (Relatively c/i) Migraine with aura (Relatively c/i)
  • Option A: Ormeloxifene is a selective progesterone receptor modulator (SPRM): It is an SERM
  • Option B: Ormeloxifene is primarily used as an emergency contraceptive: It is not used as an emergency contraceptive. LNG, Combined OCPs and Uliprostal are used as emergency contracpetives
  • Option C: Ormeloxifene is contraindicated in women with a history of breast cancer: Ormeloxifene is not contra-indicated in breast cancer; it is MEC 2 (benefits outweigh the risks) and can be safely given

Educational Objective:

  • Ormeloxifene is commonly known as CENTCHROMAN/ CHHAYA
  • It is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) Developed by the Central Drug Research Institute, Lucknow It is a non – hormonal, synthetic contraceptive Low cost Initially marketed as Saheli and now as Chhaya by the Govt of India Contains 30 mg Recommended dose for contraception: 1 tablet twice weekly from the 1st day of menstruation for 3 months followed by once a week It has recently been used as a drug for heavy menstrual bleeding 99% effective Contra-indications to Ormeloxifene PCOS (Absolutely c/i) Liver disease (Absolutely c/i) DVT (Relatively c/i) SLE (Relatively c/i) Migraine with aura (Relatively c/i)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: In the management of endometriosis, which of the following is the primary mechanism of action of danazol?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Danazol is an isoxazole derivative of 17-alpha ethinyl testosterone. It is strictly anti-gonadotropin but acts as an androgen agonist. Mode of action: The mechanism of action is complex and includes the following: Acting on the hypothalamo-pituitary gonadal axis → depression of frequency of GnRH pulses → suppression of pituitary FSH and LH surge. There is, however, no change in the basal gonadotropin level while the estrogen levels are reduced. Reduces the liver synthesis of SHBG and as such, free testosterone is increased which in turn causes endometrial atrophy. Acts directly on the ovaries, inhibiting the enzymes responsible for steroidogenesis – reducing the estrogen level Binds with steroid receptors on the endometrium and also in the ectopic endometrial sites Immunologic effects of danazol include decrease in serum immunoglobulins, interleukin-1 and TNF production. This effect helps in the regression of endometriosis. The net result is production of an hypoestrogenic state Indications of use of Danazol: (Although it is now not very commonly used)
  • Endometriosis Precocious puberty AUB PMS Symptomatic fibroid Benign fibrocystic disease of the breasts (mastalgia) Prior to hysteroscopic endometrial ablation, to make the endometrium thin

Educational Objective:

  • Indications of use of Danazol: (Although it is now not very commonly used)
  • 13. Benign fibrocystic disease of the breasts (mastalgia)
  • 14. Prior to hysteroscopic endometrial ablation, to make the endometrium thin

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following medications can be added to improve the clomiphene response in women with PCOS?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Metformin

Explanation:

  • Many women with PCOS also have insulin resistance Insulin resistance leads to compensatory hyperinsulinemia and dyslipidemia. Interventions that reduce circulating insulin levels in women with PCOS may restore normal reproductive endocrine function. Insulin-sensitizing agents like Metformin help in the treatment of PCOS. Metformin administered to women with PCOS, increased the frequency of spontaneous ovulation, menstrual cyclicity, and ovulatory response to Clomiphene citrate

Educational Objective:

  • Metformin administered to women with PCOS, increased the frequency of spontaneous ovulation, menstrual cyclicity, and ovulatory response to Clomiphene citrate

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 28-year-old pregnant woman with a history of hypertension presents to the antenatal clinic for a routine check-up. She is currently 24 weeks pregnant, and her blood pressure is well controlled on a combination of methyldopa and labetalol. She is concerned about the potential risks of these medications to her unborn baby. Which of the following statements about the use of methyldopa and labetalol in pregnancy is correct?
options: Methyldopa and labetalol are contraindicated in pregnancy due to their teratogenic effects.
Methyldopa and labetalol are safe choices for the management of hypertension during pregnancy
Methyldopa is safe, but labetalol should be avoided during pregnancy
Methyldopa and labetalol can cause intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR) and should be used with caution.

explanation.short: B. Methyldopa and labetalol are safe choices for the management of hypertension during pregnancy
explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Methyldopa and labetalol are safe choices for the management of hypertension during pregnancy

Explanation:

  • Let's go through each option:
  • Option A. Methyldopa and labetalol are contraindicated in pregnancy due to their teratogenic effects: This is Incorrect. They are not contraindicated in pregnancy. Option B. Methyldopa and labetalol are safe choices for the management of hypertension during pregnancy: This is Correct . Methyldopa and labetalol are commonly used antihypertensive medications during pregnancy because they have a well-established safety profile for both the mother and the unborn baby. Option C. Methyldopa is safe, but labetalol should be avoided during pregnancy: This statement is Incorrect. Both methyldopa and labetalol are generally considered safe during pregnancy. Option D. Methyldopa and labetalol can cause intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR) and should be used with caution: This statement INCORRECT. The benefits of controlling maternal hypertension often outweigh the potential risks of IUGR when these medications are used.

Educational Objective:

  • DOC in pregnancy : Labetalol (Max oral dose 2400mg/d); can be given IV in emergencies (Max IV dose is 220 mg) Alpha methyl dopa : Greatest safety data available, 48 h for onset of action Nifedipine: CCB, short acting, oral (S/l is c/i) used in emergencies Hydralazine: for emergencies, IV Diuretics : only if pulmonary edema Beta blockers : long term use causes Growth restriction, not preferred ACE inhibitors and ARB – Absolute c/I in pregnancy as they cause fetal renal anomalies
  • Ref: Page 1846, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 34-year-old pregnant woman with a history of preterm labor is admitted to the hospital at 32 weeks gestation with regular uterine contractions. She is diagnosed with threatened preterm labor and tocolysis is initiated to delay delivery for corticosteroid administration for fetal lung maturity which among the following should not be used?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Indomethacin

Explanation:

  • Indomethacin is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that inhibits prostaglandin synthesis and reduces uterine contractions. However, its use is contraindicated after 32 weeks gestation due to the risk of premature closure of the ductus arteriosus in the fetus, leading to potential fetal harm. Therefore, it should not be used for tocolysis in this patient.
  • Let's look at the other options:
  • Option A. Nifedipine : Nifedipine is a calcium channel blocker used for tocolysis in some cases of preterm labor. Option B. Magnesium sulfate : Magnesium sulfate is often used for tocolysis, but it is typically preferred when the primary goal is neuroprotection for the preterm infant rather than delaying delivery for corticosteroid administration. Option C. Terbutaline : Terbutaline is a beta-agonist that can be used for tocolysis, but it is not the first-line choice in this scenario when the main objective is to delay delivery for corticosteroid administration.

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page 2094, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page 2094, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 28-year-old woman is in active labor at 40 weeks gestation. Oxytocin is administered to augment her labor progress. Which of the following is NOT a potential adverse effect associated with the use of oxytocin in labor augmentation?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Cord prolapse

Explanation:

  • Adverse effects of oxytocin:
  • Let's look at each option:
  • Option A. Maternal hypotension: Oxytocin can sometimes cause maternal hypotension and tachycardia Option B. Fetal bradycardia : Fetal bradycardia (abnormally slow heart rate in the fetus) is a potential adverse effect of oxytocin administration. It can occur due to uterine hyperstimulation, which reduces fetal oxygen supply. Option C. Uterine rupture : It is a rare but serious complication associated with oxytocin use, especially when the uterus becomes hyperstimulated. It can lead to a tear or rupture in the uterine wall, which poses significant risks to both the mother and the baby. Option D. Cord prolapse: Cord prolapse occurs when the umbilical cord slips through the cervix and becomes compressed during labor. It is not directly related to the administration of oxytocin but is a potential obstetric emergency that can happen during labor.

Educational Objective:

  • Adverse effects of oxytocin:
  • Ref: Page 1260, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page 1260, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 23y primi presents at 38 weeks with prelabor rupture of membranes. Her Bishop’s score is 6 and she is induced with oxytocin. Which is incorrect regarding oxytocin?
options: Oxytocin is discontinued if the number of contractions is > 5 in a 10-minute period or > 7 in a 15-minute period or with a persistent non-reassuring fetal heart rate pattern.
The uterus contracts within 3 to 5 minutes of beginning an oxytocin infusion, and a plasma steady state is reached in 40 minutes
When oxytocin is stopped, its concentration in plasma falls slowly because the half-life is long
Oxytocin is started at doses of 0.5 – 1.5 mIU/ml and gradually increased to a maximum of 48mIU/ml

explanation.short: C. When oxytocin is stopped, its concentration in plasma falls slowly because the half-life is long
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. When oxytocin is stopped, its concentration in plasma falls slowly because the half-life is long

Explanation:

  • Let's look at each option:
  • Option A: Oxytocin is discontinued if the number of contractions is > 5 in a 10-minute period or > 7 in a 15-minute period or with a persistent non-reassuring fetal heart rate pattern.: This statement is correct. Oxytocin is discontinued if there are excessive contractions or if there is a persistent non-reassuring fetal heart rate pattern to ensure the safety of the mother and the baby. Option B: The uterus contracts within 3 to 5 minutes of beginning an oxytocin infusion, and a plasma steady state is reached in 40 minutes: This statement is correct. Oxytocin typically starts to induce uterine contractions within a few minutes of administration, and it reaches a steady state in plasma relatively quickly. Option C : When oxytocin is stopped, its concentration in plasma falls slowly because the half-life is long: This statement is incorrect. Oxytocin has a very short half-life, typically in the range of 1-6 minutes. Therefore, its concentration in plasma drops rapidly after discontinuation. Option D : Oxytocin is started at doses of 0.5 – 1.5 mIU/ml and gradually increased to a maximum of 48mIU/ml: statement is generally accurate. Oxytocin is typically started at low doses and gradually increased to achieve the desired uterine contractions while avoiding excessive stimulation.

Educational Objective:

  • Oxytocin is used for induction and augmentation of labor The goal of induction or augmentation is to effect uterine activity sufficient to produce cervical change and fetal descent, while avoiding development of a nonreassuring fetal status. In general, oxytocin is discontinued if the number of contractions persists with a frequency of more than five in a 10-minute period or more than seven in a 15-minute period or with a persistent non-reassuring fetal heart rate pattern. The uterus contracts within 3 to 5 minutes of beginning an oxytocin infusion, and a plasma steady state is reached in 40 minutes Oxytocin discontinuation nearly always rapidly lowers contraction frequency. When oxytocin is stopped, its concentration in plasma rapidly falls because the half-life is approximately 3 to 5 minutes. Uterine response to oxytocin increases from 20 to 30 weeks’ gestation and rises rapidly at term. Oxytocin Regimens:
  • Ref: Page no 1263, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1263, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: What is CORRECT regarding this?
options: This is an intravaginal oxytocin release system
This is an intracervical oxytocin release system
This is an intravaginal PGE2 release system
This is an intracervical PGE2 release system

explanation.short: C. This is an intravaginal PGE2 release system
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. This is an intravaginal PGE2 release system

Explanation:

  • This is an intra-vaginal Dinoprostone (PGE2) system It contains 10mg of PGE2 Available by the names Cervidil and Propess Used for cervical ripening/ induction of labor Thin, flat, rectangular polymeric wafer held within a small, white, mesh polyester sac The sac has a long-attached tail to allow easy removal from the vagina. The insert provides slower release of medication—0.3 mg/hC Cervidil is used as a single dose placed transversely in the posterior vaginal fornix. Following insertion, the woman remains recumbent for at least 2 hours. The insert is removed after 12 hours or with labor onset and at least 30 minutes before the administration of oxytocin. Since the active drug is encased in a mesh sac, one benefit is the ability for removal if fetal heart rate abnormalities or tachysystole develop.
  • Thus, option C: This is an intravaginal PGE2 release system is correct.

Educational Objective:

  • This is an intra-vaginal PGE2 release system called Propess or Cervidil Intravaginal prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) release systems such as Cervidil are used in obstetrics to facilitate cervical ripening and induce labour when medically indicated.
  • Ref: Page 1251, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 23y primi presents for induction of labor at 41 weeks. Her cervix is unfavourable with a Bishop score of 4. You proceed with cervical ripening using the agent shown below. This medication belongs to which group of drug?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Prostaglandins

Explanation:

  • The likely drug shown is misoprostol (PGE1) which is available as a tablet Drugs used for induction of labor are Oxytocin: Available as an injection (Given as an IV infusion) Prostaglandins PGE1 (misoprostol) – available as a tablet; given vaginally/ orally or sublingually PGE2 (Dinoprostone) – available as an intra-cervical gel or as a vaginal insert or as an oral tablet
  • Let us look at each option:
  • Option A. Oxytocin : Oxytocin is used to induce and augment labour once the cervix is favourable. It stimulates uterine contractions during labour but is not typically used for cervical ripening. Option B . Beta mimetics: Beta mimetics are a class of drugs that can be used for tocolysis in certain situations but are not typically used for cervical ripening. Option C. Prostaglandins : Prostaglandins are commonly used for cervical ripening and induction of labor in cases where the cervix is unfavorable, as described in the scenario. Option D. Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs (NSAIDs): NSAIDs are not typically used for cervical ripening or labor induction. They are primarily used for pain relief and anti-inflammatory purposes.

Educational Objective:

  • Drugs used for induction of labor are
  • Oxytocin: Available as an injection (Given as an IV infusion) Prostaglandins PGE1 (misoprostol) – available as a tablet; given vaginally/ orally or sublingually PGE2 (Dinoprostone) – available as an intra-cervical gel or as a vaginal insert or as an oral tablet
  • Ref: JB Sharma’s Textbook of Obstetrics 2nd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following is a neonatal consequence of in utero exposure to non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs?
options: Prolonged patency of the ductus arteriosus

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Neonatal pulmonary HTN

Explanation:

  • Aspirin and other NSAIDs nonspecifically inhibit both cyclooxygenase 1 (COX-1), which is an enzyme critical to normal platelet function, and COX2, which mediates inflammatory response mechanisms. NSAIDs are associated with Early spontaneous abortions Small increased risk of cardiac malformations Ductus arteriosus constriction (Esp if used > 32 weeks) Neonatal pulmonary hypertension
  • Let us look at each option:
  • Option A . Thrombocytopenia : Thrombocytopenia (low platelet count) is not typically associated with in utero exposure to nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs).
  • Option B . Gastritis and bleeding: In utero exposure to NSAIDs can lead to fetal complications such as gastroschisis or gastrointestinal bleeding in rare cases. However, these outcomes are less common compared to neonatal pulmonary hypertension.
  • Option C. Neonatal pulmonary hypertension (HTN): Exposure to NSAIDs during pregnancy, particularly in the third trimester, can lead to closure of the fetal ductus arteriosus, which can result in neonatal pulmonary hypertension.
  • Option D. Prolonged patency of the ductus arteriosus : While NSAIDs can affect the ductus arteriosus in utero, their exposure typically leads to its closure rather than prolonged patency.

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page 2910, Williams Gynecology 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page 2910, Williams Gynecology 26th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Amongst the following drugs used in Rheumatoid Arthritis which should NOT be used in pregnancy? Methotrexate Leflunomide Sulfasalazine Hydroxychloroquine Etanercept

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. 1, 2

Explanation:

  • Among the drugs used for treatment of RA:
  • Methotrexate and Leflunomide are completely contra-indicated in pregnancy due to their teratogenic effects (Both are category X) Sulfasalazine and Hydroxychloroquine are safe Biological DMARDs have revolutionized rheumatoid arthritis treatment. These include the tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF-α) inhibitors infliximab, adalimumab, golimumab, certolizumab, and etanercept. Their use in pregnancy is limited, but they appear to be safe for the fetus
  • Thus, option A; 1,2 i.e. methotrexate and Leflunomide should not be used in pregnancy

Educational Objective:

  • Methotrexate and leflunomide should not be used in pregnancy due to the risk of serious birth defects. Other medications like sulfasalazine, hydroxychloroquine, and etanercept may have different considerations and should be discussed on a case-by-case basis with the patient’s healthcare team.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 29-year-old multigravida presents at 15 weeks gestation to establish prenatal care. The patient’s first child had a neural tube defect. The patient did not know that she was pregnant till this week, so she asks you about starting folic acid as she was earlier counselled at the time of her 1 st pregnancy to take a higher dose of folic acid. Which of the following statements will you mention during counselling?
options: Starting folic acid is just as beneficial now and she should start immediately
The neural tube does not close until 21 weeks so she should start folic acid 4mg/ day in the next few days
The neural tube closes by 6 weeks so folic acid needs to be taken before 6 weeks
Having a prior child with a neural tube defect does not increase her risk so she need not worry

explanation.short: C. The neural tube closes by 6 weeks so folic acid needs to be taken before 6 weeks
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. The neural tube closes by 6 weeks so folic acid needs to be taken before 6 weeks

Explanation:

  • Option A: Starting folic acid is just as beneficial now and she should start immediately: Incorrect. While starting folic acid at any point during pregnancy is beneficial for overall fetal development, it is particularly critical to take it before the neural tube closes, which occurs by approximately 6 weeks of gestation. Option B : The next few days: Incorrect. The neural tube closes much earlier, around 6 weeks, so it is essential to start folic acid supplementation before that time. Option C: The neural tube closes by 6 weeks, so folic acid needs to be taken before 6 weeks: This statement is correct. Folic acid supplementation is most effective in preventing neural tube defects when taken before conception and during the early weeks of pregnancy when the neural tube is forming. Option D: Having a prior child with a neural tube defect does not increase her risk, so she need not worry: Incorrect. A history of a previous child with a neural tube defect does increase the risk for future pregnancies, and women in this situation should take appropriate precautions, including starting folic acid supplementation before conception.

Educational Objective:

  • Pre-conceptional Folic Acid should be taken in all women planning to conceive and if not started pre-conceptionally, should be started before 6 weeks of pregnancy (Before the neural tube closes) 0.04 mg/ day to low risk 4 mg/ day to high risk for neural tube defects – NTD. Women at high risk for NTD are H/o previous baby with NTD Pre-gestational Diabetes Mellitus Folic acid deficiency Obesity Epilepsy/ on Antiepileptic Drugs (AED) Hemoglobinopathy
  • Ref: Page 491, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page 491, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 35y G4 presents to the emergency with eclampsia at 35 weeks. After initial management, MgSO4 is administered as per Pritchard regime, and she is induced. Following delivery, for how long should the maintenance dose of MGSO4 continue?
options: For 24 h if the BP is > 160/100 mmHg
Continue only if the patient has imminent symptoms of eclampsia

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. For 24 h

Explanation:

  • Preferred regime is Pritchard’s regime: 4 g of MgSO4 as a 20% solution @ 1g/ min + 10 g of 50% MgSO4 (5 g in each buttock) f/b maintenance dose of 5 g 50% MgSO4 on alternate buttocks every 4 h till 24 h after delivery/ last convulsion; whichever is later. If 20% not available, convert 50% to 20% by adding 1.5 times distilled water (1ml of 50% MgSO4 contains 0.5g MgSO4) i.e. to 8ml of 50% MgSO4 + 12ml of distilled water Check – RR, urine output and DTR before administering MgSO4 (Risk of toxicity) 1st to disappear in Mg toxicity: DTR Other regimes – Zuspan (Continuous IV regime); higher risk of Mg toxicity if Mg levels unchecked Antidote to MgSO4 is Calcium gluconate
  • Thus, option A) For 24 hrs. post-delivery is correct.

  • 1 or more episode of arterial/venous or small vessel thrombosis in any tissue or organ
  • Lab Criteria (Present on 2 or more occasions at least 12 weeks apart)
  • Presence of lupus anticoagulant
  • Medium to high titers of IgG or IgM anticardiolipin Antibodies
  • Anti β2 glycoprotein – IgG or IgM Antibody
  • Ref: Page no 353, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All are risk factors for tubal ectopic pregnancy except?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Previous cesarean

Explanation:

  • Previous cesarean section is NOT a risk factor for tubal ectopic pregnancy
  • Let’s Discuss all the other options:
  • Option A: Endometriosis affects tubal motility due to adhesions and thus increases the risk of ectopic pregnancy Option B: Salpingitis is inflammation of the fallopian tube. A past h/o salpingitis may have led to tubal damage and hence affect tubal motility thereby leading to a higher risk of tubal ectopic Option C: Previous h/o ectopic is also a risk factor for a repeat tubal ectopic as it means there is an underlying pathology

Educational Objective:

  • Risk Factors of ectopic pregnancy
  • h/o Tubal Surgery (e.g., for fertility restoration or sterilization): As per Williams Obstetrics 25th edition (page 371): HIGHEST RISK Prior h/o salpingitis: 6 – 10-fold increased risk h/o previous ectopic: 5-fold risk (10 – 15% chance of repeat ectopic) Peri tubal adhesions (like endometriosis) Salpingitis isthmica nodosa Congenital tubal anomalies Infertility (artificial Reproductive techniques like IVF) Associated with Atypical implantations and heterotopic pregnancies Contraception: Relative numbers increased with intra-uterine devices (Absolute numbers decreased) Intra uterine devices Progesterone only pill
  • Ref: Page 371, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which is correct regarding ectopic pregnancy?
options: The incidence of heterotopic pregnancy is decreasing due to early diagnosis
Atypical sites of ectopics are decreasing due to IVF
The incidence of an ectopic increases by about 50% following a history of ectopic
The relative incidence of ectopic pregnancy is increased with the use of intrauterine devices.

explanation.short: D. The relative incidence of ectopic pregnancy is increased with the use of intrauterine devices.
explanation.detail:
Ans. D.

Explanation:

  • Let’s Discuss all the options:
  • Option A: The incidence of heterotopic pregnancy is decreasing due to early diagnosis: This is incorrect. The incidence of heterotopic pregnancy is increasing due to increasing incidence of Assisted Reproductive Techniques. Option B: Atypical sites of ectopics are decreasing due to IVF: This is also incorrect. Atypical sites of ectopic pregnancy like cervical pregnancy, ovarian pregnancy etc. are increasing due to IVF Option C: The incidence of an ectopic increases by about 50% following a history of ectopic. This is also incorrect as the incidence of an ectopic pregnancy increases by 10-15% following a prior ectopic. Option D: The relative incidence of ectopic pregnancy is increased with the use of intrauterine devices. This is correct. With use of intra-uterine devices, the overall pregnancy rate is reduced but if a woman conceives with an IUD, it is important to note that the ectopic rate is high.

Educational Objective:

  • h/o Tubal Surgery (e.g., for fertility restoration or sterilization): HIGHEST RISK Prior h/o salpingitis: 6 – 10-fold increased risk h/o previous ectopic: 5-fold risk (10 – 15% chance of repeat ectopic) Peri tubal adhesions (like endometriosis) Salpingitis isthmica nodosa Congenital tubal anomalies Infertility (ART) Associated with Atypical implantations and heterotopic pregnancies Heterotopic pregnancies and heterotopic pregnancies are increasing due to this Contraception: Relative numbers increased with intra-uterine devices (Absolute numbers decreased)
  • Ref: Page 371, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page 371, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 24y lady presented to the emergency with generalized pain and spotting PV. Her LMP was 2 months back and she has a history of irregular cycles. On examination, she is pale, her PR is 110/ min, BP is 80/60 mmHg. There is guarding and rigidity on abdominal examination and a vaginal exam reveals cervical motion tenderness. Her urine pregnancy test is weakly positive. What is the next best step?
options: Shift to the O.T. for an emergency laparotomy

explanation.short: B. Shift to the O.T. for an emergency laparotomy
explanation.detail:
Ans. B.

Explanation:

  • This is most likely a ruptured ectopic pregnancy. A RUPTURED ectopic pregnancy is a clinical diagnosis. Whenever there is a ruptured ectopic pregnancy with the patient in shock (hypotension, tachycardia), no time is to be wasted and the patient should be shifted to the OT for an emergency laparoscopy/ laparotomy and salpingectomy.
  • Option A: Dilatation and evacuation: This is not done in an ectopic pregnancy Option C: Doing an ultrasound (no matter how urgently in a patient with shock) will cause a delay and worsen patient prognosis Option D: Beta hCG (even urgent will take a few hours) is not indicated in a ruptured ectopic. It only has a role in an unruptured ectopic

Educational Objective:

  • D/d of bleeding in the 1st trimester
  • Implantation bleeding (Placental Sign) Abortion (threatened, inevitable, incomplete, complete, missed) Ectopic Pregnancy Molar pregnancy
  • Classic triad of symptoms I.e., pain, bleeding and delayed menstruation is seen in an ectopic pregnancy. In this scenario there is pain and bleeding with a positive pregnancy test and on examination there is pallor, shock and intraperitoneal bleed which points to a ruptured ectopic pregnancy. Next best management option is immediate surgical management and salpingectomy
  • Ref: Page 371, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page 371, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: The classic triad of clinical features in an ectopic pregnancy is:

explanation.detail:
Ans.

Explanation:

  • Clinical Features of an ectopic pregnancy vary but is typically characterized by the classic triad of Delayed menstruation Abdominal pain Vaginal Bleeding

Educational Objective:

  • Clinical Features of an ectopic pregnancy vary but is typically characterized by the classic triad of Delayed menstruation Abdominal pain Vaginal Bleeding Other symptoms may include: Passage of a decidual cast Severe hemoperitoneum: diaphragmatic irritation – shoulder pain Pallor, Shock – uterine rupture Abdominal tenderness Bimanual exam: cervical motion tenderness Tender boggy mass in the post fornix (collected blood) Cullen’s sign: Periumbilical bluish discoloration due to intraperitoneal bleeding

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which is incorrect regarding a pseudo sac?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Eccentrically located

Explanation:

  • A pseudo sac is seen in ectopic pregnancies. It is intrauterine fluid collection which gives the appearance of an intra-uterine gestational sac. A pseudo sac is CENTRAL, irregular and no yolk sac or fetal pole is seen. The intra-decidual and double ring sign are absent

Educational Objective:

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All are ultrasound signs of an unruptured ectopic pregnancy except?

explanation.detail:
Ans.

Explanation:

  • Let’s Discuss all the Options:
  • Option A: Bagel sign: This is a ‘bagel’ or doughnut shaped structure seen in the adnexa on ultrasound typical of an unruptured ectopic pregnancy Option B: Blob sign: This is also a sign seen in ectopic pregnancy wherein a Heteroechoic structure is seen in the adnexal region Option C: Lemon sign: This is a sign seen in spina bifida which shows pinching of the calvarium Option D: Tubal ring: This is a ring like structure on ultrasound seen in ectopic pregnancy

Educational Objective:

  • In Suspected unruptured ectopic pregnancy: Trans-vaginal Sonography (TVS) will reveal
  • An absent intra-uterine pregnancy The presence of a pseudo-sac in the uterine cavity The presence of an adnexal mass separate from the ovary Hyperechoic ring surrounding an anechoic sac- seen in 20% (bagel sign/tubal ring sign) Inhomogeneous mass – 60% (blob sign) Obvious gestational sac with a fetal pole – 13% On color doppler: “Ring of fire” seen. (D/d – Corpus luteal cyst)
  • Ref: page no 375, William Obstetrics 25th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 30-year lady presented to the gyn OPD with complaints of mild abdominal pain and spotting. She gives a history of delayed menses by about 7 days. Her UPT is faintly positive. An ultrasound with color doppler reveals the following image. What is the diagnosis and what is this sign known as?
options: Corpus luteal cyst, Circle of fire sign
Corpus luteal cyst, Ring of fire sign.
Unruptured ectopic pregnancy, Circle of fire sign
Unruptured ectopic pregnancy, Ring of fire sign

explanation.short: D. Unruptured ectopic pregnancy, Ring of fire sign
explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Unruptured ectopic pregnancy, Ring of fire sign

Explanation:

  • The patient’s history is suggestive of an ectopic pregnancy (classic triad of pain, bleeding and delayed menstruation is there) The image shows the classical “ring of fire” sign which is peri-trophoblastic vascularity. Other options: Corpus luteal cyst, Circle of fire sign: A corpus luteal cyst can present with vascularity around it on Doppler and appear like the ing of fire, not circle of fire Corpus luteal cyst, Ring of fire sign: This could be a close differential to the question given but, in the question, the classic triad of ectopic is also mentioned which will not be seen in a corpus luteal cyst Unruptured ectopic pregnancy, Circle of fire sign: The name of the sign is “Ring of fire”

Educational Objective:

  • The above image showing vascularity around a sac like structure is called the ring of fire sign and is seen in unruptured ectopic pregnancy
  • Ref: page no 376, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: page no 376, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: What is the discriminatory zone for βhCG?
options: The level above which an ectopic gestational sac should be visualized
The level below which an ectopic gestational sac should be visualized
The level above which an intra-uterine gestational sac should be visualized
The level below which an intra-uterine gestational sac should be visualized

explanation.short: C. The level above which an intra-uterine gestational sac should be visualized
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. The level above which an intra-uterine gestational sac should be visualized

Explanation:

  • Discriminatory Zone: This is the level of serum βhCG above which an intra uterine gestational sac should be seen, 1500 – 2000 IU/L for TVS 5000 – 6000 IU/L for TAS There is another concept of hCG called doubling time . This means that βhCG levels should double every 48h in early pregnancy in a normal intra-uterine pregnancy. Actually, doubling is a misnomer. The rise in βhCG should be >60% at least in 48h.

Educational Objective:

  • Discriminatory Zone: This is the level of serum βhCG above which an intra uterine gestational sac should be seen, 1500 – 2000 IU/L for TVS 5000 – 6000 IU/L for TAS
  • Ref: page no 373, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: page no 373, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: The value of βhCG above which an intra-uterine gestational sac should be seen is:

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. 1500 IU/L

  • 1500 – 2000 IU/L for TVS 5000 – 6000 IU/L for TAS

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 33-year woman comes to the OPD with amenorrhea of 6 weeks. Her urine pregnancy test is positive. A transvaginal ultrasound is done which shows normal uterus and adnexa. What is the diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Pregnancy of undetermined location

Explanation:

  • This is a PREGNANCY OF UNKNOWN LOCATION (PUL): No sign of an intra or extra uterine pregnancy on ultrasound despite a positive pregnancy test
  • Let’s discuss all the options:
  • Option A: Complete abortion: This is unlikely a complete abortion as there is no history of vaginal bleeding or passage of a tissue. Option B: Ectopic pregnancy: This could be a possibility but there is nothing in the scenario which is suggestive of an ectopic pregnancy Option C: Pregnancy of unknown location: This is what this scenario is. A pregnancy of unknown or undetermined location (PUL) means the urine or serum bhCG is positive but the ultrasound does not show an intra uterine or extra uterine gestational sac. Option D: Pseudocyesis: This is phantom pregnancy. In this the urine pregnancy test will be negative.

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: page no 373, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: page no 373, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 20y G2A1 presents at 6 weeks pregnancy with vaginal bleeding. On TVS, no gestational sac is visualized. Her serum βhCG is 2000 IU/L. Which is NOT a possible diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Ans.

Explanation:

  • Discriminatory Zone: This is the level of serum βhCG above which an intra uterine gestational sac should be seen, 1500 – 2000 IU/L for TVS 5000 – 6000 IU/L for TAS So, if serum βhCG levels are more than the discriminatory zone and still an intrauterine gestational sac is not seen on ultrasound, it is called a pregnancy of undetermined location & the following should be suspected: Ectopic pregnancy Failing intrauterine pregnancy Recent complete abortion Early multifetal pregnancy
  • In molar pregnancy, the βhCG levels are very high (usually > 100,000)

Educational Objective:

  • If serum βhCG levels are more than the discriminatory zone and still an intrauterine gestational sac is not seen on ultrasound, it is called a pregnancy of undetermined location & the following should be suspected: Ectopic pregnancy Failing intrauterine pregnancy Recent complete abortion Early multifetal pregnancy
  • Ref: page no 374, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: page no 374, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All are indications for expectant management of an unruptured ectopic pregnancy except?
options: Gestational sac size < 3.5 cm with no cardiac activity

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Mild abdominal pain

Explanation:

  • Expectant Management: This means no active intervention; just following up the patient with βhCG levels. Mild pain is NOT an indication for expectant management. There should be NO pain.
  • Only for clinically stable asymptomatic women with an ultrasound diagnosis of ectopic pregnancy with the following features
  • Clinically stable with No pain AND Serum βhCG < 1000 IU/L AND Tubal ectopic < 35mm with no visible heartbeat AND Able to return for follow up
  • Repeat βhCG levels on days 2, 4 and 7 after the original test If βhCG levels drop ≥ 15% from the previous values, then repeat weekly till a negative result If values fall < 15%, stay the same or rise from the previous value, review the condition and consider shifting to medical/ surgical management

Educational Objective:

  • In Expectant Management of ectopic pregnancy (i.e. no active intervention; just following up the patient with βhCG levels); the following criteria should be fulfilled:
  • Only for clinically stable asymptomatic women with an ultrasound diagnosis of ectopic pregnancy with the following features
  • Clinically stable with No pain AND Serum βhCG < 1000 IU/L AND Tubal ectopic < 35mm with no visible heartbeat AND Able to return for follow up
  • Ref: page no 378, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition, https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng126
explanation.ref: page no 378, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition, https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng126

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 34-year woman presents to the gyn OPD with an unruptured tubal ectopic pregnancy. She has no symptoms and is hemodynamically stable. Her βhCG is 600 IU/L. The ultrasound shows a gestational sac measuring 20 mm in the right adnexa with no yolk sac or embryo seen. Which of these is the most appropriate management?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Expectant management

Explanation:

  • This patient fulfills all the criteria for Expectant management, i.e. no active intervention needs to be done. Just following up with weekly beta hCG levels (it should decrease every week)
  • Let’s discuss the other options
  • Option B: Single dose inj Methotrexate: This is medical management and is indicated in an unruptured ectopic pregnancy with:
  • Patient hemodynamically stable No significant symptoms Gestational sac size < 3.5 cm No visible cardiac activity Serum βhCG < 5000 IU/L No contraindication to Methotrexate (Anemia, liver disease, bleeding disorder, active pulmonary disease etc.)
  • Option C and D: Laparoscopic salpingostomy/ salpingectomy: Surgical management in an unruptured ectopic is indicated in a woman with an unruptured ectopic pregnancy with
  • Significant pain OR Gestational sac size > 3.5 cm OR Visible cardiac activity on ultrasound OR Hemodynamically unstable patient OR Beta hCG > 5000 IU/L
  • The choice between salpingotomy or salpingectomy is made based on the following: Salpingectomy (Removing the whole tube) is preferred In women whose family is complete, if the tube is grossly distorted anatomically, if the ectopic has happened at the sire of a previous ectopic or at the site of a previous tubal ligation.

Educational Objective:

  • Only for clinically stable asymptomatic women with an ultrasound diagnosis of ectopic pregnancy with the following features
  • Clinically stable with No pain AND Serum βhCG < 1000 IU/L AND Tubal ectopic < 35mm with no visible heartbeat AND Able to return for follow up
  • Repeat βhCG levels on days 2, 4 and 7 after the original test If βhCG levels drop ≥ 15% from the previous values, then repeat weekly till a negative result If values fall < 15%, stay the same or rise from the previous value, review the condition and consider shifting to medical/ surgical management
  • Ref: page no 378, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition, https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng126

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All are indications for medical management of an unruptured tubal ectopic pregnancy except?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Visible cardiac activity

Explanation:

  • Medical Management of an unruptured ectopic pregnancy (Inj Methotrexate) is given in the following:
  • Indications: An unruptured ectopic pregnancy with:
  • Patient hemodynamically stable No significant symptoms Gestational sac size < 3.5 cm No visible cardiac activity Serum βhCG < 5000 IU/L No contraindication to Methotrexate (Anemia, liver disease, bleeding disorder, active pulmonary disease etc.)
  • Hence Option C is the correct option as if visible cardiac activity is present, it will not be given.

Educational Objective:

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: The drug of choice for medical management of an unruptured ectopic pregnancy is?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Methotrexate

Explanation:

  • The drug of choice for treatment of an unruptured ectopic pregnancy is Methotrexate. MOA: Inhibits dihydrofolate reductase 2 protocols: Single dose and Multidose Usually, the single dose regime is sufficient, very rarely another dose is required.
  • Option B: Mifepristone: This is also known as RU486 and is a selective progesterone receptor modulator with strong anti-progestogenic action. It is commonly used in medical methods of termination of pregnancy along with misoprostol in 1st and 2nd trimester. Option C: Misoprostol: This is PGE1 and is a uterotonic. It induces uterine contractions and is therefore used in medical methods of termination of pregnancy, induction of labor and prophylaxis and management of atonic PPH. Option D: KCl: Ultrasound guided Intracardiac injection of Kcl is used in selective fetal termination in multifetal pregnancy reduction.

Educational Objective:

  • The drug of choice for treatment of an unruptured ectopic pregnancy is Methotrexate. MOA: Inhibits dihydrofolate reductase 2 protocols: Single dose and Multidose Usually, the single dose regime is sufficient, very rarely another dose is required. 2 regimes:

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Identify this procedure:

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Culdocentesis

Explanation:

  • The image shows the procedure of culdocentesis. In the absence of TVS and if the diagnosis of a ruptured ectopic is still in doubt: Culdocentesis can be done:
  • Simple technique Cervix pulled outward and upward (holding a tenaculum/ vulsullum) on the posterior cervix. 18-gauge needle is inserted in the posterior fornix Aspiration of non-clotting blood signifies hemoperitoneum A negative Culdocentesis does not rule out a ruptured ectopic pregnancy
  • Option A. Endometrial biopsy: In this procedure, a sample is taken from inside the uterine cavity (endometrial) using an endometrial aspirator Option B. Cervical biopsy: In this a biopsy is taken from the cervix/ cervical growth using a punch biopsy forceps Option D. Pap smear: In this procedure, using an instrument called an Ayre’s spatula, a smear is taken from the ectocervix.

Educational Objective:

  • The procedure shown in this image is a culdocentesis. It is aspiration of fluid from the Pouch of Douglas and if blood is aspirated, it means there is hemoperitoneum

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 24y old lady with a history of a tubal sterilization is diagnosed to have an unruptured ectopic pregnancy of size 4 cm. Serum βhCG is 6000 IU/L. The preferred management option is:

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Laparoscopic salpingectomy

Explanation:

  • This woman is not a candidate for medical management as the size is > 3.5 cm and βhCG is > 3000 IU/L. She requires surgical management: In an unruptured ectopic: Laparoscopy is preferred and, in this woman, a salpingectomy (removing the tube) is preferred to a salpingostomy (conserving the tube) as the patient has a h/o tubal ligation.
  • Option B: Laparoscopic salpingostomy: This is not preferred in this patient as if the ectopic pregnancy has happened at the site of a previous tubal ligation, it is better to remove the tube as it can keep recurring in this fallopian tube. Option C: Inj Methotrexate single dose: This is also not preferred as in this scenario the beta hCG is > 5000 and the sac size is > 3.5 cm so she is not a candidate for medical management Option D: Inj methotrexate multiple doses: This is also not preferred as in this scenario the beta hCG is > 5000 and the sac size is > 3.5 cm so she is not a candidate for medical management whether single or multi dose.

Educational Objective:

  • Criteria for surgical management (Laparoscopic salpingectomy or salpingotomy) in a patient with unruptured ectopic is:
  • Significant pain OR Gestational sac size > 3.5 cm OR Visible cardiac activity on ultrasound OR Hemodynamically unstable patient OR Beta hCG > 5000 IU/L
  • How to decide whether to do a salpingostomy or a salpingectomy?
  • SALPINGECTOMY: Preferred if: Extensive tubal involvement/ damage Recurrent ectopic pregnancy in the same tube Completed family Ectopic at the site of a previous tubal sterilization SALPINGOSTOMY: (Linear incision on the antimesenteric border with removal of products of conception) Consider if a woman has other infertility factors also OR has a contralateral damaged/removed tube and wants to conceive in the future
  • Ref: page no 379, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: page no 379, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

question: Medical management of an unruptured ectopic pregnancy is recommended when the serum βhCG is:

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. < 5000 IU/L

Explanation:

  • Indications for medical management of unruptured ectopic pregnancy:
  • Patient hemodynamically stable No significant symptoms Gestational sac size < 3.5 cm No visible cardiac activity Serum βhCG < 5000 IU/L No contraindication to Methotrexate (Anemia, liver disease, bleeding disorder, active pulmonary disease etc.)

question: The preferred treatment in heterotopic pregnancy with a viable intra-uterine pregnancy with an unruptured ectopic pregnancy is?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Surgical

Explanation:

  • Incidence of heterotopic pregnancy: increasing due to ART Treatment of choice: Surgical i.e. laparoscopic salpingectomy/ salpingostomy Medical management with methotrexate is contra-indicated as it will kill the intrauterine pregnancy also
  • Option A: Expectant management is not preferred in a heterotopic pregnancy Option B: Medical management: Medical management with methotrexate is contra-indicated as it will kill the intrauterine pregnancy also Option D: Medical + Surgical: There is no role of both medical and surgical management in a heterotopic pregnancy

Educational Objective:

  • A heterotopic pregnancy is an intra uterine pregnancy with an ectopic pregnancy Incidence of heterotopic pregnancy: increasing due to ART Treatment of choice: Surgical i.e. laparoscopic salpingectomy/ salpingostomy Medical management with methotrexate is contra-indicated as it will kill the intrauterine pregnancy also

  • In an NST, a continuous electronic monitoring of the fetal heart rate along with fetal movements is recorded During fetal movements, the fetal heart rate increases (Acceleration) A reactive NST: Baseline heart rate of 110-160 bpm 2 or more accelerations of > 15 bpm in 20 mins Good variability No decelerations

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 22-year G2P1 with a history of a previous term still birth at term is now 37 weeks. She complains of decreased fetal movements of 2 days. A Biophysical profile is ordered. All are components of the Biophysical profile except?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Umbilical Artery Doppler

Educational Objective:

  • The five components of the biophysical profile are as follows: Nonstress test Fetal breathing movements (one or more episodes of rhythmic fetal breathing movements of 30 seconds or more within 30 minutes) Fetal movement (three or more discrete body or limb movements within 30 minutes) Fetal tone (one or more episodes of extension of a fetal extremity with return to flexion, or opening or closing of a hand; and Determination of the amniotic fluid volume (a single vertical pocket of amniotic fluid exceeding 2 cm is considered evidence of adequate amniotic fluid).
  • Ref: William’s textbook of Obstetrics 25th edition pg 338
