subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: In the biophysical profile, one of the components is fetal movement. This is defined as:
options: One or more discrete body or limb movements within 30 minutes
Two or more discrete body or limb movements within 30 minutes
Three or more discrete body or limb movements within 30 minutes
Four or more discrete body or limb movements within 30 minutes

explanation.short: C. Three or more discrete body or limb movements within 30 minutes
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Three or more discrete body or limb movements within 30 minutes

Educational Objective:

  • The five components of the biophysical profile are as follows:
  • Nonstress test Fetal breathing movements (one or more episodes of rhythmic fetal breathing movements of 30 seconds or more within 30 minutes) Fetal movement (three or more discrete body or limb movements within 30 minutes) Fetal tone (one or more episodes of extension of a fetal extremity with return to flexion, or opening or closing of a hand; and Determination of the amniotic fluid volume (a single vertical pocket of amniotic fluid exceeding 2 cm is considered evidence of adequate amniotic fluid). Each of the components is given a score of 2 (normal or present as defined previously) or 0 (abnormal, absent, or insufficient). This score is called “Manning Score”. A score of 8 or 10: Normal A score of 6: Equivocal A score of ≤ 4: Abnormal The presence of oligohydramnios should warrant further evaluation regardless of the composite score
  • Ref: William’s textbook of Obstetrics 25th edition pg 338
explanation.ref: William’s textbook of Obstetrics 25 th edition pg 338

Each of the components is given a score of 2 (normal or present as defined previously) or 0 (abnormal, absent, or insufficient). This score is called “Manning Score”. A score of 8 or 10: Normal A score of 6: Equivocal A score of ≤ 4: Abnormal The presence of oligohydramnios should warrant further evaluation regardless of the composite score

If the NST is non reassuring, it should be continued for a further 20 minutes or a Vibro-acoustic stimulation is done to reduce the fetal sleep state from quiet (non-REM) to active (REM) sleep.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 24 y primigravida is in active labor. On the CTG, the following CTG is observed. What is the likely cervical dilatation?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. 10 cm

Educational Objective:

  • Types of decelerations on a Cardiotocograph (CTG)
  • The nadir of the deceleration corresponds to the peak of contraction Due to head compression Common in 2nd stage of labor (full dilatation of the cervix)
  • The nadir (dip) of the deceleration occurs after the peak of contraction Seen in uteroplacental insufficiency (fetal distress/ hypoxia) It is an ominous sign
  • There is no fixed relationship between decelerations and the uterine contractions Seen due to cord compression (oligohydramnios) Significant if
  • Persistent FHR < 70 bpm > 60 sec
  • Ref: Page no 468, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 34y G2 is in labor. Due to persistent late decelerations, a fetal scalp blood sample is taken. Which is incorrect regarding fetal scalp blood sampling?
options: A pH < 7.20 indicates fetal hypoxia and necessitates urgent delivery
A normal fetal lactate level is > 4.8 mmol/L
It can be done only when the woman is in active labor with ruptured membranes
It is contra-indicated in < 34 weeks period of gestation

explanation.short: B. A normal fetal lactate level is > 4.8 mmol/L
explanation.detail:
Ans. B. A normal fetal lactate level is > 4.8 mmol/L

Educational Objective:

  • Fetal Scalp Blood Sampling
  • Indicated in abnormal CTG to confirm fetal hypoxia Values < 7.20: Fetal acidosis and necessitates urgent delivery pH between 7.21 and 7.25: borderline; Repeat within 30 minutes pH > 7.25: Reassuring Procedure Mother in left lateral position An illuminated plastic cone inserted through the dilated cervix (at least 4 – 5 cm dilatation required) An incision of 2 mm made with a lancet 35μl blood is adequate for estimation of pH Contra-Indications When spontaneous delivery is imminent Preterm (< 34 weeks) Fetal coagulation defects Maternal infection (HIV, Hepatitis, Herpes simplex) Fetal lactate measurements require lesser amount of blood (7 μl). Normal fetal lactate: ≤ 4.1 mmol/l Borderline: 4.2 – 4.8 mmol/l Abnormal: > 4.9 mmol/l
  • Ref: Page no 470, Williams 25th edition, Fetal monitoring in labor (NICE GUIDELINE 2022)
explanation.ref: Page no 470, Williams 25 th edition, Fetal monitoring in labor (NICE GUIDELINE 2022)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 22y primi in labor at 38 weeks has ruptured her membranes spontaneously at 5 cm dilatation. On the CTG, the below image is seen. The most likely cause is?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Cord prolapse

Explanation:

  • Defined as an isolated deceleration ≥ 15 bpm that lasts ≥ 2 minutes but < 10 mins from onset to return to baseline Causes Epidural or spinal anesthesia Placental abruption Cord prolapse Umbilical cord knots Eclampsia Epilepsy Application of a fetal scalp electrode Maternal Valsalva manuever In the scenario given, the deceleration occurs after rupture of membranes. 2 common causes of prolonged deceleration following rupture of membranes are: cord prolapse and abruptio placentae.
  • Option A. Oligohydramnios: This will lead to variable decelerations Option B. Meconium-stained amniotic fluid: In fetal hypoxia this would lead to late decelerations Option C. Head compression: This will result in early decelerations

Educational Objective:

  • Defined as an isolated deceleration ≥ 15 bpm that lasts ≥ 2 minutes but < 10 mins from onset to return to baseline Causes Epidural or spinal anesthesia Placental abruption Cord prolapse Umbilical cord knots Eclampsia Epilepsy Application of a fetal scalp electrode Maternal Valsalva manuever

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following statements regarding squamocolumnar junction is false?
options: The squamocolumnar junction everts outside with age under the influence of estrogen.
Endocervix is lined with squamous epithelium and ectocervix is lined with columnar epithelium.
Metaplastic cells in this transformation zone are prone to develop Cervical cancer.

explanation.short: B. Endocervix is lined with squamous epithelium and ectocervix is lined with columnar epithelium.
explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Endocervix is lined with squamous epithelium and ectocervix is lined with columnar epithelium.

Explanation:

  • Squamocolumnar junction is the transition zone between the endocervix and ectocervix.
  • Endocervix is lined with columnar epithelium which gets abruptly transformed into Ectocervix which is lined with squamous epithelium. This is known as Squamous metaplasia.
  • With age, under the influence of estrogen, the endocervix everts out and the transformation zone keeps coming outwards the internal os. This zone is prone to HPV infection resulting in development of carcinoma cervix.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Human Papilloma Virus (HPV) is the causative agent in majority of cervical cancers. Which of the following is TRUE in relation to HPV testing as per WHO guidelines?
options: HPV testing should be used as a method of cancer cervix screening after 25 years of age
HPV should only be used in adjunct with either Pap’s smear or liquid based cytology
An interval of 5-10 years is recommended when using HPV as primary screening
Women with previous 2 HPV screen negative can stop screening after the age of 60 years

explanation.short: C. An interval of 5-10 years is recommended when using HPV as primary screening
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • WHO recommends starting regular cervical cancer screening at the age of 30 years among the general population of women. WHO recommends using HPV DNA detection as the primary screening test rather than VIA or cytology in screening and treatment approaches among both the general population of women and women living with HIV WHO suggests a regular screening interval of every 5 to 10 years when using HPV DNA detection as the primary screening test among the general population of women After the age of 50 years , WHO suggests screening is stopped after two consecutive negative screening results consistent with the recommended regular screening intervals among both the general population of women and women living with HIV
  • Option A: HPV testing should be used as a method of cancer cervix screening after 25 years of age: This is incorrect as screening with HPV should start at age 30 Option B: HPV should only be used in adjunct with either Pap’s smear or liquid based cytology: This is also incorrect. As per WHO, HPV alone is the preferred screening test Option D: Women with previous 2 HPV screen negative can stop screening after the age of 60 years: This is also incorrect. After the age of 50 years , WHO suggests screening is stopped after two consecutive negative screening results consistent with the recommended regular screening intervals among both the general population of women and women living with HIV

Educational Objective:

  • WHO recommends starting regular cervical cancer screening at the age of 30 years among the general population of women. WHO recommends using HPV DNA detection as the primary screening test rather than VIA or cytology in screening and treatment approaches among both the general population of women and women living with HIV WHO suggests a regular screening interval of every 5 to 10 years when using HPV DNA detection as the primary screening test among the general population of women After the age of 50 years , WHO suggests screening is stopped after two consecutive negative screening results consistent with the recommended regular screening intervals among both the general population of women and women living with HIV
  • Ref: WHO guideline for screening and treatment of cervical pre-cancer lesions for cervical cancer prevention, second edition, 2021
explanation.ref: WHO guideline for screening and treatment of cervical pre-cancer lesions for cervical cancer prevention, second edition, 2021

Educational Objective:

  • Endometrial hyperplasia is a condition where there is endometrial thickening. It can be simple pr complex and with or without atypia. Simple hyperplasia is characterized by dilated or cystic glands with round to slightly irregular shapes, an increased glandular-to-stromal ratio without glandular crowding Complex hyperplasia has architecturally complex (budding and infolding) crowded glands, with less intervering stroma Atypical hyperplasia refers to cytologic atypia and is a high risk for endometrial carcinoma

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following is FALSE regarding BRCA related hereditary ovarian cancer?
options: Both BRCA1 and BRCA2 are associated with increased risk of ovarian cancer
Hereditary cancer tends to occur in women 10 years earlier than the non-hereditary ovarian cancers
Most BRCA related ovarian cancers are high grade mucinous cancers
Prophylactic salpingo oophorectomy reduces risk of BRCA related gynecological cancer by 95%

explanation.short: C. Most BRCA related ovarian cancers are high grade mucinous cancers
explanation.detail:
Educational Objective:

  • The BRCA genes increase the risk of serous epithelial ovarian cancers and breast cancers BRCA1 gene is located on chromosome 17q21. Patients with a proven mutation have a dramatically elevated risk of developing ovarian cancer (About 40%). BRCA2 is located on chromosome 13q12 and in general is less likely to lead to ovarian cancer (12 to 20%). The estimated lifetime risk of breast cancer with a BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation is 65 - 74 percent The only proven way to directly prevent ovarian cancer is surgical removal. In BRCA1 or BRCA2 carriers, prophylactic bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (BSO) may be performed either upon completion of childbearing or by age 40 years
  • Ref: Berek and Novak 16 edition, pg 2566, Page 738, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Berek and Novak 16 edition, pg 2566, Page 738, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 15-year-old girl presents with precocious puberty and solid ovarian mass suspected to be granulosa cell tumor. Which of the following is false regarding these tumors:
options: Level of Inhibin B is raised
Precocious puberty is due to associated adrenal tumors

explanation.short: D. Precocious puberty is due to associated adrenal tumors
explanation.detail:
Educational Objective:

  • Granulosa Cell Tumor of the Ovary:
  • Granulosa cell tumors: 2 percent of all ovarian neoplasms. Originates from the ‘rests’ of primitive granulosa cells unused in folliculogenesis. It is the commonest ovarian stromal tumor. It is bilateral in 2% The size varies, so also the consistency — may be solid or cystic. Cut section: yellow or orange due to its lipid content. Microscopic appearance: Coffee bean” nuclei. S Call-Exner bodies: Pathognomonic of granulosa cell tumor. The tumor cells secrete inhibin, estrogen and AMH (tumor markers) As the tumor produces estrogen, there may be associated endometrial hyperplasia (50%) and unopposed estrogenic stimulation leads to development of endometrial carcinoma in about 5–10 percent of cases. Clinical features: It occurs in all ages, 10 percent prior to puberty, 40 percent during childbearing period and 50 percent in postmenopausal women. Prior to puberty: Precocious puberty Childbearing period: Abnormal uterine bleeding Postmenopausal: Bleeding Treatment: Laparotomy and surgical staging followed by chemotherapy is done. Unilateral salpingo-oophorectomy is the optimum treatment for children or women in the reproductive age. BEP (Bleomycin, Etoposide and Cisplatin) is the chemotherapeutic regimens. It is a highly chemosensitive tumor. Overall prognosis is good. Life time follow up is essential as recurrence can occur as late as 30 years
  • Ref: Berek & novak, 16 ed, page 2646
explanation.ref: Berek & novak, 16 ed, page 2646

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 12-year-old girl presents with adnexal mass and undergoes unilateral salpingooophorectomy. Histopathology shows large clear cells with abundant cytoplasm, large nuclei with fibrous septae containing lymphocytic infiltrate as shown. Which of the following statements is TRUE?
options: She has clear cell epithelial ovarian tumor
LDH is likely to be increased
She will require completion surgery with total abdominal hysterectomy and salpingooophorectomy

explanation.short: B. LDH is likely to be increased
explanation.detail:
Educational Objective:

  • Dysgerminoma is the commonest (30–40%) malignant germ cell tumor. It arises from undifferentiated form of germ cells. In 5%: Dysgenetic gonads so karyotype may be required The counterpart of dysgerminoma in male is seminoma. Majority (75%) of the tumors occur before the age of 30 years. Tumor marker is LDH Pathology: The shape is usually round or oval and is usually 5–15 cm in diameter; It is firm rubbery. It may be bilateral (10%). Cut section shows pink or yellow color. Microscopic appearance: uniform large round cells, arranged in cords or clumps with abundant clear cytoplasm. Nuclei are large, irregular and hyperchromatic with varying degree of mitosis. There is intense infiltration of lymphocytes and plasma cells in the fibrous septum. Malignant in > 50% Clinical features: not specific for the tumor. Treatment: Majority (75%) of dysgerminomas are confined to one ovary and are stage I at the time of diagnosis In a young patient where preservation of fertility is desired, laparotomy for surgical staging and unilateral salpingo-oophorectomy is done. If there is any suspicion of involvement to the other ovary, bisection of the contralateral ovary and excisional biopsy should be done. The tumor is sensitive to both chemotherapy and radiotherapy. BEP (Bleomycin, Etoposide and Cisplatin), VBP (Vinbla-stin, Bleomycin and Cisplatin), VAC (Vincristine, Actinomycin and Cyclophosphamide) are the commonly used drugs for the germ cell tumors.
  • Ref: Berek & novak, 16 ed, page 2624-33.
explanation.ref: Berek & novak, 16 ed, page 2624-33.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A woman with molar pregnancy is undergoing suction evacuation. Suddenly she develops breathing difficulty, tachypnoea and fall in saturation. The most likely cause is:

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Trophoblastic embolization

Educational Objective:

  • Respiratory distress sometimes occurs in patients with a complete mole. It is usually diagnosed in patients with excessive uterine size and markedly elevated T hese patients may have chest pain, dyspnea, tachypnea, and tachycardia and may experience severe respiratory distress during and after molar evacuation. Auscultation of the chest usually reveals diffuse rales, and chest radiographic evaluation may show bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Respiratory distress usually resolves within 72 hours with cardiopulmonary support. In some circumstances, patients may require mechanical ventilation
  • Ref: Berek and Novak, 16th edition. 2767
explanation.ref: Berek and Novak, 16 th edition. 2767

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A woman underwent suction evacuation for complete mole. She was on weekly hCG follow up which has now returned to normal. What will be your advise to her for further follow up:
options: Till One more negative value 1 month later

explanation.short: C. Monthly follow up for 6 months
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Monthly follow up for 6 months

Educational Objective:

  • Post Evacuation Surveillance of molar pregnancy Risk of progression to GTN is higher following complete mole (15%) than partial mole (5%) Surveillance is done by serial measurement of β hCG to detect persistent/ renewed trophoblastic proliferation. The initial baseline value is taken 2 days post evacuation Then weekly beta hCGS are done till negative (This ususlaly takes 7 weeks for partial mole; 9 weeks for complete mole) Once beta hCG is negative on weekly measurements, it is measured monthly for 6 months
  • Ref: FIGO cancer report, 2021
explanation.ref: FIGO cancer report, 2021

Educational Objective:

  • Post Evacuation Surveillance of molar pregnancy Risk of progression to GTN is higher following complete mole (15%) than partial mole (5%) Surveillance is done by serial measurement of β hCG to detect persistent/ renewed trophoblastic proliferation. The initial baseline value is taken 2 days post evacuation Then weekly beta hCGS are done till negative (This ususlaly takes 7 weeks for partial mole; 9 weeks for complete mole) Once beta hCG is negative on weekly measurements, it is measured monthly for 6 months

Educational Objective:

  • The WHO prognostic scoring system in GTN determines the type of chemotherapy If the score is ≤ 6, the patient requires single agent (Methotrexate) chemotherpay. This is given as 4 doses (1mg/Kg) alternating with 4 doses of folinic acid (0.1mg/kg) every 2-3 weeks. If the score > 7 the patient requires multiagent chemotherapy (EMACO). This includes etoposide, methotrexate, actinomycin D, cyclophosphamide, vincristine

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 72y old woman who preseted with a lesion on the vulva underwent a biopsy. This was diagnosed as squamois cell carcinoma which is the most common pathology in vulvar cancers. Which of the following is the second most common vulvar malignancy and accounts for up to 10 percent of all vulvar cancers?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Of vulvar tumors, approximately 90 percent are squamous cell carcinoma. Malignant melanoma is the second most common, but rare histologic subtypes may also be encountered.

Educational Objective:

  • The etiology of vulval cancer is unclear but certain high risk factors are known to be associated Postmenopausal women; median age about 60 years White race Obesity, Hypertension and Diabetes are known associations More common in nulliparous women Lichen sclerosis HPV (esp high risk types - 16,18) Chronic vulvar irritation Premalignant lesions - vulvar intra-epithelial neoplasia

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 65y postmenopausal woman is dignosed with vulval cancer. She is very surprised as she has been having a very healthy lifestyle. She asks you the risk factors for vulval cancer. Which of the following is not a risk factor for vulval cancer?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Risk factors of vulvar cancer are
  • More common in older age (> 60y) and white race Increased association with obesity, HTN, diabetes and nulliparoty Associated vulvar epithelial disorders: Lichensclerosis, Vulval intra-epithelial neoplasia (VIN), Paget's disease, squamous cell hyperplasia, condyloma acuminata HPV (high risk type 16, 18) Chronic pruritis, chronic irritation of the vulva Vulvar intra-epithepithelial lesion (VIN)
  • Lichen planus may affect the skin anywhere on the body. It affects the mucosal surfaces (oral mucosa) and presents with polygenal flat-topped purpuric plaques. The lesion may be erosive and presents more with pain than pruritus. It is NOT commonly associated with vulvar carcinoma

Educational Objective:

  • Risk factors of vulvar cancer are
  • More common in older age (> 60y) and white race Increased association with obesity, HTN, diabetes and nulliparoty Associated vulvar epithelial disorders: Lichensclerosis, Vulval intra-epithelial neoplasia (VIN), Paget's disease, squamous cell hyperplasia, condyloma acuminata HPV (high risk type 16, 18) Chronic pruritis, chronic irritation of the vulva Vulvar intra-epithepithelial lesion (VIN)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 62-year-old woman presents for her annual well woman examination and complains of vulvar pruritis. During examination, you note an area of thickened white plaque. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Biopsy the lesion in your office
Prescribe estrogen cream and follow up in 3 months
Perform a wide local excision in the operating room

explanation.short: A. Biopsy the lesion in your office
explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Biopsy the lesion in your office

Explanation:

  • A post-menopausal woman with a vulvar lesion should be biopsied to rule out vulvar malignancy. Lesions may be raised, ulcerated, pigmented, or warty Importantly, other clinical entities may present similarly and include preinvasive neoplasia (VIN), infection, chronic inflammatory disease, and granulomatous disease, so a biopsy should be done. Multiple biopsies are recommended, from the most suspicious raised or dyspigmented skin are taken Concurrent colposcopic examination of the cervix and vagina and careful evaluation of the perianal area are recommended to diagnose any synchronous lesions or associated neoplasm of the lower genital tract
  • Clobetasol is a local steroid which may help in several skin conditions but a biopsy is important before prescribing medication Estrogen cream is prescribed for vaginal/ vulvar dryness/ dyspareunia in post menopausal women (Hormone replacement therapy). However the above image does not appear to be a menopausal related vulvar lesion Once biopsy shows a vulvar malignancy, then further evaluation is done to stage the tumor (clinical staging). For microscopic lesions, a wide local excision can be done

Educational Objective:

  • A post-menopausal woman with a vulvar lesion should be biopsied to rule out vulvar malignancy. Lesions may be raised, ulcerated, pigmented, or warty Importantly, other clinical entities may present similarly and include preinvasive neoplasia (VIN), infection, chronic inflammatory disease, and granulomatous disease, so a biopsy should be done. Multiple biopsies are recommended, from the most suspicious raised or dyspigmented skin are taken Concurrent colposcopic examination of the cervix and vagina and careful evaluation of the perianal area are recommended to diagnose any synchronous lesions or associated neoplasm of the lower genital tract

question: What technique can decrease the risk of lymphedema?
options: Avoid drain placement at the time of inguinofemoral dissection

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Chronic lymphedema is a frequent complication o inguinal lymphadenectomy. Preservation o the saphenous vein has been shown to reduce the incidence Regardless, this condition is typically much more problematic with the addition of groin radiation Supportive management is meant to minimize the edema and prevent symptomatic progression. Foot elevation, compression stockings, and, on occasion, diuretic therapy may be helpful

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following is INCORRECT regarding the tumor shown here involving the outer surface of the right labia minora?
options: It is typically a disease of the elderly
It commonly arises from the labia minora and clitoris
Differential diagnoses include seborrheic keratosis and dysplastic nevi
It is more common in African American women than Caucasian women

explanation.short: D. It is more common in African American women than Caucasian women
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • This is a vulvar carcinoma which appears like a melanoma as It is pigmented Let us discuss all the options: Option A: It is typically a disease of the elderly: This is correct. Vulvla melanoma disproprtionately affects the elderly Option B: It commonly arises from the labia minora and clitoris. This is also correct. The common sites are the laboa minora, majora and clitoris Option C: Differential diagnoses include seborrheic keratosis and dysplastic nevi. This is also correct . Diferential diagnosis include pigmented lesions like include lentigo simplex, vulvar melanosis, acanthosis nigricans, seborrheic keratosis, and nevi Option D. It is more common in African American women than Caucasian women: This is incorrect. It is more common amongst whites.

Educational Objective:

  • Vulval melanoma is the second most common vulvar cancer and accounts or 10 percent of all vulvar malignancies. Vulvar melanoma disproportionately affects the elderly and develops more commonly among whites than other races. Vulvar melanoma has an overall poor prognosis, and 5-year survival rates range from 8 to 55 percent Malignant vulvar melanomas most commonly arise from the labia minora, labia majora, or clitoris Some benign pigmented lesions can also be found here and include lentigo simplex, vulvar melanosis, acanthosis nigricans, seborrheic keratosis, and nevi Tissue sampling is mandatory for diagnosis
  • Ref: Page 716, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition

question: What is the most appropriate treatment for a 55-yearold patient with a 4-cm squamous cell carcinoma of the vulva involving the urethral meatus and a medical history significant for systemic lupus erythematosus?
options: Radical vulvectomy with bilateral inguinofemoral lymph node dissection

explanation.short: D. Radical vulvectomy with bilateral inguinofemoral lymph node dissection
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • This patient is stage 3 vulvar cancer. The management of stage 3 vulcar cancer is modified radical vulvectomy with b/l inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy

Educational Objective:

  • Treatment of vulval cancer
  • For both stage I and stage 2 disease: radical local excision and sentinel node dissection is indicated, and groin dissection is reserved for those with metastasis to the sentinel node(s). For stage 3: Modified radical or radical vulvectomy with inguinal and femoral lymphadenectomy is the standard therapy. Nodal involvement is a key determinant of survival. Radiation therapy is given to patients with large primary lesions and narrow margins. Stage 4: Radical vulvectomy and pelvic exenteration may be indicated for patients with stage IVA vulvar cancer. Surgery followed by radiation therapy may be done for large, resected lesions with narrow margins. This patient has a stage 2 or 3 vulvar carcinoma (As lymph nodal status is not mentioned); and the best treatment option is a radical vulvectomy with b/l LN inguino-femoral dissection Her medical history wil not change her treatment

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: What is the most appropriate treatment for a 3-cm basal cell carcinoma of the right vulva, 2cm from the midline?
options: Wide local excision with a 1-cm margin
Wide local excision with a 2-cm margin
Radical vulvectomy with right inguinofemoral lymph node dissection
Radical vulvectomy with bilateral inguinofemoral lymph node dissection

explanation.short: A. Wide local excision with a 1-cm margin
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • This is a basal cell carcinoma Basal cell carcinoma (BCC) of the vulva accounts or < 2 percent of all vulvar cancers and is most commonly found in elderly women Lesions typically arise on the labia majora, are characterized by poor pigmentation and pruritus, and often mimic eczema, psoriasis, or intertrigo. . Basal cell carcinoma is removed by radical partial vulvectomy using a minimum surgical margin of 1 cm. Lymphatic or distant spread is rare, but inguino femoral lymphadenectomy or Sentniel lymoh node biopsy is considered on clinically suspicious nodes.

A history of HPV genital warts have a 35-fold increased risk for developing vulvar cancer compared with women who have never developed these lesions. Chronic pruritis, chronic irritation of the vulva Vulvar intra-epithepithelial lesion (VIN)

Educational Objective:

question: You perform a radical vulvectomy with bilateral inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy on a woman with a 4-cm squamous cell carcinoma tumor of the vulva. Margins are negative for cancer, but there are three positive lymph nodes. What is the most appropriate treatment?

Explanation:

Women with stage III vulvar cancer are treated with surger followed by adjuvant radiation therapy directed to both groins and the pelvis. The addition of platinum-based chemotherapy concurrent with radiation therapy is also now given, and hence the best answer here is chemo-radiation

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 52-year-old woman presents with a suspicious vulvar lesion. A biopsy confirms the presence of squamous cell carcinoma of the vulva. Clinical exam and imaging show no evidence of metastases. Her healthcare team decided to perform a sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) to assess lymph node involvement. Which of the following is a true statement?
options: SLNB is usually not recommended in vulvar carcinoma as lymphatic spread is rare
SLNB is primarily used for staging and does not influence the management of vulvar carcinoma
SLNB is used to identify the 1st lymph node(s) that cancer cells are likely to spread to and can guide lymph node dissection if positive
SLNB is the sole definitive treatment for vulvar carcinoma and eliminates the need for further surgery or radiotherapy

explanation.short: C. SLNB is used to identify the 1st lymph node(s) that cancer cells are likely to spread to and can guide lymph node dissection if positive
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Role of Sentinel LN Biopsy in Vulvar Carcinoma
  • Reduces the requirement of LN dissection in vulvar carcinoma The main route of spread of vulvar carcinoma is lymphatic spread Done by lymphoscintigraphy with Tc99 labelled isosulfan blue dye/ indocyanine fluorescent green Groin incision made 10 mins later Colored node is excised and examined If negative: No lymphadenectomy needs to be done If positive: B/l inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy is done
  • Option A. SLNB is usually not recommended in vulvar carcinoma as lymphatic spread is rare: This is a wrong statement as lymphatic spread is the most common route of spread of vulvar cancers Option B. SLNB is primarily used for staging and does not influence the management of vulvar carcinoma: This is a wrong statement as SLNB is mainly done to reduce the risk of LN dissection thereby preventing complications Option D: SLNB is the sole definitive treatment for vulvar carcinoma and eliminates the need for further surgery or radiotherapy: This is a wrong statement as SLNB is not the mainstay of treatment.

Educational Objective:

  • Role of Sentinel LN Biopsy in Vulvar Carcinoma
  • Reduces the requirement of LN dissection in vulvar carcinoma The main route of spread of vulvar carcinoma is lymphatic spread
  • Ref: page 686, Williams Gynecology, 3rd edition
explanation.ref: page 686, Williams Gynecology, 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Mrs A, 56 year old woman, underwent Wertheim’s hysterectomy for cancer cervix. What type of hysterectomy is this :

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Type 2 hysterectomy

Educational Objective:

  • A Type 1 hysterectomy is also called an e xtra-fasical hysterectomy and is done in non malignant or pre-malignant conditions. It involves ligation of the uterine artery, uterosacral and cardinal ligaments at the proximal part No vaginal cuff is removed A Type 2 hysterectomy (Wertheim hysterectomy) Uterine arteries divided just medial to the ureter Uterosacral ligaments divided midway Cardinal ligaments divided midway 2/3rd vaginal cuff is remove Type 3- also called as Meig Hysterectomy The cardinal ligament is divided near the sidewall (type 3) instead of close to its midportion near the ureteral dissection (type 2) UTerosacral ligaments divided at their origin near the sacrum A larger margin of vagina is removed in type 3 hysterectomy Complete dissection of the ureter Uterine artery divided at its origin A Type 4 hysterectomy is an extended radical hysterectomy where more parametrial and vaginal tissue is removed A type 5 hysterectomy involves partial exenteration. Part of the bladder and distal ureteral segments are also removed.
  • Ref : Berek and Novak, 16th edition, page 2476

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Mrs Anita, a 70 year old woman has been counselled about radical hysterectomy and radiotherapy as treatment modalities for her cervcal cancer treatment. Meig’s Radical hysterectomy or type III hysterectomy involves removal of:
options: Uterus+ cervix + upper 1/3rd of vagina + pelvic lymph nodes + medial half of cardinal ligament
Uterus + cervix + upper 2/3rd of vagina + pelvic lymph nodes + most of cardinal ligament
Uterus + cervix + upper 2/3rd of vagina + pelvic lymph nodes + medial half of cardinal ligament
Uterus + cervix + upper 1/3rd of vagina + pelvic lymph nodes + most of cardinal ligament

explanation.short: B. Uterus + cervix + upper 2/3rd of vagina + pelvic lymph nodes + most of cardinal ligament
explanation.detail:
Educational Objective:

  • A Type 1 hysterectomy is also called an e xtra-fasical hysterectomy and is done in non malignant or pre-malignant conditions. It involves ligation of the uterine artery, uterosacral and cardinal ligaments at the proximal part No vaginal cuff is removed A Type 2 hysterectomy (Wertheim hysterectomy) Uterine arteries divided just medial to the ureter Uterosacral ligaments divided midway Cardinal ligaments divided midway 2/3rd vaginal cuff is remove Type 3- also called as Meig Hysterectomy The cardinal ligament is divided near the sidewall (type 3) instead of close to its midportion near the ureteral dissection (type 2) Uterosacral ligaments divided at their origin near the sacrum A larger margin of vagina is removed in type 3 hysterectomy (2/3rd) Complete dissection of the ureter Uterine artery divided at its origin A Type 4 hysterectomy is an extended radical hysterectomy where more parametrial and vaginal tissue is removed A type 5 hysterectomy involves partial exenteration. Part of the bladder and distal ureteral segments are also removed

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 54y woman presents to the OPD with post-menopausal bleeding. On a speculum examination, there is a fungating 4x3 cm growth seen arising from the cervix. A biopsy is taken but the patient starts profusely bleeding. Which of the following is not used for hemostasis in cases of life-threatening hemorrhage from suspected cervical cancer?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Cervical cancer typically bleeds on touch and sometimes can present with heavy bleeding or during an examination, torrential bleeding can start from the tumor bed In such cases, bleeding can often be controlled with Vaginal packing Combination of Monsel paste (ferric subsulfate) Topical acetone can also be used to obtain hemostasis, especially in cases refractory to Monsel paste If bleeding continues, emergent radiation can be delivered. Uterine artery or Internal iliac artery embolization or ligation can be performed in cases of refractory hemorrhage Lugol’s iodine is not going to stop the bleeding. It is used as a test for cervical cancer screening

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: In brachytherapy for cervical cancer, how is point A defined?
options: 2 cm lateral and 2 cm superior to the external os
5 cm lateral and 2 cm superior to the external os
2 cm lateral and 5 cm superior to the external os
5 cm lateral and 2 cm superior to the internal os

explanation.short: A. 2 cm lateral and 2 cm superior to the external os
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Radiotherapy in cervical cancer
  • Both external beam pelvic radiation and brachytherapy are typically delivered Of these, external beam radiation is usually given before intracavitary radiation (brachytherapy) External beam radiation is commonly administered in 25 fractions during 5 weeks (40 to 50 Gy). During brachytherapy, bowel and bladder are packed away from the intracavitary source using vaginal packing during tandem insertion to limit radiation to these organs. Treatment is prescribed to Point A : A point 2 cm lateral and 2 cm superior to the external cervical os; this point corresponds to the crossing of the uterine artery and ureter Point B: which is a point 3 cm lateral to point A; this point corresponds to the lateral pelvic wall/ obturator lymph nodes

Educational Objective:

  • In cervical cancer, radiotherapy (brachytherapy) is prescribed to 2 points Treatment is prescribed to Point A: A point 2 cm lateral and 2 cm superior to the external cervical os; this point corresponds to the crossing of the uterine artery and ureter Point B: which is a point 3 cm lateral to point A; this point corresponds to the lateral pelvic wall/ obturator lymph nodes

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

Features of disease recurrence are: Pain in the pelvis, back, unilateral leg edema, ureteral obstruction, vaginal bleeding, palpable tumor in the pelvis and lymphadenopathy. Treatment of recurrence: Single agent or multiagent chemotherapy with cisplatin, paclitaxel or ifosfamide is used along with Radiation therapy may be used to those who have been treated initially with surgery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which is incorrect regarding female genital tuberculosis (FGTB)?
options: FGTB is usually secondary to pulmonary TB through hematogenous route or lymphatic route
Rarely, infection can occur through infected semen from tuberculous orchitis in males
The most common organism is Mycobacterium bovis due to consumption of unpasteurized milk in villages
Cervical TB may mimic cervical cancer causing abnormal vaginal discharge or cervical ulcers and growths

explanation.short: C. The most common organism is Mycobacterium bovis due to consumption of unpasteurized milk in villages
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. The most common organism is Mycobacterium bovis due to consumption of unpasteurized milk in villages

Explanation:

  • Let’s discuss all options:
  • Option A: FGTB is usually secondary to pulmonary TB through hematogenous route or lymphatic route: This is a correct statement. FGTB is usually secondary to pulmonary TB through hematogenous route or lymphatic route but can occur through contagious spread from adjacent ileocecal TB and rarely through infected semen from tuberculous orchitis in males Option B: Rarely, infection can occur through infected semen from tuberculous orchitis in males. This is also a correct statement. FGTB is usually secondary to pulmonary TB through hematogenous route or lymphatic route but can occur through contagious spread from adjacent ileocecal TB and rarely through infected semen from tuberculous orchitis in males Option C: The most common organism is Mycobacterium bovis due to consumption of unpasteurized milk in villages. This is an INCORRECT statement, Although M. bovis infection can occur due to consumption of unpasteurized milk, the most common organism is Mycobacterium tuberculous. Option D: Cervical TB may mimic cervical cancer causing abnormal vaginal discharge or cervical ulcers and growth. This is also a correct statement. Among the various sites, fallopian tubes are mostly affected (90–100%) followed by uterus (70%), ovaries (30%), cervix (10%) and rarely vulva and vagina (1% each). Abdomino-pelvic TB may cause peritoneal adhesions, nodules and ascites and may simulate ovarian cancer. Similarly, cervical TB may mimic cervical cancer causing abnormal vaginal discharge or cervical, vulval and vaginal ulcers.

Educational Objective:

  • FGTB is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis but can also be rarely caused by Mycobacterium bovis due to consumption of unpasteurized milk in villages. Genital infection is usually secondary to pulmonary TB or intestinal TB through hematogenous or lymphatic route and is more common in immunocompromised people or with chronic medical diseases including HIV and COVID-19 Among the various sites, fallopian tubes are mostly affected (90–100%) followed by uterus (70%), ovaries (30%), cervix (10%) and rarely vulva and vagina (1% each). Abdomino-pelvic TB may cause peritoneal adhesions, nodules and ascites and may simulate ovarian cancer [30]. Similarly, cervical TB may mimic cervical cancer causing abnormal vaginal discharge or cervical, vulval and vaginal ulcers.
  • Ref: Sharma JB, Sharma E, Sharma S, Dharmendra S. Female genital tuberculosis: Revisited. Indian J Med Res. 2018 Dec;148(Suppl):S71-S83. doi: 10.4103/ijmr.IJMR_648_18. PMID: 30964083; PMCID: PMC6469382.
explanation.ref: Sharma JB, Sharma E, Sharma S, Dharmendra S. Female genital tuberculosis: Revisited. Indian J Med Res. 2018 Dec;148(Suppl):S71-S83. doi: 10.4103/ijmr.IJMR_648_18. PMID: 30964083; PMCID: PMC6469382.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 20y old girl presents with low grade fever and a few months of amenorrhoea. Prior to this she had scanty flow for a few months. On examination, her BMI is 16. Abdominal exam reveals ascites. There is no mass palpable. An ultrasound reveals a right sided adnexal lesion with ascites. What is the best diagnostic tool for this patient?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Laparoscopy

Explanation:

  • The suspected diagnosis in this patient is genital TB as she has a h/o fever and amenorrhoea, there is weight loss, ascites and the ultrasound shows an adnexal mass. The diagnosis of genital TB is complex and involves microbiological tests, imaging and endoscopy. As it is a paucibacillary infection, microbiology may not be positive although if there is a suspicion, an endometrial biopsy for microbiology and HPE is usually always done Laparoscopy is the best diagnostic tool to detect FGTB by direct visualization of TB lesions on tubes like tubercles and caseous nodules and to take biopsy
  • Option A: HSG: This is not usually done for diagnosing genital TB but signs in HSG are picked up if it was done for tubal factor infertility. It is in fact avoided in acute disease to avoid fare-up. Various findings of FGTB can be blocked tubes, (usually cornual block) tobacco-pouch appearance of tubes, beaded tubes, filling defects in uterine cavity. (Asherman’s syndrome) Option B: 3D USG: This is not very beneficial in suspected genital TB but very useful in Mullerian anomalies Option C: MRI: Computerized tomography (CT), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and positron emission tomography (PET): any one of the three modalities can be used for tuberculous tubo-ovarian masses. MRI has higher resolution especially for TB lymphadenitis. PET-CT can demonstrate increased FDG uptake of tuberculous tubo-ovarian masses. But tese are not the best diagnostic tools.

Educational Objective:

  • Investigations in Genital TB:
  • Ref: Sharma JB, Sharma E, Sharma S, Dharmendra S. Female genital tuberculosis: Revisited. Indian J Med Res. 2018 Dec;148(Suppl):S71-S83. doi: 10.4103/ijmr.IJMR_648_18. PMID: 30964083; PMCID: PMC6469382.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A diagnostic laparoscopy was done for a 34y woman with unexplained infertility. The following was seen on laparoscopy where the fallopian tubes appeared to be adherent to each other. What is the possible diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Genital TB

Explanation:

  • This is the kissing fallopian tube sign which is typically seen on laparoscopy in genital TB. Laparoscopy findings in female genital TB are: Perihepatic adhesions and Sharma’s hanging gall bladder sign Sharma’s ascending colonic adhesion Sharma’s sigmoid colonic adhesive band Sharma’s parachute sign (adhesions of ascending colon to abdominal wall) Sharma’s compartmentalization sign (multiple compartments are formed by omental adhesions to contain infection) Fallopian tubes distended with alternate constrictions and dilatations on laparoscopy and dye test resembling blue python in FGTB cases (Sharma’s blue python sign) Fusion of both fallopian tubes due to FGTB (Sharma’s kissing fallopian tubes sign)
  • Option A: Endometriosis: Laparoscopy findings of laparoscopy include Endometriotic lesions (blue, black, red and white lesions on different areas – commonly on the ovary, fallopian tubes, peritoneum, uterine serosa, POD, round ligaments etc) Ovarian endometrioma (Chocolate cyst) Adhesions (tubal/ peritoneal) Nodules/ adhesions/ obliteration of the Pouch of Douglas Option C: Chlamydial PID: Peri hepatic adhesions (Fits Hugh Curtis syndrome) Inflamed and congested uterus and adnexa Pelvic abscesses and tubo ovarian masses Option D: Fimbrial block: When Chromotubation is done and there is fimbrial block, dye will collect in the tube and dilate it (hydrosalpinx)

Educational Objective:

  • Findings on laparoscopy in female genital TB are: Perihepatic adhesions and Sharma’s hanging gall bladder sign Sharma’s ascending colonic adhesion Sharma’s sigmoid colonic adhesive band Sharma’s parachute sign (adhesions of ascending colon to abdominal wall) Sharma’s compartmentalization sign (multiple compartments are formed by omental adhesions to contain infection) Fallopian tubes distended with alternate constrictions and dilatations on laparoscopy and dye test resembling blue python in FGTB cases (Sharma’s blue python sign) Fusion of both fallopian tubes due to FGTB (Sharma’s kissing fallopian tubes sign)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 25y old infertile woman is started on anti-tubercular treatment in view of genital TB diagnosed on peritoneal biopsy on laparoscopy done for tubal factor infertility. What is the regime she should receive?
options: 2 months initiation phase with rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide and ethambutol followed by 4 months continuation phase with rifampicin and isoniazid
2 months initiation phase with rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide and ethambutol followed by 4 months continuation phase with rifampicin, isoniazid and ethambutol
2 months initiation phase with rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide and ethambutol followed by 10 months continuation phase with rifampicin and isoniazid
2 months initiation phase with rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide and ethambutol followed by 10 months continuation phase with rifampicin, isoniazid and ethambutol

explanation.short: B. 2 months initiation phase with rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide and ethambutol followed by 4 months continuation phase with rifampicin, isoniazid and ethambutol
explanation.detail:
Ans. B. 2 months initiation phase with rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide and ethambutol followed by 4 months continuation phase with rifampicin, isoniazid and ethambutol

Explanation:

  • Treatment of Female Genital TB consists of
  • The intensive phase (4 drugs) four drugs regimen for 2 months with daily oral: Rifampicin (r) Isoniazid (h) Pyrazinamide (z) Ethambutol (e)
  • The continuation phase, three-drug regimen (orally daily for next 4 months) is given using Rifampicin (r) Isoniazid (h) Ethambutol (e)

Educational Objective:

  • Treatment of Female Genital TB consists of
  • The intensive phase (4 drugs) four drugs regimen for 2 months with daily oral: Rifampicin (r) Isoniazid (h) Pyrazinamide (z) Ethambutol (e)
  • The continuation phase, three-drug regimen (orally daily for next 4 months) is given using Rifampicin (r) Isoniazid (h) Ethambutol (e)
  • For primary or secondary drug-resistant FGTB, longer oral regimen with reserve drugs is given for 18–20 months or shorter regimen is given for 9–12 months in selected category of patients

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Luteinizing hormone (LH) and human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) have similar biologic activity for which of the following reasons?
options: They are produced by the same pituitary cell type
They are converted to the same amino acid sequence peripheral

explanation.detail:
Ans. A) Their β-subunits have 80% homology

Explanation:

  • LH, FSH, TSH and hCG are heterodimers consisting of a common glycoprotein α-subunit linked to a unique β-subunit, which provides functional specificity. The amino acid sequence of the human LH and CG β-subunits demonstrates approximately 80-percent similarity. This similarity between LH and hCG can have clinical sequelae.
  • Also, molar pregnancies frequently produce very high levels of hCG, which can bind to TSH receptors, resulting in hyperthyroidism.
  • Now, Let's go through each option separately:
  • Option A. Their β-subunits have 80% homology : This is the correct answer. Both LH and hCG are glycoprotein hormones consisting of alpha and beta subunits. While the alpha subunits are nearly identical between these hormones, it's the beta subunits that confer their unique biological activities. The β-subunits of LH and hCG share about 80% homology, meaning they are structurally similar but not identical. This significant degree of similarity in their β-subunits is what leads to their similar biological activities.
  • Option B. They have nearly homologous α-subunits : This statement is true but not the reason for their similar biological activity. The α-subunits of LH and hCG, as well as other glycoprotein hormones like follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), are indeed nearly identical.
  • Option C. They are produced by the same pituitary cell type : This is not correct. LH is produced by gonadotropic cells in the anterior pituitary gland, while hCG is produced primarily by the placenta during pregnancy
  • Option D. They are converted to the same amino acid sequence peripherally : This is also not correct. LH and hCG are secreted in their active forms and do not undergo conversion to a common amino acid sequence in the periphery. They exert their effects through interactions with specific receptors on target cells.

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Williams Gynecology page 335, 3rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following is LEAST likely true of basal body temperature testing in the adult female?
options: It can be an insensitive test in many women
With ovulation, the temperature rises roughly 0.8°F
It is an inexpensive and easy test for ovulation monitoring
Once the temperature rises, a patient should expect ovulation in the next 12 hours

explanation.short: D. Once the temperature rises, a patient should expect ovulation in the next 12 hours
explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Once the temperature rises, a patient should expect ovulation in the next 12 hours

Explanation:

  • Basal body temperature (BBT) charting has long been used to identify ovulation. This test requires that a woman’s morning oral temperature be graphically charted Oral temperatures are usually 97.0° to 98.0°F during the follicular phase. A postovulatory rise in progesterone levels increases basal temperature by approximately 0.4° to 0.8°F. This biphasic temperature pattern is strongly predictive of ovulation. Nevertheless, although this test has the advantage of being inexpensive, it is insensitive in many women.
  • Let's examine each statement:
  • Option A. It can be an insensitive test in many women : This is true. The temperature shift indicating ovulation can be subtle and easy to miss, and factors like illness, sleep disturbances, and lifestyle factors can affect temperature readings.
  • Option B. With ovulation, the temperature rises roughly 0.8°F : This is generally true. After ovulation, the release of progesterone causes a woman's basal body temperature to rise by approximately 0.4 to 0.8 degrees Fahrenheit. However, the exact increase can vary from woman to woman.
  • Option C. It is an inexpensive and easy test for ovulation monitoring : This is true. BBT charting is a cost-effective, non-invasive method of possibly determining ovulation. It requires only a thermometer and consistent daily temperature recording upon waking.
  • Option D . Once the temperature rises, a patient should expect ovulation in the next 12 hours (Least Likely True): This statement is incorrect. In fact, a rise in BBT typically occurs after ovulation. By the time a woman notices a temperature rise, ovulation has likely already occurred, not that it will occur in the next 12 hours.

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page no 435, Wiliams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page no 435, Wiliams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following is true of luteal phase deficiency?
options: It affects approximately 50% of menstrual cycles
It is seen when the follicular phase is shorter than 14 days
Fertile women have the same incidence of luteal phase deficiency as infertile women
It is determined by histologic analysis of the endometrium and defined by a greater than 4-day discrepancy between the specimen-derived cycle date and the actual menstrual cycle day

explanation.short: C. Fertile women have the same incidence of luteal phase deficiency as infertile women
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Fertile women have the same incidence of luteal phase deficiency as infertile women

Explanation:

  • LPD is a clinical diagnosis and may be present with a luteal phase ≤10 days in length. Abnormal luteal function may occur as the result of several medical conditions. True isolated LPD implies an underlying pathologic abnormality of the luteal phase in the absence of an identifiable disease process negatively affecting normal LH support of the corpus luteum. No diagnostic test for LPD has proven to be reliable in the clinical setting or in differentiating fertile from infertile women. Endometrial biopsies (lag of > 2 days) only have the precision to distinguish the early luteal, midluteal, and late luteal phases, and have been shown to not discriminate between fertile and infertile women.
  • Let's evaluate each statement:
  • Option A. It affects approximately 50% of menstrual cycles : This is not accurate. LPD does not affect such a high percentage of menstrual cycles. It is considered relatively less common and not observed in half of all menstrual cycles.
  • Option B. It is seen when the follicular phase is shorter than 14 days : LPD is related to the luteal phase, not the follicular phase. LPD is characterized by a luteal phase that is typically shorter than normal (less than about 10 days) or functionally inadequate.
  • Option C. Fertile women have the same incidence of luteal phase deficiency as infertile women (Correct Answer): Recent studies and reviews suggest that the incidence of LPD is similar in both fertile and infertile women. This challenges previous notions that LPD is a common cause of infertility. It's now understood that the diagnostic criteria for LPD may be overly broad, and the condition might not be as directly linked to infertility as once thought.
  • Option D. It is determined by histologic analysis of the endometrium and defined by a greater than 4-day discrepancy between the specimen-derived cycle date and the actual menstrual cycle day : This was the traditional method of diagnosing LPD, based on endometrial biopsy findings. However, the reliability of this diagnostic criterion has been questioned in recent years.

Educational Objective:

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: What is your interpretation of the following hysterosalpingogram?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Submucosal fibroid

Explanation:

  • This HSG shows a large well defined lesion in the uterus. This is most likely a submucosal fibroid
  • Let us look at each option:
  • Option A. Normal : A normal HSG would show a regular, symmetrically shaped uterine cavity without any filling defects or irregularities. The fallopian tubes would be visible, with the contrast material flowing freely through them, indicating patency . No obstructions or unusual shapes should be present.
  • Option B . Asherman syndrome : This condition is characterized by the presence of intrauterine adhesions or scar tissue. On an HSG, Asherman syndrome may present as irregular filling defects within the uterine cavity or as a generally diminished size of the uterine cavity, depending on the severity of the adhesions.
  • Option C. Bilateral tubal block (B/l tubal block) : In cases of bilateral tubal blockage, an HSG would show a lack of contrast material passing through one or both fallopian tubes ie. absence of free spill.
  • Option D. Submucosal fibroid : They are fibroids that grow just beneath the lining of the uterine cavity. On an HSG, these would appear as filling defects within the uterine cavity. They might show as smooth, round, or lobulated contours projecting into the cavity, disrupting the otherwise smooth and regular shape of the uterus.

Educational Objective:

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following is the gold standard technique for tubal evaluation?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Laparoscopic evaluation

Explanation:

  • The following are tests done for tubal patency
  • HSG (usually a preliminary test) Laparoscopic Chromotubation (Gold standard) Saline salpingography (better for intra uterine lesions) Others HyCoSy Falloposcopy
  • Let's review each option:
  • Option A. Hysterosalpingography (HSG) : This is a radiographic technique where a contrast dye is injected into the uterine cavity, and X-ray images are taken to visualize the uterus and fallopian tubes. HSG is primarily used for evaluating the internal shape of the uterus and the patency of the fallopian tubes. While it's a commonly used first-line investigation for tubal assessment in infertility evaluations, it provides limited information about the external contour of the tubes and surrounding structures.
  • Option B. Laparoscopic Evaluation : Laparoscopy is considered the gold standard for tubal evaluation. It's a minimally invasive surgical procedure that provides a direct view of the pelvic organs, including the fallopian tubes. Laparoscopy not only allows for the assessment of tubal patency (often combined with chromopertubation, where a dye is passed through the tubes) but also enables visualization of the outer aspects of the tubes and the detection of other pelvic pathologies like endometriosis, adhesions, or fibroids that might affect tubal function. Its main limitation is that it's an invasive procedure requiring anesthesia.
  • Option C . Saline Infusion Sonography (SIS) : SIS involves the infusion of saline into the uterine cavity during ultrasound examination. This technique can be used to asses tubal patency, it also enhances the visualization of the uterine cavity and can be useful in detecting abnormalities like polyps or fibroids.
  • Option D. Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) : MRI can provide detailed images of the pelvic organs, including the uterus and fallopian tubes, without using ionizing radiation. It's particularly useful in assessing uterine anomalies and some types of fibroids. However, MRI is less commonly used for direct assessment of tubal patency or minor tubal abnormalities compared to laparoscopy or HSG.

Educational Objective:

  • Page 441, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: In treating hypertension, with beta blockers, which of the following semen parameter abnormalities may be seen?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Low semen volume

Explanation:

  • Low semen volume often simply reflects incomplete specimen collection or short abstinence interval. However, it may indicate partial vas deferens obstruction or retrograde ejaculation. Partial or complete vas deferens obstruction may be caused by infection, tumor, prior testicular or inguinal surgery, or trauma. Retrograde ejaculation follows failed closure of the bladder neck during ejaculation and allows seminal fluid to flow backward into the bladder. Retrograde ejaculation is suspected in men with Diabetes mellitus Spinal cord damage Prior prostate or other retroperitoneal surgery that may have damaged nerves Medications (β-blockers)
  • A postejaculatory urinalysis can detect sperm in the bladder and confirm the diagnosis. If urine is properly alkalinized, these sperm are viable and can be retrieved to achieve pregnancy
  • Let us explore the options given:
  • Option A. Azoospermia : This refers to the complete absence of sperm in the semen. While certain medications and health conditions can lead to azoospermia, beta-blockers are not typically associated with this severe a level of spermatogenic failure.
  • Option B. Oligospermia : Oligospermia means a lower than normal sperm count. Some medications and health issues can lead to oligospermia, but beta-blockers are not commonly linked to significant decreases in sperm count.
  • Option C. Teratospermia : Teratospermia is characterized by a high percentage of abnormally shaped sperm in the semen. This condition can be caused by various factors, including genetic defects, lifestyle choices, and exposure to certain chemicals or radiation. Beta-blockers are not known to specifically cause teratospermia.
  • Option D. Low semen volume (Correct Answer): Beta-blockers can lead to a decrease in semen volume. This effect is thought to be related to the general inhibitory effects of beta-blockers on the sympathetic nervous system, which plays a role in seminal emission. As a result, men taking beta-blockers may experience a reduction in the volume of ejaculate.

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page 443, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page 443, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Asthenospermia refers to which of the following semen abnormalities?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Sperm motility

Explanation:

  • Let's go through each option:
  • Option A. Sperm Count : This refers to the concentration of sperm in a given volume of ejaculate. When the sperm count is lower than normal, it is termed oligospermia. Asthenospermia is not related to the count or concentration of sperm.
  • Option B. Semen Volume : This is the total volume of the ejaculated fluid. A low semen volume can be due to various causes, including hormonal imbalances or blockages in the reproductive tract. However, asthenospermia does not pertain to the volume of semen.
  • Option C. Sperm Motility (Correct Answer): Asthenospermia is specifically related to sperm motility, which refers to the ability of sperm to move efficiently. This is an important factor in fertility since sperm must be able to swim through the female reproductive tract to reach and fertilize the egg. Asthenospermia is characterized by a decreased percentage of motile sperm in the semen.
  • Option D. Sperm Morphology : This refers to the size and shape of sperm. Abnormalities in sperm morphology (teratospermia) can impact the ability of sperm to fertilize an egg. Asthenospermia, however, is specifically about the movement ability of sperm, not their shape or size.

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page 443, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page 443, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following is NOT a test of ovarian reserve?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Day 15 Antral Follicle Count

Explanation:

  • Tests for ovarian reserve include
  • Biochemical tests FSH: D2/3: Increased in poor ovarian reserve Estradiol: Day 2: Increased in poor ovarian reserve Inhibin B: Reduced in poor ovarian reserve AMH: Reduced in poor ovarian reserve Ultrasound: Antral follicle count is done on day 2 or 3 of the cycle. The total no of follicles visible are counted. An AFC > 10 is normal. Clomiphene Citrate Challenge Test
  • Let's explore each option:
  • Option A. Anti-Müllerian Hormone (AMH) : AMH is a hormone produced by the granulosa cells of small ovarian follicles. It is a reliable indicator of a woman's ovarian reserve, as it reflects the number of remaining antral and pre-antral follicles in the ovaries. AMH levels typically decrease with age and lower levels are associated with a diminished ovarian reserve.
  • Option B. Clomiphene Citrate Challenge Test : This test involves administering clomiphene citrate, a medication that stimulates ovulation, and measuring the response of the ovaries. The response is typically assessed through changes in follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) levels. Abnormal test results can suggest reduced ovarian reserve.
  • Option C. Day 2 FSH : This test measures the level of FSH in a woman's blood on the second day of her menstrual cycle. FSH is a hormone that stimulates the ovaries to produce eggs. Elevated levels of FSH on day 2 can be indicative of a diminished ovarian reserve, as the body is having to work harder to stimulate the ovaries.
  • Option D . Day 15 Antral Follicle Count (Correct Answer): This is not a standard test for ovarian reserve. The antral follicle count (AFC) is typically performed much earlier in the menstrual cycle, generally on day 2-5. The AFC involves counting the number of antral follicles (small follicles) in the ovaries using ultrasound, which can give an estimate of the remaining egg supply. Performing this count on day 15 would not be standard practice, as this is typically after ovulation has occurred, making it less relevant for assessing ovarian reserve.

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page 437, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page 437, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which day of the menstrual cycle should an HSG be done?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Day 8

Explanation:

  • HSG is a test for tubal patency It also delineates the uterine cavity It is best done between Day 5 – 10 (Before ovulation in the post menstrual period) At this time, few intrauterine clots should remain to block tubal outflow or give the false impression of an intrauterine abnormality. Furthermore, a woman theoretically has not ovulated or possibly conceived. For this test, iodinated contrast medium is infused through an instrument (Leech Wilkinson cannula) placed into the uterus. With fluoroscopy, dye is visually followed as it fills the uterine cavity, then the tubal lumen, and finally spills out of the tubal fimbria into the pelvic cavity.
