subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: An infertile couple is being evaluated for infertility. They are advised to take a post coital test. What is the purpose of this test?
options: To determine the viability of sperm
To assess the quality of cervical mucous
To evaluate the process of ovulation

explanation.short: B. To assess the quality of cervical mucous
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) To assess the quality of cervical mucous

Explanation:

  • The postcoital test, also known as the Sims-Huhner test, was used historically to evaluate cervical mucus. There is limited consensus on the definition of a normal test and hence it does not have much utility. In regions where more specific testing is not available, it may provide information regarding cervical mucus, appropriate intercourse practices and sperm motility. Performed a few hours after sexual intercourse During the test, a sample of cervical mucous is collected using a speculum and a small brush and examined under a microscope to assess the quality, quantity and motility of sperm present in the mucus This provides information about the interaction between the sperm and cervical mucus, which is crucial for successful fertilization.
  • Now, Let's explore each of the given options for its purpose:
  • Option A. To determine the viability of sperm : The test observes how sperm interact with cervical mucus, which encompasses more than just viability.
  • Option B. To assess the quality of cervical mucus (Correct Answer): This is the primary purpose of the postcoital test. It evaluates how well sperm can survive and move through the cervical mucus. This is important because cervical mucus changes in consistency and composition throughout the menstrual cycle, and these changes can impact sperm survival and transport. The test is usually conducted close to the time of ovulation when the cervical mucus is most receptive to sperm.
  • Option C. To evaluate the process of ovulation : While the timing of the postcoital test is related to ovulation (as it should be performed around the time of ovulation for optimal cervical mucus quality), the test itself does not evaluate ovulation directly.
  • Option D. To examine the uterine lining : The postcoital test does not assess the uterine lining. Procedures like ultrasound or endometrial biopsy are more relevant for examining the uterine lining.

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page 441, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page 441, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 32-year woman presents with primary infertility. She has been trying to conceive for over a year with regular unprotected intercourse but has not been successful. On further evaluation, her menstrual cycles are regular with moderate flow and mild dysmenorrhoea and her partner’s semen analysis is normal. A tubal patency test is ordered. Which of the following test is most appropriate in this scenario?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A) Hysterosalpingography

Explanation:

  • The initial investigation of choice for tubal patency is an HSG The best investigation for tubal patency is Laparoscopy with chrompertubation
  • Let's review each of the options provided:
  • Option A. Hysterosalpingography (HSG) (Correct Answer): HSG is a radiographic procedure where a contrast dye is injected into the uterine cavity and X-ray images are taken. It's often the first-line test for evaluating tubal patency in women with infertility, as it's less invasive than laparoscopy. HSG can reveal whether the fallopian tubes are open and can also provide information about the shape of the uterine cavity. It's particularly suitable in this scenario where there are no other major indications for laparoscopy.
  • Option B. Laparoscopy with Chromopertubation : This is a more invasive procedure. While it's considered the gold standard for evaluating tubal patency, it's typically reserved for cases where HSG results are inconclusive or when there's a suspicion of other pelvic pathology that needs direct visualization, like endometriosis or adhesions.
  • Option C. Saline Infusion Sonography (SIS) : SIS involves the infusion of saline into the uterine cavity during an ultrasound exam. It's effective for evaluating the uterine cavity for abnormalities like polyps or fibroids but may not be as effective as HSG for assessing tubal patency.
  • Option D. Chlamydia trachomatis antibody testing : While testing for Chlamydia trachomatis antibodies can be important in infertility evaluations, particularly if there's a history suggesting a risk of pelvic inflammatory disease, it doesn't directly assess tubal patency. It can be used as an adjunct to other tests when determining the cause of infertility.

Educational Objective:

  • The initial investigation of choice for tubal patency is an HSG The best investigation for tubal patency is Laparoscopy with chrompertubation
  • Ref: Page 440, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page 440, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 29-year woman presents to the Gyn OPD wit her husband. She has been unsuccessfully trying to conceive since 1.5 years. On asking, she reveals that she has irregular menstrual cycles. There is no other significant history. Which of the following is NOT a test for determining if she is ovulation?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Urinary FSH

Explanation:

  • Tests for Ovulation include
  • History: Regular menstrual cycles; Mittelschmerz, dysmenorrhoea, Premenstrual Syndrome Basal body temperature: Just prior to ovulation, there is a drop in temperature by 0.25, then it rises by 0.5 deg C Cervical mucus study: At the time of ovulation, cervical mucus is thin and stretchy; It is thin and viscous at the time of luteal phase Hormonal Studies: Mid Luteal Progesterone: Peak on Day 21 (> 10 ng/ml) Urinary LH kits Endometrial Biopsy (Secretory phase of the cycle at day 21) Ultrasound:
  • Trilaminar endometrium seen in the proliferative phase. In the secretory phase, the endometrium becomes homogeneous. Ovary: Shows a dominant follicle at the time of ovulation (18-20 mm)
  • Laparoscopy: If performed mid-cycle, a large follicle may be seen
  • Let's review each option:
  • Option A. Day 21 Progesterone : This test is used to confirm ovulation. Progesterone levels rise following ovulation, due to the formation of the corpus luteum. A blood test for progesterone is typically done about 7 days after expected ovulation (around day 21 in a typical 28-day cycle) to check if the levels are high enough to suggest that ovulation has occurred.
  • Option B. Pre-menstrual Endometrial Biopsy : This procedure involves taking a small sample of the uterine lining just before menstruation. It's used to check for luteal phase defects. The endometrium undergoes specific changes after ovulation, and analyzing the tissue can help determine if these changes are occurring properly, suggesting that ovulation has taken place.
  • Option C. Serial Transvaginal Scans : These are ultrasounds done transvaginally at different stages of the menstrual cycle. They can be used to monitor the growth and development of follicles in the ovaries, and to observe the rupture of a follicle (indicative of ovulation). This is a direct metho d to visualize the process of ovulation.
  • Option D. Urinary FSH (Correct Answer): Urinary FSH (Follicle Stimulating Hormone) levels are not typically used to determine if ovulation is occurring. FSH is important in the development of follicles in the ovaries, but its level in urine isn't directly correlated with ovulation. Blood levels of FSH are often measured, but usually to assess ovarian reserve rather than to confirm ovulation.

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page 435, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page 435, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 38-year woman marries for the 2 nd time, her 1 st husband having expired. She has a history of a D & C for a missed abortion with her 1 st husband. She now reports to the gyn OPD with complaints of inability to conceive after trying for 1 year. She has regular periods. A pelvic ultrasound is normal. Husband semen analysis is normal. What is the next best step in management?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Laparoscopy with chrompertubation

Explanation:

  • This is a patient with secondary infertility One of the common causes of secondary infertility is tubal causes following PID Tubal patency needs to be checked in this patient Hence the best option is laparoscopic chrompertubation
  • Let's evaluate each option:
  • Option A. Ovulation Induction : This is typically considered when there is evidence of anovulation or irregular ovulation. However, in this case, the patient reports regular menstrual cycles, which suggests that she is likely ovulating normally.
  • Option B. Laparoscopy with Chromopertubation (Correct Answer): Given her history of a prior dilation and curettage (D&C), which can sometimes lead to intrauterine adhesions or other uterine abnormalities, and the fact that other basic fertility evaluations are normal, a laparoscopy with chromopertubation is a reasonable next step. This procedure allows for direct visualization of the pelvic organs and can assess for any adhesions, endometriosis, or other factors that could be contributing to infertility. Chromopertubation during laparoscopy will assess the patency of the fallopian tubes.
  • Option C. Endometrial Biopsy : This test is used to evaluate the health of the endometrium, often in cases of abnormal uterine bleeding or to assess for luteal phase defects. Given her history and current presentation, an endometrial biopsy might not be the most informative next step in the absence of specific indications for this test.
  • Option D. IVF (In Vitro Fertilization) : IVF is a more advanced fertility treatment option and is often considered when other less invasive methods have failed, or when there is a clear indication for its use (such as blocked fallopian tubes, severe male factor infertility, etc.). Jumping straight to IVF without first assessing for other potential issues (like tubal patency or intrauterine adhesions) may be premature in this scenario.

Educational Objective:

  • The key educational objective here is to understand the stepwise approach in managing infertility, especially in cases with a potentially complicated obstetric history. Initial assessments should include semen analysis, ovulation confirmation, and an evaluation of the uterine cavity and tubal patency.
  • Ref: Page 430, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page 430, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 23-year woman is brought to the OPD by her mother-in-law who is very anxious that she hasn’t conceived since her marriage which was 8 months ago. The woman has regular cycles and coital frequency is about 3 times/ week. What is the next best step?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Explain fertile period

Explanation:

  • As the woman is young and has no significant history, the best management would be reassurance with advice to follow up after 4 more months if she doesn’t conceive. Evaluation for infertility is started after 1 year of unprotected intercourse or earlier if the woman is > 35 years or has irregular cycles.
  • Let's evaluate each option:
  • Option A. Pelvic Ultrasound : This imaging test can be useful to assess the uterus and ovaries for any structural abnormalities. However, without any specific symptoms or history suggesting a problem (such as irregular periods, pain, or a history of pelvic disease), jumping to a pelvic ultrasound might be premature at this stage.
  • Option B. Hysterosalpingography (HSG) : HSG is used to evaluate the uterine cavity and the patency of the fallopian tubes. While it's an important test in the fertility workup, it's usually not the first step, especially in a young woman without a significant duration of infertility (generally considered to be one year of trying without conception for women under 35).
  • Option C. Examination of Male Partner : Male factor infertility contributes to around half of all infertility cases. However, starting with the examination of the male partner without first addressing potential misunderstandings about the timing of intercourse relative to the fertile window may not be the most efficient first step.
  • Option D. Explain Fertile Period (Correct Answer): Since the couple has only been trying to conceive for 8 months, and the woman has regular cycles and there are no immediate indications of infertility, the most appropriate next step is to ensure that they have accurate information about the fertile window in the menstrual cycle. Understanding the timing of ovulation and the days during which conception is most likely can significantly increase the chances of natural conception.

Educational Objective:

  • Before proceeding any investigations, it's crucial to ensure that couples are aware of the most fertile period in the menstrual cycle, typically the days leading up to and including the day of ovulation. This knowledge is fundamental for any couple trying to conceive and is especially pertinent when the duration of trying is less than a year and there are no obvious symptoms or history suggesting infertility.
  • ReF: Page no 430, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page no 430, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following medications is used for achieving a pregnancy in a patient with hypogonadotropic hypogonadism?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Gonadotropins

Explanation:

  • In Hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism, the cause of anovulation is in the pituitary or hypothalamus. Hence ovulation can be achieved by use of gonadotropins (FSH or LH) In ovarian causes of anovulation lie PCOS, clomiphene citrate and letrozole is used Estradiol is not an ovulation inducing agent
  • Let's review each of the options provided:
  • Option A. Estradiol : Estradiol plays a crucial role in the menstrual cycle and is used in various hormonal therapies, it is not primarily used to induce ovulation.
  • Option B. Letrozole : Letrozole is an aromatase inhibitor that's often used for ovulation induction, particularly in cases of polycystic ovary syndrome .It works by reducing estrogen levels, which in turn stimulates the release of follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and luteinizing hormone (LH), promoting ovulation. However, it's not the primary choice for hypogonadotropic hypogonadism.
  • Option C. Gonadotropins (Correct Answer): Gonadotropins, which include FSH and LH, are the primary treatment for hypogonadotropic hypogonadism. This condition is characterized by low levels of these hormones due to problems in the hypothalamus or pituitary gland. Administering gonadotropins directly stimulates the ovaries to produce eggs, making it the most effective treatment for inducing ovulation in women with this condition.
  • Option D. Clomiphene Citrate (Clomid) : Clomiphene is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) used commonly to induce ovulation in women with certain types of infertility, particularly when the issue is anovulation or oligoovulation. Clomiphene works by blocking estrogen receptors, which increases the release of FSH and LH. However, in hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, where FSH and LH are deficient, clomiphene is not typically effective .

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page 481, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page 481, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 28-year-old obese female who is seeking pregnancy is recently diagnosed with polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS). Which of the following should be recommended as first-line management for her anovulation?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Letrozole

Explanation:

  • All the options mentioned will help in ovulation in PCOS Lifestyle management (Weight loss if BMI is high + exercise) is the PRIMARY mode of management The 1st line ovulation induction agents are Letrozole Clomiphene citrate Amongst these 2, the recent ESHRE guidelines says that Letrozole is to be preferred over clomiphene in PCOS. Gonadotropins may be used as second line agents for ovulation induction after oral drugs. Insulin sensitizing agents such as metformin and myoinositol are also used in PCOS patients as adjunctive treatment.
  • Let's review each option separately:
  • Option A. Gonadotropins : These are injectable hormones (FSH and LH) used to stimulate ovulation. While effective, they are typically not the first line of treatment for PCOS due to the increased risk of multiple pregnancies and ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome. They are often considered when other treatments have failed.
  • Option B. Clomiphene Citrate : This is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) that has traditionally been used as the first-line treatment for inducing ovulation in women with PCOS. It works by blocking estrogen receptors in the brain, which increases the release of FSH and LH, thereby stimulating ovulation.
  • Option C. Letrozole (Correct Answer): Letrozole, an aromatase inhibitor, has recently become preferred over clomiphene citrate for the first-line management of anovulation in women with PCOS, particularly in obese patients. Studies have shown that letrozole is more effective in inducing ovulation in women with PCOS.
  • Option D. Insulin-Sensitizing Agents : These agents, such as metformin, are used to improve insulin resistance, a common feature in PCOS. While they can help in regulating menstrual cycles and improving the efficacy of ovulation-inducing agents, they are generally not used as a standalone first-line treatment

Educational Objective:

  • Letrozole has emerged as a preferred first-line treatment due to its higher efficacy in inducing ovulation and also due to mono-follicular development compared to clomiphene citrate, especially in obese patients with PCOS.
explanation.ref: https://www.eshre.eu/Guidelines-and-Legal/Guidelines/Polycystic-Ovary-Syndrome

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: In women with hyperprolactinemia caused by a pituitary micro-prolactinoma, which of the following is true?
options: Surgical therapy is preferred over medical therapy.
During pregnancy, cabergoline is preferred over bromocriptine.
Clomiphene citrate may be ineffective for ovulation induction.
Dopamine-agonist therapy should be increased once pregnancy is achieved.

explanation.short: C. Clomiphene citrate may be ineffective for ovulation induction.
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Clomiphene citrate may be ineffective for ovulation induction.

Explanation:

  • If hyperprolactinemia is found, then physiologic, pharmacologic, or other secondary causes of hormone hypersecretion are sought Dopamine agonists are the primary treatment of hyperprolactinemia. Surgical therapies are only considered with prolactin-secreting adenomas resistant to medical therapy. During pregnancy, if hyperprolactinemia is not associated with a pituitary lesion or a lesion is less than 10 mm (microadenoma), then dopamine-agonist therapy is stopped because the tumor expansion risk is low Bromocriptine is preferred in pregnancy.
  • Let's evaluate each option:
  • Option A. Surgical therapy is preferred over medical therapy : This is generally not true. Medical therapy, typically with dopamine agonists like bromocriptine or cabergoline, is the first-line treatment for hyperprolactinemia, including that caused by micro-prolactinomas. These medications are effective in reducing prolactin levels and often lead to tumor shrinkage. Surgical therapy is usually reserved for cases where medical therapy is ineffective or not tolerated.
  • Option B. During pregnancy, cabergoline is preferred over bromocriptine : Actually, bromocriptine is generally preferred over cabergoline during pregnancy. Bromocriptine has a longer track record and more data supporting its safety in pregnancy
  • Option C. Clomiphene citrate may be ineffective for ovulation induction (Correct Answer): This is true. Clomiphene citrate is an ovulation induction agent that works by blocking estrogen receptors, leading to an increase in the release of FSH and LH. However, in the presence of elevated prolactin levels, as seen in hyperprolactinemia, the effectiveness of clomiphene may be diminished. Therefore, normalizing prolactin levels with dopamine agonists is often necessary before or in conjunction with using clomiphene.
  • Option D. Dopamine-agonist therapy should be increased once pregnancy is achieved : This is not typically recommended. In fact, dopamine agonist therapy is often discontinued once pregnancy is confirmed in women with micro-prolactinomas. This is because the risk of tumor growth during pregnancy is low, and the safety of continued use of these drugs during pregnancy is not fully established.

Educational Objective:

  • First-line treatment of hyper-prolactinemia with a mico-adenoma typically involves medical therapy with dopamine agonists, which are effective in lowering prolactin levels and can restore ovulatory function.
  • Ref: Page 450, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page 450, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Compared with clomiphene citrate therapy, gonadotropin therapy has which of the following characteristics?
options: Greater negative effect on the endometrium

explanation.detail:
Ans. A) Higher ovulation rate

Explanation:

  • Gonadotropins are usually 2nd line drugs for ovulation induction They are preferred in hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism They have a higher ovulation rate and a higher multiple pregnancy rate also. Women with PCOS have ovaries highly sensitive to gonadotropin stimulation. They have a higher risk of excessive ovarian response and of multifetal pregnancy than those with normal ovaries. Unlike clomiphene, they do not have a negative effect on the endometrium.
  • Let's examine each option separately :
  • Option A. Higher Ovulation Rate (Correct Answer): This is because gonadotropins more directly stimulate the ovaries, whereas clomiphene citrate works indirectly by blocking estrogen receptors, which in turn promotes the release of FSH and LH.
  • Option B . Lower Multiple Pregnancy Rate : This is not true for gonadotropin therapy. In fact, gonadotropin therapy has a higher risk of multiple pregnancies compared to clomiphene citrate. This is due to the more potent and direct ovarian stimulation with gonadotropins, which can lead to the development of multiple follicles.
  • Option C. Lower Ovarian Hyperstimulation Rate : Gonadotropin therapy actually has a higher risk of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) compared to clomiphene citrate. The ovaries become swollen and painful, and it is more likely with stimulation of the ovaries by gonadotropins.
  • Option D. Greater Negative Effect on the Endometrium : Clomiphene citrate, not gonadotropin therapy, is more commonly associated with a negative effect on the endometrium.

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page 479, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page 479, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following is LEAST likely to be true regarding clomiphene citrate therapy?
options: The typical starting dose is 100 mg.
It is classified as a category X drug by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA).
It can be initiated on the second day of the menstrual cycle.
Most pregnancies will occur during the first three treatment cycles.

explanation.short: A. The typical starting dose is 100 mg.
explanation.detail:
Ans. A) The typical starting dose is 100 mg.

Explanation:

  • Clomiphene citrate is an anti-estrogen It is used in ovulation induction in anovulatory women (PCOS) It is given as a starting dose of 50mg from Day2 to Day 6 followed by an follicular imaging scan from the 10th day of the period It is contra-indicated in pregnancy (FDA Category X)
  • Let's evaluate each option:
  • Option A. The typical starting dose is 100 mg (Correct Answer): This is the least likely to be true. The typical starting dose of clomiphene citrate is usually 50 mg per day, not 100 mg. The dose may be increased in subsequent cycles if there is no response, but starting at a higher dose increases the risk of side effects, including the chance of multiple pregnancies.
  • Option B. It is classified as a category X drug by the FDA : Clomiphene citrate is actually classified as FDA pregnancy category X, meaning that it should not be used during pregnancy. The drug is used to induce ovulation, but if a woman becomes pregnant, the use of clomiphene should be discontinued due to potential risks to the fetus.
  • Option C. It can be initiated on the second day of the menstrual cycle : This is true. Clomiphene citrate is often initiated on the second, third, fourth, or fifth day of the menstrual cycle.
  • Option D. Most pregnancies will occur during the first three treatment cycles : This is true. The majority of pregnancies achieved with clomiphene citrate occur within the first three cycles of treatment. If conception has not occurred after this time, the likelihood of success with continued use of clomiphene decreases, and other treatment options may be considered.

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page 450, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page 450, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 38-year-old with infertility secondary to tubal blockage just underwent in vitro fertilization (IVF). Her peak estradiol was 4500 pg/mL. Seventeen oocytes were retrieved, and two embryos were transferred. She conceived with a singleton gestation. She developed ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome. Which of the following was a predisposing factor in developing ovarian hyperstimulation?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Estradiol level

Explanation:

  • Risk factors for OHSS include
  • Young age (< 35y) Low BMI PCOS History of allergy/ atopy High serum estradiol Previous h/o OHSS Multiple follicles (PCOS) High dose/ repeated doses of hCG Pregnancy
  • Let's examine each option in relation to the predisposition to OHSS:
  • Option A . Her Age : While age can impact various aspects of fertility and responses to fertility treatments, it is not typically considered a direct predisposing factor for the development of OHSS. Women of varying ages can develop OHSS, and it's more related to the response to the ovarian stimulation rather than age per se.
  • Option B. Estradiol Level (Correct Answer): A high estradiol level is indeed a key predisposing factor for OHSS. A peak estradiol level of 4500 pg/mL, as mentioned in the scenario, is significantly high and can be associated with an increased risk of developing OHSS. This is due to the large number of growing follicles producing estradiol, which can lead to increased vascular permeability and the symptoms associated with OHSS.
  • Option C. Etiology of Her Infertility : The underlying cause of infertility (in this case, tubal blockage) is generally not a direct risk factor for OHSS
  • Option D. Number of Embryos Transferred : The number of embryos transferred does not directly predispose to OHSS. OHSS is related to the ovarian response to stimulation during the IVF cycle, not the embryo transfer process

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page no 456, Williams Gynecology 3rd editio
explanation.ref: Page no 456, Williams Gynecology 3 rd editio

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following hormones is thought to play a major role in ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Human chorionic gonadotropin

Explanation:

  • OHSS is characterized by multiple follicular development and ovarian enlargement following hCG stimulation. It occurs mostly in IVF due to controlled ovarian hyperstimulation (COH) but can also happen with simple ovulation induction It is an iatrogenic and potentially a life-threatening complication of COH hCG and VEGF have been implicated in the etiology of OHSS
  • Let's review each option:
  • Option A. Progesterone : Progesterone is a hormone produced by the corpus luteum after ovulation and during pregnancy. While it plays a critical role in the menstrual cycle and maintaining pregnancy, it is not primarily implicated in the development of OHSS.
  • Option B. Luteinizing Hormone (LH) : LH is involved in the natural ovulation process. It triggers ovulation and the development of the corpus luteum. While controlled ovarian hyperstimulation often involves drugs that mimic LH action, LH itself is not the primary hormone implicated in OHSS.
  • Option C. Human Chorionic Gonadotropin (hCG) (Correct Answer): hCG is the hormone primarily implicated in OHSS. In fertility treatments, hCG is often used to mimic the natural LH surge to trigger ovulation. However, its use, especially in high doses or in sensitive individuals, can lead to OHSS. hCG stimulates the ovaries to produce more estrogen, leading to ovarian enlargement and increased vascular permeability, which are key features of OHSS.
  • Option D. Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH) : FSH is used in fertility treatments to stimulate the development of ovarian follicles. While it plays a role in controlled ovarian hyperstimulation, it is not directly responsible for the development of OHSS.

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page 456, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page 456, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: If concern for ovarian hyperstimulation is present during ovulation induction, which of the following can be used to induce the endogenous luteinizing hormone (LH) surge?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist

Explanation:

  • OHSS can be prevented by the following ways:
  • Use of GnRH antagonists (instead of GnRH agonists) for down regulation Low starting dose of gonadotropins in high-risk women Metformin cotreatment during gonadotropin stimulation in women with PCOS or at high risk of OHSS Close monitoring of the superovulation cycles using TVS and serum estradiol estimation Replacing hCG with leuprolide (GnRH agonist) as a maturation trigger (i.e. induce the LH surge) To withhold ovulatory dose of hCG in susceptible cases and to cancel the cycle or to delay the dose of hCG injection (coasting) Cryopreservation of oocytes or embryos for future use Aspiration of immature oocytes and in vitro maturation (IVM) Progesterone should be used for luteal phase support instead of hCG Cabergoline and albumin have a role in reducing the incidence of OHSS in those at high risk
  • Let's review each option:
  • Option A. Urinary Human Chorionic Gonadotropin (hCG) : This is commonly used to induce ovulation, as it acts like the body's natural luteinizing hormone (LH) surge. However, hCG can increase the risk of OHSS, especially in patients who are overly responsive to ovarian stimulation.
  • Option B. Recombinant Human Chorionic Gonadotropin (hCG) : Similar to urinary hCG, recombinant hCG is used to induce ovulation but can also contribute to the development of OHSS. Both forms of hCG have a longer duration of action, which can exacerbate OHSS in high-risk patients.
  • Option C. Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone (GnRH) Agonist (Correct Answer): In patients at risk for OHSS, a GnRH agonist can be used to trigger ovulation. Unlike hCG, GnRH agonists cause a shorter and more physiologic LH surge, which can induce ovulation with a lower risk of OHSS. This is particularly beneficial in patients undergoing controlled ovarian stimulation as part of an IVF cycle who have a high number of follicles.
  • Option D. Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone (GnRH) Antagonist : GnRH antagonists are used during ovarian stimulation protocols to prevent premature ovulation. They do not trigger the LH surge.

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page 456, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page 456, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following infertility scenarios would warrant intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI)?
options: Infertility secondary to diminished ovarian reserve
Infertility secondary to bilateral distal fallopian tube occlusion
Infertility secondary to the male partner’s severe oligospermia

explanation.short: D. Infertility secondary to the male partner’s severe oligospermia
explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Infertility secondary to the male partner’s severe oligospermia

Explanation:

  • ICSI is intra cytoplasmic sperm injection It is a variation of IVF and is most applicable to male actor infertility. During the micromanipulation technique of ICSI, cumulus cells surrounding an ovum are enzymatically digested, and a single sperm is directly injected through the zona pellucida and oocyte cell membrane. Pregnancy rates with ICSI are comparable with those achieved with IVF or other causes of infertility.
  • Let's review each option:
  • Option A. Infertility secondary to Anovulation : Anovulation, the absence of ovulation, is often treated with medications that induce ovulation, such as clomiphene citrate or gonadotropins. ICSI is not typically indicated in cases where the primary issue is anovulation, as the main challenge is to produce a viable egg, not the fertilization process itself.
  • Option B. Infertility secondary to Diminished Ovarian Reserve : While IVF may be recommended in such cases to maximize the chance of fertilization, ICSI is not necessarily indicated unless there is also a concurrent male factor infertility.
  • Option C. Infertility secondary to Bilateral Distal Fallopian Tube Occlusion : When the fallopian tubes are blocked, particularly at the distal end (farther from the uterus), IVF is the usual treatment of choice because it bypasses the fallopian tubes altogether. ICSI may be added if there is an additional factor of male infertility.
  • Option D. Infertility secondary to the Male Partner’s Severe Oligospermia (Correct Answer): Severe oligospermia, characterized by a very low sperm count, is a classic indication for ICSI. In such cases, the chances of sperm naturally fertilizing an egg are significantly reduced. ICSI addresses this by directly injecting a single sperm into the egg, thus bypassing the need for a large number of motile sperm.

Educational Objective:

  • ReF: Page 465, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page 465, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following assisted reproductive technologies (ART) techniques is shown in the image?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A) Embryo biopsy

Explanation:

  • This is an image showing embryo biopsy which is done in pre-implantation genetic diagnosis During this technique, cells are removed from a developing embryo. Multiple options exist regarding biopsy timing and genetic material source. Biopsy of the first and second polar body has the advantage of avoiding cell removal from the developing embryo, but the disadvantage is that genetic abnormalities of paternal origin are not detected. Biopsy of cleavage-stage embryos (6- to 8-cell stage) allows evaluation of both maternal and paternal contribution to the genome Biopsy of tropho-ectoderm at the blastocyst stage
  • Let us look at each option separately:
  • Option A. Embryo Biopsy : This technique involves removing one or more cells from an embryo to test for genetic conditions before transferring the embryo to the uterus. It is often used in conjunction with preimplantation genetic testing (PGT) to identify embryos with genetic abnormalities or specific inherited disorders.
  • Option B. Assisted Hatching : This technique involves making a small hole in the zona pellucida (the outer layer of the embryo) to assist the embryo in breaking free during the process of implantation. It's sometimes used to improve the chances of implantation, particularly in certain groups of patients such as older women or those with previous failed IVF cycles.
  • Option C. Oocyte In Vitro Maturation (IVM) : In this process, immature eggs are collected from the ovaries and matured in the laboratory. This is different from conventional IVF, where medication is used to mature the eggs inside the ovaries before they are collected.
  • Option D. Intracytoplasmic Sperm Injection (ICSI) : ICSI involves injecting a single sperm directly into an egg to achieve fertilization. It's used especially in cases of severe male factor infertility, where sperm may have difficulty penetrating the egg on their own.

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page 466, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page 466, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following is an indication for gestational surrogacy

explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Mullerian agenesis

Explanation:

  • Mullerian agenesis is an example of absolute uterine factor infertility which is an indication for gestational surrogacy. Gestational surrogacy is a variation to IVF in which a fertilized egg is placed into the uterus of a surrogate rather than into the “intended mother.” Indications: Women with uncorrectable uterine factors Those in whom pregnancy would pose significant health risks h/o repetitive unexplained abortions
  • Thus, option B: Mullerian agenesis is an indication for surrogacy.
  • Let's review each option to determine the most appropriate indication for gestational surrogacy:
  • Option A. Tubercular Salpingitis : This condition involves inflammation of the fallopian tubes due to tuberculosis, which can lead to infertility. Women with tubercular salpingitis may be treated with anti-tubercular therapy and potentially IVF if the fallopian tubes are significantly damaged.
  • Option B. Mullerian Agenesis (Correct Answer): Mullerian agenesis, also known as Mayer-Rokitansky-Küster-Hauser (MRKH) syndrome, is a condition where a woman is born without a uterus or with an underdeveloped uterus. Since these women cannot carry a pregnancy themselves, gestational surrogacy is a viable option for them to have a child genetically related to them (using their own eggs, if ovaries are functional, and a partner's or donor's sperm).
  • Option C. Azoospermia : This is a male infertility condition where there is no sperm in the ejaculate. Azoospermia itself is not an indication for gestational surrogacy but rather for male fertility treatments, such as surgical sperm retrieval combined with IVF/ICSI (Intracytoplasmic Sperm Injection).
  • Option D. Premature Ovarian Failure : This condition occurs when a woman's ovaries stop functioning normally before the age of 40. While it can lead to infertility, it's not a direct indication for gestational surrogacy. Women with premature ovarian failure may consider options like IVF using donor eggs.

Educational Objective:

  • Gestational surrogacy is particularly relevant for women who are unable to carry a pregnancy, such as those with Mullerian agenesis. In contrast, conditions affecting the fallopian tubes, male factor infertility, or ovarian function may require different assisted reproductive technologies, but not necessarily surrogacy.
  • Ref: Page no 465, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page no 465, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A patient comes to your OPD for infertility treatment. Her HSG shows the following picture. What would be the best management for her?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Hysteroscopic bilateral tubal cannulation

Explanation:

  • This is an HSG showing a bilateral cornual block (block at the proximal portion as the tubes cannot be seen at all). Cornual block (proximal tubal obstructions) can be managed with proximal tubal catheterization and cannulation (via hysteroscopy or HSG) with 85% success in restoring tubal patency. Distal tubal occlusion is usually secondary to inflammatory conditions, endometriosis and previous h/o surgery. If < 35y, microsurgery can be considered (Tuboplasty). IF > 35 y, IVF should be considered. The choice of treatment largely depends on the specific findings of the HSG:
  • Let us look at each option
  • Option A. IVF (In Vitro Fertilization) : IVF is often considered when there are severe tubal abnormalities (such as bilateral tubal blockage), severe male factor infertility, or when other fertility treatments have failed. If the HSG shows severely damaged or blocked fallopian tubes that are unlikely to be successfully treated with surgery, IVF may be the recommended option.
  • Option B. Tuboplasty : This surgical procedure is used to repair or reconstruct the fallopian tubes. It's considered in cases where there's tubal damage or blockage that is amenable to surgical correction. The success of tuboplasty depends on the location and extent of the tubal damage.
  • Option C. Hysteroscopic Bilateral Tubal Cannulation : This procedure is used in cases of proximal tubal blockage (blockage near the uterus). It involves the use of a hysteroscope and a cannula to open up the blocked segments of the fallopian tubes. If the HSG shows proximal tubal blockage, this procedure can be an effective treatment.
  • Option D. Ovulation Induction : This treatment is used when the primary cause of infertility is anovulation. If the HSG shows that the fallopian tubes are open and the main issue is a lack of ovulation, ovulation induction might be the appropriate treatment.

Educational Objective:

  • HSG is a valuable diagnostic tool in evaluating the fallopian tubes and the uterine cavity, and its findings are critical in guiding the appropriate management approach, whether surgical or medical. Hysteroscopic Bilateral Tubal Cannulation is used in cases of proximal tubal blockage (blockage near the uterus).
  • Ref: Page 1158, Berek and Novak’s Gynecology 15th edition
explanation.ref: Page 1158, Berek and Novak’s Gynecology 15th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following is true regarding semen analysis parameters?
options: For semen analysis, abstinence of 4-5 days is a must
In healthy semen, morphologically normal sperms should be > 32%
Sperm morphology is the best parameter for assessing sperm function

explanation.short: D. Sperm morphology is the best parameter for assessing sperm function
explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Sperm morphology is the best parameter for assessing sperm function

Explanation:

  • The single most important investigation is Semen Analysis The WHO has defined semen analysis parameters which were recently revised in 2020. These are important to know.
  • The WHO recommends an abstinence of 2-7 days prior to collection for standard semen evaluation Sperm morphology is the best parameter for assessing sperm function.
  • Let's review each option separately:
  • Option A. Sperm Count < 50 million/ml is Oligospermia : According to the World Health Organization (WHO) standards, oligospermia is defined as a sperm concentration lower than 15 million/ml.
  • Option B. For Semen Analysis, Abstinence of 4-5 Days is a Must : The WHO recommends an abstinence period of 2 to 7 days before semen analysis. While 4-5 days is within this range and often advised, it's not an absolute "must”
  • Option C. In Healthy Semen, Morphologically Normal Sperms Should be > 32% : According to the WHO’s latest criteria, a sample is considered normal if 4% or more of the sperm have normal morphology.
  • Option D . Sperm Morphology is the Best Parameter for Assessing Sperm Function : The ability of sperm to fertilize an egg depends on a combination of factors, including count, motility (movement), and morphology (shape). A comprehensive evaluation of all these factors is generally needed.

Educational Objective:

  • Sperm count, morphology, and motility are all important aspects of the analysis. It's important to note that a comprehensive approach, considering all semen parameters, is crucial in assessing male fertility Sperm Morphology is the Best Parameter for Assessing Sperm Function : The ability of sperm to fertilize an egg depends on a combination of factors, including count
explanation.ref: https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789240030787

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 28-year women with history of an ectopic pregnancy presents with subfertility of 3 years. She complains of recurrent vaginal discharge. A transvaginal ultrasound reveals the following. What is your diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Hydrosalpinx

Explanation:

  • The above scenario is that of a hydrosalpinx. Points in favor of this are:
  • H/o ectopic pregnancy H/o subfertility Recurrent discharge PV USG showing a retort shaped hypoechoic collection suggestive of hydrosalpinx
  • Let's review each option in the context of the provided information:
  • Option A. Ovarian Malignancy : This diagnosis would be suggested by the presence of complex masses or irregularities in the ovaries. However, ovarian malignancies often present with additional clinical signs and symptoms, and they might have more complex features on ultrasound than what is typically seen in cases of hydrosalpinx.
  • Option B. Chocolate Cyst (Endometrioma) : A chocolate cyst or endometrioma is associated with endometriosis and typically appears as a cystic ovarian mass with homogenous, low-level internal echoes on ultrasound, due to the presence of old blood.
  • Option C. Hydrosalpinx (Correct Answer): Hydrosalpinx refers to the accumulation of fluid in a fallopian tube, often resulting from past infection or inflammation, such as from an ectopic pregnancy. On ultrasound, it appears as an elongated, fluid-filled sac typically adjacent to the ovary. Given the patient's history of ectopic pregnancy and the description of the ultrasound findings, hydrosalpinx is the most likely diagnosis.
  • Option D. Broad Ligament Fibroid : A fibroid in the broad ligament would typically appear as a solid mass on ultrasound, often with a characteristic whorled pattern. It is distinct from a fluid-filled structure like a hydrosalpinx.

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page 566, Berek and Novak’s Gynecology, 15th edition
explanation.ref: Page 566, Berek and Novak’s Gynecology, 15 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 28-year-old P1L1 woman complains of significant dysmenorrhea & dyspareunia. She has menses every month & has no past history of any abdominal surgery or any vaginal infection. Which of the following tests would most likely identify the etiology of the infertility in this patient?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A) Laparoscopy

Explanation:

  • This is likely a case of endometriosis Regular periods rules out ovarian causes like PCOS; hence basal body temperature and progesterone assay are not essential to identify pathology in this patient HSG is useful for tubal pathology, but endometriosis is best diagnosed by laparoscopy C/F of endometriosis are: 5 D’s Dysmenorrhea Dyspareunia Dull aching abdominal pain Dysfertility (Infertility) Dyschezia
  • Let's review each option separately:
  • Option A. Laparoscopy : Given the symptoms of significant dysmenorrhea and dyspareunia, with a normal menstrual cycle and no history of abdominal surgery or vaginal infection, endometriosis is a likely differential diagnosis. Laparoscopy is the gold standard for the diagnosis of endometriosis. It allows direct visualization of the pelvic organs and the identification, biopsy, and potentially treatment of endometriotic lesions
  • Option B. Basal Body Temperature Chart : While a basal body temperature chart can help in understanding ovulation patterns, it is less likely to identify the cause of dysmenorrhea and dyspareunia.
  • Option C. Hysterosalpingogram (HSG) : It can be used to identify uterine abnormalities or tubal blockage but is less effective in diagnosing conditions like endometriosis, which more commonly cause dysmenorrhea and dyspareunia.
  • Option D. Progesterone Assay : This test measures the level of progesterone to confirm ovulation, but it would not directly identify the cause of her pain or infertility, especially in the context of a regular menstrual cycle.

Educational Objective:

  • ReF: Page no 512, 516, Berek and Novak’s Gynecology 15th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 512, 516, Berek and Novak’s Gynecology 15 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: 22-year-old woman is anxious to conceive and is recently married since a few months. Her menstrual cycle ranges from 30-35 days. She explained the fertile period. In addition, which test will help her determine when she is ovulating?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Urine LH

Explanation:

  • Basal body temperature Progesterone causes an increase in 0.5 - 1 ° F Inexpensive Limitation: It is a retrospective diagnosis Cervical mucus Under the influence of progesterone, the cervical mucous becomes thick and sticky called “Spinnbarkeit” LH monitoring 2 hours following serum LH surge, urinary LH is detected Commercially available urinary LH kits can detect urinary LH by ELISA Once LH surge detected; ovulation occurs within 48 h Midluteal serum Progesterone Done on Day 21; >10 ng/ml – confirms ovulation Transvaginal ultrasound Serial scans from Day 10; Dominant follicle when it is 18-20 mm Endometrial biopsy Secretory changes in the endometrium indicate ovulation Done between Day 24-28 Invasive so not preferred
  • Let's review each option separately:
  • Option A. Serum FSH : Follicle-stimulating hormone is important for the growth and maturation of ovarian follicles. While FSH levels rise at the beginning of the menstrual cycle, they are not typically used to predict ovulation .
  • Option B. Serum LH : Luteinizing hormone surges in the bloodstream just before ovulation, triggering the release of a mature egg from the ovary. Measuring serum LH can indicate impending ovulation, but blood tests are not as practical for routine ovulation tracking.
  • Option C. Urine LH (Correct Answer): Urine LH tests, often known as ovulation predictor kits are a practical and widely used method to detect the surge in LH that precedes ovulation by about 24-36 hours. These home tests are user-friendly and allow women to track their most fertile days with good accuracy.
  • Option D. Urine FSH : Similar to serum FSH, urine FSH is not typically used to predict ovulation.

Educational Objective:

  • The use of ovulation predictor kits that measure LH in urine is a practical and effective method for women to identify their fertile window.
  • Ref: Page no 1150, Berek and Novak’s Gynecology, 15th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1150, Berek and Novak’s Gynecology, 15 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 35-year woman was planned to undergo a laparoscopic tubal ligation. The instrument shown below was used to create pneumoperitoneum. All are signs that carbon dioxide is in the peritoneal cavity except?
options: A drop of saline on the hub of the needle is sucked in if the needle is in the peritoneal cavity
On pushing saline, the fluid can’t be aspirated if it is in the peritoneal cavity
The pressure in the peritoneal cavity will be > 10 mmHg, if the needle is in the peritoneal cavity.
Obliteration of liver dullness if gas is intraperitoneal

explanation.short: C. The pressure in the peritoneal cavity will be > 10 mmHg, if the needle is in the peritoneal cavity.
explanation.detail:
Educational Objective:

  • An important part of laparoscopic surgeries is creation of pneumoperitoneum The image shows a Verres needle which is used to create a pneumoperitoneum. It is typically inserted at the level of the umbilicus as there is no fat of muscle at this site. Palmer’s point is preferred if adhesions are anticipated. This point is 3 cm below the left costal margin in the mid-clavicular line. Intraperitoneal placement of the needle is confirmed by Drop test: place a drop of saline on the hub of the needle and life the abdominal wall; the drop is sucked in The insufflator will show a reading of intraperitoneal pressure < 10 mmHg (single digit reading) Push 5 ml of saline in the peritoneal cavity and then attempt to aspirate it. No fluid can be aspirated if the needle is in the peritoneal cavity. Liver dullness is obliterated if CO2 is flowing intra-peritoneally.
  • Ref: Page no 273, Te Linde’s Operative Gynecology 11th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: The following instruments are required for an IUCD insertion except?

explanation.detail:
Educational Objective:

  • The posterior vaginal wall is retracted with Sim’s speculum and the anterior lip of cervix is held with the Vulsellum The cervix is cleaned with a betadine swab (on a sponge holding forceps) Uterine sound is introduced and the uterocervical length is measured. The horizontal limbs of the CuT are inserted into the inserter using a no-touch technique The tip of the plunger is placed in contact with the end of the vertical limb of the CuT The whole assembly is placed in the uterine cavity till the guard is in contact with the external os The plunger is stabilized, and the inserter is withdrawn to release the CuT and then both plunger and inserter are withdrawn together The threads are trimmed to about 1 inch from the external os with a suture cutting scissors.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 32-year-old primigravida woman at 39 weeks gestation is admitted to the labor and delivery unit in active labor. She has been progressing well, but there is delayed progress in the 2nd stage of labor. The obstetrician decides to use vacuum extraction to assist with the delivery. The appropriate application of ventouse in this scenario involves:
options: Placing the cup on the fetal occiput and applying traction during maternal contractions
Placing the cup on the fetal occiput and applying continuous traction until delivery
Placing the cup on the fetal forehead and applying continuous traction until delivery
Placing the cup on the fetal forehead and applying traction during maternal contractions

explanation.short: A. Placing the cup on the fetal occiput and applying traction during maternal contractions
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • The vacuum cup is to be applied as close to the occiput as possible. This is the flexion point (3cm anterior to the posterior fontanellae) and traction on this point encourages flexion. Traction is applied during the contractions whilst encouraging the mother also to bear down.
  • Option B: Placing the cup on the fetal occiput and applying continuous traction until delivery. This is incoorect as although placement is correct, traction is not continuois and only with uterine contractions
  • Option C: Placing the cup on the fetal forehead and applying continuous traction until delivery: This is incoorect as the cup placement has to be near the occiput. By placing the cup on the forehead, extension will happen which is not a favoured attitude. Also continuous traction is NOT applied.
  • Option D: Placing the cup on the fetal forehead and applying traction during maternal contractions. This is incorrect as the cup placement has to be near the occiput. By placing the cup on the forehead, extension will happen which is not a favoured attitude

Educational Objective:

  • Application of Vacuum Application of the cup The cup is lubricated and introduced into the vagina The center of the cup should be placed over the sagittal suture as close to the posterior fontanelle as possible (FLEXION POINT) The Flexion point is a point 3 cm anterior to the post fontanellae Traction at this point causes flexion of the head Creation of the vacuum First feel all around the cup to see that no maternal tissue is stuck in the cup Cup is connected to the suction machine and vacuum till 0.8 kg/cm 2 is created. Traction Traction is applied at right angles to the cup Traction is applied intermittently with uterine contraction Direction of traction: Along the pelvic curve (First downwards then horizontal then upwards)
  • Ref: Page no 1366, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1366, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: What is the use of this instrument?
options: Insertion of an intra-uterine device post-partum
Painting the parts prior to surgery

explanation.short: A. Insertion of an intra-uterine device post-partum
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • This is a Kelly’s forceps It is a long forceps without a lock It is used in post placental insertion of an intra-uterine device Post placental IUCD insertion is done within 10 minutes of placental delivery
  • Option A: Painting the parts prior to surgery: This is done by using a sponge holding forceps (aka ring forceps) and although very similar to a KElly;s forceps, has a lock and looks like as shown below
  • Option B: Removing retained products of conception: This is done by using an ovum forceps which is a long forceps without a lock but has an egg shaped end. It looks like:
  • Option C: Removing the placenta following delivery: This si done manually, sometimes membranes need to be removed and this can be done using an artery forceps or a sponge holding forceps

Educational Objective:

  • This is a Kelly’s forceps It is a long forceps without a lock It is used in post placental insertion of an intra-uterine device Post placental IUCD insertion is done within 10 minutes of placental delivery

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: What is the volume of the instrument shown below?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • This is a Manual Vacuum Aspirator and the volume of the cannula is 60ml

Educational Objective:

  • This is a Manual Vacuum Aspirator (MVA). It consists of a large syringe which has a 60 ml cannula with a double valve system and a plunger It is used in dilatation and evacuation procedures or suction and evacuation procedures in Missed abortion Incomplete abortion Inevitable abortion Surgical MTP till 12 weeks Evacuation of molar pregnancy After creating a vacuum manually, it is attached to the Karman cannula and the loaded apparatus is introduced into the uterus The size of the Karman cannula is the POG in weeks Using rotatory and back and forth movements, the products

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 28-year-old primigravida woman is in the second stage of labor. The obstetrician determines that an episiotomy is necessary to facilitate the delivery of the baby's head. When performing an episiotomy, which of the following instruments is commonly used?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • An episiotomy is given by a Braun-Stadler Episiotomy Scissors which looks like this:
  • Option A: Scalpel: A scalpel is not used to give an episiotomy Option B: Metzembaum scissors: This is a tissue cutting scissors and is used for dissection of tissue especialy fine and delicate structures. It looks like this:
  • Option D: Curved Mayo scissors is used to cut through tougher tissue structures like the uterus or muscle. It looks like this

Educational Objective:

  • An episiotomy is given by a Braun-Stadler Episiotomy Scissors which looks like this:
  • This is a scissors that is characteristically angulated in the middle and very easy to identify The inside blade is guided by 2 fingers so as to avoid inadvertent injury to the fetus Episiotomy should NOT be given routinely; rather restrictively in conditions like Macrosomia Assisted breech delivery Use of instrumental delivery Shoulder dystocia Rigid perineum The layers cut in an episiotomy from inside out are Vaginal mucosa Muscle (superficial and deep transverse perinei, Bulbospongiosus and part of levator ani) Perineal skin An episiotomy is a type of 2nd degree perineal tear

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 32-year woman undergoes a lower segment cesareans section for obstructed labor. Following delivery of the baby, you note that the angles of the incision are bleeding profusely. The following instrument is best used to catch the bleeding angles:

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • The preferred forceps in this scenario is the Green Armytage forceps which is as shown below.
  • This is used to hold the angles and edges of the cesarean scar if there is excessive bleeding. Normally the edges are held with an allis forceps but in case of heavy bleeding, a Green Armytage forceps is better as it is less traumatic and has a wider surface area. Usually, the angles of the incision are held with an Allis forceps but in the given scenario, a Green Armytage wd be better
  • Option B: : This is an allis forceps. It is usually used to hold tough structures including the angles and edges of a cesarean section uterine incision but in the event of a large bleeded, a green armytage is preferred Option C: : This is an artery forceps and is used to catch and grasp a bleeding vessel but usually this isn;'e useful in large uterine vessels seen at the angles of the uterine incision Option D: : This is a babcocks forceps which is used to hold delicate structures like the fallopian tube and the appendix

Educational Objective:

  • This is a Green Armytage Forceps This is used to hold the angles and edges of the cesarean scar if there is excessive bleeding. Normally the edges are held with an allis forceps but in case of heavy bleeding, a Green Armytage forceps is better as it is less traumatic and has a wider surface area
  • Ref: DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics
explanation.ref: DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics

question: The following instrument helps in which important step during a cesarean?

explanation.short: D. Keeping the bladder away from the uterine incision

Explanation:

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which is INCORRECT regarding the following instrument?
options: It is used to remove retained products of conception
It does not have a lock to enable a faster procedure time
It can be used in places where a vacuum aspirator is not available
The end is “egg-shaped” to collect and remove tissue

explanation.short: B. It does not have a lock to enable a faster procedure time
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Let's discuss all the options:
  • Option A: It is used to remove retained products of conception: This is correct. An ovum forceps is used to remove retained products of conception in incomplete and missed abortions and also in surgical MTP Option B: I t does not have a lock to enable a faster procedure time: This is incorrect. The reasonf ro not having a lock is to avoid inadvertent injury to the uterus Option C: It can be used in places where a vacuum aspirator is not available: This is correct. Evacuation of products can be done either by a manual or electrical vacuum aspirator or by an ovum forceps Option D: The end is “egg-shaped” to collect and remove tissue: This is also correct. The end is egg shaped; hence the name ovum. The reason for this is to collect tissue within the endometrial cavity.
  • This is an Ovum forceps It is a long instrument used to evacuate products of conception in Missed abortion Incomplete abortion Inevitable abortion Surgical MTP till 12 weeks Evacuation of molar pregnancy Imp features of this instrument End is oval or egg shaped There is no lock; this is to prevent inadvertent injury to the uterus in case uterine tissue gets caught in the jaws
  • Ref: DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics
explanation.ref: DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 30-year-old woman is undergoing a cesarean section for previous 2 cesareans. She requests tubal ligation to be done also. Which of the following instruments will you ask the OT assistant to keep ready along with the cesarean instrument set?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • As the tubal ligation will be done along with the cesarean section, a Pomeroy’s ligation will be done and hence Babcock forceps is used
  • Option A: Falope ring: This is an inert, radio-opaque silicone band that is applied to a looped section of the fallopian tube with a laprosator Option B: Laparoscator is the laparoscopic instrument used to apply the falope ring Option D: Filshie clip: This is a silicone lined titanium occlusive device used to clip the fallopian tubes in laparoscopic tubal ligation

Educational Objective:

  • This is a Babcocks forceps. It is used to hold delicate structures like the fallopian tube. This is how it looks like:
  • Ref: DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics
explanation.ref: DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 23-year primi presents with watery vaginal discharge which wet her clothes at 32 weeks pregnancy. There is no abdominal pain and she appreciates fetal movements well. Abdominal exam reveals a 32 weeks uterus with a live fetus in cephalic presentation. Which instrument will you use to confirm if the patient has ruptured her membranes?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Preterm Prelabor Rupture of Membranes (PPROM) presents as sudden vaginal watery discharge To confirm PPROM or PROM, a speculum exam is done Ideally, a Sim’s speculum is inserted into the vagina and pooling of liquor on the blade of the speculum confirms the diagnosis
  • Option A: Cuscos speculum: This can be used to look for vaginal leaking but the preferred instrument is the Sims speculum as pooling of fluid can be seen in the central groove of the speculum Option C: Fetoscope: This is used to auscultate the fetal heart rate Option D: Hysteroscope: This is a scope to look inside the uterine cavity and is used for diagnostic procedures like AUB and operative procedures like Ashermam syndrome or hysteroscopic polypectomy. It is contra-indicated in pregnancy

Educational Objective:

  • A Sims speculum is the preferred instrument used to look for pooling of liquor in a patient with suspected PROM or PPROM
  • Ref: DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics
explanation.ref: DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A woman is undergoing a surgical procedure to remove products of conception following an incomplete abortion. The following instrument is used. What sign will tell you that the procedure is complete?
options: A grating sensation on the anterior uterine wall
A grating sensation on the posterior uterine wall
A grating sensation on the lateral uterine walls
A grating sensation on all 4 uterine walls

explanation.short: D. A grating sensation on all 4 uterine walls
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • This is an instrument which is used to curette the endometrium It is used following a suction evacuation after suctioning out the products or after removing them with an ovum forceps to ensure everything has been removed It is also used in gyn procedures like a dilatation and curettage to get an endometrial biopsy It has 2 ends Blunt: used in obstetric procedures Sharp: Used in gyn procedures Procedure is complete if Grating on all 4 walls Presence of air bubbles No bleeding
  • Ref: DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics
explanation.ref: DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: True about breech presentation?
options: Incidence is about 7% at term pregnancy
External cephalic version increases incidence of breech presentation

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. More in pelvic anomalies

Educational Objective:

  • The incidence of breech at term is 3-4% (At 28 weeks it is 25%) Etiology of Breech Presentation Prematurity: MC cause Factor preventing spontaneous version: Breech with extended legs, twins, Oligohydramnios; Congenital malformation of uterus such as septate or bicornuate uterus; Short cord & IUD of the fetus Favourable adaptation: Hydrocephalous; Placenta previa Contracted pelvis &Cornu-fundal attachment of the placenta Undue mobility of fetus: Hydramnios & Multipara with lax abdominal wall Fetal abnormalities: Trisomy 13, 18, 21; Anencephaly &myotonic dystrophy due to alternation of fetal muscular tone & mobility External Cephalic Version It has been considered from 36 weeks Success rate of version is about 50-60% Benefit Reduction in the incidence of breech presentation at term Reduction in the incidence of breech delivery & associated complication. Reduction in the incidence of caesarean section

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: In the pelvic inlet, which is the shortest anteroposterior diameter?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Obstetric conjugate

Educational Objective:

  • Diameters of the Pelvic Inlet
  • True (anatomical) conjugate (11 cm): Extends from uppermost margin of symphysis pubis to the sacral promontory. Obstetric conjugate (10 cm): It is the shortest AP diameter of the pelvic inlet and hence obstetrically most important. It is measured from the middle of sacral promontory to symphysis pubis. It cannot be measured directly on clinical examination. It is calculated by subtracting 1.5 cm from diagonal conjugate. Diagonal conjugate (12.5 cm): It is measured from tip of sacral promontory to the lower margin of pubic symphysis. It is the only diameter that can be assessed clinically.
  • Transverse Diameter (13 cm)
  • Distance between the farthest two points on the iliopectineal line. It is the largest diameter of pelvic inlet
  • Oblique Diameters (12 cm)
  • Right oblique diameter: Passes from right sacroiliac joint to left ilio-pubiceminence Left oblique diameter: Passes from left sacroiliac joint to right ilio-pubic eminence
  • Ref: Page no 92 Textbook of obstetrics by JB Sharma
explanation.ref: Page no 92 Textbook of obstetrics by JB Sharma

question: Engagement is defined as:

When the largest transverse diameter of the fetal head, the bitemporal diameter enters the pelvic mid cavity

question: If the fetus enters the pelvis with its sagittal suture in the transverse diameter of the pelvis, internal rotation will happen by how many degrees?

Educational Objective:

  • In left or right occipito anterior positions, the internal rotation will be by 45˚, so that the occiput comes to lie anteriorly In left or right occipito transverse, the internal rotation will be by 90˚, so that the occiput comes to lie anteriorly In right or right occipito posterior positions, there could either be Internal rotation anteriorly by 135˚ (long arc rotation), so that the occiput comes to lie anteriorly OR Internal rotation anteriorly by 135˚ (long arc rotation), so that the occiput comes to lie in the antero-posterior diameter OR Internal rotation posteriorly by 45˚ (short arc rotation), so that the occiput comes to lie in the posteriorly

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which is Not an indication for induction of labor?
options: A primigravida at 38 weeks with Rh negative pregnancy with ICT negative
A G2A1 at 37 weeks with preeclampsia

explanation.detail:

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 32-year G2 presents to the antenatal OPD. Her period of gestation is 40 weeks. She is planned for induction of labor. On a vaginal exam, the cervix is 2 cm dilated, 1 cm long, soft inconsistency and mid-position. The station of the fetal head is at -1. She asks if induction of labor will be successful? What will you tell her?
options: She will not respond favorably to induction of labor and will require pre-induction cervical ripening
She will not respond favorably to induction and LSCS should be done
She will respond favorably to induction of labor
The given information is insufficient to make a conclusion

explanation.short: C. She will respond favorably to induction of labor
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. She will respond favorably to induction of labor

Educational Objective:

  • The Bishop score is as shown in the table. Each component is scored from 0 to 3 The highest score is 13 and the lowest is 0 A favourable score is > 6. This means the patient will respond favorably to methods of induction of labor.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: According to WHO guidelines on management of labor, which of the following is true about management of second stage of labor?
options: Episiotomy should be performed as a routine
A warm cloth should be applied to the perineum to prevent trauma
Delivery should be ideally performed in a lithotomy position

explanation.short: C. A warm cloth should be applied to the perineum to prevent trauma
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. A warm cloth should be applied to the perineum to prevent trauma

Educational Objective:

  • As per WHO recommendations of 2018,
  • As per WHO guidelines on intrapartum care for a positive birth experience (2018), these are the recommendations in 2nd stage
  • Definition and duration of the second stage of labor: The second stage is the period of time between full cervical dilatation and birth of the baby, during which the woman has an involuntary urge to bear down, as a result of expulsive uterine contractions. In first labors, birth is usually completed within 3 hours whereas in subsequent labors, birth is usually completed within 2 hours. Recommended Birth position (for women with or without epidural analgesia) For women without epidural analgesia, encouraging the adoption of a birth position of the individual woman’s choice, including upright positions, is recommended. Method of pushing Women in the expulsive phase of the second stage of labor should be encouraged and supported to follow their own urge to push. Method of pushing (for women with epidural analgesia) For women with epidural analgesia in the second stage of labor, delaying pushing for one to two hours after full dilatation or until the woman regains the sensory urge to bear down is recommended in the context where resources are available for longer stay in second stage and perinatal hypoxia can be adequately assessed and managed. Techniques for preventing perineal trauma. For women in the second stage of labor, techniques to reduce perineal trauma and facilitate spontaneous birth (including perineal massage, warm compresses and a “hands on” guarding of the perineum) are recommended, based on a woman’s preferences and available options. Episiotomy policy Routine or liberal use of episiotomy is not recommended for women undergoing spontaneous vaginal birth. Fundal pressure Application of manual fundal pressure to facilitate childbirth during the second stage of labor is not recommended.
  • In brief in 2nd stage of labor (WHO guidelines 2018)
  • Duration of 2nd stage in primi – 3h, multi – 2h Birthing position of choice Encouraged to follow their own urge to push Reduce perineal trauma by – hands on guarding/ warm compress/ perineal massage
  • Routine episiotomy Fundal pressure
  • Ref: WHO recommendations: Intrapartum care for a positive childbirth experience (Feb 2018)
explanation.ref: WHO recommendations: Intrapartum care for a positive childbirth experience (Feb 2018)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A midwife at a PHC is monitoring pregnancy and maintaining the WHO modified partograph of pregnancy progression. At how much cervical dilation should the partograph plotting be started?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. 4 cm

Educational Objective:

  • Partograph is a graphical representation of progress of labor, which is depicted using cervical dilatation and descent of head against the duration of labor. It is used for early detection of abnormal labor patterns for timely intervention. In a partograph the active phase of labor starts when the cervix is 4 cm dilated. In active phase, cervix should dilate at the rate of 1 cm/hr. (Alert Line) Partograph has two lines for assessment of progression of dilation Alert line: Alert line starts at 4 cm cervical dilation & ends with full dilation of the cervix (10 cm) in 7 hours (1 cm/hour dilation rate) A ction line: Action line is drawn four hours to the right of the alert line. If action line is crossed while plotting the progression, it indicates need for urgent intervention.
  • Remember that in the modified partograph, plotting starts at 4 cm onward, and the 1st mark is always put on the alert line If asked, when does plotting start in the Labor care guide (Next generation partograph), then the answer is 5cm
  • Ref: Page no 212, Textbook of Obstetrics by JB Sharma 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page no 212, Textbook of Obstetrics by JB Sharma 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All are components of active management of the third stage of labor except?
options: Uterotonic agent within 1 minute of birth

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Early cord clamping

Educational Objective:

  • Active Management of 3rd Stage of Labor
  • Described by the WHO in 2007 Involves mainly 3 basic steps. Administering a uterotonic Controlled cord traction Uterine massage AMTSL prevents atonic PPH. In 2012, the WHO issued new guidelines for AMTSL which essentially emphasized the main 3 things but with additional information which was Oxytocin is the uterotonic of choice for AMTSL (10 IU IM/IV) Do Controlled cord traction only if a skilled birth attendant is present. Sustained uterine massage is not recommended for all women who’ve received uterotonic; instead; postpartum uterine tone assessment for early identification of uterine atony is done. Delayed cord clamping (performed after 1 to 3 minutes after birth) is recommended for all births while initiating simultaneous essential newborn care (This is not to prevent atonic PPH but was introduced along with AMTSL to ensure it is followed) In 2018, the WHO issued further guidelines on which other uterotonics can be used for AMTSL. These include: Oxytocin (10 IU IM/IV) Carbetocin (100 mcg IM/IV) Misoprostol (400 OR 600 mcg PO – to be used where Skilled birth attendant isn’t available) Ergometrine/methylergometrine (200 mcg IV EXCEPT in hypertensive women)) Oxytocin and ergometrine fixed-dose combination (5 IU/500 µg, IM) Injectable prostaglandins (carboprost or sulprostone) are not recommended for the prevention of PPH AMTSL decreases Incidence of PPH. Length of third stage of labor. Percentage of third stages of labor lasting longer than 30 minutes. Need for blood transfusion. Need for uterotonic drugs to manage PPH
  • Ref: Williams Textbook of Obstetrics 25th edition pg 438
explanation.ref: Williams Textbook of Obstetrics 25 th edition pg 438

  • First degree: Injury to perineal skin Second degree: Injury to perineum involving perineal body (muscles) but not involving the anal sphincter. Third degree: Injury to perineum, involving the anal sphincter complex (both the external and internal). 3a: if half thickness external anal sphincter involved. 3b: if full thickness external anal sphincter involved. 3c: if internal anal sphincter involved. Fourth degree: Injury to perineum involving the anal sphincter complex (external anal sphincter and internal anal sphincter) and anal epithelium.
