subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Mrs. Shiny, a G2P1L1 presented to the hospital in labor pains. On examination she had 3 uterine contractions of 20 seconds in 10 minutes, Cervical dilation was 6 cm and HR 145 bpm. What is the stage of labor?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Stage I

Educational Objective:

  • Uterine contractions which bring about cervical effacement and dilatation.
  • From 0 cm to 10 cm dilatation Divided into latent phase and active phase. Latent phase: The point at which mother perceives regular contractions. Active phase: Cervical dilatation of 4cm (as per the WHO modified partograph) 5 cm (As per the new WHO labor care guide) 6 cm (As per ACOG).
  • Complete cervical dilatation with active expulsive efforts by the mother
  • Delivery of placenta and membranes
  • “The Golden hour” One hour after delivery of baby. The mother should be monitored for signs of post-partum hemorrhage.
  • Ref: Page no 227, Textbook of Obstetrics by JB Sharma, 3rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Labor is said to be prolonged when the first and second stage extend arbitrarily beyond?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. 18 hours

Educational Objective:

  • Two phases of cervical dilatation are defined: Latent phase Active phase The latent phase corresponds to the preparatory divisions, and the active phase, to the dilatational division. Friedman subdivided the active phase into the: Acceleration phase Phase of maximum slope Deceleration phase Latent phase The onset of latent, as defined by Friedman (1972), is the point at which the mother perceives regular contractions. The latent phase for most women ends at between 3 and 5 cm of dilatation. Prolonged latent phase Friedman and Sachtleben (1963) defined this by a latent phase exceeding 20 hours in the nullipara and 14 hours in the multipara. Active labor Cervical dilatation of 3 to 5 cm or more, in the presence of uterine contractions, can be taken to reliably represent the threshold for active labor Protraction as a slow rate of cervical dilatation or descent: For nulliparas was less than 1.2 cm dilatation per hour or less than 1 cm descent per hour For multiparas, protraction was defined as less than 1.5 cm dilatation per hour or less than 2 cm descent per hour.
  • This stage begins when cervical dilatation is complete and ends with fetal delivery The median duration it is highly variable but is approximately: 50 minutes for nulliparas 20 minutes for multiparas The mean length of first-and second - stage labor was approximately: 9 hours in nulliparas women were a mean of 6 hours with a 95th percentile maximum was 18.5 hours. Corresponding times for multiparas women were a mean of 6 hours with a 95th percentile maximum of 13.5 hours
  • Labor is said to be prolonged when the combined duration of first and second stage is more than 18 hours Causes of prolonged labor: Abnormal uterine action Malposition Malpresentation Macrosomia Fetal malformation Contracted pelvis. Cephalopelvic disproportion Prolonged latent phase - in prime more than 20 hours and in multi more than 14 hours Prolonged active phase - this is the most common. Cervical dilatation less than 1.2 cm/hour in nullipara and less than 1.5 cm/hour in multipara Secondary arrest of dilatation - there is arrest in cervical dilatation for at least 2 hours Arrest of descent - there is arrest of descent of fetal head for at least one hour
  • Ref: Williams Textbook of Obstetrics 25th edition Pg 443,444
explanation.ref: Williams Textbook of Obstetrics 25 th edition Pg 443,444

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 22-year primigravida is in labor and on examination, the cervix is 2cm dilated. The fetal heart rate is 148bpm and rises to 156 bpm for 20 seconds then falls to 142 bpm. This type of presentation is?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. It’s a normal pattern

Educational Objective:

  • Baseline FHR — 110-150 bpm. Baseline variability — 5-25 bpm Acceleration — 2 in 20 min Deceleration — None or early Baseline FHR is the mean level of FHR between the peaks and the depressions in beats per minute (bpm) Baseline variability is the oscillation of baseline FHR excluding the accelerations and decelerations. A baseline variability of 5-25 bpm is a sign of fetal wellbeing. Accelerations are increase in FHR by 15 bpm or more lasting for at least 15 secs. Denotes healthy fetus. Deceleration is decrease in FHR below the baseline by 15 bpm or more. Three basic types of decelerations are observed:
  • Non-stress test (NST): continuous electronic monitoring of fetal heart
  • Reactive (Reassuring): 2 or more contractions of more than 15 bpm above the baseline and longer than 15seconds in duration are present in a 20-minute observation. Non-Reactive (Non-Reassuring): absence of fetal reactivity, Perinatal death rate of 40/1000.
  • Ref: Page no 1138, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1138, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which are features of false labor? Steady intensity of pain Cervical dilation Discomfort is in the back and abdomen Intervals remain long Discomfort usually is relieved by sedation

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. A, D, E

Explanation:

  • Characteristic of True & False Labor
  • Contractions occur at regular intervals. Intervals gradually shorten. Intensity gradually increases. Discomfort is in the back and abdomen. Cervix dilates. Discomfort is not stopped by sedation.
  • Contractions occur at irregular intervals. Intervals remain long. Intensity remains unchanged. Discomfort is chiefly in the lower abdomen. Cervix does not dilate. Discomfort usually is relieved by sedation.
  • Thus A, D and E are seen in false labor correct answer is option B) A, D, E

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page no 212, Textbook of Obstetrics by JB Sharma 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page no 212, Textbook of Obstetrics by JB Sharma 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A midwife at a PHC is monitoring pregnancy and maintaining the partograph of progression of labor of a 22y primigravida. At how much cervical dilation should the WHO modified partograph plotting be started?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. 4 cm

Educational Objective:

  • The WHO modified Partograph is a graphical representation of progress of labour, which is depicted using cervical dilatation and descent of head against the duration of labour. It is used for early detection of abnormal labour patterns for timely intervention. In a partograph the active phase of labour starts when the cervix is 4 cm dilated. In active phase, cervix should dilate at the rate of 1 cm/hr. Partograph has two lines for assessment of progression of dilation. Alert line: Alert line starts at 4 cm cervical dilation & ends with full dilation of the cervix (10 cm) in 7 hours (1 cm/hour dilation rate) Action line: Action line is drawn four hours to the right of the alert line. If action line is crossed while plotting the progression, it indicates need for urgent intervention.
  • The WHO has now introduced the WHO Labor care guide in 2018 where several changes have been made. This is also called the “Next generation partograph”. In this, the active labor starts at 5cm.

  • Eclampsia & severe preeclampsia As a tocolytic in preterm labor For neuroprotection in very preterm
  • Ref: page no 439, Textbook of Obstetrics, Dr JB Sharma 1st edition
explanation.ref: page no 439, Textbook of Obstetrics, Dr JB Sharma 1 st edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: To define preeclampsia, the proteinuria defined is?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. ≥ 300 mg/ 24 h

Educational Objective:

  • The diagnosis of preeclampsia involves assessing for the presence of hypertension and significant proteinuria, defined as the excretion of 300 mg or more of protein in a 24-hour urine collection.
  • Ref: Page no 712, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 712, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All are true regarding gestational hypertension except?
options: The diagnosis is made if BP > 140/90 mmHg after 20 weeks in the absence of proteinuria
10% of eclamptic seizures develop in women with gestational hypertension
The diagnosis is made only if the blood pressure does not return to normal after 12 weeks postpartum
It is also known as transient hypertension as the BP returns to normal postpartum

explanation.short: C. The diagnosis is made only if the blood pressure does not return to normal after 12 weeks postpartum
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. The diagnosis is made only if the blood pressure does not return to normal after 12 weeks postpartum

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: page no 711, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: page no 711, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All are true regarding predictive tests for preeclampsia except?
options: An exaggerated diastolic notch is seen beyond 24 weeks on uterine artery Doppler in women at risk for developing pre-eclampsia.
A drop in levels of PAPP-A and PlGF is a predictor for preeclampsia.
Angiogenic factors sFlt-1 and s-Eng are decreased in women who are at risk of developing preeclampsia
Cell free DNA is increased in women who are at risk of developing preeclampsia due to accelerated apoptosis of cytotrophoblasts

explanation.short: C. Angiogenic factors sFlt-1 and s-Eng are decreased in women who are at risk of developing preeclampsia
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Angiogenic factors sFlt-1 and s-Eng are decreased in women who are at risk of developing preeclampsia

Educational Objective:

  • Biophysical markers : Raised PI in the uterine Artery (11 – 14 weeks); notching of uterine Artery waveform (mid trimester). Biochemical markers:
  • S-Flt-1 and s-Eng are Anti-angiogenic factors (Not angiogenic) Roll over test : 28- 32 weeks: BP is measured with patient on her side first and then she is asked to roll on her back and BP is checked again -an increase of 20mmHg in diastolic BP from lateral to supine position is a positive test – 30% of such women will develop pre-eclampsia in the future. This test is not done anymore. Angiotensin II sensitivity test : Rise in BP with Angiotensin II infusion.
  • Ref: Page no 1818, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1818, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 35-year primigravida is worried about her risk of developing preeclampsia. What intervention would help reduce the risk in her?
options: Calcium supplementation started prior to 12 weeks
Aspirin 150 mg/ day prior to 16 weeks
Antioxidants – ascorbic acid and α-tocopherol prior to 12 weeks

explanation.short: C. Aspirin 150 mg/ day prior to 16 weeks
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Aspirin 150 mg/ day prior to 16 weeks

Educational Objective:

  • Low-dose aspirin started before 16 weeks of gestation is a recommended intervention to reduce the risk of developing preeclampsia, particularly for women at high risk.
  • Ref: Page no 1825, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1825, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Patellar reflexes disappear when the plasma magnesium level reaches:

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. 10 mEq/L

Educational Objective:

  • Monitoring for magnesium toxicity is crucial when administering magnesium sulfate, especially in obstetric settings for conditions like preeclampsia or eclampsia. The loss of deep tendon reflexes, such as the patellar reflex, is the first clinical sign and usually occurs at plasma magnesium levels around 10 mEq/L.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 25-year-old P2L2 had just delivered following induction for severe preeclampsia. Her BP on the 2 nd postnatal day is 160/100mmHg. Which anti-hypertensive should be avoided in a breastfeeding mother?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Amlodipine

Educational Objective:

  • Antihypertensive agents with no known adverse effects on infants receiving breast milk: Mnemonic: ECLAM-N Enalapril Captopril Labetalol Atenolol Metoprolol Nifedipine
  • Ref: Smith, M , Waugh, J, Nelson‐Piercy, C Management of postpartum hypertension The Obstetrician & Gynecologist 2013; 15: 45– 50
explanation.ref: Smith, M , Waugh, J, Nelson‐Piercy, C Management of postpartum hypertension The Obstetrician & Gynecologist 2013; 15: 45– 50

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 37-year lady with chronic hypertension visits the gynecologist for pre-conceptional counselling. She is currently taking Telmisartan for her hypertension. Which is the correct statement?
options: Stop Telmisartan 3 months prior to conception and re-start it at the 3rd month of pregnancy
Stop Telmisartan 3 months pre-conceptionally and start Labetalol
Continue Telmisartan and add Labetalol if BP is uncontrolled in pregnancy

explanation.short: B. Stop Telmisartan 3 months pre-conceptionally and start Labetalol
explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Stop Telmisartan 3 months pre-conceptionally and start Labetalol

Educational Objective:

  • ACE inhibitors and Angiotensin Receptor Blockers (like Telmisartan) cause fetal renal hypoplasia and lead to early onset oligohydramnios as the fetal kidneys are nor formed and are therefore contra-indicated in pregnancy.
  • Ref: Page no 438, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 438, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Anti-hypertensives contra-indicated in pregnancy are all except?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Alpha-methyldopa

Educational Objective:

  • The anti-hypertensive drug of choice in pregnancy is labetalol Alpha-methyldopa is considered safe and is one of the preferred anti hypertensives in pregnancy. However, ACE inhibitors (like captopril and enalapril) and ARBs (like losartan) are contraindicated due to their potential adverse effects on the developing fetus.
  • Ref: Page no 438, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 438, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All are regimes of MgSO4 for treatment of eclampsia except?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Magpie regime

Educational Objective:

  • The key educational takeaway is that while there are several recognized regimens for the administration of magnesium sulfate in the management of eclampsia (like the Dhaka, Sibai, and Zuspan regimens), the "Magpie regime" is not one of them. Instead, the Magpie Trial was a significant research study that helped establish the effectiveness of magnesium sulfate in this context.
  • Ref: page no 439, Textbook of Obstetrics, Dr JB Sharma 1st edition
explanation.ref: page no 439, Textbook of Obstetrics, Dr JB Sharma 1 st edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All the following precautions are taken in labor in a woman with pre-eclampsia except?
options: Cut short the 2nd stage of labor
Prophylactic methylergometrine at the delivery of anterior shoulder

explanation.short: B. Prophylactic methylergometrine at the delivery of anterior shoulder
explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Prophylactic methylergometrine at the delivery of anterior shoulder

Educational Objective:

  • Precautions to be taken in labor in women with pre-eclampsia
  • Restrict IV fluids to avoid pulmonary edema Cut short 2nd stage of labor using instrumental delivery Avoid methylergometrine at delivery of anterior shoulder Active management of 3rd stage of labor including Oxytocin 10IU IM
  • Ref: page no 440, Textbook of Obstetrics, Dr JB Sharma 1st edition
explanation.ref: page no 440, Textbook of Obstetrics, Dr JB Sharma 1 st edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 32-year-old primigravida at 39 weeks has a BP recording of 150/100 which when repeated is the same. Her baseline BP during earlier visits was 120/80 mmHg. She does not complain of headache/ blurring of vision or epigastric pain. Abdominal exam reveals a term uterus with cephalic presentation and FHR of 140 bpm. Urine albumin is nil. Which is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Gestational hypertension

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: page 1780, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: page 1780, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All of the following indicate superimposed pre-eclampsia in a pregnant female of chronic hypertension except:
options: Increase in systolic BP by 30mm Hg and diastolic by 15 mm Hg

explanation.short: C. Increase in systolic BP by 30mm Hg and diastolic by 15 mm Hg
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Increase in systolic BP by 30mm Hg and diastolic by 15 mm Hg

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page no 1780, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following is not a part of HELLP syndrome?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Retroplacental hemorrhage

Educational Objective:

  • It is an acronym for Hemolysis (H), Elevated liver enzymes (EL) and Low Platelet count (LP) of < 1,00,000/mm 3 It is a rare complication of pregnancy induced hypertension. It may be associated even with mild hypertension and proteinuria
  • Ref: Page no 1849, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1849, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A multiparous patient who did not attend any antenatal visit and came to causality with complaint of bleeding. On examination uterine tone was increased with BP>144/92 mmHg. Which of the following tests supports the diagnosis of preeclampsia?
options: Total protein of 252mg in a 24-hour urine collection

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Creatinine levels 1.24mg/dl

Educational Objective:

  • Hypertension and proteinuria or In absence of proteinuria, new-onset hypertension with the new onset of any of the following Thrombocytopenia: Platelets <100 x 10 9 /L Renal insufficiency: serum creatinine >1.1 mg/dl or doubling of serum creatinine in the absence of other renal disease Impaired liver function: Elevated blood concentrations of liver transaminases to twice normal concentration Pulmonary edema Neuro: Unexplained new-onset headache unresponsive to medication (without an alternative diagnosis) or visual symptoms
  • Preeclampsia with severe features
  • Preeclampsia diagnosis, above, with any of the following Severe hypertension On two occasions at least 4 hours apart while on bed rest (unless already on antihypertensive therapy) Thrombocytopenia: Platelets <100 x 10 9 /L Impaired liver function (without an alternative diagnosis): Elevated liver transaminases greater than twice upper limit of normal or severe persistent right upper quadrant or epigastric pain not responsive to medications Progressive renal insufficiency: serum creatinine >1.1 mg/dl or doubling of serum creatinine in the absence of other renal disease Pulmonary edema Neuro: Unexplained new-onset headache unresponsive to medication (without an alternative diagnosis) or visual symptoms
  • Clinically evident edema Rapid weight gain Massive proteinuria Does not qualify as a ‘severe feature’ Fetal growth restriction Expectant management ‘may be reasonable’ if mother and fetus appear stable and no other clinical indication is present that would indicate the need for early delivery ACOG states that while it is important to monitor fetal status, FGR in the setting of all other fetal assessment being within normal limits (e.g., AFV, Doppler) Uric acid Hyperuricemia in hypertensive pregnancy is not a diagnostic marker, but is an important finding as a risk factor for adverse maternal and fetal outcomes Small for gestational age (SGA) infant Prematurity Risk for adverse maternal outcomes if include patients with preeclampsia and risks increase with increasing concentration of uric acid May be warranted in the setting of ‘diagnostic dilemmas’ such as diagnosing superimposed preeclampsia in the setting of chronic hypertension
explanation.ref: https://www.preeclampsia.org/frontend/assets/img/advocacy_resource/Gestational_Hypertension_and_Preeclampsia_ACOG_Practice_Bulletin,_Number_222_1605448006.pdf

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 36-year primigravida presents with seizures to the emergency. On examination, she is 36 weeks pregnant with BP 150/100mmHg. She is given loading dose of MGSO4 as per Pritchards regime but has another seizure. Which of the following should be done next?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Additional magnesium sulphate

Educational Objective:

  • Short acting benzodiazepines like midazolam may be used to control the ongoing seizure. Consider thiopental if the seizures are refractory. Ensure that the patient is also receiving the maintenance dose of magnesium sulfate to prevent further seizures.
  • Ref: Page no 1862, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1862, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Preeclampsia is a marker for all of the following morbidities later in life except?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Non-Alcoholic Steatohepatitis

Educational Objective:

  • LONG TERM CONSEQUENCES ASSOCIATED WITH PREECLAMPSIA:
  • The earlier preeclampsia is diagnosed during the index pregnancy, the greater the likelihood of recurrence. Nulliparous diagnosed with preeclampsia before 30 weeks have a recurrence risk as high as 40 percent during a subsequent pregnancy. Women with HELLP have risk of 5 to 26 percent risk of recurrence in subsequent pregnancy 20 percent of preeclampsia women had hypertension at interval of 2 years. Ischemic heart disease Stroke Increased risk of chronic hypertension and diabetes and even metabolic syndrome 4-fold increased risk of chronic renal disease Increased risk of impaired cognitive functioning and lower vision related quality of life
  • Ref: Page 1880, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page 1880, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A Primigravida presents at 32 weeks with pre-eclampsia. She complains of decreased fetal movements. An umbilical artery Doppler is done which is as shown in the image. What is the next best step in management?

explanation.detail: Ans. C. Immediate delivery

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following are feature(s) of magnesium toxicity in pregnancy? Loss of deep tendon reflex Decreased respiratory rate Decreased urine output Hypertension Increased heart rate

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. a, b

Educational Objective:

  • Features of Magnesium Toxicity:
  • Loss of deep tendon reflex (Depression of patellar reflex is the earliest sign of patellar toxicity): Earliest Sign Decreased respiratory rate (<12 per minute) Chest pain, heart block, pulmonary edema Cardiac arrest O 2 saturation monitoring (PaO2 < 95%) Double vision Slurred speech Nausea-vomiting Muscular weakness
  • **Decreased urine output is not a feature of Mg toxicity. Renal failure can be seen in severe preeclampsia/ eclampsia and as Mg is excreted through the kidneys, decreased renal function can increase the Mg levels in the blood thereby leading to Mg toxicity.
  • Ref: Page no 1880, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1880, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 34-year Primigravida presents to the antenatal OPD. Her mother is a diabetic and she is screened for Diabetes at her 8 weeks antenatal visit. What was the most likely screening method used?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. HBA1C

Educational Objective:

  • Hemoglobin A1C (HBA1C) is the most likely screening method used for diabetes at the 8-week antenatal visit, especially when there is a family history of diabetes. It provides a longer-term view of blood sugar control.
  • Ref: Page 1108, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page 1108, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: During a routine antenatal visit, an 24-year G1 at 20 weeks gestational age undergoes a urinalysis. The dipstick done indicates the presence of glycosuria. All other parameters of the urine test are normal. Which of the following is the most likely etiology of sugar detected in the urine?
options: The patient has a urine infection
The patient’s urinalysis is consistent with normal pregnancy
The patient’s urine sample is contaminated.

explanation.short: C. The patient’s urinalysis is consistent with normal pregnancy
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. The patient’s urinalysis is consistent with normal pregnancy

Educational Objective:

  • Physiological glycosuria, a normal response to hormonal changes in pregnancy, may lead to the presence of glucose in the urine without underlying diabetes. It is essential to consider other factors, such as diabetes, urinary tract infections, and sample contamination, and perform further tests if necessary to determine the cause of glycosuria.
  • Ref: Page 66, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page 66, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: An infant was born at 36 weeks of pregnancy with a birthweight 4.5 kg as shown in the image. Which of the following conditions is associated with this presentation?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Gestational Diabetes mellitus

Educational Objective:

  • The birth of an infant with a birthweight of 4.5 kg at 36 weeks of pregnancy is suggestive of macrosomia. Macrosomia is commonly associated with maternal gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), which can lead to larger birthweights in babies.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: For diagnosis of overt gestational diabetes, the fasting blood sugar (FBS] level should be?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. ≥ 126 mg/dl

Educational Objective:

  • To diagnose overt gestational diabetes, the fasting blood sugar (FBS) level should be ≥ 126 mg/dl (milligrams per decilitre).
  • It's important to note that the diagnostic criteria for gestational diabetes may vary in different guidelines and regions. However, a fasting blood sugar level of ≥ 126 mg/dl is a commonly recognized threshold for diagnosing overt gestational diabetes.
  • Ref: Page 1108, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page 1108, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which is NOT seen in gestational diabetes mellitus?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Malformations

Educational Objective:

  • Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is typically not associated with congenital malformations GDM can lead to complications such as polyhydramnios, especially if blood sugar levels are uncontrolled during pregnancy. Obesity is a risk factor for the development of GDM and a previous history of giving birth to a macrosomic baby increases the risk of GDM in subsequent pregnancies.
  • Ref: Page no 1110, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1110, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 34-year Primi who is a known diabetic on insulin presents at 34 weeks pregnancy. On examination, the uterus is 34 weeks and FHR is 140 bpm. Her blood sugar is well controlled. At what period of gestation should her delivery be planned?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. 38 weeks

Educational Objective:

  • In Overt Diabetes (Pre-gestational diabetes) on insulin, delivery is planned at 38 weeks. Earlier delivery may be planned if there are poorly controlled sugars or non-reassuring fetal status
  • Ref: Page no 1107, Williams Obstetrics, Page no 509, Textbook of Obstetrics by JB Dutta
explanation.ref: Page no 1107, Williams Obstetrics, Page no 509, Textbook of Obstetrics by JB Dutta

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All are true regarding physiology of pregnancy except?
options: Insulin resistance is due to placental hormones
Maternal insulin can cross the placenta
The fetus starts secreting insulin at 12 weeks

explanation.short: C. Maternal insulin can cross the placenta
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Maternal insulin can cross the placenta

Educational Objective:

  • In pregnancy, there is an increase in insulin secretion due to increased insulin resistance, largely driven by placental hormones. However, maternal insulin does not cross the placenta; the fetus produces its own insulin in response to glucose that crosses from the maternal circulation.
  • Ref: Page 66, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page 66, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Identify the maternal condition that can lead to the findings in the baby as shown below

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Pre-gestational diabetes

Educational Objective:

  • Mermaid syndrome or sirenomelia is characteristically associated with overt (pre-gestational) Diabetes mellitus. The most common anomaly associated with pre-gestational diabetes mellitus in pregnancy is cardiac anomalies (VSD being the most common).
  • Ref: Page 1110, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page 1110, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Pederson’s hypothesis states that:
options: Maternal hyperglycemia leads to fetal hyperglycemia resulting in fetal hyperinsulinemia and related complications
Maternal insulin can cross the placenta resulting in neonatal hypoglycemia
Maternal hyperglycemia results in an increased incidence of congenital anomalies
Fetal hyperinsulinemia results in a lower incidence of respiratory distress syndrome

explanation.short: A. Maternal hyperglycemia leads to fetal hyperglycemia resulting in fetal hyperinsulinemia and related complications
explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Maternal hyperglycemia leads to fetal hyperglycemia resulting in fetal hyperinsulinemia and related complications.

Educational Objective:

  • Pederson's hypothesis emphasizes the direct impact of maternal hyperglycemia on the fetus, leading to fetal hyperglycemia and resultant fetal hyperinsulinemia. This cascade underscores the importance of strict glycemic control in pregnant women with diabetes to prevent fetal complications, including macrosomia, birth injuries, and neonatal hypoglycemia.
  • Ref: Page no 1102, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1102, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 36-year diabetic woman on insulin is planning conception. She is worried about the risk of fetal congenital anomalies and visits for preconception counseling. Which of the following is the correct statement?
options: Caudal regression syndrome is a commonly associated anomaly seen in diabetes
The risk of anomalies in the fetus is inversely proportional to the level of HBA1c.
Folic acid in a dose of 5 mg/day started in the preconception period will not reduce the risk of neural tube defects.
Metformin and Glyburide can be safely continued in pregnancy even though they cross the placenta.

explanation.short: D. Metformin and Glyburide can be safely continued in pregnancy even though they cross the placenta.
explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Metformin and Glyburide can be safely continued in pregnancy even though they cross the placenta.

Educational Objective:

  • While higher HbA1c levels are associated with increased risks, the use of certain oral hypoglycemic agents like metformin and glyburide is considered safe during pregnancy. Additionally, folic acid supplementation is important for reducing the risk of neural tube defects.
  • Ref: Page no 1110, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1110, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 23-year primigravida presents to the OPD at 24 weeks. She is advised to get a Glucose Challenge Test done. All are true regarding the glucose challenge test (GCT) except?
options: It is done in the fasting state
Patients with a positive GCT are subjected to a GTT
The lower the threshold taken in a GCT, the higher the sensitivity for detection of gestational diabetes mellitus

explanation.short: A. It is done in the fasting state
explanation.detail:
Ans. A. It is done in the fasting state.

Educational Objective:

  • The Glucose Challenge Test is a non-fasting, initial screening test for gestational diabetes conducted between 24 to 28 weeks of pregnancy. A positive GCT necessitates further testing with a Glucose Tolerance Test. Adjusting the threshold level of the GCT can impact its sensitivity and specificity for detecting gestational diabetes.
  • Ref: Page no 262, DC Dutta's Textbook of Obstetrics 9th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 262, DC Dutta's Textbook of Obstetrics 9 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: As per IADPSG recommendations, pre-gestational diabetes is diagnosed in the 1 st antenatal visit by all of the values given below except?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. PPBS ≥ 140 mg/dl

Educational Objective:

  • In diagnosing pre-gestational diabetes during the first antenatal visit, IADPSG guidelines include criteria such as a fasting blood sugar of ≥ 126 mg/dL, an HbA1c of ≥ 6.5%, or a random blood sugar of ≥ 200 mg/dL.
  • Ref: Page 1108, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page 1108, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Target blood glucose levels for women with gestational diabetes on treatment are:
options: FBS < 95 mg/dL, 1h PP < 140 mg/dL, 2h PP < 120 mg/dL
FBS < 90 mg/dl, 1h PP < 145 mg/ dl, 2h < 125 mg/dl
FBS < 105 mg/dl, 1h PP < 140 mg/dl, 2h PP < 120 mg/dl
FBS < 95 mg/dl, 1h PP < 120 mg/ dl, 2h PP < 140 mg/dl

explanation.short: A. FBS < 95 mg/dL, 1h PP < 140 mg/dL, 2h PP < 120 mg/dL
explanation.detail:
Ans. A.  FBS < 95 mg/dL, 1h PP < 140 mg/dL, 2h PP < 120 mg/dL

Educational Objective:

  • For the management of gestational diabetes, target blood glucose levels are set to ensure both maternal and fetal well-being. The recommended targets are: fasting blood sugar less than 95 mg/dL 1-hour postprandial less than 140 mg/dL 2-hour postprandial less than 120 mg/dL.
  • Ref: Page no 264, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics 9th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 264, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics 9 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which hypoglycemic drug cannot be used in diabetes in pregnancy?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Pioglitazone

Educational Objective:

  • When treating diabetes in pregnancy, insulin is the preferred medication due to its safety profile. Metformin and glyburide can also be used, albeit with some caution and monitoring. Pioglitazone, on the other hand, is not recommended for use during pregnancy due to the lack of sufficient safety data and potential fetal risks.
  • Ref: Page no 1110, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1110, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Elective cesarean delivery is indicated in a diabetic woman if the estimated fetal weight is

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. > 4500g

Educational Objective:

  • As per ACOG, for diabetic pregnant women, an elective cesarean delivery is recommended if the estimated fetal weight exceeds 4.5 kg. This is in contrast to non-diabetic women, where the threshold for an elective cesarean is set at an estimated fetal weight of more than 5 kg. The lower threshold for diabetic women is due to the increased risk of macrosomia and related birth complications, including shoulder dystocia.
  • Ref: Page 859, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page 859, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 32-years old G3P2L2 visits an antenatal clinic at 28 weeks. She reveals during history that her first baby weighed 4.6 kg, second baby weighed 4.8 kg, and both were delivered by C-section. Neonatal complications of an infant of diabetic mother except?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D.  Neonatal hyperglycemia

Educational Objective:

  • Neonatal complications (Seen because of Pedersen Hypothesis)
  • Hypoglycemia Respiratory distress syndrome Hyperbilirubinemia Necrotizing enterocolitis Polycythemia Hypocalcemia Hypomagnesemia Cardiomyopathy
  • Ref: Page no 1102, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1102, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: The WHO recommendation for universal iron supplementation to pregnant woman includes:
options: 100 mg elemental iron + 500 mcg folic acid OD
100 mg elemental iron + 250 mcg folic acid daily
60 mg elemental iron + 500 mcg folic acid daily
60 mg elemental iron + 400 mcg folic acid daily

explanation.short: D. 60 mg elemental iron + 400 mcg folic acid daily
explanation.detail:
Ans. D. 60 mg elemental iron + 400 mcg folic acid daily

Educational Objective:

  • Suggested scheme for daily iron and folic acid supplementation in pregnant women by the WHO

References:
explanation.ref: ↳ https://apps.who.int/iris/bitstream/handle/10665/77770/9789241501996_eng.pdf

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: The Anemia Mukt Bharat strategy comprises 6 strategies to reduce the prevalence of anemia in India. One of these is deworming of pregnant women. Deworming of pregnant women is ideally done by:
options: Tab Albendazole 400 mg in the 1st trimester
Tab Albendazole 400 mg in the 2nd trimester

explanation.short: B. Tab Albendazole 400 mg in the 2nd trimester
explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Tab Albendazole 400 mg in the 2ndtrimester

Educational Objective:

  • As per the Anemia Mukt Bharat program Pregnant women and lactating mothers are advised one dose of 400 mg Albendazole (1 tablet), after the first trimester, preferably during the second trimester. Women of reproductive age group (20 – 49) – not pregnant and not lactating: Biannual dose of 400 mg Albendazole.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A primigravida presents at 12 weeks for a booking visit. On routine testing, her Hb is 9g%. As per the center for Disease Control, the definition of anemia in pregnancy is:
options: < 11g% in the 1st and 2nd trimester and < 10.5g% in the 3rd trimester
< 11g% in the 1st and 3rd trimester and < 10.5g% in the 2nd trimester
< 11g% in the 2nd and 3rd trimester and < 10.5g% in the 1st trimester

explanation.short: B. < 11g% in the 1st and 3rd trimester and < 10.5g% in the 2nd trimester
explanation.detail:
Ans. B. < 11g% in the 1st and 3rd trimester and < 10.5g% in the 2nd trimester

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A couple went for pre-conceptional counseling and was detected to be anemic. On investigation, they were both found to have beta thalassemia minor. The chance their fetus will have thalassemia major is:

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. 25%

Educational Objective:

  • Beta Thalassemia major is an autosomal recessive condition in which both the beta globin genes are defective and no beta chains are produced If the mother has thalassemia trait (beta thalassemia minor), the father’s status is checked. If the father also has the beta thalassemia trait, there is a 25% (1 in 4 chance) the fetus has beta thalassemia major. Prenatal diagnosis is done by Chorionic villous sampling in the 1st trimester OR Amniocentesis in the 2nd trimester
  • Ref: Page no 494, Textbook of Obstetrics by JB Sharma, 1st edition
explanation.ref: Page no 494, Textbook of Obstetrics by JB Sharma, 1 st edition

Educational Objective:

  • Beta Thalassemia major is an autosomal recessive condition in which both the beta globin genes are defective and no beta chains are produced If the mother has thalassemia trait (beta thalassemia minor), the father’s status is checked. If the father also has the beta thalassemia trait, there is a 25% (1 in 4 chance) the fetus has beta thalassemia major. Prenatal diagnosis is done by Chorionic villous sampling (CVS) in the 1st trimester OR Amniocentesis in the 2nd trimester
  • Ref: Page no 494, Textbook of Obstetrics by JB Sharma, 1st edition

question: The single best test to diagnose iron deficiency anemia in pregnancy is:

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Serum ferritin

Educational Objective:

  • The single best test to diagnose iron deficiency anemia is serum ferritin Serum ferritin is a high molecular weight glycoprotein Normal concentration is 15 – 300 mcg/ L It is the single indicator of iron deficiency Levels < 30 mcg/ L show iron deficiency anemia with a 90% sensitivity and a 85% specificity It is unaffected by recent iron intake and is the first abnormal laboratory test in iron deficiency anemia

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 34y G2 is diagnosed to have anemia during her routine prenatal visits. She is started on oral iron but is unable to tolerate it. As per Anemia Mukt Bharat strategy, which parenteral preparations of iron is recommended for treatment of iron deficiency anemia?

explanation.short: C. Iron Sucrose and iron Carboxymaltose
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Iron Sucrose and Iron Carboxymaltose

Educational Objective:

  • As per Anemia Mukt Bharat strategy, the following is recommended as first line strategy for treatment of anemia
  • For mild anemia (10 – 10.9 g%) Two tablets of Iron and Folic Acid tablet (60 mg elemental Iron and 500 mcg Folic Acid) daily, orally given by the health provider during the ANC contact. Parental iron (IV Iron Sucrose or Ferric Carboxy Maltose (FCM) may be considered as the first line of management in pregnant women who are detected to be anemic late in pregnancy or in whom compliance is likely to be low (high chance of lost to follow-up) Follow up every 2 monthly Once Hb is at the normal level, continue with prophylactic dose For moderate anemia (7 – 9.9 g%) Two tablets of Iron and Folic Acid tablet (60 mg elemental Iron and 500 mcg Folic Acid) daily, orally given by the health provider during the ANC contact Parental iron (IV Iron Sucrose or FCM) may be considered as the first line of management in pregnant women who are detected to be anemic late in pregnancy or in whom compliance is likely to be low (high chance of lost to follow-up) If no improvement in haemoglobin (<1 g/dl increase) after two months of treatment, refer to First Referral Unit (FRU)/District Hospital (DH) by health provider For severe anemia (5 – 6.9 g%) Management of severe anemia in pregnant women will be done by the medical officer at PHC/CHC/FRU/DH The treatment will be done using IV Iron Sucrose/Ferric Carboxy Maltose (FCM) by the medical officer Immediate hospitalization recommended in the third trimester of pregnancy at a health facility where round-the-clock specialist care is available
explanation.ref: https://anemiamuktbharat.info/home/interventions/

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A pregnant woman in her second trimester presents with mildly elevated liver enzymes and itching over her body. She has no history of liver disease. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy

Explanation:

  • Option A: Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy: This is diagnosed by the Swansea criteria. Patients with AFLP usually present in the 3rd trimester with jaundice, abdominal pain, vomiting. There is altered liver function along with worsening renal and coagulation parameters. Hypoglycemia is typically seen. Option B: HELLP Syndrome: This is a form of severe preeclampsia so a h/o high BP would usually be mentioned. HELLP syndrome is a triad of H emolysis, E levated L iver Enzymes and L ow P latelets. Option D: Acute viral hepatitis: This can be seen in any trimester. There is a history of fever, constitutional symptoms and jaundice. Serum bilirubin is raised and AST and ALT are very highly raised.

Educational Objective:

References:

  • ↳ Reference- Page no 270, DC Dutta’s textbook of Obstetrics, 9th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 270, DC Dutta’s textbook of Obstetrics, 9 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 26-year-old pregnant woman presents with new onset hypertension, proteinuria, and elevated serum creatinine. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Preeclampsia

Explanation:

  • Option B: CKD is characterized by a gradual loss of kidney function over time. While patients with CKD can present with hypertension, proteinuria, and elevated serum creatinine, the history of pregnancy and the acute presentation make preeclampsia a more likely diagnosis in this case.
  • Option C: Acute Pyelonephritis is a bacterial infection of the kidneys that can cause flank pain, fever, nausea, vomiting, and urinary symptoms, in addition to elevated serum creatinine and sometimes proteinuria. However, the key distinguishing feature of acute pyelonephritis is usually a fever and signs of infection, which are not mentioned in the scenario.
  • Option D: Glomerulonephritis is an inflammation of the glomeruli in the kidneys. It can cause hypertension, proteinuria, and elevated serum creatinine. However, in the context of pregnancy, especially without a prior history of renal disease, preeclampsia is a more common cause of these symptoms.

Educational Objective:

  • Preeclampsia is defined as Hypertension with Proteinuria in pregnancy
  • Blood pressure criteria includes systolic BP ≥ 140 mmHg or diastolic BP ≥ 90mmHg or both on 2 occasions at least 4h apart after 20 weeks gestation with previously normal BP Proteinuria is defined as a 24h urine collection > 300mg protein OR single voided urine protein/ creatinine ration ≥ 0.3 Or dipstick reading of 2 + (Recent ACOG guidelines).
  • Ref: Williams Textbook of Obstetrics 25th edition pg 720
explanation.ref: Williams Textbook of Obstetrics 25 th edition pg 720

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 28-year-old pregnant woman in her second trimester presents with jaundice, fatigue, and elevated liver enzymes. She reports a recent history of consuming seafood at a local restaurant. Which type of viral hepatitis is most likely?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Hepatitis A

Explanation:

  • Option B, C and D: Hepatitis B and C are more commonly transmitted through blood and bodily fluids, and while they can cause similar symptoms, the patient's history makes them less likely in this case. Hepatitis E, also transmitted feco-orally and linked to contaminated water, is a consideration, but Hepatitis A is more commonly associated with seafood consumption
  • Pregnant women, especially those in the second or third trimester, are at a higher risk of developing severe disease if they contract Hepatitis E. The infection can lead to fulminant hepatic failure, a life-threatening condition characterized by rapid deterioration of liver function. The mortality rate for pregnant women with Hepatitis E, particularly in the third trimester, can be significantly higher than in the general population. Hepatitis E is primarily transmitted through the fecal-oral route, typically via contaminated water.

Educational Objective:

  • Hepatitis A is typically transmitted via the fecal-oral route, often through contaminated food or water. The recent history of consuming seafood, a common vehicle for Hepatitis A transmission, along with the presentation of jaundice, fatigue, and elevated liver enzymes, strongly suggests Hepatitis
  • Ref: Williams Textbook of Obstetrics 25th edition Pg 1062
explanation.ref: Williams Textbook of Obstetrics 25 th edition Pg 1062

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following is a recommended management strategy for a pregnant woman with chronic kidney disease (CKD)?
options: Close monitoring of renal function and blood pressure

explanation.short: C. Close monitoring of renal function and blood pressure
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Close monitoring of renal function and blood pressure

Explanation:

  • Option A: Discontinuation of all antihypertensive medications is not recommended. Many women with CKD require antihypertensive medications to control their blood pressure, which is crucial in preventing further renal damage and managing complications like preeclampsia. However, the choice of antihypertensive medication may need to be adjusted due to pregnancy, as some medications are contraindicated or not preferred during pregnancy.
  • Option B: Strict protein restriction: This approach is controversial and not generally recommended during pregnancy. While protein restriction can sometimes be beneficial in non-pregnant CKD patients to slow disease progression, during pregnancy, adequate protein intake is essential for fetal growth and development. Dietary modifications in pregnant CKD patients should be individualized and carefully balanced.
  • Option D: Starting or continuing dialysis in a pregnant woman with CKD depends on the severity of the renal impairment and is not routinely initiated based solely on the presence of CKD. Dialysis is typically reserved for those with advanced kidney failure or when there are indications such as refractory fluid overload, severe uremia, or life-threatening electrolyte imbalances.

Educational Objective:

  • Prenatal care for women with CKD incorporates several important aspects.
  • Frequent monitoring of blood pressure is paramount, and serum creatinine levels and 24-hour protein excretion are quantified as indicated. Bacteriuria is treated to lower the risk of pyelonephritis and further nephron loss. Protein-restricted diets are not recommended. In some women with anemia from chronic renal insufficiency, a response is seen with recombinant erythropoietin. Serial sonography is performed to follow fetal growth. The differentiation between worsening hypertension and superimposed pre-eclampsia is problematic. Angiogenic biomarkers placental growth factor (PlGF) and its soluble receptor, soluble fms-like tyrosine kinase 1 (sFlt-1), may be useful to separate chronic from gestational hypertension
  • Ref: Williams Textbook of Obstetrics 26th edition pg 2614
explanation.ref: Williams Textbook of Obstetrics 26 th edition pg 2614

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A pregnant woman with no prior history of renal disease develops sudden onset of flank pain and hematuria in her second trimester. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Ans.  B. Renal calculus

Explanation:

  • Option A: Acute cystitis is an infection of the bladder that often presents with symptoms like urinary frequency, urgency, dysuria (painful urination), and suprapubic pain. Hematuria can sometimes be present, but acute cystitis typically does not cause flank pain. Flank pain is more characteristic of upper urinary tract infections or obstructions, such as pyelonephritis or renal stones.
  • Option C: Acute Pyelonephritis is a bacterial infection of the kidneys and is a more severe form of urinary tract infection. While it can cause flank pain and hematuria, it typically also presents with fever, chills, and other systemic symptoms such as nausea and vomiting. The sudden onset of severe pain is more characteristic of renal calculus than acute pyelonephritis, which often has a more gradual onset.
  • Option D: Glomerulonephritis is an inflammation of the glomeruli in the kidneys. It can present with hematuria, proteinuria, and sometimes hypertension. However, it is less likely to cause sudden onset flank pain.

Educational Objective:

  • Sudden onset flank pain and hematuria in a pregnant woman with no prior renal disease history are suggestive of a renal calculus (kidney stone). Pregnancy increases the risk of kidney stones due to changes in urinary tract physiology.
  • Ref: Williams Textbook of Obstetrics 25th edition pg1030
explanation.ref: Williams Textbook of Obstetrics 25 th edition pg1030

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A pregnant woman with a history of congenital heart disease presents for her first prenatal visit. Which of the following is the most important initial evaluation?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Echocardiogram

Explanation:

  • Option A: 24-hour Holter Monitoring records the electrical activity of the heart over a 24-hour period using a portable device. It's useful for detecting arrhythmias (abnormal heart rhythms) that may not be captured during a brief ECG. However, while helpful in certain contexts, it does not provide information about the heart's structure or function, which is crucial in assessing congenital heart disease. Option C: Cardiac MRI provides detailed images of the heart's structure and function and can be used for complex congenital heart disease evaluation. However, its use in pregnancy is generally limited due to concerns about the potential effects of the strong magnetic field and contrast agents on the fetus. Therefore, it is not the first-line diagnostic tool in pregnant women unless specifically indicated and after careful consideration of the risks and benefits. Option D: Treadmill Stress Test assesses the heart's response to physical stress and is typically used to diagnose and evaluate coronary artery disease. It involves monitoring the heart while the patient walks on a treadmill with increasing speed and incline. However, it is not the primary tool for evaluating congenital heart disease and is less practical in pregnancy due to physical limitations and the risk of inducing stress on the cardiovascular system.

Educational Objective:

  • The most appropriate and important initial evaluation for a pregnant woman with a history of congenital heart disease.
  • Ref: Page no 951 , William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 951 , William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 30-year-old pregnant woman with no prior medical history suddenly develops dyspnea and chest pain at 32 weeks gestation. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Pulmonary embolism

Explanation:

  • Option A: While symptoms of an anxiety attack can mimic those of more serious conditions (like shortness of breath and chest pain), the sudden onset of these symptoms in a pregnant woman, particularly in the third trimester, warrants a thorough evaluation for more serious conditions first. Anxiety should be considered a diagnosis of exclusion in this context. Option B: Peripartum Cardiomyopathy is a form of heart failure that occurs during the last month of pregnancy or within five months after delivery. Symptoms include dyspnea, fatigue, and swelling in the legs and feet. It typically does not present suddenly but rather develops gradually.
  • Given the acute onset of dyspnea and chest pain in a pregnant woman in her third trimester, pulmonary embolism (Option C) should be considered the most likely diagnosis and warrants immediate medical evaluation due to its potentially life-threatening nature. Top of Form

Educational Objective:

  • Pulmonary Embolism in Pregnancy:
  • Causes 10% of maternal deaths in the developed world; up to 70% will have evidence of DVT Clinical Presentation: Symptoms : Dyspnea (most common symptom), chest pain, cough, syncope and hemoptysis Signs : Tachypnea, tachycardia. In some cases, an accentuated pulmonic closure sound, rales, and/or friction rub is heard. ECG : Right axis deviation and T-wave inversion in the anterior chest leads Chest X Ray: may be normal or nonspecific findings of atelectasis, an infiltrate, cardiomegaly, or an effusion. Vascular markings in the lung region supplied by the obstructed artery can be lost. ECHO : Echocardiography is useful to detect other conditions that mimic pulmonary embolism—acute myocardial infarction, pericardial tamponade, and aortic dissection. Diagnostic Tests: the American Society of Hematology recommends ventilation perfusion lung scanning as the first-line diagnostic tool. Other guidelines prefer CT-pulmonary angiography for suspected pulmonary embolism Management: Anti coagulation with heparin Vena caval filter may be required Prior to cesarean (As anti-coagulation may lead to torrential surgical hemorrhage) If thrombolysis fails.
  • Ref: Page no 2571, William’s Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 2571, William’s Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which heart condition is most associated with a high risk of mortality in pregnancy?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Eisenmenger syndrome

Explanation:

  • Option A: Mitral Valve Prolapse is a condition in which the mitral valve leaflets bulge (prolapse) into the left atrium during the heart's contraction. Most women with mitral valve prolapse have a mild form and can go through pregnancy without significant complications. Option B: An atrial septal defect (ASD) is a hole in the wall between the two upper chambers of the heart. If it has been repaired, the risk of complications during pregnancy is relatively low, especially if the repair was successful and there are no residual cardiac issues. Option D: Aortic stenosis involves narrowing of the aortic valve, which can impede blood flow from the heart to the aorta and onward to the rest of the body. If the aortic stenosis is mild to moderate and stable, most women can tolerate pregnancy well. However, severe aortic stenosis can be risky during pregnancy, although not as high-risk as Eisenmenger syndrome.
  • Among these options, Eisenmenger syndrome (Option C) is the one most associated with a high risk of mortality in pregnancy.

Educational Objective:

  • This new classification of severity of heart disease in pregnancy by the WHO known as modified WHO risk stratification and os divided into 4 categories. Category 4 includes those diseases like Eisenmenger syndrome (Pulmonary artery HTN) where pregnancy is not recommended.
  • Ref: Page no 952, William’s Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 952, William’s Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A pregnant woman with a mechanical heart valve is being managed for anticoagulation. Which anticoagulant is preferred during the first trimester of pregnancy?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Low molecular weight heparin

Explanation:

  • Option A: Warfarin- This is preferred in non-pregnant women but it is teratogenic and can lead to warfarin embryopathy in doses > 5mg/ day. It is safe in the 2nd trimester but again in the 3rd trimester increases the risk of fetal hemorrhage Option C: LMWH- This is insufficient to provide the coagulation needed for mechanical heart valves Option D: Direct oral anticoagulants - (DOACs) are not recommended for use in mechanical heart valves and are also not recommended for use in pregnancy

Educational Objective:

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 25-year-old woman with a history of rheumatic heart disease presents in her second trimester. Which of the following findings would most warrant intervention?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Mitral stenosis with symptoms

Explanation:

  • Mitral stenosis, which involves narrowing of the mitral valve, restricts blood flow from the left atrium to the left ventricle. When symptomatic, it can lead to reduced cardiac output, pulmonary congestion, and even heart failure. Pregnancy increases the blood volume and heart rate, which can exacerbate these symptoms and complications. Severe symptomatic mitral stenosis often requires medical or even surgical intervention to manage the increased hemodynamic burden and to reduce the risk of maternal and fetal complications.
  • Option A: Mild mitral regurgitation, especially in the absence of symptoms, is usually well-tolerated during pregnancy and often does not require intervention. Pregnancy can increase the volume load on the heart, but mild regurgitation typically doesn't pose a significant risk. Option C: Trivial or mild aortic regurgitation typically does not pose a significant risk during pregnancy and often does not require intervention. The aortic valve allows blood to flow from the left ventricle to the aorta. If the regurgitation is only trivial, the heart can usually compensate well Option D: A small ASD, a hole in the septum between the heart's two upper chambers (atria), often does not significantly impact hemodynamics, especially if there is no evidence of right heart overload or pulmonary hypertension. In most cases, a small ASD does not require intervention during pregnancy and is generally well-tolerated.
  • Among these options, severe mitral stenosis with symptoms (Option B) is the finding that most warrants intervention.

Educational Objective:

  • Mitral stenosis, which involves narrowing of the mitral valve, restricts blood flow from the left atrium to the left ventricle. When symptomatic, it can lead to reduced cardiac output, pulmonary congestion, and even heart failure. Pregnancy increases the blood volume and heart rate, which can exacerbate these symptoms and complications. Severe symptomatic mitral stenosis often requires medical or even surgical intervention to manage the increased hemodynamic burden and to reduce the risk of maternal and fetal complications.
  • Ref: Page no 2390 , William’s Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 2390 , William’s Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 25-year-old pregnant woman with epilepsy is experiencing increased seizure frequency. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Double the current dose of antiepileptic drugs
Monitor serum levels of antiepileptic drugs and adjust dosage accordingly
Switch to a different class of antiepileptic drugs

explanation.short: C. Monitor serum levels of antiepileptic drugs and adjust dosage accordingly
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Monitor serum levels of antiepileptic drugs and adjust dosage accordingly

Explanation:

  • Pregnancy can alter the metabolism and serum levels of antiepileptic drugs, potentially necessitating dosage adjustments. Monitoring serum levels and adjusting the dosage can help control seizures without excessively increasing medication levels.
  • By discontinuing the drug, the seizure frequency will further increase Doubling the dose of the drug is not advised as this may increase the teratogenic potential of the drug. Also ideally, the dose should be calculated based on monitoring serum levels of the drug and then adjusting the dose. Switching to a different class of a drug should not be done during pregnancy as it can lead to increased seizure risk

Educational Objective:

  • There is an increased risk of major congenital malformations due to Anti-epileptic Drugs (AEDs). However certain AEDS like lamotrigine, levetiracetam and carbamazepine are commonly used and considered the safest agents. Poly-therapy increases the risk of malformations. Sodium valproate infers the highest risk of congenital malformations (especially Neural tube defects). Increased risk of Fetal growth restriction in women on AEDs. There is an increased risk of developing childhood epilepsy in the fetus. Folic acid (5mg) should be taken pre conceptionally and throughout pregnancy to reduce the risk of congenital malformations and long-term cognitive effects Aim for monotherapy with the longest effective AED dose if possible. If taking sodium valproate, this needs to be changed to an alternative AED prior to conception.
  • Ref: Williams Textbook of obstetrics 25th edition pg 1159
explanation.ref: Williams Textbook of obstetrics 25 th edition pg 1159

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following antiepileptic drugs is most associated with teratogenicity?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Valproate

Explanation:

  • Valproate is strongly associated with teratogenicity and is generally avoided during pregnancy unless absolutely necessary. The defects can include neural tube defects, craniofacial defects, cardiovascular malformations, and limb defects. Due to these risks, valproate is generally avoided in women of childbearing age and during pregnancy unless there are no suitable alternatives.
  • Lamotrigine is generally considered one of the safer antiepileptic drugs during pregnancy. While there is some risk of teratogenicity with any antiepileptic drug, the risk with lamotrigine is relatively low compared to others, particularly valproate. Like lamotrigine, levetiracetam is also considered to be relatively safe in pregnancy. Gabapentin is less commonly associated with teratogenicity than valproate, but it is not considered as safe as lamotrigine or levetiracetam. Gabapentin may be used in pregnancy when the benefits outweigh the risks, but it is not the first choice.

Educational Objective:

  • There is an increased risk of major congenital malformations due to Anti-epileptic Drugs (AEDs). However certain AEDS like lamotrigine, levetiracetam and carbamazepine are commonly used and considered the safest agents. Poly-therapy increases the risk of malformations. Sodium valproate infers the highest risk of congenital malformations (especially Neural tube defects). Increased risk of Fetal growth restriction in women on AEDs. There is an increased risk of developing childhood epilepsy in the fetus. Folic acid (5mg) should be taken pre conceptionally and throughout pregnancy to reduce the risk of congenital malformations and long-term cognitive effects Aim for monotherapy with the longest effective AED dose if possible. If taking sodium valproate, this needs to be changed to an alternative AED prior to conception.
  • Ref : Williams Textbook of obstetrics 25th edition pg 1160
explanation.ref: Williams Textbook of obstetrics 25 th edition pg 1160

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: What is the initial recommended management for a pregnant woman with epilepsy who has a seizure?
options: Maintenance of left lateral decubitus position and airway management

explanation.short: C. Maintenance of left lateral decubitus position and airway management
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Maintenance of left lateral decubitus position and airway management

Explanation:

  • If a pregnant woman with epilepsy has a seizure, the priority is to prevent injury and maintain oxygenation. Placing her in the left lateral decubitus position helps maintain uterine perfusion and prevent supine hypotension, while ensuring airway management is crucial.
  • Immediate cesarean delivery (Option A) is not indicated unless there are obstetric reasons Intravenous benzodiazepines (Option B) might be used for status epilepticus but are not the first step. Immediate discontinuation of antiepileptic drugs (Option D) is not recommended as it can increase the risk of further seizures.

Educational Objective:

  • Management of a seizure attack during pregnancy/ in labor
  • Left lateral tilt (or manual displacement of the uterus) Oxygenation Benzodiazepines (IV lorazepam 2-4 mg bolus repeated every 10-20 mins or IV diazepam 5-10 mg in slow bolus if lorazepam is unavailable) Continuous electronic fetal heart rate monitoring. In refractory cases IV phenytoin can be used: Loading dose of 18mg/ Kg; increased to 5 mg/ Kg to a maximum rate of 50 mg/ minute.
  • Ref: Williams Textbook of obstetrics 25th edition pg 734
explanation.ref: Williams Textbook of obstetrics 25 th edition pg 734

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A pregnant patient is diagnosed with hyperthyroidism. Which of the following drugs is considered the safest for the treatment of hyperthyroidism in pregnancy by the American Thyroid association?
options: Methimazole in the 1st trimester and Propylthiouracil in the 2nd and 3rd trimesters
Propylthiouracil (PTU) in the 1st trimester and methimazole in the 2nd and 3rd trimesters

explanation.short: C. Propylthiouracil (PTU) in the 1st trimester and methimazole in the 2nd and 3rd trimesters
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Propylthiouracil (PTU) in the 1st trimester and methimazole in the 2nd and 3rd trimesters

Explanation:

  • PTU is preferred in the first trimester due to the teratogenic risk of methimazole Propylthiouracil (PTU) has been historically preferred because it partially inhibits the conversion of T4 to T3 and crosses the placenta less readily than methimazole. Methimazole Is associated with a rare methimazole embryopathy, characterized by: Esophageal or choanal atresia Aplasia cutis, a congenital skin defect. However, treatment with PTU is associated with a higher risk for liver injury The American Thyroid Association and the American Association of Clinical Endocrinologists (2011) recommend PTU therapy during the first trimester followed by a switch to methimazole beginning in the second trimester. More recently, the American Thyroid Association recommends PTU therapy through 16 weeks’ gestation but does not make a recommendation regarding switching to methimazole treatment because both agents are associated with adverse effects and the harm from poorer control during the switch is unknown but the better answer to this question is to start with PTU and then switch to methimazole. Transient leukopenia can be documented in up to 10 percent of women taking antithyroid drugs, but this does not require therapy cessation. If fever or sore throat develops, women are instructed to discontinue medication immediately and report for a complete blood count
  • Option A: Methimazole is not to be given in the 1st trimester due to its teratogenic effects Option B: Although the recent American Thyroid Association guidelines do not mention what is to be given after 16 weeks, the older guidelines specify that in the 2nd and 3rd trimester methimazole should be given. Option D: Methimazole is not given in the 1st trimester

Educational Objective:

  • Methimazole Is associated with a rare methimazole embryopathy, characterized by: Esophageal or choanal atresia Aplasia cutis, a congenital skin defect.
  • Ref: Page no 2841, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 2841, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 32-year-old woman in her second trimester of pregnancy has a normal TSH but elevated free T4 levels. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Subclinical hyperthyroidism

Explanation:

  • Option A: Subclinical Hypothyroidism is characterized by a mildly elevated TSH level with normal free T4 levels. It indicates a mild form of hypothyroidism where the thyroid is beginning to underperform but has not yet resulted in decreased thyroid hormone production. This option does not align with the patient's lab results, which show a normal TSH and elevated free T4.
  • Option B: Overt hypothyroidism is diagnosed when both TSH levels are high, and free T4 levels are low. This condition indicates a significant deficiency in thyroid hormone production. The patient's lab results do not support this diagnosis since her free T4 levels are elevated, not low.
  • Option C: In overt hyperthyroidism, both TSH levels are suppressed (usually below the normal range), and free T4 levels are elevated. This condition indicates a significantly overactive thyroid. The patient's normal TSH level makes this diagnosis less likely compared to subclinical hyperthyroidism.

Educational Objective:

  • Normal TSH with elevated free T4 suggests subclinical hyperthyroidism (Option C), where the thyroid is overactive but not enough to suppress TSH. Overt hyperthyroidism (Option D) would typically present with a suppressed TSH.
  • Ref: Page no 1122, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1122, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: During pregnancy, a woman with no prior history of thyroid disease presents with a low TSH but normal free T4 and free T3 levels. What is the most likely explanation for these findings?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Gestational transient thyrotoxicosis

Explanation:

  • Option A: Primary Hypothyroidism is characterized by high TSH levels and low free T4 levels, as the primary problem lies in the thyroid gland itself, which is unable to produce sufficient thyroid hormones. This diagnosis does not fit the patient's lab results, which show a low TSH level.
  • Option B: In secondary hypothyroidism, both TSH and free T4 levels are low. This condition is due to a problem in the pituitary gland, which fails to produce enough TSH to stimulate the thyroid. Again, this option does not align with the patient's lab results, as her free T4 and T3 levels are normal.
  • Option D: While hyperemesis gravidarum, a condition characterized by severe nausea and vomiting in pregnancy, can be associated with thyroid function changes, it typically does not cause isolated low TSH with normal free T4 and T3 levels. The thyroid function changes seen in hyperemesis gravidarum are usually a part of gestational transient thyrotoxicosis.

Educational Objective:

  • Gestational Transient Thyrotoxicosis is characterized by transiently low TSH levels in the first trimester of pregnancy, usually due to the high levels of human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG), which can stimulate the thyroid gland. It typically occurs without significant thyroid dysfunction, and free T4 and free T3 levels remain within normal limits. This condition is usually self-limiting and resolves as pregnancy progresses.
  • Ref: Page no 1122, Williams Obstetrics 25th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1122, Williams Obstetrics 25 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 28-year-old pregnant woman in her first trimester presents with fever, rash, and arthralgia. She recently returned from a trip to a region where Zika virus is endemic. What is the most appropriate next step?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Order Zika virus testing

Explanation:

Educational Objective:

  • This RNA flavivirus is the first major mosquito-borne teratogen recognized Zika virus is primarily transmitted by mosquito bite, but sexual transmission is possible. Maternal-Fetal Infection: Zika infection is asymptomatic in approximately 80 percent of adults. It may cause mild rash, fever, headache, arthralgia, and conjunctivitis lasting a few days. The fetus can be severely infected whether the mother is symptomatic. In the most severely affected fetuses, a congenital Zika syndrome has been described that includes microcephaly, lissencephaly, ventriculomegaly, intracranial calcifications, ocular abnormalities, and congenital contractures Diagnosis and Management. Laboratory testing for Zika infection is recommended in symptomatic gravidas with residence in or recent travel to areas with active dengue transmission and risk for Zika transmission Serum IgM antibodies typically become detectable within the first 2 weeks and may persist for months. For a pregnant woman within 12 weeks of symptom onset, PCR testing of serum and urine specimens for both dengue and Zika virus RNA is recommended. For women with prenatal ultrasound findings suggesting congenital Zika syndrome, Zika virus PCR and IgM are initially performed on serum and urine. Amnionic fluid PCR may be performed, although less is known about its diagnostic accuracy for congenital infection. Currently, no specific treatment or vaccine is available for Zika infection, although several vaccines are in development
  • Ref: Williams Textbook of Obstetrics 26th edition pg 3107
explanation.ref: Williams Textbook of Obstetrics 26 th edition pg 3107

question: Assertion: Hot flushes are common after menopause Reason: Estrogen withdrawal happens in menopause
options: Both Assertion and Reasoning are independently true/correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion
Both Assertion and Reasoning are independently true/correct and Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion
Assertion is independently a true/correct statement but the Reason is independently an incorrect/false statement
Assertion is independently a false/incorrect statement but the Reason is independently a true/correct statement

explanation.short: A. Both Assertion and Reasoning are independently true/correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion
explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Both Assertion and Reasoning are independently true/correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion

Educational Objective:

  • Menopause is defined as the cessation of ovarian function resulting in permanent amenorrhea. It takes 12 months of amenorrhea to confirm that menopause has set in.
  • Most common symptom experienced Hot flushes are the waves of vasodilation affecting face and neck lasting for 2-5 minutes followed by profuse sweating Occur throughout the day, but severe at night Symptoms – headache, palpitations, anginal pain
  • Cause: Oestrogen deficiency – reduces hypothalamic endorphins which release more norepinephrine and serotonin – inappropriate heat loss mechanism Treatment: HRT with conjugated equine estrogen + Medroxyprogesterone acetate (cyclical if peri-menopause and continuous if postmenopausal) Raloxifene (SERM) Tibolone (steroid having weak estrogenic properties)
  • Ref: Page 55, DC Duttas Textbook of Gynecology 8th edition

question: Which is incorrect regarding Hormone Replacement Therapy in menopause?
options: It is effective for vasomotor symptoms during perimenopausal period
It should be given for young women with premature menopause

explanation.short: C. It is useful for primary prevention of heart disease
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. It is useful for primary prevention of heart disease

Educational Objective:

  • Relief of vasomotor symptoms Prevention of osteoporosis To maintain the quality of life in menopausal years Premature ovarian failure Gondal dysgenesis Surgical or radiation menopause
  • **Although HRT may be cardio protective, it is not given for prevention as the risks outweight the benefits
  • Undiagnosed genital tract bleeding Estrogen dependent neoplasm in the body History of venous thromboembolism Active liver disease Gallbladder disease
  • MONITORING PRIOR TO AND DURING HRT
  • A base level parameter of the following and their subsequent check up (at least annually) are mandatory.
  • Physical examination including pelvic examination. Blood pressure recording. Breast examination and Mammography. Cervical cytology Pelvic ultrasonography (TVS) to measure endometrial thickness (normal <5 mm).
  • Any irregular bleeding should be investigated thoroughly (endometrial biopsy, hysteroscopy).
  • Ideal serum level of estradiol should be 100 pg/ml during HRT therapy. Serum level of estradiol is useful to monitor the HRT therapy rather than that of serum FSH.
  • Ref: Page 51, DC Duttas Textbook of Gynecology 8th edition
explanation.ref: Page 51, DC Duttas Textbook of Gynecology 8 th edition
