subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 54y woman presents to the OPD with several months of irregular vaginal spotting. She does not give a history of heavy bleeding but only that her undergarments are stained with blood. She attained menopause at the age of 52 and she had hot flushes and night sweats for which she was started on the transdermal estrogen patch and progesterone only pills. She has been consistently using the estrogen patches but stopped the progesterone in view of severe nausea. She has no significant medical history. She is on regular HPV screening and her last HPV was done 2 year earlier which was normal. Speculum exam reveals a normal cervix and vagina with minimal blood in the upper vagina. Bimanual exam reveals a small uterus with no adnexal mass. What is the next best step in management?
options: Stop the estrogen patch and place an LNG-IUD

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Transvaginal ultrasound

Educational Objective:

  • HRT in women with a uterus should always be given Estrogen + Progesterone as Estrogen alone can lead to endometrial hyperplasia In hysterectomized women, Estrogen alone can be given
  • Ref: Page no 49, DC Duttas Textbook of Obstetrics, 8th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 49, DC Duttas Textbook of Obstetrics, 8 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 60y woman presents for a routine checkup. She has a sedentary lifestyle and her BMI is 26. She used to smoke 1 pack/ day but has recently stopped. She was advised calcium and vitamin D supplementation at her last checkup but has not been taking these. She has no significant medical history. A DEXA scan is done which shows her T score as -2.5. What is the most appropriate treatment option for her?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Bisphosphonates

Educational Objective:

  • The drug of choice for treatment of Osteoporosis is Bisphosphonates
  • Ref: Page no 49, DC Duttas Textbook of Obstetrics, 8th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 32-year-old woman complains that she has not had a period for 3 months. Four home pregnancy tests have all been negative. She started her periods at the age of 15 years and until 30 years she had a normal 28-day cycle. She had one daughter by normal delivery 2 years ago, following which she breast-fed for 6 months. After that she had normal cycles again for several months and then her periods stopped abruptly. She was using the progesterone only pill for contraception while she was breastfeeding and stopped 6 months ago as she is keen to have another child. She reports symptoms of dryness during intercourse and has experienced sweating episodes at night as well as episodes of feeling extremely hot at any time of day. The only medical history of note is that she has been hypothyroid for 10 years and takes thyroxine 100 mg per day. She does not take any alcohol, smoke or use recreational drugs. On lab investigation FSH level is 45 IU/L. what is the diagnosis?
options: Delay in return of fertility after contraception

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Premature ovarian failure

Educational Objective:

  • Premature ovarian failure is defined as cessation of due to loss of function of the ovaries before the age of 40 years FSH and LH will be high (FSH > 20) Estradiol levels will be low
  • Ref: Page no 395, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics, 8th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 395, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics, 8 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 19-year-old patient presents to your office with primary amenorrhea. She has normal breast and pubic hair development. But the uterus and vagina are absent. The most likely possibility is?

explanation.detail:
Ans. ‘C‘ Mullerian agenesis

Educational Objective:

  • Mullerian agenesis (Mayer-Rokitansky-Kuster-Hauser syndrome): It presents as amenorrhea with absence of a vagina
  • The karyotype is 46 XX. There is normal development of breasts, sexual hair, ovaries, external genitalia. There are associated urinary tract (33%) and skeletal (12%) anomalies.
  • The best way to differentiate between Mullerian agenesis and Androgen Insensitivity syndrome is to get a karyotype.
  • Mullerian agenesis is 46XX Androgen Insensitivity is 46 XY
  • Ref: Page no 44, Textbook of Gynecology by DC Dutta, 6th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 44, Textbook of Gynecology by DC Dutta, 6 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: The most common Mullerian anomaly is:

explanation.detail:
Ans. ‘D’ Septate uterus

Educational Objective:

  • Septate uterus is the most common Mullerian anomaly
  • WHO classification of Mullerian anomalies
  • Class I = Mullerian agenesis (MRKH) Class II = Unicornuate uterus (10%) Class III = Didelphys uterus (complete duplication: two uteri, two cervices, and longitudinal vaginal septum (8%) Class IV = Bicornuate uterus (26%) Class V = Septate uterus (35%) Class VI = Arcuate uterus (18%) Class VII = DES-related abnormalities/T-shaped uterus
  • Ref: Page no 44, Textbook of Gynecology by DC Dutta, 6th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 44, Textbook of Gynecology by DC Dutta, 6 th edition

This is incorrect. HSG is not a good investigation for Mullerian anomalies. The best non-invasive ix of choice for Mullerian anomaly is an MRI Option C: It has best reproductive outcome . This is incorrect. It has poor reproductive outcome.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Complete failure of mullerian duct fusion will result in?

explanation.detail:
Ans. ‘A’ Uterus didelphys

Educational Objective:

  • The two Mullerian ducts fuse to form the complete uterus. The anomalies can arise because of vertical or lateral fusion The Mullerian ducts come into being at around 6 weeks and failure to appear will lead to uterine aplasia. The fusing mullerian ducts form a singular cavity after dissolution of the septa Failure of resorption of the midline septum leads to formation of uterine septum which can be of full length i.e. from the
  • Ref: Page no 34, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Gynecology, 8th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 34, DC Dutta’s Textbook of Gynecology, 8 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: While evaluating a 30-year-old woman for infertility, you diagnose a bicornuate uterus. You explain that additional testing is necessary for one organ system because of the woman’s increased risk of congenital anomalies. Which is that organ system?

explanation.detail:
Ans. ‘C’ Urinary

Educational Objective:

  • Failed fusion of the Mullerian ducts can give rise to several types of uterine anomalies, e.g., bicornuate uterus. This condition is associated with a higher risk of obstetric complications, such as an increase in the rate of second-trimester abortion and premature labor. An intravenous pyelogram or urinary tract ultrasound is mandatory in patients with Mullerian anomalies, since approximately 30-40%% of patients with Mullerian anomalies have coexisting congenital urinary tract anomalies. Other associated abnormalities: skeletal (!2%)
  • Ref: Page no 32, DC Duttas Textbook of Gynecology 8th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 32, DC Duttas Textbook of Gynecology 8 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Rokitansky Kuster Hauser syndrome is associated with?

explanation.detail:
Ans. ‘C’ Vaginal atresia

Educational Objective:

  • The characteristic feature of MRKH syndrome (Mullerian agenesis) is absent uterus, absent fallopian tubes, absent cervix and absent upper part of vagina, absent

Ans. A. I & III

question: A 14-year girl presents with primary amenorrhea. She complains of monthly abdominal pain since a few months. On examination, there is a suprapubic bulge. The vaginal appears blind but she does not allow a vaginal or rectal exam to be done. Which of the following is NOT a possibility?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. MRKH Syndrome

Educational Objective:

  • An obstructive Mullerian anomaly like cervical agensesis, transverse vaginal septum, imperforate hymen will present with hematocolpos/ hematometra and features of endometriosis

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 24-year woman presents with recurrent mid trimester losses. A 3-D scan is ordered which shows the following. What is the diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • This is a 3D scan showing bicornuate uterus. A 3D scan differentiates between bicornuate and septate uterus much better than an HSG The main confusion is between a bicornuate and a septate uterus.
  • A line is traced joining both horns of the uterine cavity. If this line crossed the fundus or is ⩽ 5 mm from it, the uterus is considered bicornuate (a and b) If it is >5 mm from the fundus it was considered septate, regardless of whether the fundus was dome-shaped, smooth or discretely notched
  • On HSG, to differentiate between the two, it is important to not that
  • In septate uterus, the inter-cornual angle < 75 ° In bicornuate uterus, the inter-cornual angle > 105 °

Educational Objective:

question: The OHVIRA syndrome is seen in which type of Mullerian Anomaly?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Uterine Didelphys is associated with Obstructed Hemi-vagina (OHV) Ipsilateral Renal Agenesis (IRA) This is called the OHVIRA syndrome

Educational Objective:

  • Uterine Didelphys is associated with Obstructed Hemi-vagina (OHV) Ipsilateral Renal Agenesis (IRA) This is called the OHVIRA syndrome

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 13-year-old female presents with increasing abdominal pain. She has begun appropriate pubertal development, although menarche has not occurred. During examination, a central abdominopelvic mass is appreciated and is shown in the image. Physical examination findings prompt sonography and then magnetic resonance (MR) imaging with contrast. The MR imaging results are shown in the 2 nd Figure. Which of the following congenital anomalies is most likely present?
options: Obstructed hemi-vagina and ipsilateral renal anomaly (OHVIRA) syndrome

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • This is a case of imperforate hymen. On the MRI, a large hematocolpos is seen (blood collected in the vagina) Treatment is by incising the imperforate hymen by a cruciate incision and draining the hamatocolpos
  • Option A: Mullerian agenesis: As there is no uterus or vagina, there is no collection of blood (no hematocolpos or hematometra) Option B: Cervical agenesis: In cervical agenesis, there will be hematometra (collection of blood in the uterus_. Not hematocolpos. Option D: Obstructed hemi-vagina and ipsilateral renal anomaly (OHVIRA) syndrome : This will present in a didelphys uterus where there is an bstructed hemo vagina. So there will be partial hematocolpos but she will not have primary amenorrhoea (would have attained menarche) as the obstruction in the vagina is partial

Educational Objective:

  • An imperforate hymen will present as primary amenorrhoea with secondary sexual characters with cyclical abdominal pain O/e: bluish bulge at introitus, abdominal pulge (hematocolpos/ hematometra) MRI will show a hematocolpos with the obstruction low down
  • Ref: Page 444, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page 444, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: What is the preferred imaging modality when a congenital mullerian anomaly is suspected?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • MR imaging is currently preferred or imaging complex defects MR imaging provides clear delineation of both the internal and external uterine anatomy and has a reported accuracy of up to 100 percent in the evaluation of Müllerian anomalies Moreover, complex anomalies and commonly associated secondary diagnoses such as renal or skeletal anomalies can be concurrently evaluated. In some women undergoing an in fertility evaluation, hysteroscopy and laparoscopy may be selected to assess or Müllerian anomalies; screen or endometriosis, which is often coexistent; and exclude other tubal or uterine cavity pathologies

Educational Objective:

  • MR imaging is currently preferred or imaging complex defects MR imaging provides clear delineation of both the internal and external uterine anatomy and has a reported accuracy of up to 100 percent in the evaluation of Müllerian anomalies

question: You are presented with a patient with a Unicornuate uterus that is similar in shape to the one shown here. What is the next appropriate step in your management?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Müllerian defects are associated with renal anomalies in 30 to 50 percent of cases, and defects include unilateral renal agenesis, severe renal hypoplasia, horseshoe kidney, pelvic kidney, and ectopic or duplicate ureters Spinal anomalies have been reported in 10 to 12 percent of cases and include wedge, supernumerary, or asymmetric and rudimentary vertebral bodies The combination of Müllerian and renal aplasia and cervicothoracic somite dysplasia has been described as MURCS association An association with Klippel-Feil syndrome has also been reported. Other anomalies associated with vaginal agenesis include ear anomalies and hearing loss
  • Option B: Diagnostic laparoscopy: This is not indicated as it will not provide any additional information and a diagnostic hystero-laparoscopy is done only whent he diagnosis is in doubt Option C: Diagnostic hysteroscopy: This is not indicated as it will not provide any additional information and a diagnostic hystero-laparoscopy is done only whent he diagnosis is in doubt Option D: Brain MRI: This is not indicated in Mullerian anomalies

Educational Objective:

  • Müllerian defects are associated with renal anomalies in 30 to 50 percent of cases, and defects include unilateral renal agenesis, severe renal hypoplasia, horseshoe kidney, pelvic kidney, and ectopic or duplicate ureters Spinal anomalies have been reported in 10 to 12 percent of cases and include wedge, supernumerary, or asymmetric and rudimentary vertebral bodies The combination of Müllerian and renal aplasia and cervicothoracic somite dysplasia has been described as MURCS association An association with Klippel-Feil syndrome has also been reported. Other anomalies associated with vaginal agenesis include ear anomalies and hearing loss

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 22-year-old G3P0 presents to your office as a new patient for consultation regarding her history of multiple miscarriages. Her gynecologic history is otherwise unremarkable. During physical examination, you note that the uterus is markedly deviated to the left. Transvaginal sonography is performed, and three-dimensional images reveal a single uterus that is deviated and has a banana-shaped cavity, as shown below? What is your diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • This is a patient with unicornuate uterus. With Unicornuate uterus, a functional uterus, normal cervix, and normal round ligament and fallopian tube are found on one side. On the contralateral side, Müllerian structures develop abnormally, and agenesis or more frequently a rudimentary uterine horn is identified. A rudimentary horn may communicate or more commonly not communicate with the unicornuate uterus. In addition, the endometrial cavity of the rudimentary horn may be obliterated or may contain some functioning endometrium. Active endometrium in a noncommunicating horn will eventually be symptomatic with cyclic unilateral pain and possibly with hematometra.
  • Option A: Septate uterus: This presents as poor reproductive outcome. On an HSG, the 2 uterine horns, the angle will be < 75 degree Option B: Bicornuate uterus: This presents as a wide intercornual angle on the HSG Option C: Didelphys: This presents as 2 completely seprate uterine covities which are far apart

Educational Objective:

  • A line is traced joining both horns of the uterine cavity. If this line crossed the fundus or is ⩽5 mm from it, the uterus is considered bicornuate (a and b) If it is >5 mm from the fundus it was considered septate, regardless of whether the fundus was dome-shaped, smooth or discretely notched
  • On HSG, to differentiate between the two, it is important to not that
  • In septate uterus, the inter-cornual angle < 75 ° In bicornuate uterus, the inter-cornual angle > 105 °

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the mullerian anomalies, as illustrated in this sonogram, has the best reproductive prognosis? A gestational sac is seen on the image’s left?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Of the major uterine malformations, the didelphic uterus has a good reproductive prognosis. Compared with the unicornuate uterus, although the potential or uterine growth and capacity appears similar, uterine didelphys probably has an improved blood supply from collateral connections between the two horns.

Educational Objective:

  • Of the major uterine malformations, the didelphic uterus has a good reproductive prognosis. An arcuate uterus also will not usually present with any major complication
  • Ref: Page no 448, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page no 448, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: When should surgical reconstruction of a bicornuate uterus (Strassman metroplasty) be performed?
options: In all women with the diagnosis of bicornuate uterus
Strassman metroplasty is not performed for repair of bicornuate uterus
For women in whom recurrent pregnancy loss occurs with no other identifiable cause except their uterine anomaly

explanation.short: D. For women in whom recurrent pregnancy loss occurs with no other identifiable cause except their uterine anomaly
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Surgical reconstruction of the bicornuate uterus is infrequently done in bicornuate uterus (Strassman metroplasty) and is been advocated in women with multiple spontaneous abortions in whom no other causative actors are identified. Strassman described the surgical technique that unified equal-sized endometrial cavities. Reproductive outcome after unification generally has been good. As in surgery or uterine didelphys, metroplasty is reserved or women in whom recurrent pregnancy loss occurs with no other identifiable cause.
  • Let's discuss the other options:
  • Option A: Only for complete bicornuate anomalies: This is incorrect. It is performed only for those bicornuate uterus where ther eis no other reason for pregnancy loss Option B: In all women with the diagnosis of bicornuate uterus. This is also incorrect. Option C: Strassman metroplasty is not performed for repair of bicornuate uterus. This is also incorrect. The surgery for bicornuate uterus is Strassman metroplasty.

Educational Objective:

  • Surgical reconstruction of the bicornuate uterus is infrequently done in bicornuate uterus (Strassman metroplasty) and is been advocated in women with multiple spontaneous abortions in whom no other causative actors are identified.
  • Ref: Page no 449, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page no 449, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Septate uterus has a significantly higher spontaneous abortion rate and early pregnancy loss rate than bicornuate uterus. As illustrated in the figure below, what is the primary mechanism thought to be responsible for this extraordinarily high pregnancy wastage?
options: Distortion of the uterine serosal contour
Partial or complete implantation on the largely avascular septum

explanation.short: D. Partial or complete implantation on the largely avascular septum
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • High pregnancy wastage likely results from partial or complete implantation on a largely avascular septum, from distortion of the uterine cavity, and from associated cervical or endometrial abnormalities.

Educational Objective:

  • High pregnancy wastage in spetate uterus likely results from partial or complete implantation on a largely avascular septum, from distortion of the uterine cavity, and from associated cervical or endometrial abnormalities. Complications associated with a septate uterus are: Infertility Recurent pregnancy loss Fetal growth restriciton Intra uterine fetal demise Malpresentations Preterm labor

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following are seen in offspring of women who took diethylstilbestrol (DES)? Impaired conception rates in female offspring Cryptorchidism, testicular hypoplasia, and malformations of the penis in male offspring T-shaped uterus “Cockscomb” cervix Increased rates of clear cell adenocarcinoma in female offspring

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Diethylstilbestrol (DES), a synthetic nonsteroidal estrogen, was prescribed to an estimated 3 million pregnant women in the United States from the late 1940s through the early 1960s. The drug was used in treating abortion, preeclampsia, diabetes, hot flushes, and preterm labor. DES exposure in utero has been linked to the development of a “T-shaped” uterus An increased incidence of clear cell adenocarcinomas of the vagina and cervix. Vaginal and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia, Genitourinary malformations following DES exposure in utero have also been noted and include those of the cervix, vagina, uterine cavity, and fallopian tubes. transverse septa, circumferential ridges involving the vagina and cervix, and cervical collars (“cockscomb cervix”) have been found. Fallopian tube abnormalities include shortened and narrowed dimensions and absent fimbria. Males exposed to DES in utero also have structural abnormalities. Cryptorchidism, testicular hypoplasia, microphallus, and hypospadias have been reported.

Educational Objective:

  • Problems associated with DES exposure in utero are:
  • Development of a “T-shaped” uterus Increased incidence of clear cell adenocarcinomas of the vagina and cervix Vaginal and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia Genitourinary malformations of cervix, vagina, uterine cavity, and fallopian tubes. transverse septa, circumferential ridges involving the vagina and cervix, and cervical collars (“cockscomb cervix”) Fallopian tube abnormalities: shortened and narrowed dimensions and absent fimbria Males: cryptorchidism, testicular hypoplasia, microphallus and hypospadias
  • Ref: Page no 450, Williams Gynecology
explanation.ref: Page no 450, Williams Gynecology

An inbuilt scissors can then cut the desiccated tissue. Laser coagulation Hemostatic clips Laparoscopic suturing

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: All are indications of a total laparoscopic hysterectomy except?

explanation.detail:
Educational Objective:

  • Routes of hysterectomy include
  • 1. Abdominal route (laparotomy): This is preferred for large uteruses or if hysterectomy is done for malignant condition or if laproscopic facilities are not available/ laparoscopy is contra-indicated like in cardio-pulmonary conditions
  • 2. Laparoscopy and robotic hysterectomy: Can be done for all benign conditions and has more or less replaced abdomnal hysterectomies.. C/i to laparoscopy include cardio-pulmonary conditions and hysterectomy for advanced malignancy.
  • This is mostly done in women with uterine prolapse It can also be done in women without prolapse with uterus size < 14 weeks (like small fibroids or adenomyosis) and is called as Non Descent Vaginal Hysterectomy (NDVH)
  • Ref: Page no 756, Novak Gynecology 15th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A gynecologist is doing a laparoscopic myomectomy for a 32 year woman with a large intramural fibroid. The intra-abdominal pressure needed to be maintained for optimum gynecological laparoscopic surgery is:

explanation.detail:
Educational Objective:

  • Laparoscopic surgery involves creating pneumo-peritoneum wherein CO2 gas is introduced in the peritoneal cavity. At the beginnng of laparoscopic surgery, CO2 is insufflated to 25-30 mmHg for positioning of ports. This reduces the chance of trauma to the bowel or post abdominal wall as there is more gas inside the abdomen. After placement of cannulas, the pressure should be dropped to 10-15 mmHg. This reduces the risk of hypercarbia .
  • Ref: Page no 761, Novak Gynecology 15th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 34y woman who presented with scanty periods following an abortion 6 months earlier undergoes a diagnostic hysteroscopy. This is the finding. What is your diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • This image is showing intra-uterine synechiae (Adhesions) typically seen in Asherman syndrome Asherman syndrome presents as Hypomenorrhea (reduced menstrual flow) OR Secondary amenorrhoea OR Secondary infertility Treatment is by hysteroscopic adhesiolysis
  • Option B: Endometritis: Findings on hysteroscopy include endometrial hyperemia, micro-polyps or endometrial interstitial edema. This would appear as shown below:
  • Option C: Endometrial Hyperplasia: This is suspected on increased endometrial thickness on an ultrasound. And is confirmed by endometrial biopsy. On Hysteroscopy, the endometrium will appear fluffy and thickened as shown
  • Option D: Septate uterus: This appears as a single band dividing the uterus into 2 cavities. It appears on hysteroscopy as shown below:

Educational Objective:

  • Asherman syndrome (intra uterine synechiae) commonly develops after a h/o curettage of the endometrium. It is also seen in Genital TB It presents as Hypomenorrhea (reduced menstrual flow) OR Secondary amenorrhoea OR Secondary infertility Treatment is by hysteroscopic adhesiolysis
  • Ref: Page no 372, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition

question: A 23y primigravida is undergoing an elective LSCS for breech presentation. What is the ideal timing of cesarean section?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. 39 Weeks

Educational Objective:

  • The ideal timing for elective cesarean sections should consider the balance between fetal maturity and the avoidance of complications associated with preterm or post-term birth. In the case of a primigravida with a breech presentation, scheduling the cesarean section around 39 weeks of gestation is often recommended to optimize the baby's readiness for birth while minimizing the risk of neonatal complications.

question: Prerequisite for outlet forceps application are all except?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Station 0 or +1

Educational Objective:

  • It's important to assess the engagement of the fetal head, cervical dilation, the presence of uterine contractions, and the station of the baby's head to determine the appropriate timing for forceps application during childbirth. The outlet forceps are applied when station of fetal head is ≥ +3 station and not at 0 or +1.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 39 y primigravida female presents in labor with breech presentation at 38 weeks. She has conceived via IVF. She has multiple large fibroids in the anterior lower uterine segment. The most appropriate management is?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Classical cesarean

Educational Objective:

  • A cesarean section is often recommended for breech presentations, especially in primigravida women, as it reduces the risks associated with delivering a baby in a breech position. When the lower uterine segment is not approachable due to multiple large fibroids in the lower segment, classical cesarean section (upper segment cesarean) may be done. Indications for a classical cesarean section include: Large fibroid in the lower segment Dense adhesions in the lower segmenr Placenta accreta syndrome Carcinoma cervix Perimortem cesarean section
  • Ref: Page no 1442, Willaims Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1442, Willaims Obstetrics 26 th edition

Vacuum has a feature of autorotation; so even if internal rotation is not complete – traction on the vacuum will cause rotation

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which structure is NOT cut in a mediolateral episiotomy?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Obturator muscles

Educational Objective:

  • In summary, the structure that is NOT cut in a mediolateral episiotomy is B, the Obturator Muscles. These muscles are located deeper and more laterally in the pelvis and are not involved in the episiotomy incision. The purpose of a mediolateral episiotomy is to enlarge the vaginal opening for childbirth, involving an incision through the perineal skin and potentially affecting the bulbospongiosus and transverse perineal muscles.

Which instrument will you use to hold the angles of the uterine incision?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Green Armitage Forceps

Educational Objective:

  • Green-Armitage Forceps are specially designed for situations encountered in obstetric surgery, such as holding the angles of a uterine incision during a cesarean section with complications like extended incisions and bleeding. They provide the necessary grip and stability to manage the surgical field effectively and safely.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 1 st year resident is doing a cesareans section for the 1 st time. How will you help her identify the lower uterine segment?
options: It appears slightly more pink than the upper segment
It lies under the loose uterovesical fold of peritoneum
It is just below the round ligaments
It is where the presenting part can be palpated

explanation.short: B. It lies under the loose uterovesical fold of peritoneum
explanation.detail:
Ans. B. It lies under the loose uterovesical fold of peritoneum

Educational Objective:

  • The lower uterine segment in a cesarean section is best identified as the area lying under the loose uterovesical fold of peritoneum. This anatomical landmark provides a reliable guide for surgeons to make a safe and effective incision, minimizing the risk of injury to the bladder and ensuring optimal surgical outcomes.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Identify this instrument shown in the image which is placed vaginally and is used to elevate the fetal head in a difficult cesarean section where the head is deeply engaged:

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Fetal pillow

Educational Objective:

  • The Fetal Pillow, is an instrument used to elevate the fetal head in a difficult cesarean section where the head is deeply engaged. This tool is part of various methods employed to facilitate the safe and efficient delivery of the baby, especially in challenging situations where there is a risk of extension of incisions and bleeding
  • Ref: Page no 1430, Williams Obstetrics 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1430, Williams Obstetrics 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: What is the first structure to be sutured following a median episiotomy?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Mucosa

Educational Objective:

  • The correct approach to suturing an episiotomy involves starting with the vaginal mucosa, followed by the muscle layer, and finally the skin. This method ensures effective hemostasis, alignment for optimal healing, and restoration of anatomical and functional integrity of the perineal region. The suture material used is often a rapidly absorbable suture like Vicryl rapid or catgut, which reduces the need for suture removal and provides comfort to the patient
  • Ref: Page no 1306, Williams Obstetrics, 26th edition
explanation.ref: Page no 1306, Williams Obstetrics, 26 th edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A lady underwent vaginal hysterectomy for Carcinoma cervix. Following the surgery after her urethral catheter was removed, she complained of leaking of urine. Methylene blue dye was instilled in her bladder through her urethra, and she was given oral Phenazopyridine dye. After some time, her pads were checked and it showed yellow staining at the top most pad, while the middle or bottom pads were unstained. She is likely to have?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Ureterovaginal fistula

Explanation:

  • In this patient methylene blue dye was instilled in bladder and she was given oral phenazopyridine dye. Pad showed yellow staining at the top portion, but not the middle or bottom portions. She is likely to have Ureterovaginal fistula. The urine, which is being brought by ureters is yellow due to oral intake of phenazopyridine which stains the urine yellow. The methylene blue which is in the bladder is not staining the swab as there is no Vesico or urethero-vaginal fistula.
  • Option A: Urge Incontinence: This is a type of urinary incontinence in adults, which involves sudden compelling urges to void and results in involuntary leakage of urine Option C: Neurogenic bladder: This presents as urgency, frequency or even as continuois urinary incontience. However as there is no fistula, on the 3 swab test, there will be no staining or wettingof the swabs Option D: Urinary tract infection: This will present as frequency and burning micturition. It can also present as fever, flank pain and constitutional symptoms in pyelonephritis.

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page 607, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page 607, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 55 years old multigravida with history of frequently micturating due to coughing laughing & sneezing. The diagnosis is?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Stress incontinence

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Spontaneous healing of a genitourinary fistula via continuous bladder drainage using an indwelling urinary catheter is more likely to happen in which of the following settings?
options: The fistula is large, greater than 2 cm.
The fistula is small, 2 to 3 mm.
The fistula is related to pelvic radiation
The catheter is left in place for a minimum of 8 weeks

explanation.short: B. The fistula is small, 2 to 3 mm.
explanation.detail:
Ans. B. The fistula is small, 2 to 3 mm

Explanation:

  • Occasionally, genitourinary fistulas may spontaneously close during continuous bladder drainage using an indwelling urinary catheter. Urinary fistulas close spontaneously after 2 to 8 weeks of transurethral catheterization, especially if the fistula is small (2- to 3-mm diameter).
  • Option A: The fistula is large, greater than 2 cm: Large fistulas are unlikely to heal spontaneusly Option C: The fistula is related to pelvic radiation: Post Radiotherapy fistulas heal poorly Option D: The catheter is left in place for a minimum of 8 weeks: If the fistula is small, urinary fistulas close spontaneously after 2 to 8 weeks of transurethral catheterization

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Page 608, Williams Gynecology 3rd edition
explanation.ref: Page 608, Williams Gynecology 3 rd edition

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following diagnostic tools or techniques would help identify a ureterovaginal fistula?
options: “Three-swab” or tampon test with diluted solution of methylene blue instilled (retrograde) into the bladder

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Intravenous pyelogram

Explanation:

  • For ureterovaginal fistulas,the investigation of choice is a CT urography as CT urography will clearly show extravasaton from the ureter and the site of injury. However since this is not there in the option and the closest option to a CT urography is an intravenus pyelogram which will also demonstrate extravasation of contrast from ureter with mild to moderate hydro-uretero nephrosis. Also it is important to remember that the pr esence of contrast in the vagina gives a typical cup and saucer sign in vesico-vaginal fistula.
  • Option A: Cystourethroscopy: This is for diagnosis of vesico vaginal fistula or urethro vaginal fistula Option C: Voiding cystourethrogram: It is not useful in uretero vaginal fistulas. Evaluation of a uro genital fistula is incomplete without a functional study like a IV or CT urography as 12% of VVF patietns have concomitant uretro vaginal fistulas Option D: 3 swab test: It is not a confirmatory test. It is a clinical test when a uro-genital fistulas is suspected

Educational Objective:

  • The investigation of choice to diagnose a uretero vaginal fistula is a CT urography Other investigationa are Cystoscopy + Retrograde Pyelography Intra venous pyelogram MR urography

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Your patient, who underwent hysterectomy 3 months ago, complains of urine leakage from her vagina. You suspect she has developed a vesicovaginal fistula. Which of the following radiologic tests is the most appropriate to order in her evaluation?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. CT urography

Explanation:

  • Intravenous contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT – urography) scanning in the excretory phase has become the preferred diagnostic test for genito-urinary fistulas after initial cystourethroscopic survey is completed
  • Option A: Intravenous pyelography: This is not the investigation of choice for a VVF now as CT urography is now widely available. The typical appearance of a VVF on an IVP is a cup and saucer appearance. Where CT is not available, IVP can be used. Option B: Voiding cystourethrography: This can be used for VVF but is not preferred as upto 12% of VVF will have a concomitant uretero vaginal fistula which can be missed. Option D: Positive pressure urethrography : IS for diagnosing urethral diverticula

Educational Objective:

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: When the patient’s history suggests an overlap in both stress and urge incontinence symptoms, which of the following terms is used?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Mixed urinary incontinence

Explanation:

  • Urinary incontinence is defined as involuntary leakage of urine. Stress urinary incontinence (SUI) is the involuntary leakage of urine with increases in intraabdominal pressure. Urgency urinary incontinence (previously “urge” urinary incontinence) is the involuntary leakage accompanied or immediately preceded by a perceived strong imminent need to void. A related condition, overactive bladder, describes urinary urgency with or without incontinence and usually with increased daytime urinary frequency and nocturia When both stress and urgency symptoms are present, it is called mixed urinary incontinence.
  • Option A: Overflow incontinence: Overflow urinary incontinence is the involuntary leakage of urine from an overdistended bladder due to impaired detrusor contractility and/or bladder outlet obstruction. Neurologic diseases such as spinal cord injuries, multiple sclerosis, and diabetes can impair detrusor function. Bladder outlet obstruction can be caused by external compression by abdominal or pelvic masses and pelvic organ prolapse, among other causes. Option C: Complex urinary incontinence: There is no such terminology Option D: Augmented urinary incontinence: There is no such terminology

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: In the POP-Q system of uterine prolapse classification, how many fixed points are there?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. 6

Explanation:

  • POP-Q is Pelvic Organ Prolapse – Quantification System. The hymen acts as the fixed point of reference. There are 6 defined fixed points for measurement in this system Anterior: Aa, Ba, C Posterior: Ap, Bp, D Three other landmarks are described: Genital hiatus (gh) Total vaginal length (tvl) Perineal body (pb) Each of these 3 measurements is measured in cm above or proximal to the hymen (negative) or below/ distal to the hymen (positive) with the plane of the hymen as zero (0)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: How is a 3 rd degree prolapse differentiated from a 4 th degree prolapse (procidentia)?
options: Passing a sound through the cervix

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • As per the Shaw’s classification of utero-vaginal prolapse, there are 4 degrees of prolapse Stage 0 – No prolapse Stage I – Descent of the cervix into the vagina Stage II – Descent of the cervix up to the introitus Stage III – Descent of the cervix outside the introitus Stage IV / Procidentia – whole of the uterus is outside the introitus Now on inspection, stage 3 and 4 will appear the same like this:
  • They are differentiated by approximating the thumb and finger of the examining hand at the introitus over the swelling If the thumb and finger approximate, this means it is a procidentia i.e. the thumb and finger are able to get above the uterine fundus If the thumb and finger don’t approximate, it is a 3rd degree uterine prolapse
  • An ultrasound (trans-perineal or trans-vaginal) does not help in determining the degree of prolapse A uterine sound helps in differentiating between a chronic uterine inversion and a prolapse

Educational Objective:

  • A thumb finger test helps differentiate a 3rd degree prolapse from a 4th degree prolapse (Procidentia)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Which of the following statements is FALSE regarding development of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis in the female fetus and neonate?
options: The gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) “pulse generator” remains functionally dormant until shortly after birth.
By 5 months’ gestation, 6 to 7 million oocytes have been created from accelerated germ cell division.
At birth, follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and luteinizing hormone (LH) concentrations rise and remain high during the first 3 months of life.
Neonatal breast budding, minor uterine bleeding, and transient ovarian cysts may occur as a normal response to initially high gonadotropin levels.

explanation.short: A. The gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) “pulse generator” remains functionally dormant until shortly after birth.
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • A carefully orchestrated cascade of events unfolds in the neuroendocrine system and regulates development of the female reproductive system. In utero, gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) neurons develop in the olfactory placode. These neurons migrate through the forebrain to the arcuate nucleus of the hypothalamus by 11 weeks’ gestation. Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH), a decapeptide, is influenced by higher cortical centers and is released from these neurons in a pulsatile fashion into the pituitary portal plexus. As a result, by mid gestation, the GnRH “pulse generator” stimulates secretion of gonadotropins, that is, follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and luteinizing hormone (LH), from the anterior pituitary. In turn, the pulsatile release of gonadotropins stimulates ovarian synthesis and release of gonadal steroid hormones. Concurrently, accelerated germ cell division and follicular development begins, resulting in the creation of 6 to 7 million oocytes by 5 months’ gestation. By late gestation, gonadal steroids exert negative feedback on secretion of both hypothalamic GnRH and pituitary gonadotropins. During this time, oocyte number decreases through a process of gene related apoptosis to reach a level of 1 to 2 million by birth At birth, FSH and LH concentrations rise abruptly in response to the fall in placental estrogen levels and are highest in the first 3 months of life. This transient rise in gonadotropin levels is followed by an increase in gonadal steroid concentrations, which is thought to explain instances of neonatal breast budding, minor bleeding from endometrial shedding, short-lived ovarian cysts, and transient white vaginal mucous discharge. Following these initial months, gonadotropin levels gradually decline to reach prepubertal levels by age 1 to 2 years. The childhood years are thus characterized by low plasma levels of FSH, LH, and estradiol. Estradiol levels typically measure < 10 pg/mL, and LH values are < 0.3 mIU/mL.

Educational Objective:

  • A carefully orchestrated cascade of events unfolds in the neuroendocrine system and regulates development of the female reproductive system. In utero, gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) neurons develop in the olfactory placode. These neurons migrate through the forebrain to the arcuate nucleus of the hypothalamus by 11 weeks’ gestation. Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH), a decapeptide, is influenced by higher cortical centers and is released from these neurons in a pulsatile fashion into the pituitary portal plexus. As a result, by mid gestation, the GnRH “pulse generator” stimulates secretion of gonadotropins, that is, follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and luteinizing hormone (LH), from the anterior pituitary. In turn, the pulsatile release of gonadotropins stimulates ovarian synthesis and release of gonadal steroid hormones. Concurrently, accelerated germ cell division and follicular development begins, resulting in the creation of 6 to 7 million oocytes by 5 months’ gestation. By late gestation, gonadal steroids exert negative feedback on secretion of both hypothalamic GnRH and pituitary gonadotropins. During this time, oocyte number decreases through a process of gene related apoptosis to reach a level of 1 to 2 million by birth At birth, FSH and LH concentrations rise abruptly in response to the fall in placental estrogen levels and are highest in the first 3 months of life. This transient rise in gonadotropin levels is followed by an increase in gonadal steroid concentrations, which is thought to explain instances of neonatal breast budding, minor bleeding from endometrial shedding, short-lived ovarian cysts, and transient white vaginal mucous discharge. Following these initial months, gonadotropin levels gradually decline to reach prepubertal levels by age 1 to 2 years. The childhood years are thus characterized by low plasma levels of FSH, LH, and estradiol. Estradiol levels typically measure < 10 pg/mL, and LH values are < 0.3 mIU/mL.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: During the past several decades, the ages at which girls experience thelarche and menarche have shown which of the following trends?
options: No clear trend has been observed

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Earlier trends of puberty are now being experienced In most girls, breast budding, termed thelarche, is the first physical sign of puberty and begins at approximately age 10 years Following breast and pubic hair growth, adolescents undergo an accelerated increase in height, termed a growth spurt, during a 3-year span from ages 10.5 to 13.5 years. Higher BMI correlates with earlier pubertal development. The mean age o menarche in white girls is 12.7 years and 6 months earlier, or 12.1, in black girls

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 33-year-old female is being followed for a 5-mm prolactin-producing pituitary adenoma, which is well controlled on bromocriptine 0.5 mg orally daily. She presents with a positive pregnancy test result. What is the next best step?
options: Check a prolactin level and if it is elevated, continue bromocriptine therapy
Obtain magnetic resonance (MR) imaging of the brain and base decisions on these findings

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • The pituitary gland enlarges during pregnancy, primarily due to hypertrophy and hyperplasia of the lactotrophs in response to elevated serum estrogen levels. Although the tumor can enlarge during pregnancy, the risk of clinically significant growth is small. Significant expansion may lead to headaches or compression of the optic chiasm and blindness, visual field testing is considered in every trimester or women with macroadenomas. Dopamine agonist therapy has been associated with an increased risk of spontaneous abortion, preterm deliveries, and recommendations are that dopamine agonist therapy be discontinued during pregnancy when possible.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: Meiosis I of the oocytes is completed at which of the following times?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Primary oogonia enter meiosis in utero (in fetal life) to become primary oocytes. These oocytes are arrested in development at prophase during the first meiotic division. Meiotic progression resumes each month in a cohort follicle. Meiosis I is completed in the oocyte destined for ovulation in response to the LH surge. Meiosis II begins, and the process is arrested, this time in the second meiotic metaphase. Meiosis II is completed only if the ovum is fertilized

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Proliferative phase: straight to slightly coiled, tubular glands are lined by pseudostratified columnar epithelium with scattered mitoses. Early secretory phase: coiled glands with a slightly widened diameter are lined by simple columnar epithelium with clear subnuclear vacuoles. Stroma is variably edematous in the secretory phase. Late secretory phase: serrated, dilated glands with intraluminal secretion are lined by short columnar cells. Menstrual phase: fragmented endometrium with condensed stroma and glands with secretory vacuoles are seen in a background of blood.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 7-year girl is brought to the OPD. Her mother has noted vaginal bleeding for 2 days which is small in quantity staining her undergarments. She shows no signs of pubertal development. The cause of the bleeding is not apparent during physical examination, and the decision is made to proceed with examination under anesthesia and saline vaginoscopy. What is the most likely finding?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • Neonates may present with vaginal bleeding during the first week of life due to the withdrawal of maternal hormones at birth and this b leeding typically resolves after a few days. Prepubertal bleeding in a child, however, merits careful evaluation Most instances of vaginal bleeding in these girls are due to local causes and can be elucidated with a simple history and physical examination. Occasionally, an examination under anesthesia with saline vaginoscopy is required to rule out a foreign body in the upper vagina
  • Option B: Atrophic vaginitis: This is a common cause of bleeding in the post-menopausal age group Option C: Endocervical polyp: This is a common cause of inter-mentrual bleeding in the reproductive age group and less common in children Option D: Sarcoma botryoides: This is a malignant tumor that arises from the immature skeletal muscle cells (rhabdomyoblasts) of hollow organs like the vagina or bladder or biliary tract. It most commonly affects children < 4y and classically presents as a mass resembling a bunch of grapes.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 5-year girl develops precocious puberty. Gonadotropin levels are low, even following gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) infusion stimulation testing. Estrogen levels are elevated. Which of the following could NOT be a cause of her disorder?

explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • This is a case of peripheral (or GnRH independent) precocious puberty Features of peripheral precocious puberty: Elevated estrogen levels LH and FSH are low GnRH stimulation shows no elevation in serum LH level. Causes of peripheral precocious puberty include all mentioned except pituitary tumors Pituitary tumors cause central (GnRH dependent) precocius puberty

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 28-year-old woman delivered her first child two days ago. She now presents with low-grade fever, uterine tenderness, and foul-smelling vaginal discharge. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the recommended treatment?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Puerperal sepsis; broad-spectrum antibiotics

Explanation:

  • Option A: Normal puerperium is not associated with foul smelling discharge and fever or uterine tenderness, the normal puerperal vaginal discharge is referred to as lochia. Option C: Post partum hemorrhage presents as heavy vaginal bleeding Option D: Pre-eclampsia and PPH do not cause foul smelling discharge or fever

Educational Objective:

  • Puerperal fever is defined as fever > 100.4 ° F OR > 38 ° C on more than 2 occasions after 24h of delivery Puerperal sepsis is defined as an infection of the genital tract occurring at any time between the onset of rupture of membranes or labor and the 42nd day post-partum with Puerperal fever Abnormal vaginal discharge Foul smelling vaginal discharge Uterine sub-involution
  • Ref: Williams Textbook of Obstetrics 25th edition pg 667
explanation.ref: Williams Textbook of Obstetrics 25 th edition pg 667

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 32-year-old woman, P2L2, gave birth to a healthy baby girl. She is breastfeeding and is concerned about her postpartum contraception options. What is the most suitable method for postpartum contraception during lactation for this patient?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Progestin-only contraceptives

Explanation:

  • During lactation, progestin-only contraceptives are generally the most suitable method as they do not interfere with breastfeeding and have a lower risk of side effects compared to combined oral contraceptives.
  • Option. A. Combined oral contraceptives: Combined oral contraceptives (COCs) contain both estrogen and progestin. These are not typically recommended for breastfeeding women, especially in the immediate postpartum period, because estrogen can reduce milk supply. The World Health Organization (WHO) recommends avoiding these during the first six months postpartum for women who are breast feeding Option B. Withdrawal method: is a natural method wit very high failure rate and hence not preferred Option D. Barrier methods : Barrier methods include condoms, diaphragms, and cervical caps. These do not interfere with breastfeeding. However, they are generally less effective at preventing pregnancy than hormonal methods or IUDs
  • Thus, while breastfeeding, progestin-only contraceptives are considered the most appropriate option due to their minimal impact on milk production and safety for the infant

Educational Objective:

  • Contraceptives in breast feeding women
  • Prefer Progesterone containing contraceptives as they do not interfere with lactation and do not increase the risk of thrombo-embolism Intra-uterine devices can also be used. These are to be inserted either within 48h of birth or after 6 weeks of delivery
  • Ref: Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics 8th edition pg 177
explanation.ref: Dutta’s Textbook of Obstetrics 8 th edition pg 177

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
question: A 26-year-old woman, P1L1, is attending her postpartum visit. She gave birth vaginally four weeks ago. Her vital signs are stable, and she has no complaints. What is the most important component of the postpartum visit for this patient?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Contraceptive counseling

Explanation:

  • Contraceptive counseling is a crucial component of the postpartum visit, ensuring that the patient is informed about and has access to appropriate birth control methods to prevent unintended pregnancies.
  • Option A: Blood pressure monitoring and pelvic examinations are important but may not be the primary focus at this stage. Option C: Pap smears are done as per guidelines for cervical cancer screening.

Educational Objective:

  • Ref: Williams Textbook of Obstetrics 25th edition pg 697
